Native speakers use かな constantly; learners often underuse it or confuse it with more direct question forms.

Tier Pattern Description Example N5 — Foundation 〜か (question) Direct question — demands answer 行いきますか？ N4 — Foundation 〜だろう Conjecture — "probably" 行いくだろう。 N4 — Core ← You are here 〜かな Self-directed musing — "I wonder" / soft question / tentative plan 行いくかな。 N3 — Extended 〜ようかな Volitional + かな — tentative self-suggestion 行いこうかな。 N3 — Extended 〜かな + rising intonation Soft question to others — gentle, indirect これでいいかな？ N2 — Awareness 〜かしら Feminine-coded soft question — external-facing 行いくかしら。

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • かな is a sentence-final particle that turns a statement into a gentle mental event — a thought spoken aloud rather than a message delivered to a listener.
  • It is fundamentally self-directed: the primary audience is the speaker themselves. Even when others overhear, the grammar treats the utterance as soliloquy.
  • With falling intonation, it is pure musing: "I wonder if…" With rising intonation, it leaks outward as a soft question: "I wonder… [what do you think?]" With volitional verbs, it becomes tentative planning: "Maybe I'll…"
  • かな never attaches to polite 〜ます forms. It lives in the plain-form, casual register. Using it in formal contexts is a register crash.
  • The か carries the shape of a question; the な softens it into contemplation. Together they create a sound that is questioning without demanding, uncertain without anxious.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Use かな when you want to think aloud, wonder gently, pose a question without pressuring the listener, or float a plan without committing to it. The form says: "This thought is occurring to me, and I am letting it hover."

明日あす雨あめ降ふるかな。 I wonder if it'll rain tomorrow [falling intonation, to myself]. Literal: Tomorrow rain fall-question-contemplate. Context: Pure soliloquy. The speaker is not asking anyone; they are letting a thought drift out. Common when looking at the sky, checking a weather app, or simply starting the day. これでいいかな？ I wonder if this is okay [rising intonation, gentle invitation to respond]. Literal: This-with good-question-contemplate? Context: Soft question to another person. The rising tone leaks the internal musing outward, creating space for the listener to answer without being directly asked. そろそろ帰かえろうかな。 Maybe I'll head home soon [tentative plan, self-suggestion]. Literal: Soon return-volitional-contemplate. Context: The speaker is thinking about leaving. The かな keeps the plan soft — not a decision announced to the room, just a thought being tested aloud.

docs.title: The Three Faces of かな
docs.text: かな operates in three distinct modes. Intonation and context determine which mode is active.

docs.title: Face 1: Self-Directed Musing (Soliloquy)
docs.text:
The default function. The speaker wonders about something to themselves. The listener, if present, is an eavesdropper on the speaker's internal monologue.

どうしようかな。 What should I do, I wonder. The canonical Japanese expression of being lost in thought. Used when facing any minor dilemma. 彼かれ、何なん考かんがえてるかな。 I wonder what he's thinking. Curiosity without demand. The speaker does not expect to find out; they are simply acknowledging the gap in their knowledge. あの人ひと、本当ほんとうに学生がくせいかな。 I wonder if that person is really a student. Gentle skepticism. The doubt is soft, almost affectionate — not an accusation, just a passing thought.

The soliloquy function is socially valuable

In Japanese, thinking aloud is not a failure of privacy — it is a way of sharing your mental state without imposing on others. かな makes the boundary clear: "You may hear this, but I am not addressing you."

docs.title: Face 2: Soft Question to Others (Rising Intonation)
docs.text:
When intonation rises, かな transforms from internal monologue into a gentle, indirect question. The listener is invited to respond, but not required to.

これでいいかな？ Is this okay, I wonder? Rising tone. Much softer than これでいいですか？ which demands a verdict. かな creates space for the listener to say "yes" or simply remain silent. 君きみも行いくかな？ Are you going too, I wonder? Rising tone. A gentle probe. The speaker is interested but not pressuring. Compare to 君くんも行いくの？ which is more direct and potentially intrusive. 明日あす都合つごう悪わるくないかな？ I wonder if tomorrow isn't inconvenient [for you]? Rising tone. A very soft way of checking availability. The negative + かな double-softens the request.

The response is optional

A question in かな does not require an answer. If the listener doesn't respond, the conversation can continue without awkwardness. This is a key face-saving feature.

docs.title: Face 3: Tentative Planning (Volitional + かな)
docs.text:
When attached to a volitional verb (〜よう/〜おう), かな creates tentative self-suggestion — "Maybe I'll…" or "I'm thinking of…"

週末しゅうまつ、映画えいがでも見みようかな。 Maybe I'll watch a movie this weekend. The でも adds "or something" — further softening. The whole utterance is a bubble of possibility, not a plan. そろそろ寝ねようかな。 I guess I'll go to sleep soon. Common bedtime self-talk. The かな makes it a gentle decision rather than a sudden announcement. 新あたらしいカフェ、行いってみようかな。 Maybe I'll try that new café. 〜てみようかな combines attemptive みる with volitional + かな. Highly productive pattern for tentative intentions.

The ようかな collocation

Learn 〜ようかな as a single pragmatic unit. It is one of the most natural ways to express tentative intention in casual Japanese. It is less committal than 〜ようと思おもう ("I'm thinking of…") and softer than 〜よう ("Let's…").

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
かな is grammatically simple but prosodically complex. The same string of sounds can mean three different things depending on pitch and context.

The か layer: Carries the grammatical shape of a question — an open information gap. Without か, the sentence is a statement. With か, it becomes a wondering.

The な layer: Softens the question into contemplation. Where か alone demands an answer (何なんですか?), かな lets the question float. The な is etymologically related to the sentence-ending な that marks prohibition or strong emotion, but in かな it has fully grammaticalized into a softener.

Intonation as meaning:

Intonation Function Example English Falling Pure musing 明日あす雨あめ降ふるかな。 "I wonder if it'll rain." Rising Soft question これでいいかな？ "Is this okay, I wonder?" Level / fall-rise Tentative plan 帰かえろうかな。 "Maybe I'll head home."

Intonation is everything

A falling これでいいかな is a thought the speaker is not sharing. A rising これでいいかな？ is an invitation to the listener. The difference is entirely in the voice.

docs.title: Attachment Rules
docs.text:
かな attaches to the plain form of verbs, adjectives, and nouns. It never attaches to polite 〜ます or 〜です forms.

Category Plain Form + かな Note Verb (present) 行いく 行いくかな Standard Verb (past) 行いった 行いったかな Wondering about past Verb (negative) 行いかない 行いかないかな "I wonder if [they] won't go" i-Adjective 高たかい 高たかいかな "I wonder if it's expensive" na-Adjective 静しずか 静しずかかな No だ needed (静しずかだかな is non-standard) Noun 学生がくせい 学生がくせいかな "I wonder if [they're] a student"

No ます attachment

行いきますかな is ungrammatical. If you need politeness, use 〜でしょうか or 〜ますか. かな is fundamentally casual and plain-form.

No だ before かな with nouns/na-adjectives

While 静しずかだ can take sentence-final particles like ね or よ, かな attaches directly to the stem: 静しずかかな. Adding だ (静しずかだかな) is non-standard and sounds awkward.

docs.title: In Real Life
docs.text:
Pure soliloquy

Situation: 春ハル is packing for a trip. She holds up a jacket, looks out the window at gray clouds, speaks to no one in particular.

明日あす雨あめ降ふるかな。

"I wonder if it'll rain tomorrow."

Why かな fits here: Haru is not asking her roommate for a weather forecast. She is thinking aloud, letting the concern surface. The かな marks the utterance as internal. If her roommate happens to answer, it's a bonus, not an obligation.

What makes this natural: The falling intonation, the lack of eye contact, the context of solitary activity. The かな is the sound of a mind working in real time.

Soft question to a partner

Situation: 健ケン has cooked dinner for 由衣ユイ. He sets the plate down and hesitates.

これでいいかな？

"I wonder if this is okay?"

Why かな fits here: Ken could ask これでいい？ (direct) or これでいいですか？ (formal request for judgment). かな creates a gentle vulnerability — he is exposing his uncertainty rather than demanding validation. The rising intonation invites Yui to reassure him, but the かな makes it easy for her to simply start eating if she doesn't want to perform reassurance.

What the かな protects: Direct questions can feel like tests. かな feels like a shared wondering.

Tentative planning

Situation: Friday afternoon. 涼リョウ is messaging friends about weekend plans, but nothing is confirmed. He thinks aloud.

特とくに予定よていないし、家いえでゴロゴロしようかな。

"I don't have any particular plans, so maybe I'll just laze around at home."

Why ようかな fits here: Ryo is not announcing a decision. He is floating a possibility. The かな keeps the door open — if a friend invites him out, he can easily abandon this tentative plan without having committed to it. The し adds "and so" — causal softness.

What makes this natural: The ようかな pattern is the grammar of uncommitted weekends. It is less lonely than 家かでゴロゴロする (flat declaration) and less demanding than 家かでゴロゴロしよう (suggestion to others).

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
Japanese communication often avoids direct statements of intent or direct questions that pin the listener down. かな is a key tool in this indirectness — it allows speakers to exist in a state of productive uncertainty, to think aloud without imposing, to plan without committing.

This reflects the broader value of 間ま — the space between things. かな creates verbal 間ま: a pause, a breath, a gap where the thought can hover without landing.

  • Soliloquy as social grace. By making your thought process audible but not directive, you allow others to opt in or opt out. They can answer your かな, ignore it, or simply acknowledge it with うん. None of these responses is socially wrong.
  • Tentative planning as face-saving. A plan announced boldly (行いくよ) creates accountability. A plan mused in かな (行いこうかな) creates wiggle room. If circumstances change, the speaker can retreat without having broken a promise.
  • Gentle doubt as kindness. 本当ほんとうかな ("I wonder if that's true") is softer than 本当ほんとう？ ("Really?"). The first expresses curiosity; the second challenges. かな turns potential confrontation into shared contemplation.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text:
Grammar Point Core Meaning Emotional / Pragmatic Nuance Trigger Context JLPT 〜かな "I wonder…" Self-directed musing; soft question; tentative plan Thinking aloud; gentle probing; uncommitted planning N4 〜かしら "I wonder…" Feminine-coded; more external-facing; slightly more certain in questioning Women speaking to others; gentle but less internal N3 〜だろう "Probably…" Conjecture; expects agreement; more assertive than かな Guessing with confidence; seeking confirmation N4 〜かもしれない "Maybe…" Possibility marker; attaches to predicate, not sentence Expressing probability N4 〜ね "…right?" Seeks confirmation; assumes shared knowledge Checking alignment; gentle assertion N5 〜よ "…you know" Asserts information; pushes toward listener Informing; insisting N5 〜の Feminine explanatory Intimate, self-explanatory Casual feminine speech N4

かな vs. かしら: Both mean "I wonder." かしら is more strongly associated with female speech and is more often directed at others (外部がいぶ向むけ). かな is more internal, more gender-neutral, and more common in self-talk. A man using かしら sounds theatrical or feminine-coded; a woman using かな is completely standard.

かな vs. だろう: だろう says "I think X, and I'm fairly sure." かな says "X occurs to me, and I'm not sure." だろう is conjecture; かな is wonder.

かな vs. かもしれない: 行いくかもしれない means "I might go" — probability. 行いくかな means "I wonder if I'll go" — internal questioning. The first is about likelihood; the second is about mental state.

docs.title: 〜かしら (feminine soft question)
docs.text:
More external-facing, slightly more decorative.

Example 彼かれ、行いくかしら。 English "I wonder if he'll go." Intent Gentle question, often to another person or to oneself with feminine texture vs. かな かしら is more social; かな is more internal. かしら sounds softer and slightly more elegant; かな sounds more everyday and neutral.

docs.title: 〜だろう / でしょう (conjecture)
docs.text:
More assertive, expects agreement.

Example 明日あす雨あめ降ふるだろう。 English "It'll probably rain tomorrow." Intent Confident guess, often seeking listener agreement vs. かな だろう closes the gap; かな keeps it open. だろう is a statement with a question mark; かな is a question with no demand for answer.

docs.title: 〜かもしれない (possibility)
docs.text:
Probability, not musing.

Example 明日あす雨あめが降ふるかもしれない。 English "It might rain tomorrow." Intent Objective assessment of likelihood vs. かな かもしれない is about the world; かな is about the speaker's mind.

docs.title: 〜？ (direct question with plain form)
docs.text:
More direct, more demanding.

Example これでいい？ English "Is this okay?" Intent Direct question requiring response vs. かな かな softens the demand almost to zero. これでいい？ is a test; これでいいかな？ is a whisper.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
かな is fundamentally casual and plain-form. It does not survive formalization.

Casual Casual (tentative plan) Basic Polite (avoiding かな) Extra Polite (avoiding かな)

明日あす雨あめ降ふるかな。

"I wonder if it'll rain tomorrow."

Shift: Pure casual soliloquy. Falling intonation. No politeness markers.

そろそろ帰かえろうかな。

"Maybe I'll head home soon."

Shift: Volitional + かな. The standard pattern for uncommitted plans.

明日あす雨あめが降ふるでしょうか。

"I wonder if it will rain tomorrow [polite]."

Shift: かな cannot be polite. The alternative is 〜でしょうか, which carries similar wondering but with formal distance. The self-directed intimacy is lost.

明日あすは雨あめが降ふるかどうか、存ぞんじ上あげません。

"I do not know whether it will rain tomorrow."

Shift: Complete lexical replacement. かな is impossible in formal register. The humble form and explicit embedding replace the soft musing entirely.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: Step Pattern What It Adds Example N5 — Foundation 〜か (question) Direct question formation 行いきますか？ N5 — Foundation 〜ね / 〜よ Sentence-final particles — confirmation / assertion 行いくね。 / 行いくよ。 N4 — Foundation 〜だろう Conjecture — "probably" 行いくだろう。 N4 — Core ← You are here 〜かな Self-directed musing; soft question; tentative plan 行いくかな。 N3 — Extended 〜ようかな Volitional + かな — tentative self-suggestion 行いこうかな。 N3 — Extended 〜かな + rising intonation Soft question to others これでいいかな？ N2 — Awareness 〜かしら Feminine-coded soft question 行いくかしら。

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: どうしようかな。 What should I do, I wonder. The universal expression of mild dilemma. Used for everything from spilled coffee to life decisions. 彼かれ、何なん考かんがえてるかな。 I wonder what he's thinking. Curiosity without intrusion. The speaker does not demand to know; they simply let the thought exist. これでいいかな？ I wonder if this is okay [gentle check]? Rising intonation. Soft question to another person. Creates space for reassurance or simple acceptance. そろそろ寝ねようかな。 I guess I'll go to sleep soon. Volitional + かな. Tentative bedtime decision. Common self-talk. 新あたらしいカフェ、行いってみようかな。 Maybe I'll try that new café. 〜てみようかな — attemptive + volitional + かな. Highly productive pattern for tentative intentions. あの人ひと、本当ほんとうに学生がくせいかな。 I wonder if that person is really a student. Gentle skepticism. The doubt is soft, almost affectionate — not an accusation. 週末しゅうまつ、何なんしようかな。 What should I do this weekend, I wonder. Volitional question + かな. The open weekend stretched out in thought. 本当ほんとうかな。 I wonder if that's true. Minimal, powerful. Can express doubt, hope, or simple curiosity depending on intonation.

docs.title: 1. Attaching かな to polite ます form
docs.text:
Ungrammatical

行いきますかな。

~かな attaches to plain form only. ますかな is a register collision.~

Correct

行いくかな。

"I wonder if [someone/I] will go." — Plain form + かな.

docs.title: 2. Using かな in formal contexts
docs.text:
Register crash

明日あすの会議かいぎに出席しゅっせきしますかな。

"I wonder if I'll attend tomorrow's meeting." — In a business email or formal conversation, かな sounds childish or inappropriate.

Appropriate

明日あすの会議かいぎに出席しゅっせきするかどうか、まだ検討けんとう中ちゅうです。

"Whether I will attend tomorrow's meeting is still under consideration." — Formal, appropriate.

docs.title: 3. Confusing かな with embedded question か + copula な
docs.text:
Structural misread

Parsing 彼かれが来くるかな as [彼かれが来くるか] + [な] (copula)

~In かな as a single particle, the な is not the copula. It is a softener. However, in very rare contexts, you might see [clause]か + な (copula) + ね, but this is distinct from the particle かな.~

Understanding

かな is a single sentence-final particle unit. Do not break it apart analytically in normal usage.

docs.title: 4. Using かな for direct questions that require answers
docs.text:
Socially confusing

あなたの名前なまえは何なんですかな？

"I wonder what your name is?" — Bizarre. Direct factual questions should use 〜ですか. Using かな makes it sound like you're talking to yourself about someone who should introduce themselves.

Appropriate

お名前なまえは何なんですか。

"What is your name?" — Direct, polite, standard.

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text:
Collocation Meaning どうしようかな "What should I do, I wonder" — the universal dilemma phrase 行いこうかな "Maybe I'll go" — tentative volitional 食たべようかな "Maybe I'll eat" — tentative plan 見みてみようかな "Maybe I'll try watching/looking" — tentative attempt そろそろ〜ようかな "Maybe I'll [do] soon" — gentle initiation 〜かな、やっぱり "I wonder… after all" — reconsideration 本当ほんとうかな "I wonder if it's true" — gentle doubt 寂さびしいかな "I wonder if I'm lonely" — vulnerable musing これでいいかな "I wonder if this is okay" — soft check 何なんしようかな "What should I do, I wonder" — open planning

These collocations are worth learning as complete pragmatic units. どうしようかな and 〜ようかな especially are daily-use patterns.

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• かな can express loneliness. 寂さびしいかな is not necessarily a question about whether one is lonely — it is an acknowledgment of loneliness in soft, bearable form. The かな creates distance from the emotion, making it easier to express.
  • The ellipsis is part of the grammar. どうしようかな… with trailing dots is more common than without. The dots represent the thought trailing off into silence — the natural habitat of かな.
  • かな can be used rhetorically to stall. When a speaker doesn't want to answer directly, they might respond with そうかな… ("I wonder if that's so…") — a gentle, face-saving disagreement that never becomes explicit.
  • Volitional + かな is less committal than volitional + と思おもう. 行いこうかな means "I'm thinking about going, maybe." 行いこうと思おもう means "I'm planning to go." The first is a bubble; the second is a trajectory.
  • かな can soften commands into suggestions. これやっといてかな ("I wonder if I should get this done…") is actually a self-directed command that sounds like a question. The かな turns obligation into contemplation.
  • In some contexts, かな sounds childlike. An adult using かな too frequently can sound like they're constantly unsure. But in intimate contexts, this same quality sounds vulnerable and trustworthy.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N4 Recognize かな in speech; understand the self-directed musing function; produce basic 〜かな and 〜ようかな correctly; avoid attaching to ます form; distinguish from かしら Developing — N4→N3 Deploy かな naturally for tentative plans; use rising intonation for soft questions; understand when だろう or かもしれない would be more appropriate; control the social softness of かな Automatic — N3→N2 かな registers as a mental state marker — you hear it and immediately know the speaker is thinking aloud, not asserting. The three faces (musing, soft question, tentative plan) become intuitively distinct. Mastery You use かな to create social space — to think without imposing, to plan without committing, to doubt without confronting. You know when it sounds vulnerable and when it sounds evasive. You also know when to replace it with でしょうか in formal contexts without losing the wondering intent.

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 〜か (question) — the standalone question particle
  • 〜ようかな — volitional + かな; tentative planning
  • 〜かしら — feminine-coded soft question

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 〜かしら — more feminine, more external-facing
  • 〜だろう — conjecture, more assertive
  • 〜かもしれない — possibility, objective probability
  • 〜ね — confirmation-seeking, not wondering
  • 〜？ (plain question) — direct, demanding answer

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• Plain verb forms (N5) — required for かな attachment
  • Volitional form (N4) — 〜よう/〜おう + かな
  • Sentence-final particles (N5) — the な softener
  • Question intonation (N5) — rising vs. falling

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 〜かしら (N3) — feminine soft question
  • 〜かのように (N2) — "as if…"
  • 〜かと思おもった (N3) — "I wondered if…"
  • 〜かどうか (N4) — embedded yes/no questions

docs.title: 〜みたい — Seems Like / Looks Like / Apparently
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — Upper N4 / Lower N3

The base meaning of 〜みたい as "resemblance" is N4. But the full pragmatic range — soft observation, hearsay packaging, gentle disagreement avoidance, and the gendered/casual texture that distinguishes it from 〜そう and 〜よう — is solidly lower N3. Native speakers choose みたい over its rivals constantly; learners often default to そう because it's taught first.

Tier Pattern Description Example N5 — Foundation 〜そう (na-adj type) "Seems…" — immediate sensory impression 美味おいしそう。"Looks delicious." N4 — Foundation 〜よう（だ） "Seems like…" — reasoned inference, formal 雨あめが降ふるようだ。"It appears it will rain." N4 — Core ← You are here 〜みたい Resemblance + soft conjecture; casual, personal, hearsay-friendly 彼かれ、疲つかれてるみたい。"He seems tired." N3 — Extended 〜みたいだよね Soft observation + confirmation seeking 明日あした雨あめみたいだよね。 N3 — Extended 〜みたいな + noun Resemblance modifier 夢ゆめみたいな話はなし。"A dream-like story." N2 — Awareness 〜みたいに / 〜みたく Adverbial resemblance 彼かれみたいに上手じょうずに話はなしたい。

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 〜みたい expresses resemblance and soft conjecture — "seems like," "looks like," "apparently," "kind of like." It is the most casual and interpersonal of the Japanese conjecture markers.
  • Unlike 〜そう (immediate sensory impression) or 〜ようだ (formal reasoned inference), 〜みたい carries a personal, subjective, often hearsay-tinged quality. It says "This is my impression" rather than "This is what the evidence shows."
  • The form derives from 見みたい ("want to see") but has fully grammaticalized into a resemblance marker. The etymological ghost of "wanting to see" still lingers — みたい often introduces something the speaker has just noticed or is tentatively offering for consideration.
  • In hearsay contexts, 〜みたい packages reported information softly — "I heard something like…" — without committing to the source or the truth. This is gossip grammar, gentle correction grammar, and save-face grammar all in one.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Use 〜みたい when you want to express a personal impression, resemblance, or soft conjecture — especially when you want to avoid sounding certain, when relaying hearsay, or when making an observation feel gentle and shareable rather than analytical and definitive.

彼かれ、疲つかれてるみたい。 He seems tired [that's my impression]. Literal: He, tired [te-form] seems-like. Context: Soft observation. The speaker isn't diagnosing — they're offering an impression. The みたい makes it tentative and open to correction. 明日あした雨あめみたいだよね。 Seems like it'll rain tomorrow, right? Literal: Tomorrow rain seems-like is, right? Context: Weather hearsay or personal prediction. The みたい softens the claim; the よね invites shared acknowledgment. 夢ゆめみたい。 It's like a dream. Literal: Dream seems-like. Context: Pure resemblance. No conjecture — just comparison. The みたい here is closest to its etymological root: "like something I might see."

docs.title: The Three Conjecture Markers: そう・よう・みたい
docs.text:
Japanese has three major conjecture markers, and choosing between them is one of the most nuanced decisions in casual speech. They are not interchangeable.

Marker Core Meaning Source of Evidence Register Certainty Example 〜そう Immediate sensory impression Direct visual/auditory/sensory evidence Neutral High (but superficial) 美味おいしそう。"Looks delicious [I can see it]." 〜よう（だ） Reasoned inference Logical deduction, formal observation Formal High (analytical) 雨あめが降ふるようだ。"It appears it will rain [based on evidence]." 〜みたい Personal impression / resemblance Intuition, hearsay, soft observation Casual Low (tentative) 雨あめみたいだね。"Seems like rain, huh [I heard / I feel]."

docs.title: The Critical Distinctions
docs.text:
〜そう requires direct sensory access. You say 美味おいしそう while looking at food. You cannot say 明日あす雨あめが降ふりそう based on a weather report — that would require みたい or よう, because you haven't directly sensed tomorrow's rain.

〜ようだ is analytical and formal. It says "The evidence points to X." It distances the speaker from the claim, making it objective. 雨あめが降ふるようだ is what a meteorologist says — or what you say when you want to sound like one.

〜みたい is personal and soft. It says "I get the impression of X" or "Someone said something like X." It invites agreement without demanding it. It is the grammar of tentative sharing.

彼かれ、怒おこってるみたい。 He seems mad [that's my impression / I heard]. みたい: Personal, soft, possibly hearsay. You might not have seen him yourself. 彼かれ、怒おこってるようだ。 He appears to be angry [based on observable evidence]. ようだ: Analytical, formal. You've seen the evidence and drawn a conclusion. 彼かれ、怒おこってるそうだ。 I heard he's angry [hearsay, formal]. そうだ: Reported information, formal hearsay. Someone told you.

docs.title: Layer 1: Resemblance (Noun + みたい)
docs.text:
The original function: X is like Y. Pure comparison, no conjecture.

夢ゆめみたいな場所ばしょ。 A dream-like place. Literal: Dream seems-like place. The みたい modifies the noun 場所ばしょ through な. Pure resemblance. 彼かれみたいな人ひとになりたい。 I want to become a person like him. Literal: He seems-like person become-want. Resemblance as aspiration. The みたい creates the model.

docs.title: Layer 2: Soft Conjecture (Clause + みたい)
docs.text:
The grammaticalized function: It seems that… Personal impression, tentative claim.

彼かれ、知しらないみたい。 He doesn't seem to know [my impression]. Literal: He, not-know seems-like. Soft observation. You're not certain — you're offering what you think. 明日あした試験しけんがあるみたい。 Seems like there's a test tomorrow [I heard / I think]. Literal: Tomorrow test exists seems-like. Could be hearsay, could be deduction from context. The みたい leaves the source vague.

docs.title: Layer 3: Hearsay Packaging (Reported + みたい)
docs.text:
When relaying information from others, みたい softens the claim and distances the speaker from its truth.

田中たなかさん、転職てんしょくするみたいよ。 Seems like Tanaka is changing jobs [I heard]. Literal: Tanaka, job-change do seems-like [feminine softener]. The よ marks this as shareable gossip. The みたい protects the speaker — "This is just what I heard."

docs.title: In Real Life
docs.text:
Soft observation

Situation: ユイ and ハル are at a café. They see their friend ケン across the room. He's staring at his phone, shoulders slumped.

ケン、疲つかれてるみたいだね。

"Ken seems tired, huh."

Why みたい fits here: Yui isn't diagnosing Ken — she's offering an impression for shared consideration. 疲つかれてるそう would require direct sensory evidence she might not have (she's across the room). 疲つかれてるようだ would sound clinical and certain. みたい is exactly right: soft, personal, inviteable.

What the だね adds: The だ grounds the observation; the ね invites Haru's agreement. Together they create a conspiratorial moment of shared noticing.

Hearsay gossip

Situation: マユ tells リナ something she heard at work.

部長ぶちょう、来月らいげつ異動いどうみたいよ。

"Seems like the department head is transferring next month [I heard]."

Why みたい fits here: Mayu didn't see the transfer order. She heard a rumor. みたい packages the rumor as impression rather than fact, protecting both herself and the listener from misinformation liability. The よ marks it as juicy shareable news.

What そうだ would change: 異動いどうするそうだ would be more formal hearsay — "It is said that…" This sounds like official reporting. みたい sounds like break-room gossip. The choice is social, not factual.

Gentle correction avoidance

Situation: シュン thinks the meeting is at 3. ミキ thinks it's at 2. Miki doesn't want to contradict directly.

私わたし、2時じみたいな気きがするんだけど…

"I kind of feel like it's 2 o'clock…"

Why みたい fits here: Miki isn't saying "You're wrong." She's saying "I have an impression of 2 o'clock." The みたい + 気きがする double-softens the contradiction. The んだけど trails off, inviting Shun to correct himself without losing face.

docs.title: Attachment Rules
docs.text:
Preceding Form Attachment Example Noun noun + みたい 夢ゆめみたい Na-adjective (stem) stem + みたい 静しずかみたい i-adjective (plain) adj + みたい 高たかいみたい Verb (plain) verb + みたい 行いくみたい Verb (past) past + みたい 行いったみたい Negative (plain) nai + みたい 行いかないみたい

No だ before みたい with nouns/na-adjectives

Unlike 〜ようだ which requires だ (静しずかだようだ), 〜みたい attaches directly: 静しずかみたい. Adding だ produces 静しずかだみたい, which is acceptable in very casual speech but non-standard.

Standard Very casual (acceptable) 静しずかみたい 静しずかだみたい 学生がくせいみたい 学生がくせいだみたい

docs.title: Modifier Forms
docs.text:
Function Form Example Predicate 〜みたい（だ） 疲つかれてるみたいだ。 Noun modifier 〜みたいな + noun 夢ゆめみたいな話はなし。 Adverbial 〜みたいに / 〜みたく 彼かれみたいに走はしる。

The みたく contraction

みたいに sometimes contracts to みたく in casual speech, especially before adjectives: 彼かれみたく上手うまくなりたい ("I want to become skilled like him"). This is informal but common.

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
Japanese communication values tentativeness over certainty in social contexts. Direct claims risk contradiction, embarrassment, or social friction. 〜みたい is the grammar of productive uncertainty — it allows speakers to share observations, relay information, and even disagree without committing to truth.

The form is deeply interpersonal. Unlike 〜ようだ which can be solitary (you observe, you conclude), 〜みたい almost always implies an audience — someone to share the impression with, someone to confirm or correct.

  • Impression as social gift. Offering a みたい observation ("He seems tired") is not just reporting — it's inviting the listener into your perceptual world. The listener can agree, disagree, or add their own impression. The conversation builds from there.
  • Hearsay as protection. Packaging information as みたい protects both speaker and source. If the rumor is false, the speaker can retreat to "I only said it seemed that way." This is not dishonesty — it is social grace.
  • Resemblance as aspiration. 〜みたいな is the grammar of role models and ideals. 彼かれみたいになりたい doesn't mean "I want to become him" — it means "I want to become the kind of person he represents." The resemblance is categorical, not literal.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text:
Grammar Point Core Meaning Evidence Source Register Certainty JLPT 〜みたい Seems like / looks like Personal impression, hearsay, intuition Casual Low (tentative) N4 〜そう (na-adj type) Looks / seems [sensory] Direct visual/auditory evidence Neutral High (superficial) N4 〜よう（だ） Appears that / seems that Reasoned inference, formal observation Formal High (analytical) N4 〜らしい Apparently / reportedly Hearsay, external report Neutral-formal Medium (reported) N3 〜だろう / でしょう Probably Subjective probability Neutral Medium N4 〜かもしれない Maybe Possibility Neutral Low (possibility) N4

The hearsay spectrum: - 〜そうだ (formal hearsay): 転職てんしょくするそうだ — "It is said that…" [official, distanced] - 〜らしい: 転職てんしょくらしい — "Apparently…" [neutral, external source] - 〜みたい: 転職てんしょくみたい — "Seems like…" [casual, personal, gossipy]

docs.title: 〜そう (sensory impression)
docs.text:
Direct visual evidence only. More certain but more superficial.

Example 美味おいしそう。 English "Looks delicious [I can see it]." Intent Immediate sensory judgment vs. みたい You must be experiencing the stimulus directly. You cannot say 明日あす雨あめが降ふりそう based on a forecast.

docs.title: 〜よう（だ） (formal inference)
docs.text:
Reasoned, analytical, distanced.

Example 雨あめが降ふるようだ。 English "It appears it will rain." Intent Objective observation based on evidence vs. みたい Removes personal impression. Sounds like a report, not a sharing.

docs.title: 〜らしい (reported/hearsay)
docs.text:
External source, more formal than みたい.

Example 転職てんしょくするらしい。 English "Apparently they're changing jobs." Intent Relaying information from an external source vs. みたい らしい implies you have a source; みたい leaves the source vague. らしい is more journalistic; みたい is more conversational.

docs.title: 〜だろう / でしょう (probability)
docs.text:
Subjective guess, often with rising intonation.

Example 雨あめだろう？ English "Probably rain, right?" Intent Probability judgment, often seeking confirmation vs. みたい だろう is more assertive; みたい is more impressionistic.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
〜みたい is fundamentally casual. Using it in formal contexts creates register clash.

Casual Basic Polite Extra Polite

彼かれ、疲つかれてるみたいだね。

"He seems tired, huh."

Shift: Standard casual. Soft observation with confirmation seeking. The みたい is natural and expected.

彼かれ、疲つかれているようですね。

"He appears to be tired, doesn't he."

Shift: みたい → ようです. The observation becomes more analytical and distanced. The ね remains for soft confirmation.

彼かれは疲労ひろうを感かんじさせる様子ようすです。

"He gives the impression of experiencing fatigue."

Shift: Complete lexical replacement. みたい is impossible in this register. The observation is packaged as objective behavioral analysis.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: Step Pattern What It Adds Example N5 — Foundation Noun + のよう（だ） Formal resemblance 夢ゆめのようだ。"It's like a dream." N5 — Foundation 〜そう（na-adj type） Sensory impression 美味おいしそう。"Looks delicious." N4 — Foundation 〜よう（だ） Formal conjecture 雨あめが降ふるようだ。"It appears it will rain." N4 — Core ← You are here 〜みたい Casual resemblance + soft conjecture 疲つかれてるみたい。"Seems tired." N3 — Extended 〜みたいな + noun Resemblance modifier 夢ゆめみたいな話はなし。"A dream-like story." N3 — Extended 〜みたいに / 〜みたく Adverbial resemblance 彼かれみたいに話はなす。"Speak like him." N2 — Awareness 〜みたいだよね Soft observation + confirmation 明日あした雨あめみたいだよね。

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
彼かれ、知しらないみたい。 He doesn't seem to know [my impression]. Literal: He, not-know seems-like.

~Context: Offering a tentative read of someone's mental state. The みたい protects against being wrong — "This is just how it looks to me."~

田中たなかさん、来月らいげつ結婚けっこんするみたいよ。 Seems like Tanaka is getting married next month [I heard]. Literal: Tanaka, next-month marriage do seems-like [feminine softener].

~Context: Gossip packaging. The みたい + よ creates shareable, non-committal news. The speaker isn't vouching for the information — just passing along an impression.~

夢ゆめみたいな場所ばしょだね。 It's a dream-like place, isn't it. Literal: Dream seems-like place, right?

~Context: Pure resemblance. No conjecture — just comparison. The みたい creates the poetic frame; the だね invites shared wonder.~

私わたし、間違まちがってるみたい… I seem to be wrong… Literal: I, mistaken seems-like…

~Context: Self-correction, soft admission. The みたい makes the mistake feel discovered rather than confessed — "It appears I'm wrong" is gentler than "I was wrong."~

昔むかしみたいに遊あそびたい。 I want to play like we used to. Literal: Old-times seems-like play-want.

~Context: Nostalgic resemblance. The みたいに creates the temporal comparison — not literally the past, but the quality of the past.~

彼かれみたいになりたくない。 I don't want to become like him. Literal: He seems-like become-want-not.

~Context: Negative aspiration. The みたい defines the type of person — the category one rejects.~

docs.title: 1. Using みたい for direct sensory evidence
docs.text:
Evidence mismatch

このケーキけーき、美味おいしみたい。

"This cake seems delicious [but I haven't tasted or seen it]." — If you're looking at the cake, 美味おいしそう is correct. みたい implies impression without direct evidence.

Correct

このケーキけーき、美味おいしそう。

"This cake looks delicious." — Direct visual evidence.~

docs.title: 2. Using みたい in formal writing
docs.text:
Register crash

本件ほんけんにつきまして、問題もんだいがあるみたいです。

"Regarding this matter, there seems to be a problem." — In business writing, みたい is inappropriately casual. Use ようです or あるようです.

Correct

本件ほんけんにつきまして、問題もんだいがあるようです。

"Regarding this matter, it appears there is a problem." — Formal, appropriate.~

docs.title: 3. Adding だ before みたい with nouns
docs.text:
Grammatically non-standard

学生がくせいだみたい。

"Seems like a student [with intrusive だ]." — Acceptable in very casual speech but non-standard. Most contexts prefer 学生がくせいみたい.

Standard

学生がくせいみたい。

"Seems like a student." — Clean, standard casual.~

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text:
Collocation Meaning 〜みたいだよね "Seems like…, right?" — soft observation + confirmation 〜みたいな気きがする "I kind of feel like…" — double-softened impression 〜みたいなもの "Something like…" — vague categorization 〜みたいに見みえる "Looks like…" — visual resemblance 〜みたいに聞きこえる "Sounds like…" — auditory resemblance 〜みたいに思おもえる "Feels like…" — subjective impression 夢ゆめみたい "Like a dream" — pure resemblance, wonder 嘘うそみたい "Like a lie / unbelievable" — skeptical resemblance 本当ほんとうみたい "Like it's real / seems real" — authenticity judgment

~These collocations are worth learning as complete pragmatic units. The みたいな気きがする pattern especially is a daily-use softener.~

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• 〜みたい is gender-neutral but intonation-coded. The same form can sound masculine (flat, dropped) or feminine (rising, extended) depending on delivery. The grammar itself carries no gender, but the prosody does.
  • The よね combination is socially powerful. みたいだよね transforms personal impression into collective assumption. It says "We both see this, don't we?" — creating shared reality through soft suggestion.
  • 〜みたい can be used for self-deprecating resemblance. 私わたくしみたいな人ひと ("a person like me") is often humble or self-critical. The form allows speakers to categorize themselves without claiming identity.
  • Hearsay みたい protects the source. Unlike 〜そうだ which often implies a specific informant, 〜みたい leaves the information trail muddy. This is useful for gossip, rumors, and sensitive information.
  • 〜みたい can soften corrections to near-invisibility. 違ちがうみたい ("Seems like that's different") is so soft it barely contradicts. The speaker can retreat completely if challenged — "I only said it seemed that way."
  • The etymological ghost of 見みたい ("want to see") still flickers. In some contexts, みたい carries a faint sense of "I want to see if…" or "It looks like something I'd want to see." This is not active meaning but residual flavor.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N4 Produce 〜みたい correctly with all verb/adjective types; distinguish from 〜そう and 〜ようだ in basic contexts; use for soft observation and resemblance Developing — N4→N3 Deploy みたい naturally in hearsay and gossip; use みたいな for noun modification; recognize when そう or ようだ would be more appropriate; control the だ insertion with nouns Automatic — N3→N2 The form arrives as a single social move — you hear みたい and immediately register "soft impression, possibly hearsay." The choice between みたい and らしい becomes intuitive. Mastery You control the full conjecture spectrum: そう (sensory), ようだ (analytical), みたい (personal), らしい (reported), だろう (probabilistic). You know when みたい protects face, when it shares gossip, when it creates poetry, and when it avoids confrontation. You also hear the ghost of 見みたい in nostalgic or wistful usage.

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 〜そう（だ） — Sensory impression; immediate evidence
  • 〜よう（だ） — Formal conjecture; reasoned inference
  • 〜らしい — Reported information; external hearsay

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 〜そう — Requires direct sensory access; more certain
  • 〜ようだ — Formal, analytical, distanced
  • 〜らしい — More formal hearsay; implies external source
  • 〜だろう — Probability judgment; more assertive

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• Noun + のよう（だ） (N5) — Formal resemblance
  • Na-adjective + な (N5) — The な linker that みたい replaces
  • Plain verb forms (N5) — Required for みたい attachment
  • Hearsay そうだ (N4) — The formal counterpart

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 〜みたいな + noun (N3) — Resemblance modification
  • 〜みたいに / 〜みたく (N3) — Adverbial resemblance
  • 〜みたいだよね (N3) — Soft observation + confirmation
  • 〜かのよう（だ） (N2) — "As if…" — theatrical resemblance

docs.title: 謙譲けんじょう語ご — Humble and Extra-Modest Speech
Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 謙譲けんじょう語ご (kenjōgo) is the Japanese humble language — it lowers the speaker (or the speaker's in-group) in order to show respect to the listener or to a third party.
  • There are two types of 謙譲けんじょう語ご:
    • 謙譲けんじょう語ご I (Humble I): The speaker's action is directed toward the respected person. The lowering highlights the target's elevated status. e.g. ご説明せつめいする (I humbly explain to you).
    • 謙譲けんじょう語ご II (Humble II / Extra-Modest): The speaker lowers themselves as a manner of speaking, regardless of who the action targets. It shows formal politeness to the listener. e.g. 私わたくしが参まいります (I will go [formal]).
  • Some verbs have special humble forms — いる → おる, 行いく・来くる → 参まいる, 言ゆう → 申もうす, する → 致いたす.
  • The お・ご + verb stem + する pattern is the standard way to create humble expressions from most verbs when the action involves the respected person.
  • The reference sentence from the 〜てある grammar page — そのことはもう社長しゃちょうにお話はなししてあります — uses this pattern: お + 話はなし (stem of 話はなす) + する. It humbly describes the speaker's action toward the company president, while still claiming responsibility through 〜てある.

docs.title: Why "Extra Modest"? — The Two Layers of 謙譲けんじょう語ご
docs.text:
Many learners first encounter 謙譲けんじょう語ご as simply "humble" — the speaker lowers their own actions when those actions affect someone higher. But Japanese grammar divides this into two distinct categories. Understanding the difference is essential for sounding natural in formal and professional settings.

謙譲けんじょう語ご I (Humble I) 謙譲けんじょう語ご II (Humble II / 丁重ていちょう語ご) What it does Lowers the speaker's action toward a respected person. Lowers the speaker's existence/actions as a formal mannerism, showing politeness to the listener (regardless of the action's target). Who is respected? The person the action is directed at. The listener (the person you're speaking to). Does the action need a target? Yes — the action goes to someone, and that someone is respected. No — the action can be self-contained (going, being, saying). Example Verb 伺うかがう (to visit/humble → go to a superior) 参まいる (to go/come → formal self-reference) When to use Talking about doing something for/at/to a superior or customer. Formal speech, announcements, business letters, speeches — showing general modesty to the audience.

The terms

In Japanese, 謙譲けんじょう語ご I is sometimes called 謙譲けんじょう語ごA, and 謙譲けんじょう語ご II is 謙譲けんじょう語ごB or 丁重ていちょう語ご (teichōgo). The "extra modest" in the title of this page refers primarily to 謙譲けんじょう語ご II — the layer of formality that goes beyond direct action-target respect.

docs.title: Special Humble Verbs — The Core Set
docs.text: Some of the most frequently used verbs have fixed humble equivalents that you must memorise. The same verb often has different forms depending on whether you're using Humble I or Humble II.

docs.title: Humble I vs. Humble II — Side by Side
docs.text:
Plain Verb Humble I (action toward respected person) Humble II / Extra-Modest (formal self-reference) いる (to be / to exist) — (rarely used in Humble I) おる 行いく (to go) 伺うかがう (visit a superior) 参まいる (go / come, formal) 来くる (to come) — 参まいる (come, formal) 言いう (to say) 申もうし上あげる (say to a superior) 申もうす (say, formal self-reference) する (to do) 致いたす (do for a superior, context-dependent) 致いたす (do, formal) 見みる (to see) 拝見はいけんする (look at / see, humble) — 聞きく (to ask / listen) 伺うかがう (ask / visit) 伺うかがう can also be formal self-reference 食たべる・飲のむ (to eat/drink) 頂いたく (receive / partake) 頂いたく (formal humble consumption) 知しっている (to know) 存ぞんじ上あげる (I am aware of [your situation]) 存ぞんじる (I know, formal) あげる (to give) 差さし上あげる (give to a superior) — もらう (to receive) 頂いたく (receive from a superior) —

おる — the extra-modest いる

おる is the Humble II equivalent of いる. It lowers the speaker's own existence in formal settings. You will hear it constantly in business Japanese, announcements, and customer service.

いる おる 私わたくしはここにいます。 私わたくしはここにおります。 "I am here." (neutral/polite) "I am here." (humble/formal — the speaker is lowering themselves to the listener)

おる is also used to humbly refer to one's own in-group members: 父ちちは家いえにおります ("My father is at home").

docs.title: The お・ご + Verb Stem + する Pattern
docs.text:
For verbs that do not have a special humble form, the standard 謙譲けんじょう語ご I construction is:

お／ご + [verb stem] + する

This describes the speaker doing something for or directed toward a respected person. It lowers the action because it's being done in service of someone higher.

docs.title: Formation Rules
docs.text:
Verb Type Prefix Pattern Example Native Japanese verb (和語わご) お お + stem + する 話はなす → お話はなしする Sino-Japanese verb (漢語かんご, often する-verbs) ご ご + stem + する 説明せつめいする → ご説明せつめいする Exceptions / special verbs (use special form) — する → 致いたす, not ご〜する Plain Verb Humble (お/ご + stem + する) Meaning 話はなす お話はなしする To speak (to a superior) 伝つたえる お伝つたえする To convey / inform (to a superior) 送おくる お送おくりする To send (to a superior) 持もつ お持もちする To carry / hold (for a superior) 説明せつめいする ご説明せつめいする To explain (to a superior) 案内あんないする ご案内あんないする To guide / show (to a superior) 報告ほうこくする ご報告ほうこくする To report (to a superior) 連絡れんらくする ご連絡れんらくする To contact (a superior)

The polite です・ます goes on する

お話はなしする → お話はなしします (polite humble). You conjugate する as usual: お話はなしして (te-form), お話はなしした (past), お話はなししてある (humble + prepared state).

docs.title: The Reference Sentence — Connecting to 〜てある
docs.text:
そのことはもう社長しゃちょうにお話はなししてあります。

This is a perfect fusion of two grammar points: - お話はなしする = humble form of 話はなす (the speaker lowers themselves toward the president). - 〜てある = the prepared state construction (the action was done deliberately earlier, and the result — the president being informed — is now in place).

Together, お話はなししてあります means: "I have humbly spoken (to the president) and the matter is now taken care of." The speaker claims responsibility (〜てある) while showing proper deference (お〜する). This combination is extremely common in professional Japanese.

docs.title: When to Use 謙譲けんじょう語ご I vs. 謙譲けんじょう語ご II
docs.text:
The choice depends on whether your action has a respected target, and who you are speaking to.

Situation Use Example You are doing something for or to a superior/customer 謙譲けんじょう語ご I (special verb or お/ご + stem + する) 部ぶ長ちょうを駅えきまでお送おくりします。 "I'll drive the department chief to the station." You are simply stating your own action in a formal setting (announcement, speech, business letter) — no specific superior as target 謙譲けんじょう語ご II (special verbs like おる, 参まいる, 申もうす, 致いたす) 私わたくしは田中たなかと申もうします。 "I am Tanaka." (formal introduction) You are talking about your own in-group member to an outsider 謙譲けんじょう語ご II 父ちちは今いまおりますが、後のちほど折おり返かえしお電話でんわ致いたします。 "My father is here now, but I'll have him call you back." You are writing a formal email to a client Both — 謙譲けんじょう語ご I for directed actions; 謙譲けんじょう語ご II for general self-modesty 資料しりょうをご送付そうふ致いたします。 "I will send you the documents." (送付そうふ = sending, 致いたす = humble II)

docs.title: Humble vs. Respectful — Avoid the Confusion
docs.text:
A common learner error is using humble forms to describe the superior's action, or respectful forms for oneself. The rule is absolute:

謙譲けんじょう語ご (Humble) 尊敬そんけい語ご (Respectful) Whose action? Speaker's own action (or in-group's) The respected person's action Effect Lowers the speaker Elevates the other person Example: いる 私わたくしがおります (I am here, humbly) 先生せんせいがいらっしゃいます (The teacher is here, respectfully) Example: 話はなす 私わたくしがお話はなしする (I speak, humbly) 先生せんせいがお話はなしになる (The teacher speaks, respectfully) Example: 見みる 私わたくしが拝見はいけんする (I look, humbly) 先生せんせいがご覧らんになる (The teacher looks, respectfully)

Deadly error

先生せんせいがお話はなしします。

~お話はなしする is humble — it can only describe the speaker's own action. Using it for the teacher's action is grammatically wrong and socially bizarre — you've lowered the person you meant to elevate.~

Correct

先生せんせいがお話はなしになります。

~お話はなしになる is respectful — it elevates the teacher's action.~

docs.title: Scenario 1: Answering the Phone
docs.text:
はい、田中たなか商事しょうじでございます。私わたくし、佐藤さとうと申もうします。いつもお世話せわになっております。

  • ございます = extra-modest for です (there is / it is)
  • 申もうします = humble II for 言いう (I am called)
  • お世話せわになっております = humble II of 世話せわになる (to be in someone's care); おる is the base

Every element is 謙譲けんじょう語ご II — there's no specific superior being targeted; the speaker is lowering the entire self-presentation to show formal politeness to the caller.

docs.title: Scenario 2: In a Meeting with a Client
docs.text:
資料しりょうをご覧らんください。私わたくしからご説明せつめい申もうし上あげます。

  • ご覧らんください = respectful request (please look) — elevates the client's action
  • ご説明せつめい申もうし上あげる = 尊敬そんけい語ご? No, it's humble: ご説明せつめい (humble prefix) + 申もうし上あげる (humble I of 言ゆう — I will humbly explain to you). The action is directed at the client, so 謙譲けんじょう語ご I is used.

docs.title: Scenario 3: Talking About Your Own Whereabouts
docs.text:
午後ごごはオフィスにおりますので、いつでもお呼よびください。

  • おります = humble II for いる. The speaker states their own location modestly.
  • お呼よびください = respectful request — "please call me." Elevates the listener's action.

docs.title: The お・ご + Verb Stem + する Pattern — Full Conjugation
docs.text:
Once you form the humble stem with お／ご, you treat the whole thing as a する-verb. This includes the 〜てある combination.

Form Example (お話はなしする) Meaning Dictionary お話はなしする To humbly speak Polite お話はなしします I humbly speak Negative お話はなししない I do not humbly speak Polite Negative お話はなししません I do not humbly speak (polite) Past お話はなしした I humbly spoke Te-form お話はなしして (connective) 〜てある お話はなししてある／お話はなししてあります I have humbly spoken (and the state is in place) 〜ておく お話はなししておく I will humbly speak in advance 〜ている お話はなししている I am humbly speaking (rare; but possible)

docs.title: 1. Using お・ご + stem + する for someone else's action
docs.text:
Incorrect

社長しゃちょうが私わたしにご説明せつめいする。

~ご説明せつめいする is humble — it can only describe the speaker's (or in-group's) action. The president cannot "humbly explain" to you using this form.~

Correct

社長しゃちょうが私わたしにご説明せつめいになる／説明せつめいしてくださる。

~Use respectful forms (ご〜になる) or giving verbs (〜てくださる) for the president's action.~

docs.title: 2. Mixing up お and ご
docs.text:
Incorrect

お説明せつめいする

~説明せつめい is a Sino-Japanese word (kango). It takes ご, not お.~

Correct

ご説明せつめいする

~General rule: お for native Japanese words, ご for Sino-Japanese words. Exceptions exist (お電話でんわ, お料理りょうり), but the rule holds most of the time.~

docs.title: 3. Overusing 謙譲けんじょう語ご II in everyday speech
docs.text: おります and 申もうします are formal. Using them with close friends or in casual settings sounds stiff or joking (unless you're being ironically formal). Reserve them for business, customer service, and formal occasions.

docs.title: Japanese Mindset — Self-Lowering as Social Glue
docs.text:
The very existence of 謙譲けんじょう語ご II — a layer of language that lowers the speaker without any specific target for that lowering — reveals a deep Japanese cultural value: self-effacement as a default mode of formality. You don't just lower yourself when interacting with a superior; you lower yourself whenever you speak formally in public, as a way of acknowledging the formality of the situation itself.

  • おる is not just a humble いる — it is a posture. Saying 私わたくしはここにおります rather than 私わたくしはここにいます is the linguistic equivalent of bowing slightly as you enter a room. It signals awareness of the social context.
  • The お・ご + する pattern makes you small, but the grammar makes you clear. The humble form acknowledges the status difference, but the rest of the sentence — especially with constructions like 〜てある — lets you communicate facts and claim responsibility cleanly. Humility doesn't mean vagueness.
  • Humble and assertive can coexist. The reference sentence お話はなししてあります demonstrates this perfectly: the humble form shows deference to the president, while 〜てある declares plainly that the task is done. Professional Japanese rewards this combination.

docs.title: Quick Reference — All Patterns on This Page
docs.text: Pattern Type Purpose Example おる Humble II "to be" — formal self-existence 私わたくしはここにおります 参まいる Humble II "to go / come" — formal self-movement 私わたくしが参まいります 申もうす Humble II "to say / be called" — formal self-reference 田中たなかと申もうします 致いたす Humble II "to do" — formal self-action 私わたくしが致いたします お＋stem＋する Humble I Humble action toward a respected target お話はなしします ご＋stem＋する Humble I Humble action toward a respected target (Sino-Japanese verb) ご説明せつめいします お＋stem＋してある Humble I + てある Humble prepared state お話はなししてあります お＋stem＋しておく Humble I + ておく Humble preparatory action お送おくりしておきます

docs.title: In This Reference Series
docs.text:
• 〜てある — prepared state: often paired with humble forms (お話はなししてある) to combine accountability with deference
  • 〜ている (resultative) — contrast with 〜てある; no agentive/respect nuance

docs.title: Related Grammar
docs.text:
• 尊敬そんけい語ご (sonkeigo) — respectful language: elevates the other person's actions
  • 丁寧ていねい語ご (teineigo) — polite language: です・ます, not humble but the baseline for formal speech
  • 〜てくださる／〜ていただく — giving/receiving as a politeness strategy
  • お・ご〜になる — respectful pattern (contrast with humble お・ご〜する)

docs.title: Extra Modest Expressions — 謙譲語けんじょうご
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — Upper N4 / Lower N3

The individual patterns (おる, お〜する, ご〜する) are N4 grammar. But deploying them correctly — knowing which verbs have special humble forms, which use お vs. ご, and when extra modesty becomes excessive — is solidly lower N3. This is the first major keigo hurdle where learners must abandon one-to-one translation and think in social layers.

Tier Pattern Description Example N5 — Foundation です／ます Basic polite forms — distance without humility 行いきます。"I go [polite]." N4 — Foundation 謙譲語けんじょうご I — お〜する Humble prefix + stem + する for group 1 verbs 持もつ → 持もちます → 持もち致いたす → お持もちします N4 — Core ← You are here 謙譲語けんじょうご II — Special verbs & おる Verbs with dedicated humble forms; いる→おる 私わたしは社長室しゃちょうしつに居おります。 N3 — Extended お〜する vs. ご〜する Choosing prefix based on verb origin (和語わご vs. 漢語かんご) 連絡れんらくする → ご連絡れんらくする N3 — Extended 謙譲語けんじょうご overuse & avoidance When humility sounds servile or when 丁寧語ていねいご is safer 食たべる → 頂いただく (humble receive) vs. お食たべする (unnatural) N2 — Awareness 謙譲語けんじょうご + 受身形うけみけい (double keigo) お持もちいただく — humble + receive 資料しりょうをお持もちいただきます。

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 謙譲語けんじょうご (kenjougo / humble language) lowers the speaker's status relative to the listener or a third party. Unlike 丁寧語ていねいご (polite language) which simply adds distance, humble language actively degrades the speaker's actions.
  • Special humble verbs replace common verbs entirely: いる→居おる, 行いく・来くる→参まいる, する→致いたす, 言いう→申もうす, 食たべる・飲のむ→頂いただく, 知しる→存ぞんじる, 見みる→拝見はいけんする.
  • お〜する (for wago / native Japanese verbs) and ご〜する (for kango / Sino-Japanese nouns/verbs) create humble forms for verbs that lack special dedicated forms.
  • おる is the humble form of いる — not merely "to be" but "to humbly be." Using it positions the speaker as occupying space in the listener's world at the listener's tolerance.
  • The boundary between appropriate humility and servile overuse is thin. Native speakers often prefer 丁寧語ていねいご (ます) over 謙譲語けんじょうご when the social distance is unclear.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Use 謙譲語けんじょうご when describing your own actions (or your in-group's actions) in the presence of someone whose status is higher than yours, or when you want to show deference through self-deprecation. The key principle: lower yourself, elevate the other.

私わたしは明日あした参まいります。 I will come/go tomorrow [humble]. Literal: I tomorrow humbly-come/go [polite]. Context: Speaking to a superior about visiting them. 参まいる replaces both 行いく and 来くる in humble contexts. 私わたしは社長室しゃちょうしつに居おります。 I am in the president's office [humbly occupying space]. Literal: I president's-office-in humbly-am [polite]. Context: The input pattern. おる replaces いる. The nuance: "I am present here at the company's tolerance, in the president's space." 資料しりょうをお持もちします。 I will bring the materials [humbly performing the bringing]. Literal: Materials [object] humble-bringing do [polite]. Context: Speaking to a superior. 持もつ→お持もちする. The お elevates the action; the する degrades the agent.

docs.title: The Two Systems: Special Verbs & お・ご〜する
docs.text: 謙譲語けんじょうご operates through two parallel systems. Mastering both — and knowing when each applies — is the core N4/N3 keigo challenge.

docs.title: System 1: Special Humble Verbs
docs.text:
Certain common verbs have dedicated humble forms that must be memorized. These are not derived from the original verb; they are separate lexical items with built-in humility.

Plain Humble Meaning Nuance いる 居おる (おる) to be, to exist (animate) Humble existence — occupying space at others' tolerance 行いく / 来くる / いる 参まいる to go / to come / to be Humble movement — approaching or being in another's space する 致いたす to do Humble action — performing at another's service 言いう 申もうす to say Humble speech — speaking to a superior 食たべる / 飲のむ 頂いただく to eat / to drink / to receive Humble receiving — accepting what is given from above 知しる 存ぞんじる to know Humble knowledge — knowing as a lesser person 見みる 拝見はいけんする to see / to look Humble seeing — viewing what is above one's station 聞きく 伺うかがう to hear / to ask / to visit Humble hearing/asking — receiving information from above あげる 差さし上あげる to give (to superior) Humble giving — offering upward もらう 頂いただく / 戴いただく to receive (from superior) Humble receiving — accepting from above 知しっている 存ぞんじて上あげる to know (humble progressive) Progressive humble knowledge

おる is not casual

In some dialects (especially Kansai), おる is used casually as a plain form equivalent to いる. In standard keigo, however, おる is strictly humble. Using it casually in Tokyo sounds dialectal or rough; using it formally without keigo intent sounds bizarre.

docs.title: System 2: お〜する & ご〜する
docs.text:
For verbs without special humble forms, create one with prefix + stem + する.

Pattern Applies To Example お + [verb stem] + する Wago (和語わご / native Japanese verbs) 持もつ → お持もちする ご + [noun/verb] + する Kango (漢語かんご / Sino-Japanese) 連絡れんらくする → ご連絡れんらくする

お vs. ご — the origin rule

This is the single most common keigo error. お attaches to native Japanese (和語わご) verb stems. ご attaches to Sino-Japanese (漢語かんご) nouns and verbs. Saying お連絡れんらくする or ご持もちする is ungrammatical.

Correct Incorrect Why お持もちする ご持もちする 持もつ is wago — takes お ご連絡れんらくする お連絡れんらくする 連絡れんらく is kango — takes ご お書かきする ご書かきする 書かく is wago — takes お ご紹介しょうかいする お紹介しょうかいする 紹介しょうかい is kango — takes ご

The wago/kango test

If the verb can be written in pure hiragana without 漢字かんじ (持もつ→もつ), it's likely wago and takes お. If it's already in 漢字かんじ and feels "compound-y" (連絡れんらく, 紹介しょうかい, 説明せつめい), it's likely kango and takes ご. This is a rough heuristic but works for most N4/N3 vocabulary.

docs.title: In Real Life
docs.text:
In Real Life

Situation: 田中たなか (Tanaka), a junior sales representative at a trading company, is speaking to 山本部長やまもとぶちょう (Yamamoto, the department head). Tanaka has been summoned to the director's office and is now reporting back.

私わたしは今いま、社長室しゃちょうしつの前まえに居おります。お呼よびでしょうか。

"I am now humbly positioned in front of the president's office. Might this be a summons?"

Why おる fits here: Tanaka could have said います — grammatically correct, socially acceptable. But おります signals awareness of hierarchy: he is in the president's space, at the president's building, on the president's time. His very existence there is contingent. The おる acknowledges this power geometry.

Why お呼よび: 呼よぶ (to call/summon) has no special humble form, so Tanaka uses お〜する. But he doesn't complete the pattern — he says お呼よびでしょうか ("Is it a calling?"), using the noun form + copula rather than the full verb (お呼よびしますでしょうか would be ungrammatical). This is a common keigo compression: the humble prefix elevates the noun, and the copula handles the predication.

What makes this natural: The combination of おる (special humble) and お呼よび (derived humble) shows 田中たなか operating in two keigo systems simultaneously. Native speakers do this automatically; learners must build the pattern recognition.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
謙譲語けんじょうご is not merely "polite Japanese." It is a status negotiation system where every verb choice repositions the speaker relative to the listener. Understanding this repositioning is more important than memorizing forms.

Layer 1 — Self-deprecation: The speaker describes their own actions as lesser, smaller, or performed in service. This is not self-hatred; it is social grace. By lowering yourself, you create space to elevate the other.

Layer 2 — Elevation of other: The 謙譲形けんじょうけいimplies a 受取人うけとりにん or 観察者かんさつしゃ who is 高たかい. お持もちしますdoesn't just mean "I bring" — it means "I bring [for you, who are above me]." The other 人ひと is built into the 文法ぶんぽう.

Layer 3 — Spatial humility: Many humble verbs encode physical or social space. 居おる (to humbly be) implies occupying space at another's tolerance. 参まいる (to humbly go/come) implies moving within another's sphere. 伺うかがう (to humbly hear/ask/visit) implies approaching another's presence.

私わたしが行いきます。 I will go [polite, neutral]. The speaker is going. The destination and purpose are neutral. 私わたしが参まいります。 I will go/come [humble, deferential]. The speaker's movement is framed as approaching a superior's space. The going is an act of service or obligation. 私わたしはここに居おります。 I am here [humbly existing]. The speaker's presence is contingent, tolerated, perhaps even intrusive. The おる acknowledges this.

docs.title: Real Speech vs. Textbook Form
docs.text:
Textbooks present keigo as a fixed system: "Use 謙譲語けんじょうご for your own actions, 尊敬語そんけいご for others' actions." Real speechスピーチ is messier — speakers often avoid keigo entirely when uncertain, or mix levels within a single utterance.

Form Example Note Textbook (pure keigo) 私わたしは明日あした参まいります。 Clean, correct, slightly stiff Real speech (keigo + polite) 明日あした、お邪魔じゃまさせていただきます。 "I will humbly receive the favor of disturbing you tomorrow" — 謙譲語けんじょうご + 使役しえき + 受身形うけみけい, extremely common in business Real speech (keigo avoidance) 明日あした行いきますね。 Neutral polite — when the relationship is close or the keigo boundary unclear Real speech (overkeigo) 私わたしがお食たべいたします。 Excessive — 食たべるhas special form 頂いただく; お食たべするis unnatural and sounds like parody

docs.title: Function in Context
docs.text:
• 私わたしは marks the speaker as topic — standard. In keigo contexts, first-person pronouns are often dropped because the humble verb itself implies "I."
  • 社長室しゃちょうしつの前まえに locates the speaker spatially. The location (in front of the president's office) is high-status space; the おる acknowledges this.
  • おります is the humble form of いる, conjugated to polite ます-form. The stem is おり; the ます attaches to the i-stem (おり → おります). This is a regular Godan conjugation despite the verb's special status.
  • お呼よびでしょうか uses the derived humble pattern incompletely — 呼よぶ→お呼よび (stem + prefix), then treated as a noun with copula でしょうか. This is a common keigo compression: rather than "Will you summon me?" (direct, potentially demanding), the speaker asks "Is this a summons?" (indirect, self-effacing).
  • Without おる: 私わたくしは社長室しゃちょうしつの前まえにいます would be a neutral location report. With おる: the location becomes a statement of social position — "I am positioned here, aware of my place."
  • Without お呼よび: 呼よばれるんですか ("Will I be called?") uses passive and sounds passive-aggressive or anxious. お呼よびでしょうか is proactively humble.
社長しゃちょう、お戻もどりですか。 President, have you returned [to your esteemed presence]? Literal: President, humble-return is? Context: Staff to president. 戻もどる→お戻もどり, then nominalized with copula. The staff does not ask "Did you come back?" — they ask about the state of the president's presence.

docs.title: Special Humble Verbs — Conjugation
docs.text:
Plain Humble Stem Polite (ます) Te-form Past いる 居おり (おり) 居おります 居おって 居おった 行いく / 来くる 参まいり (まいり) 参まいります 参まいりまして 参まいりました する 致いたし (いたし) 致いたします 致いたしまして 致いたしました 言いう 申もうし (もうし) 申もうします 申もうしまして 申もうしました 食たべる / 飲のむ 頂いただき (いただき) 頂いただきます 頂いただきまして 頂いただきました 知しる 存ぞんじ (ぞんじ) 存ぞんじます 存ぞんじまして 存ぞんじました

おる conjugation is Godan

Despite being a special humble verb, おる conjugates as a standard Godan verb: おらない, おろう, おれば. Do not treat it as irregular.

docs.title: お〜する / ご〜する — Formation
docs.text:
Verb Type Dictionary Stem Prefix Humble Form Wago Godan 持もつ 持もち お お持もちする Wago Godan 書かく 書かき お お書かきする Wago Ichidan 見みる 見み お お見みする (but 拝見はいけんする preferred) Kango noun 連絡れんらく 連絡れんらく ご ご連絡れんらくする Kango noun 説明せつめい 説明せつめい ご ご説明せつめいする

Not all verbs take お〜する

Verbs with special humble forms (行いく→参まいる, 見みる→拝見はいけんする) should use those forms instead of お〜する. Saying お見みする or お行いきする is unnatural — not ungrammatical, but like wearing a tuxedo t-shirt.

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
Keigo is not a costume you put on for formal occasions. It is a social positioning system that operates constantly, even unconsciously. The choice between います and おります is not about politeness level — it is about acknowledging that you exist in a web of relationships where some positions are higher than others.

The humble speaker does not say "I am less than you" (which would be self-abasement). They say "I am performing a role in which my actions are oriented toward you." The humility is relational, not personal.

  • Space as social geometry. おる is humble because it implies occupying space that belongs to another. When Tanaka says 社長室しゃちょうしつの前まえにおります, he is not saying "I am small" — he is saying "I am in the president's space, positioned by his authority." The humility is spatial and relational.
  • Action as service. お持もちします doesn't mean "I am a lowly being who brings." It means "My bringing is an act performed in your service." The action is redefined by its orientation toward the superior.
  • The paradox of keigo fluency. Native speakers often claim keigo is "difficult" even while using it flawlessly. This is because keigo is not a set of rules but a social intuition — the forms are easy, but the calibration is hard. Knowing when おります is respectful and when it is servile requires reading the room, not the textbook.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text: Grammar Point Core Meaning Emotional / Pragmatic Nuance Trigger Context JLPT 謙譲語(けんじょうご) Humble language — lowering self Self-deprecation as social grace; actions oriented toward superior Speaking to superiors; customer service; formal requests N4 尊敬語そんけいご Respectful language — elevating other Direct elevation of other's actions Describing superiors' actions; addressing customers N4 丁寧語ていねいご (ていねいご) Polite language — adding distance Neutral respect; no status negotiation General polite conversation; safe default when unsure N5 お〜する Derived humble (wago) Standard humility for verbs without special forms Most native Japanese verbs in humble contexts N4 ご〜する Derived humble (kango) Standard humility for Sino-Japanese vocabulary Most Sino-Japanese nouns/verbs in humble contexts N4 おる Special humble for いる Spatial humility; occupying space at another's tolerance Being present in superior's space; formal self-location N4 参まいる Special humble for 行いく/来くる Movement as approach to superior Visiting, coming, going in service contexts N4 お〜い致いたす Double humble (お〜する + 致いたす) Extreme humility; potentially servile Ultra-formal business; risk of overdoing N2

docs.title: 丁寧語ていねいご (neutral polite) — ます／です
docs.text:
The safe default when keigo boundaries are unclear. No status negotiation, just distance.

Example 私わたしは明日あした行いきます。 English "I will go tomorrow [polite]." Intent Neutral, respectful, no implication of hierarchy vs. humble Removes the self-deprecation. Safe when you don't know if the listener expects keigo or would find it excessive.

When to use this instead

Use 丁寧語ていねいご when the social relationship is unclear, when speaking to strangers of unknown status, or when you want to be polite without implying hierarchy. Many Japanese prefer this in ambiguous situations.

docs.title: 尊敬語そんけいご (respectful language) — いらっしゃる
docs.text:
Elevates the other person's actions, not your own. Often paired with 謙譲語けんじょうご in the same sentence.

Example 社長しゃちょうは今いまお戻もどりになりました。 English "The president has now returned [respectful]." Intent Elevates the president's action. Contrast with 私わたしは戻もどりました (humble) or 社長しゃちょうは戻もどりました (neutral). vs. humble 尊敬語そんけいご and 謙譲語けんじょうご are complementary, not alternatives. Use both in the same sentence: 私わたしがお持もちします (humble self) + 社長しゃちょうがお戻もどりになります (respectful other).

docs.title: 使役受身形しえきうけみけい (causative-passive) — させていただく
docs.text:
Extreme humility — "I will humbly receive the favor of being allowed to do X."

Example 説明せつめいさせていただきます。 English "I will take the liberty of explaining [humble + receive permission]." Intent The speaker frames their action as something they are being allowed to do by a superior. Ultra-humble. vs. お〜する ご説明せつめいします is humble. 説明せつめいさせていただきます is ultra-humble — the speaker is not even the agent, just the recipient of permission.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
謙譲語けんじょうご is by definition formal, but its intensity varies. The key is matching the humility level to the social gap.

Casual (keigo inappropriate)

私わたし、明日あした行いくよ。

"I'm going tomorrow, okay?"

Shift: Plain form, no keigo. Appropriate for friends, family, peers. Using 参まいる here would be bizarre — like bowing to your roommate.

Basic Polite (丁寧語ていねいご — safe default)

私わたしは明日あした行いきます。

"I will go tomorrow [polite]."

Shift: です／ます only. No keigo. Appropriate for most workplace interactions where hierarchy is present but not rigid, or when unsure of keigo boundaries.

Extra Polite (謙譲語けんじょうご — humble)

私わたしは明日あした参まいります。

"I will humbly go/come tomorrow."

Shift: 行いく→参まいる. Appropriate for speaking to clear superiors or in formal service contexts. The humility is explicit but not excessive.

Ultra-Formal (double keigo)

明日あした、お邪魔じゃまさせていただけますでしょうか。

"Might I be permitted to humbly disturb you tomorrow?"

Shift: 邪魔じゃまする→お邪魔じゃまさせていただく (humble + causative + receive). The speaker is not even the agent of the action — they are the recipient of permission to perform it. Appropriate for highest-stakes formal requests.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: Step Pattern What It Adds Example N5 — Foundation です／ます Basic politeness — distance without humility 行いきます。 N5 — Foundation いる / 行いく / 来くる / する / 言いう Core verbs with humble counterparts 私わたしはここにいる。 N4 — Core ← You are here Special humble verbs (居おる, 参まいる, 致いたす, 申もうす, 頂いただく, etc.) Dedicated humble forms replacing common verbs 私わたしはここに居おります。 N4 — Core お〜する / ご〜する Derived humble pattern for verbs without special forms お持もちします。 N3 — Extended お vs. ご selection Choosing prefix based on wago/kango origin ご連絡れんらくする N3 — Extended 謙譲語けんじょうご overuse avoidance Knowing when 丁寧語ていねいご is safer than 謙譲語けんじょうご 食たべる → 頂いただく (ok) vs. お食たべする (avoid) N2 — Awareness 使役受身形しえきうけみけい (causative-passive humble) させていただく — receiving permission to act 説明せつめいさせていただきます。 N2 — Awareness 謙譲語けんじょうご + 尊敬語そんけいご mixing Using both systems in same sentence for full keigo 私わたしがお持もちします。社長しゃちょうはいらっしゃいますか。

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
私わたしは会議室かいぎしつに居おります。 I am in the meeting room [humbly present]. Literal: I meeting-room-in humbly-am [polite].

~Context: Speaking to a superior who is looking for you. The おる acknowledges that your presence is at their disposal.*~

資料しりょうをお持もちします。 I will bring the materials [humbly]. Literal: Materials [object] humble-bringing do [polite].

~Context: To a superiorしゅぺりおる or clientくらいえんと. 持もつ→お持もちする. The お elevates the materialsまてりある; the する degrades the agentえーじぇんと.*~

明日あした、ご連絡れんらくします。 I will contact you tomorrow [humbly]. Literal: Tomorrow, humble-contact do [polite].

~Context: To a superior or customer. 連絡れんらくする→ご連絡れんらくする. Kango takes ご, not お.*~

社長しゃちょう、お戻もどりですか。 President, have you returned [to your esteemed presence]? Literal: President, humble-return is?

~Context: Staff to president. 戻もどる→お戻もどり, nominalized with copula. The question is about the state of the president's presence, not the action of returning.*~

何なにかご質問しつもんがございましたら、お申もうし付つけください。 If you have any questions, please humbly command me [to answer them]. Literal: Something questions if-humbly-exist, humble-saying-attach please.

~Context: Ultra-formal service language. 質問しつもんがある→ご質問しつもんがございましたら (humble existence); 言ゆう→申もうす (humble speech); 付つける→お申もうし付つけください (humble command). The speaker positions themselves as entirely at the listener's disposal.*~

私わたしどもでございますが… It is we [humble we] who… Literal: We-humble [humble copula] but…

~Context: Formal self-introduction by company representative. 私わたくしども (humble "we") + でございます (humble copula). The entire self-reference is wrapped in humility.*~

docs.title: 1. Using お〜する for verbs with special humble forms
docs.text:
The most common keigo error — creating derived humble forms when dedicated ones exist.

Unnatural

お行いきします

~行いく has special humble form 参まいる. お行いきします sounds like a learner's overcorrection — grammatically comprehensible but socially off-key.~

Correct

参まいります

~Use the special form. Memorize the list: 行いく/来くる→参まいる, する→致いたす, 言いう→申もうす, 食たべる/飲のむ→頂いただく, 見みる→拝見はいけんする, 知しる→存ぞんじる.~

docs.title: 2. Confusing お and ご prefixes
docs.text:
Mixing wago and kango prefixes produces ungrammatical forms that immediately mark the speaker as non-native.

Incorrect

お連絡れんらくする

~連絡れんらく is kango (Sino-Japanese) — must take ご, not お.~

Correct

ご連絡れんらくする

~Kango → ご prefix. The wago/kango distinction is non-negotiable in standard keigo.~

Incorrect

ご持もちする

~持つ is wago (native Japanese) — must take お, not ご.~

Correct

お持もちする

~Wago → お prefix.~

docs.title: 3. Overusing 謙譲語けんじょうご in inappropriate contexts
docs.text:
Excessive humility can sound servile, sarcastic, or simply foreign.

Socially awkward

友達ともだちに会あいに参まいります。

"I will humbly go to meet my friend." — Using 参まいる for peer relationships sounds bizarre, like bowing to your cat.

Appropriate

友達ともだちに会あいに行いきます。

"I'm going to meet a friend." — Neutral polite. Keigo is for hierarchical relationships, not intimacy.

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text:
Collocation Meaning 私わたしは〜に居おります "I am [humbly] at/in ~" — formal self-location お〜します / ご〜します "I [humbly] do ~" — the standard derived humble pattern お〜致いたします "I [humbly] do ~ [extreme]" — お〜する + 致いたす double humble 何なにかございましたら "If there is anything [humble existence]" — formal service opener お申もうし付つけください "Please command me [humbly]" — ultra-deferential request for instruction お気きを付つけて下くださいませ "Please take care [humbly]" — formal farewell 私わたしども "We [humble]" — formal company/organization self-reference でございます "[Humbly] is" — humble copula replacing です

~These collocations are worth memorizing as complete units. 何なにかございましたら and お申もうし付つけください especially are service-industry staples.~

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• おる carries dialectal baggage. In Kansai and some other regions, おる is plain-form casual (equivalent to standard いる). A Kyoto shopkeeper saying おるで is not being humble — they're being local. In Tokyo standard, おる is keigo only. This regional split causes genuine confusion among native speakers too.
  • 参まいる means both "go" and "come" — but only in humble contexts. It replaces 行いく when the movement is toward a superior's space, and 来くる when the movement is from the speaker's perspective toward the listener. The directionality is determined by social geometry, not physical direction.
  • 頂いただく (いただく) is the Swiss Army knife of humility. It means "to humbly eat," "to humbly drink," "to humbly receive," and even "to humbly be allowed to do" (〜させていただく). Its semantic range is vast because the core meaning is simply "to accept from above."
  • 存ぞんじる has two readings. 存ぞんじる (ぞんじる) for "to know" and 存ぞんじ上あげる (ぞんじあげる) for "to know [about you, respectfully]." ご存知ぞんじです (そんちです) is actually 尊敬語そんけいご (respectful), not humble — it elevates the listener's knowledge. The 存ぞんじ family is a keigo minefield.
  • お〜する can be split by particles. You can say お持もちします (continuous) or お持もちいたします (humble + extreme humble). You can also nominalize: お持もちすること (the act of humbly bringing). The pattern is productive once internalized.
  • The "humble we" (私わたくしども) vs. "humble I" (私わたくし). Using 私わたくしども for a single speaker sounds archaic or ultra-formal in some contexts, but standard in company representatives. The ども (共とも / "together") historically meant "companions" but now functions as a humble pluralizer.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N4 Memorize special humble verbs (おるおる, 参まいる, 致いたす, 申もうす, 頂いただく, 拝見はいけんする, 存ぞんじる, 伺うかがう); produce お〜する and ご〜する correctly; distinguish wago from kango for prefix selection Developing — N4→N3 Deploy 敬語けいご naturally in service and workplace contexts; know when 丁寧語ていねいご is safer than 謙譲語けんじょうご; avoid overkeigo; begin mixing 謙譲語けんじょうご and 尊敬語そんけいご in same utterance Automatic — N3→N2 Keigo choices become automatic — you feel the social distance and the right form arrives without analysis. You recognize when someone uses おる dialectally vs. humbly. Mastery You control the full keigo spectrum from casual plain form through double keigo (させていただく). You know when humility sounds sincere and when it sounds servile. You can read the social geometry of any room and calibrate accordingly.

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 尊敬語そんけいご (そんけいご) — Respectful language; elevates the listener's actions
  • 丁寧語ていねいご (ていねいご) — Polite language; adds distance without status negotiation
  • 美化語びかご (びかご) — Beautified language; elevates objects/actions (お茶ちゃ, お仕事しごと)

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 丁寧語ていねいご (です／ます) — Neutral politeness; no humility or elevation
  • 尊敬語そんけいご (いらっしゃる, おっしゃる, なさる) — Elevates other's actions; complementary to 謙譲語けんじょうご
  • お〜になる — Respectful pattern (尊敬語そんけいご), not humble — 社長しゃちょうがお読よみになる ("The president reads [respectfully]")

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• です／ます (N5) — The polite foundation
  • Verb stems (N4) — Required for お〜する formation
  • Wago vs. kango awareness (N4) — Essential for お vs. ご selection
  • Transitive/intransitive (N4) — Relevant for some special humble verb distinctions

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 使役受身形しえきうけみけい (させていただく) (N2) — Ultra-humble: receiving permission to act
  • 謙譲語けんじょうご + 尊敬語そんけいご mixing (N3) — Full keigo sentences with both systems
  • お〜い致たすす (N2) — Double humble: お〜する + 致いたす
  • 存ぞんじ上あげる (N3) — Humble knowledge about the listener
  • うかがう (伺うかがう) — Humble hearing/asking/visiting; complex semantic range

docs.title: 謙けん譲じょう語ご I (Kenjōgo) — Show Respect by Lowering Your Own Status
1. Overview & Nuance
docs.text:
Extra Modest Expressions (謙けん譲じょう語ご) are a category of 敬けい語ご used when speaking about your own actions — or the actions of your in-group — in order to show respect to the listener. Rather than elevating the listener directly (which is the job of Sonkeigo), humble speech works by lowering the speaker, creating vertical social distance that flatters the listener by comparison.

The psychological intent is crucial: you are symbolically bowing through language. By choosing a verb form that means "I humbly do X" instead of simply "I do X," you communicate awareness of the other person's higher status — in age, position, or social role such as "customer."

In Japanese society, respect is often expressed through the concept of distance. This isn't about being weak; it's about being culturally intelligent. Using these forms demonstrates that you understand the social hierarchy and your place within it.
