docs.title: How it differs from 丁てい寧ねい語ご
docs.text:
Form Purpose Direction of Respect Teineigo (-masu) General politeness; neutral distance Flat — simply polite Kenjōgo (Extra Modest) Lower yourself to elevate the listener ↓ Speaker goes down Sonkeigo (Honorific) Directly elevate the listener's actions ↑ Listener goes up

A simple way to think about it: -masu is polite neutrality. Kenjōgo is a verbal bow. If Teineigo is business casual, Kenjōgo is a pressed suit.

docs.title: 2a. Special Modest Verbs
docs.text:
Several high-frequency verbs have completely irregular humble forms that must be memorized. These are the ones you will encounter most often in real life — in customer service, business, and formal introductions.

Plain Verb Meaning Humble Form With -masu 行いく / 来くる to go / to come 参まいる 参まいります いる to be / exist (animate) おる おります ある to exist (inanimate) ございます ございます です to be (copula) でございます でございます する to do 致いたす 致いたします ている ongoing / continuous ております ております 言いう to say 申もうす 申もうします / 申もうし上あげます 食たべる / 飲のむ / もらう to eat / drink / receive いただく いただきます 知しっている to know 存ぞんじております 存ぞんじております 見みる to see / look 拝はい見けんする 拝はい見けんします

docs.title: 2b. The Productive Pattern: お + Verb Stem + する／いたす
docs.text:
For verbs that don't have a special humble form, Japanese provides a productive template. Three steps:

  1. Take the -masu stem. Remove ます from the polite form. 例れい: 書かきます → 書かき

  2. Add お before the stem. This prefix is a respect marker. 例れい: お書かき

  3. Attach する (or the more formal いたす) after the stem. 例れい: お書かきする → お書かきいたします

Plain Verb -masu Stem Standard Humble Extra Formal Humble 持もつ (to carry) 持もち お持もちする お持もちいたします 送おくる (to send) 送おくり お送おくりする お送おくりいたします 待まつ (to wait) 待まち お待まちする お待まちいたします 呼よぶ (to call) 呼よび お呼よびする お呼よびいたします

Sino-Japanese verbs use ご, not お

For する verbs with Chinese-origin nouns like 連れん絡らくする, 説せつ明めいする, 案あん内ないする, the prefix changes from お to ご, and you skip the -masu stem logic entirely: ご + noun + する／いたす.

例れい: ご連れん絡らくいたします = "I will (humbly) contact you." 例れい: ご案あん内ない致いたします = "I will (humbly) guide you."

docs.title: 3. Progressive Usage Ladder
docs.text: The following examples escalate in social weight. Notice how the same grammar is recycled across vastly different contexts — the grammar itself is fixed; what changes is who you're talking to.

docs.title: Level 1 — Strict / Formal (Interview, Ceremonial Self-Introduction)
docs.text:
Example 1 · Self-Introduction

田た中なかと申もうします。アメリカから参まいりました。 "My name is Tanaka. I have come from America."

Classic job interview opener. 申もうします replaces 言いいます; 参まいりました replaces 来きました.

Example 2 · Interview Statement

現げん在ざい、ABC会がい社しゃで働はたらいております。 "I am currently working at ABC Company."

おる is used in its て-form → 〜ており(ます), the humble equivalent of the progressive 〜ている.

Example 3 · Showing Respect for Expertise

お名な前まえは以い前ぜんから存ぞんじております。 "I have known your name for some time."

存ぞんじております is the humble form of 知しっています. Crucial in first meetings with someone famous or senior.

docs.title: Level 2 — Business Polite (Client, Superior, Customer Service)
docs.text:
Example 4 · Phone / Email

明あ日した、資し料りょうをお送おくりいたします。 "I will send the documents tomorrow."

Textbook example of the お + stem + いたす pattern. Standard in client email.

Example 5 · Offering Help

お荷に物もつ、お持もちしましょうか。 "Shall I carry your luggage for you?"

The humble pattern combines naturally with 〜ましょうか to make humble offers. Very common in hotels and stores.

Example 6 · Customer Service

少しょう々しょうお待まちください。担たん当とう者しゃにご連れん絡らくいたします。 "Please wait a moment. I will contact the person in charge."

Notice the ご + 連れん絡らく + いたす pattern for a Sino-Japanese verb.

Example 7 · Referring to Your In-Group (Uchi-Soto in Action)

社しゃ長ちょうの山やま田だはただいま席せきを外はずしております。 "Our president, Yamada, is currently away from his desk."

Even though Yamada is your boss, you use humble language about him to an outside customer. This is the uchi-soto rule in action — your boss belongs to your group relative to the outsider.

docs.title: Level 3 — Social Context: Humble Forms in Everyday Formal Speech
docs.text:
When does humble speech appear outside the office?

The full 致いたします register would feel stiff between friends. But 〜ております and お + stem + する appear in softer social contexts — introducing yourself at a PTA meeting, speaking to your partner's parents, or making a humble offer to a neighbor.

Example 8 · Meeting a Partner's Parents

東とう京きょうで英えい語ごを教おしえております。 "I teach English in Tokyo."

〜ております softens a casual fact into something appropriate for a respectful first meeting.

Example 9 · Polite Social Offer

タクシーをお呼よびしましょうか。 "Shall I call a taxi for you?"

Works when hosting older guests — more respectful than タクシーを呼よびましょうか, but not as stiff as business speech.

Example 10 · Expressing Gratitude

おいしいお茶ちゃをいただきました。ありがとうございます。 "I received some delicious tea. Thank you very much."

いただく is the quiet workhorse of humble speech — used constantly and perfectly natural even in moderately formal social situations.

Example 11 · Awaiting a Reply (Closing an Email)

連れん絡らくをお待まちしております。 "I am humbly awaiting your contact."

Uses て + おります (the modest て-form). Perfect for closing emails where you are waiting for a decision or reply.

Example 12 · Acknowledging a Superior's Message

そのメールは、もう拝はい見けんしました。 "I have already humbly looked at that email."

拝はい見けん is the humble version of 見みる. Shows you have paid careful attention to the superior's message.

docs.title: 4. The Keigo Hierarchy — Side-by-Side
docs.text:
Memorizing these triplets is one of the fastest ways to cement the logic of keigo. The golden rule: my actions → Kenjōgo. Their actions → Sonkeigo. Neutral / general description → Teineigo.

Meaning Kenjōgo (Humble — self) Teineigo (Polite — neutral) Sonkeigo (Honorific — other) to be / exist おります います いらっしゃいます to go / come 参まいります 行いきます / 来きます いらっしゃいます to do 致いたします します なさいます to say 申もうします 言いいます おっしゃいます to eat / drink いただきます 食たべます / 飲のみます 召めし上あがります to see 拝はい見けんします 見みます ご覧らんになります to know 存ぞんじております 知しっています ご存ぞんじです to exist (inanimate) ございます あります N/A to be (copula) でございます です N/A

docs.title: 5. The Mistake Monitor
docs.text:
Social Pitfall — The Uchi-Soto Rule

Humble speech is for you and your in-group (内うち) — never for the listener or their group (外そと). A common early mistake:

❌ お客きゃく様さまが参まいりました。 (lowers the customer — the opposite of your intent) ✅ お客きゃく様さまがいらっしゃいました。 (Sonkeigo — correct)

Conversely, when referring to your own boss while speaking to an outside client, use humble speech for your boss — because your boss is in your group relative to the outsider. This is counterintuitive for learners from hierarchical cultures that always elevate the boss.

Remember: use modest speech for your boss when talking about your boss to a client, but use honorific speech when talking directly to your boss.

Grammatical Pitfall — Keigo Double-Dipping (二に重じゅう敬けい語ご)

Do not stack humble and honorific structures, or apply the お〜する pattern on top of a verb that already has a special humble form.

❌ お申もうしいたします。 (申もうす is already humble — don't add お〜いたす on top) ✅ 申もうします。 / 申もうし上あげます。

Another classic error — applying humble speech to the listener's action:

❌ 先せん生せいはコーヒーをいただきますか。 (uses humble "receive/drink" for the teacher) ✅ 先せん生せいはコーヒーを召めし上あがりますか。 (Sonkeigo — elevating the teacher's action)

Grammar Pitfall — Using お〜する When a Special Verb Exists

If a verb has an irregular humble form, use it — don't invent a pattern-based version.

❌ お食たべします。 (invented pattern — unnatural) ✅ いただきます。 (correct special humble form for eating/drinking/receiving)

Social Mismatch — Using Keigo with Friends

Extra Modest forms are for formal settings with a clear social gap. Using 致いたします or おります with close friends creates a cold, yoso-yososhii distance.

Stick to casual or standard polite speech with peers.

docs.title: 6. Practice Drill
docs.text:
1. Translate to humble Japanese: "My name is Smith. I come from Canada." (formal self-introduction)
  2. Translate to humble Japanese: "I will send the report by email tomorrow." (to a client)
  3. Convert: 「今いま、会かい議ぎ室しつにいます。」(talking about your own location to a boss on the phone)
  4. Convert: 「駅えきまで車くるまで送おくりましょうか。」(humble offer to drive someone to the station)
  5. Spot and fix the error: 部ぶ長ちょうの鈴すず木きはただいまいらっしゃいません。(said to an outside client)

docs.title: 7. Answer Key
docs.text:
Click to reveal answers

Question 1 スミスと申もうします。カナダから参まいりました。 言いう → 申もうす for stating one's own name; 来くる → 参まいる for humbly "coming." Past tense 参まいりました is correct because the arrival is a completed fact at the moment of speaking. Crucially, drop the -san from your own name when being modest to outsiders.

Question 2 明あ日した、レポートをメールでお送おくりいたします。 送おくる has no special humble form, so use the productive pattern: お + 送おくり (stem) + いたす. Using いたす rather than する raises the register to business-appropriate. Both お送おくりします and お送おくりいたします are grammatically fine; the latter is more formal.

Question 3 今いま、会かい議ぎ室しつにおります。 いる → おる. The -masu form is おります. Used for describing your own location or state — common on the phone.

Question 4 駅えきまで車くるまでお送おくりしましょうか。 Classic お + stem + する pattern combined with 〜ましょうか to make a humble offer. The humble pattern works best when your action directly benefits the listener — which is exactly the case here.

Question 5 ❌ 部ぶ長ちょうの鈴すず木きはただいまいらっしゃいません。 ✅ 部ぶ長ちょうの鈴すず木きはただいま席せきを外はずしております。/ おりません。 Error: いらっしゃいません is Sonkeigo (honorific) — but Buchou Suzuki is your in-group relative to the outside client. You must switch to humble speech: おりません, or the more natural 席せきを外はずしております ("is away from his desk"). This is the uchi-soto rule at its trickiest — elevating your own boss to an outsider is a social faux pas in Japanese.

docs.title: "That's How It Is" — Explanatory and Discovery 〜んだ
〜んだ (〜のだ) — Signalling Explanation, Realisation, and Emotional Emphasis
Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 〜んだ (the casual spoken form of 〜のだ) attaches to plain-form predicates to mark the sentence as an explanation, a realisation, or an emotionally charged statement.
  • It is not simply a masculine variant of 〜の; it adds a layer of meaning: the speaker is presenting the information as something that clarifies, answers a question, or is newly understood.
  • In the reference sentence — よかったね。そんなに変かわるんだ、すごい。 — the んだ expresses discovery with admiration: the speaker has just witnessed or understood how much someone changes, and the んだ conveys "So that's how it is!" / "I see that you change that much!"
  • Without んだ, そんなに変かわる is a plain statement of fact ("you change that much"). With んだ, the sentence becomes a reaction — the speaker is processing new information and sharing their realisation.
  • 〜んだ is fundamental to natural spoken Japanese. It appears constantly in everyday conversation, often with no direct English translation, but its absence can make speech sound flat or robotic.

docs.title: The Core Function — 〜んだ as an Explanation / Realisation Marker
docs.text:
What 〜んだ Does

The fundamental job of 〜んだ (のだ) is to frame a sentence as an explanation — the speaker is not just stating a fact, but providing context, answering a question (stated or implied), or sharing a realisation. It connects the statement to the situation, making it relevant.

んだ can mark: 1. Explanation: "The reason is…" / "The situation is that…" 2. Realisation / Discovery: "Oh, so that's how it is!" / "I see that…" 3. Emotional emphasis / Insistence: "I'm telling you, …" / "It's just that…"

The reference sentence falls primarily into category 2 — realisation — with a strong emotional charge of admiration.

docs.title: The Sentence
docs.text: よかったね。そんなに変かわるんだ、すごい。

docs.title: Natural Translation
docs.text: "That's great! So you change that much — amazing." Or, with the んだ nuance unpacked: "That's great. I didn't realise you change so much — wow, that's impressive."

docs.title: Component Breakdown
docs.text: Component Form Function よかった Past of いい (good) Expresses relief or approval — "That's good / I'm glad." ね Sentence-final particle Adds shared feeling; softens the statement. そんなに Adverb "To that extent / that much." Indicates degree. 変かわる Plain dictionary form of "to change" The core verb — describes the action of changing. んだ Contraction of のだ Explanatory / realisation marker. Frames 変かわる as newly understood information. すごい i-adjective "Amazing / incredible." The speaker's evaluative reaction.

docs.title: Why んだ Here?
docs.text:
Without んだ, the second part would be:

そんなに変かわる、すごい。 "You change that much. Amazing."

This is grammatically correct but feels flat — two disconnected statements. The んだ links the observation (変かわる) to the preceding situation, making it a shared realisation. The speaker is saying, in effect:

"Oh, so the reality is that you change that much! That's amazing."

The んだ shows that the speaker has just put the pieces together — perhaps they've seen the change, or someone told them about it, and now they're reacting with delighted surprise.

docs.title: How 〜んだ Works — The Underlying Structure
The Grammar — のだ / んだ
docs.text:
のだ is composed of the nominaliser の (which turns the preceding clause into a noun-like element) + the copula だ (is). Together, they create a structure meaning "It is that [clause]."

Form Usage Example のだ Full form — written, formal spoken, or emphatic 変かわるのだ。 "It is that [I/you] change." んだ Contraction — standard casual spoken form 変かわるんだ。 "So [I/you] change!" んです Polite version (の + です → んです) 変かわるんです。 (polite) の Casual sentence-final (often feminine or softer) 変かわるの。 (soft, explanatory)

In casual spoken Japanese, んだ is the default for both men and women when expressing explanation or realisation. の without だ is softer and often associated with feminine or gentle speech, but んだ is gender-neutral and extremely common.

docs.title: Formation
docs.text:
Attach んだ to the plain form of the predicate. For nouns and na-adjectives, な is inserted before んだ.

Predicate Plain Form + んだ Meaning Verb 変かわる 変かわるんだ So [subject] changes! Verb (past) 変かわった 変かわったんだ So [subject] changed! i-adjective すごい すごいんだ It's that it's amazing! / It IS amazing. na-adjective 元気げんきだ 元気げんきなんだ So [subject] is well! / It's that [subject] is well. Noun 休やすみだ 休やすみなんだ So it's a holiday! / It's that it's a holiday.

Don't forget the な for nouns and na-adjectives

休やすみだんだ ❌ 休やすみなんだ ✅ 元気げんきだんだ ❌ 元気げんきなんだ ✅

docs.title: Realisation and Discovery — The んだ of New Understanding
docs.text:
In the reference sentence, んだ signals discovery. The speaker has just learned or noticed something — the extent of the change — and the んだ conveys "Oh, I see!" or "So that's the case."

This use of んだ often pairs with: - Observing something directly. - Hearing new information from someone. - Suddenly understanding something that was unclear.

Situation Without んだ With んだ You see a friend after a long time, looking very different. あ、変かわったね。 "Oh, you changed." あ、変かわったんだ。 "Oh, so you did change!" (realisation hitting) Someone tells you they're moving overseas. 海外かいがいに行いく？ "You're going abroad?" 海外かいがいに行いくんだ？ "Oh, so you're going abroad!" (processing the news) You find out a restaurant is closed on Mondays. あ、月曜げつよう休やすみだ。 "Oh, it's closed Mondays." あ、月曜げつよう休やすみなんだ。 "Ah, so it's closed on Mondays!" (I see!)

docs.title: Other Uses of 〜んだ — Beyond Realisation
1. Explanation — Answering a Question or Filling a Gap
docs.text:
When someone asks "why?" or "what's the matter?", んだ provides the reason.

A: どうして遅おくれたの？ Why were you late? B: 電車でんしゃが遅おくれたんだ。 The train was late (you see). / It's that the train was late.

The んだ here makes B's reply an explanation, not just a fact.

docs.title: 2. Emotional Emphasis — Asserting with Feeling
docs.text:
んだ can add force to a statement, making it sound more insistent or heartfelt.

私わたしがやるんだ！ I'll do it! (insisting) どうしても行いきたいんだ。 I really want to go (that's the situation).

docs.title: 3. Prefacing a Request or Further Point
docs.text:
Often, んだ appears before a request, suggestion, or explanation, setting up the context.

ちょっと話はながあるんだけど… I have something to talk to you about (and that's why I'm here)… 頭あたまが痛いたいんだ。薬くすりある？ I have a headache (that's the situation). Do you have medicine?

docs.title: 〜んだ vs. Plain Form — The Nuance at a Glance
docs.text:
Plain Statement 〜んだ Statement 雨あめが降ふっている。 雨あめが降ふっているんだ。 "It's raining." (simple fact) "So it's raining!" (realisation) / "It's raining (that's why I'm late)." (explanation)

The plain form reports; んだ relates the information to the context.

docs.title: 1. Using んだ for every sentence
docs.text:
んだ is not a default sentence-ender. Overusing it makes speech sound heavy or overly explanatory. If you simply state a fact with no connection to the context, plain form is better.

Overuse

今日きょうは月曜日げつようびなんだ。それで、仕事しごとがあるんだ。天気てんきがいいんだ。

~Every sentence is marked as an explanation — it sounds like a constant justification.~

Better

今日きょうは月曜日げつようびだ。それで、仕事しごとがあるんだ。天気てんきがいい。

~Use んだ only when you are explaining or reacting. The plain statement of day is fine without it.~

docs.title: 2. Forgetting the な with nouns and na-adjectives
docs.text:
Incorrect

これは秘密ひみつだんだ。

~だんだ is ungrammatical. Nouns require な before んだ.~

Correct

これは秘密ひみつなんだ。

~So it's a secret!~

docs.title: 3. Using polite form directly before んだ
docs.text:
んです is already the polite form. You cannot add です to んだ to make it polite — you must use んです.

Incorrect

変かわるんだです。

~Clumsy and ungrammatical.~

Correct

変かわるんです。

~Polite explanatory form.~

docs.title: Japanese Mindset — Sharing Understanding Rather Than Just Facts
docs.text:
English often leaves the connection between a statement and its context implicit. "You've changed so much! That's amazing." The listener infers the speaker's realisation from tone and words.

Japanese んだ makes that cognitive move explicit. そんなに変かわるんだ says, "I am now processing the fact that you change that much; this is a realisation I'm sharing with you." It invites the listener into the speaker's moment of understanding. This aligns with a broader Japanese communication pattern: explicitly marking shared understanding (the ね in よかったね does this too).

んだ often has no translation in English because the function is grammaticalised in Japanese: it signals the speaker's cognitive state (realising, explaining, insisting) rather than adding propositional content.

docs.title: Quick Reference Table — 〜んだ Forms
docs.text: Form Usage Example んだ Casual explanatory/realisation 変かわるんだ。 んです Polite version 変かわるんです。 のだ Formal/written/emphatic 変かわるのだ。 の Soft explanatory (casual, often feminine) 変かわるの。 んだけど Prefacing a request or background 変かわるんだけど… んだって Hearsay / I heard that… 変かわるんだって。

docs.title: Related Explanatory Patterns
docs.text:
• 〜わけだ — "So that means…" / "No wonder…" (a logical conclusion based on information)
  • 〜はずだ — "It should be the case that…" (expectation, not yet confirmed)
  • 〜のだろう／んだろう — Conjectural explanatory: "I wonder if it's that…"

docs.title: Contrast With
docs.text:
• Plain form — simple statement without explanatory framing
  • 〜からだ — "Because…" (explicit reason as main clause)
  • 〜の (sentence-final) — soft explanation, often without the assertiveness of んだ

docs.title: Reference Sentence: そんなに変かわるんだ、すごい。
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — Upper N4 / Lower N3

The base form 〜のだ is N4 grammar — nominalization with の + copula だ. But the explanatory/realization usage in casual speech (〜んだ), particularly its role in processing new information aloud and sharing that cognitive shift with a listener, sits firmly at the upper N4 / lower N3 boundary.

Tier Pattern Description Example N5 — Foundation 〜です／〜だ Plain copula — stating facts 猫ねこだ。"It's a cat." N4 — Foundation 〜のだ (formal) Explanatory nominalization — "the situation is that…" 雨あめなのだ。"The thing is, it's raining." N4 — Core ← You are here 〜んだ (casual explanatory) Casual contraction of のだ; marks explanation, realization, or emotional processing shared with listener そんなに変かわるんだ、すごい。 N3 — Extended 〜んだ + emotional response 〜んだ as the trigger for an emotional or evaluative follow-up — the explanation itself provokes feeling 一人ひとりで来きたんだ、偉えらいね。 N2 — Awareness 〜んだった (past counterfactual) "So it was that…" — retroactive realization with regret or surprise そうだったんだった。"So that's how it was."

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 〜んだ is the casual spoken form of 〜のだ — it nominalizes the preceding statement and wraps it in the copula だ, creating an explanatory or emphatic frame.
  • In real speech, 〜んだ does far more than explain causes. It marks moments of realization ("So that's how it is"), shares cognitive processing aloud ("I see — it changes that much"), and softens emotional reactions by grounding them in freshly understood fact.
  • In the input sentence, 変かわるんだ is not explaining why something changes — it's processing the fact of change with surprise. The んだ marks the speaker's cognitive shift: "I'm realizing this as I say it."
    • The comma followed by すごい is critical — the んだ utterance triggers an emotional response. This two-beat structure (realization + reaction) is one of the most natural patterns in casual Japanese.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Use 〜んだ when you want to frame a statement as an explanation, realization, or piece of shared understanding — not just a bare fact, but a fact that matters to the current conversational context. The ん softens the assertion and invites the listener into your cognitive process.

雨あめが降ふるんだ。 It's going to rain [you see / that's the situation]. Literal: Rain falls [explanatory]. Context: Explaining why you're carrying an umbrella, or why plans need to change. The んだ makes it shared information, not a weather report. そんなに変かわるんだ、すごい。 So it changes that much — amazing. Literal: That-much changes [realization], amazing. Context: The input sentence. Someone has shown the speaker a before-and-after photo, or described a transformation. The speaker processes the scale of change aloud (変かわるんだ), then reacts emotionally (すごい).

docs.title: In Real Life
docs.text:
In Real Life

Situation: ミカ is looking at a photo of her friend ユイ from six months ago. Yui has lost weight, changed her hairstyle, and looks completely different — but Mika hadn't seen her during the transition, only the result.

よかったね。そんなに変かわるんだ、すごい。

*"That's great. So you change that much — amazing."*

Why 〜んだ fits here: 変かわる without んだ would be a flat observation: "You change that much." With んだ, it becomes a moment of cognitive adjustment — Mika is updating her mental model of Yui in real time. The んだ says "I'm processing this as we speak."

What makes this natural: The two-beat structure is essential. First beat: そんなに変かわるんだ (realization, spoken aloud). Second beat: すごい (emotional reaction, now justified by the realization). Without the comma and follow-up, 変かわるんだ sounds incomplete — like the speaker was cut off. The pattern demands completion.

**The よかったね that precedes:** This is a separate evaluative comment (likely responding to Yui mentioning she feels better or achieved a goal). It sets up the emotional frame — the speaker is already in a supportive, appreciative mode when the 〜んだ realization arrives.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
〜んだ is built from two layers: の (nominalizer) + だ (copula), with の contracting to ん in casual speech. This creates a "nominalized statement + copula" structure — literally "It is [the case that] X." But the pragmatic function goes far beyond literal grammar.

Layer 1 — Nominalization (の): The の turns the preceding clause into a noun-like concept. 変かわる (changes) becomes 変かわるの (the fact/situation of changing). This abstraction is what allows the speaker to comment on the statement as a whole, rather than just asserting it.

Layer 2 — Copula (だ): The だ treats the nominalized statement as a situation to be recognized. It says "this is the case" — not as raw fact, but as shared understanding. The combination creates a frame: "The situation is that X, [and I'm pointing this out for us both]."

Layer 3 — Realization mode: In the input sentence, the んだ doesn't explain a cause. It marks a discovery. The speaker thought they understood the scale of change; new evidence has updated that understanding. The んだ is the audible click of comprehension.

そんなに変かわる。 You change that much. Flat assertion — could be accusatory, observational, or simply stated. そんなに変かわるんだ。 So you change that much [I'm realizing this now]. Cognitive processing — the speaker's understanding has just shifted. The んだ invites the listener to witness the realization. そんなに変かわるんだ、すごい。 So you change that much — amazing. Two-beat structure: realization (んだ) triggers emotional response (すごい). The comma is the breath between cognitive shift and feeling.

docs.title: Real Speech vs. Textbook Form
docs.text:
Textbooks teach 〜のだ as "giving explanations" — answering why something happened. In real speech, 〜んだ is more often a social tool for sharing mental states: surprise, realization, confirmation, soft insistence, or emotional processing.

Form Example Note Textbook (causal explanation) 電車でんしゃが遅おくれたんだ。 "The train was late [that's why I'm late]" — classic explanatory usage Real speech (realization) そんなに変かわるんだ、すごい。 "So it changes that much — wow" — processing new information aloud Real speech (soft insistence) そうなんだよ。 "That's how it is [I'm telling you]" — gentle insistence, not harsh correction Real speech (emotional grounding) 寂さびしいんだ。 "I'm lonely [and that's the situation I'm in]" — vulnerable sharing Real speech (rhetorical question) どうして来こないんだ？ "Why aren't you coming [I'm genuinely trying to understand]" — んだ softens the question

docs.title: Function in Context
docs.text:
• そんなに (to that extent) scopes 変かわる — it establishes the scale of change as surprising. Without そんなに, 変かわるんだ would be mild realization; with it, the realization carries weight.
  • 変わるんだ is the core realization utterance. The る (dictionary form) + んだ pattern is standard for ongoing/general truths being recognized — if this were a specific past event, it would be 変わったんだ.
  • The comma after んだ is prosodic — it marks the breath between cognitive processing and emotional release. In casual speech this pause is often longer than the comma suggests; the speaker is genuinely absorbing the information.
  • すごい is the emotional payoff. It stands alone as an exclamation, receiving its justification from the preceding んだ clause. This is a common pattern: 〜んだ、[emotional reaction].
  • Without んだ: そんなに変かわる、すごい would sound like two disconnected observations — "You change that much. Amazing." With んだ, the second clause flows from the first: "The fact that you change that much [is what makes this] amazing."
一人ひとりで来きたんだ、偉えらいね。 You came alone [I just realized this] — impressive. Literal: Alone came [realization], impressive, right? Context: Parent to child, or friend to friend. The んだ marks the moment of recognition; 偉いね is the praise that follows.

docs.title: Construction
docs.text:
Form Result What Changed Verb (plain present) 行いくんだ Dictionary form + んだ Verb (plain past) 行いったんだ Past plain form + んだ i-Adjective (plain) 高たかいんだ i-adjective plain + んだ な-Adjective 静しずかなんだ だ → な before んだ (same rule as のに) Noun 学生がくせいなんだ だ → な before んだ Noun (past) 学生がくせいだったんだ だった + んだ for past state

The な rule

な before んだ is required for nouns and 名詞めいし and な-adjectives — it is not optional. Saying 学生がくせいんだ is ungrammatical. This is the same な that appears in なのに and なので.

Formal equivalent

In formal speech, んだ becomes のです or んです (polite contraction). Never use んだ in formal writing or polite conversation with strangers — it sounds inappropriately casual.

Register Form Example Casual 〜んだ 変かわるんだ Polite spoken 〜んです 変かわるんです Formal written 〜のだ 変かわるのだ Formal polite 〜のです 変かわるのです

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
English speakers process surprises internally and then report the conclusion: "Wow, you changed a lot!" Japanese speakers often process aloud, sharing the cognitive moment itself: 変かわるんだ、すごい. The んだ is not just grammar — it's a social invitation to witness the speaker's realization.

This reflects a broader Japanese conversational preference for shared cognitive experience over reported conclusions. The んだ makes the listener a participant in the mental event, not just a recipient of its result.

  • Processing aloud as social bonding. By verbalizing the realization (変かわるんだ), the speaker makes their inner cognitive shift visible and shared. The listener is not just told a fact — they are invited to experience the surprise alongside the speaker.
  • Emotional reactions need grounding. In Japanese, bare exclamations (すごい！） can feel abrupt or performative. Preceding them with 〜んだ (変かわるんだ、すごい) grounds the emotion in a freshly established fact, making the reaction feel authentic rather than rehearsed.
  • Softening assertion through nominalization. 変かわる is a claim. 変かわるんだ is a discovered situation. The んだ removes the speaker's authority and replaces it with shared witness — "This is what we're both seeing, right?"

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text:
Grammar Point Core Meaning Emotional / Pragmatic Nuance Trigger Context JLPT 〜んだ "The situation is that X" / "So X is the case" Realization, explanation, soft insistence, emotional grounding Processing new information; sharing cognitive shifts; softening assertions N4 〜のだ (formal) Same as 〜んだ More explicit, written-style explanation; less emotional processing Written explanations, formal presentations N4 〜よ "I tell you / you know" Direct assertion, emphasis, slight insistence Adding weight to a claim the listener might doubt N4 〜ね "Right? / Isn't it?" Seeking agreement, softening, shared confirmation Checking listener alignment; gentle assertions N5 〜んだよ "The thing is, X [I'm telling you]" Explanation + insistence combined Gentle correction or persistent explanation N4 〜んだね "So X is the case, right?" Explanation + confirmation seeking Processing aloud while checking listener understanding N4 〜だろう "Probably X / I suppose X" Speculation, hedging, tentative conclusion Uncertain situations; avoiding overcommitment N4 〜ってば "I said X [why don't you get it]" Frustrated insistence — repetitive When listener isn't accepting what speaker says N3

The closest lookalike is 〜よ. Both add emphasis, but よ asserts ("Listen to me") while んだ discovers ("Look at this"). 変かわるよ sounds like "It changes, okay? Believe me." 変かわるんだ sounds like "So that's how much it changes — I'm seeing it now."

docs.title: 〜よ (direct assertion)
docs.text:
The emphatic alternative. Same informational content, but よ pushes the fact toward the listener rather than sharing a discovery with them.

Example そんなに変かわるよ、すごい。 English "It changes that much, you know — amazing." Intent The speaker already knew this and is asserting it; the amazement feels slightly performative vs. original よ removes the cognitive processing layer. The speaker sounds like they're showing off knowledge rather than experiencing surprise.

When to use this instead

Use よ when you're confirming something the listener might doubt, or when you have prior knowledge you want to emphasize. Use んだ when the realization is fresh and shared.

docs.title: 〜ね (confirmation seeking)
docs.text:
Softens the statement by seeking agreement. Less about realization, more about mutual recognition.

Example そんなに変かわるね、すごい。 English "You change that much, right — amazing." Intent Assumes both speaker and listener already recognize this; seeks confirmation rather than processing discovery vs. original ね skips the cognitive shift. It says "We both see this, don't we?" rather than "I'm just now seeing how big this is."

docs.title: 〜の (feminine softening, casual)
docs.text:
In very casual speech (often feminine-associated), の can replace んだ as a soft explanatory marker. Functionally similar, socially coded.

Example そんなに変かわるの、すごい。 English "So you change that much — amazing." Intent Same realization structure, softer and more intimate register vs. original の is slightly more personal and less explanatory than んだ. It sounds like sharing a secret thought rather than processing aloud.

Learner trap — register mismatch

Using の in masculine-coded speech or formal contexts sounds affected or childish. んだ is the safer default for casual mixed-gender speech.

docs.title: 〜わ (feminine assertion)
docs.text:
A more assertive feminine variant. Less common in modern speech but still heard.

Example そんなに変かわるわ、すごい。 English "It certainly changes that much — amazing." Intent Feminine assertiveness — the speaker is impressed and wants to state it clearly vs. original わ is a statement of personal reaction; んだ is a shared cognitive event.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
The realization function — processing new information aloud and reacting to it — must be preserved across register shifts. The formality changes the packaging, not the cognitive event.

Casual Basic Polite Extra Polite

よかったね。そんなに変かわるんだ、すごい。

"That's great. So you change that much — amazing."

Shift: Input sentence as-is. Casual register — んだ contraction, comma pause, standalone すごい as emotional reaction.

よかったですね。そんなに変かわるんです、すごいです。

"That's wonderful. So you change that much — it's amazing."

Shift: んだ → んです; すごい → すごいです. The realization structure is preserved but delivered with polite distance. The comma pause remains.

よかったですね。そんなに変かわられるのですね、素晴すばらしいです。

"That is wonderful indeed. So you change to such a degree — it is remarkable."

Shift: 変かわる → 変かわられる (respectful form); のですね adds formal confirmation; すごい → 素晴すばらしいです (elevated lexical choice). The cognitive processing is now ceremonial — appropriate for commenting on a superior's transformation.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: Step Pattern What It Adds Example N5 — Foundation 〜だ／〜です Plain copula — stating facts 猫ねこだ。"It's a cat." N5 — Foundation 〜よ／〜ね Sentence-final particles — emphasis and confirmation 猫ねこだよ／猫ねこだね。 N4 — Foundation 〜の (nominalizer) Turns clauses into noun-like concepts 行いくの。"The act of going." N4 — Core ← You are here 〜んだ (explanatory) Nominalization + copula + casual contraction; frames statement as explanation or realization そんなに変かわるんだ。"So it changes that much." N4 — Extended 〜んだよ／〜んだね んだ combined with よ (insistence) or ね (confirmation) 知しってるんだよ。"I do know, okay?" N3 — Extended 〜んじゃない Negative explanatory — "It's not that…" 嫌きらいなんじゃない。"It's not that I dislike it." N2 — Awareness 〜んだった (retroactive realization) Past form — "So it was that…" そうだったんだった。"So that's how it was."

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
一人ひとりで来きたんだ、偉えらいね。 You came alone [I just realized] — impressive. Literal: Alone came [realization], impressive, right?

~Context: Parent to child who arrived at an event solo. The んだ marks the moment of noticing; 偉えらいね is the praise that naturally follows.~

毎日まいにち練習れんしゅうしてるんだ、凄すごいね。 So you practice every day — that's amazing. Literal: Every day practicing [realization], amazing, right?

~Context: Friend discovering another friend's dedication. The 凄すごいね evaluates it; んだ processes the new information.~

日本にほんに来きたばかりなんだ、大変たいへんだね。 So you just got to Japan [I see now] — that must be tough. Literal: Japan came-just-now [realization], tough, right?

~Context: Expressing empathy upon learning someone's situation. The んだ turns the fact into a shared understanding that justifies the sympathy.~

そうなんだ。 I see / So that's how it is. Literal: So [it is] that way [realization].

~Context: The universal Japanese response to new information. そうなんだ is the sound of mental files being updated. Said alone, it completes a conversation turn.~

好すきなんだ、本気ほんきで。 So you really do like [them/it] — seriously. Literal: Like [realization], seriously.

~Context: Processing someone's true feelings that were previously unclear. The んだ marks the moment of clarity; 本気ほんきで adds weight to the realization.~

docs.title: 1. Using 〜んだ as a universal explanation
docs.text:
Learners often apply 〜んだ to every sentence, thinking it makes their Japanese more natural. But んだ carries specific pragmatic weight — overuse sounds like you're constantly explaining yourself or processing aloud.

Overused

今日きょうは月曜日げつようびなんだ。私わたしは学生がくせいなんだ。

"Today is Monday [explanatory]. I'm a student [explanatory]." — Sounds like you're anxiously justifying your existence. Bare facts don't need んだ.

Appropriate usage

今日きょうは月曜日げつようびだ。だから授業じゅぎょうがあるんだ。

"Today is Monday. That's why I have class [explanation]." — んだ justifies the relevance of the second clause.

docs.title: 2. Confusing 〜んだ with questions
docs.text:
In questions, 〜んだ seeks explanation. Learners sometimes use it as a plain question marker, which can sound demanding.

Socially pushy

何なにを食たべるんだ？

"What are you going to eat [explain yourself]?" — Without context, this sounds like "What the hell are you eating?" The んだ adds pressure.

Natural question

何なにを食たべる？

"What are you going to eat?" — Plain question, neutral tone. Or add context: 何なにを食たべたいんだ？"What do you want to eat [I'm trying to understand your preference]?"

docs.title: 3. Using casual 〜んだ in formal writing
docs.text:
Register crash

〜んだ is strictly casual spoken. Using it in essays, business emails, or presentations is inappropriate.

Avoid Use instead 重要じゅうようなんだ。 重要じゅうようなのである。／重要じゅうようである。

In formal contexts, use のだ or drop the nominalization entirely.

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text:
Collocation Meaning そうなんだ "I see / So that's how it is" — the universal realization response そうだったんだ "So that's how it was" — retroactive processing of past information 〜なんだ、すごい "[Realization], amazing" — the canonical two-beat praise pattern 〜なんだ、大変たいへんだね "[Realization], that must be hard" — empathy pattern 〜なんだ、偉えらいね "[Realization], impressive" — praise for effort or achievement 知しらなかったんだ "I didn't know that [but now I do]" — newly learned information 好すきなんだ "So you do like [them/it]" — processing confirmed feelings 寂さびしいんだ "I'm lonely [and that's my situation]" — vulnerable emotional sharing

~These collocations are worth learning as complete pragmatic units. The 〜なんだ、[reaction] pattern especially is a foundational rhythm of casual Japanese conversation.~

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• 〜んだ can stand alone as a response. If someone tells you something surprising, responding with just そうなんだ completes the conversational turn. It says "I have received and processed your information." The lack of follow-up is not rude — it's a valid social move.
  • The comma is prosodic, not just grammatical. In 変かわるんだ、すごい, the comma represents a genuine pause where the speaker's cognition catches up with their emotion. In fast speech this pause may be minimal; in emotional speech it can stretch. Writing it without the comma (変かわるんだすごい) looks wrong because it erases the breath.
  • 〜んだ softens contradiction. When correcting someone gently, 違ちがうんだ ("That's not quite right [let me explain]") is softer than 違ちがうよ ("Wrong!"). The んだ frames the correction as a shared discovery rather than an opposing claim.
  • Feminine の vs. masculine んだ is not absolute. While の is stereotypically feminine and んだ masculine, modern usage is more fluid. Women use んだ constantly in casual speech; men using の in very intimate contexts is not unheard of. The "rule" is more about register than gender.
  • 〜んだ can create emotional distance from oneself. Saying 寂さびしいんだ ("I'm lonely [and that's the situation]") is slightly more bearable than 寂さびしい ("I'm lonely") because the んだ frames the loneliness as an observable fact rather than a raw feeling. This self-objectification is a subtle emotional survival strategy.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N4 Recognize 〜んだ in speech; understand it as explanatory/realization marker; produce basic patterns correctly; avoid overuse in plain factual statements Developing — N4→N3 Deploy the two-beat structure naturally (〜んだ、[reaction]); distinguish んだ from よ and ね based on pragmatic intent; begin using んだ for soft insistence and emotional sharing Automatic — N3→N2 〜んだ arrives as a cognitive signal — you hear it and immediately register "this speaker is processing something." The two-beat pattern feels as natural as breathing. Mastery You control the emotional temperature with precision — dropping んだ for clinical distance, adding it for warmth, using の for intimacy, and knowing when んだよ would sound pushy rather than explanatory.

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 〜のだ (formal explanatory) — the full, uncontracted form; used in writing and formal speech
  • 〜んです (polite explanatory) — the polite contraction; safe for most spoken contexts
  • 〜の (feminine casual explanatory) — intimate register alternative to んだ

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 〜よ (assertion) — pushes information toward listener; んだ shares processing with listener
  • 〜ね (confirmation) — seeks agreement; んだ marks realization
  • 〜だろう (speculation) — uncertain conclusion; んだ treats the statement as established fact
  • 〜ってば (frustrated insistence) — repetitive and annoyed; んだ is explanatory and inviting

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• の (nominalizer) (N4) — turns clauses into noun-like concepts
  • だ (copula) (N5) — the "is" that frames nominalized statements
  • Casual contraction rules (N4) — の + だ → んだ in spoken Japanese

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 〜んだった (N2) — retroactive realization with surprise or regret
  • 〜んじゃない (N3) — negative explanatory; "It's not that…"
  • 〜んじゃなかった (N2) — negative retroactive realization; "I shouldn't have…"

docs.title: Reference Sentence: そんなに変かわるんだ、すごい。
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — Upper N4 / Lower N3

The base form 〜のだ is N4 grammar — nominalization with の + copula だ. But the explanatory/realization usage in casual speech (〜んだ), particularly its role in processing new information aloud and sharing that cognitive shift with a listener, sits firmly at the upper N4 / lower N3 boundary.

Tier Pattern Description Example N5 — Foundation 〜です／〜だ Plain copula — stating facts 猫ねこだ。"It's a cat." N4 — Foundation 〜のだ (formal) Explanatory nominalization — "the situation is that…" 雨あめなのだ。"The thing is, it's raining." N4 — Core ← You are here 〜んだ (casual explanatory) Casual contraction of のだ; marks explanation, realization, or emotional processing shared with listener そんなに変かわるんだ、すごい。 N3 — Extended 〜んだ + emotional response 〜んだ as the trigger for an emotional or evaluative follow-up — the explanation itself provokes feeling 一人ひとりで来きたんだ、偉えらいね。 N2 — Awareness 〜んだった (past counterfactual) "So it was that…" — retroactive realization with regret or surprise そうだったんだった。"So that's how it was."

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 〜んだ is the casual spoken form of 〜のだ — it nominalizes the preceding statement and wraps it in the copula だ, creating an explanatory or emphatic frame.
  • In real speech, 〜んだ does far more than explain causes. It marks moments of realization ("So that's how it is"), shares cognitive processing aloud ("I see — it changes that much"), and softens emotional reactions by grounding them in freshly understood fact.
  • In the input sentence, 変かわるんだ is not explaining why something changes — it's processing the fact of change with surprise. The んだ marks the speaker's cognitive shift: "I'm realizing this as I say it."
    • The comma followed by すごい is critical — the んだ utterance triggers an emotional response. This two-beat structure (realization + reaction) is one of the most natural patterns in casual Japanese.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Use 〜んだ when you want to frame a statement as an explanation, realization, or piece of shared understanding — not just a bare fact, but a fact that matters to the current conversational context. The ん softens the assertion and invites the listener into your cognitive process.

雨あめが降ふるんだ。 It's going to rain [you see / that's the situation]. Literal: Rain falls [explanatory]. Context: Explaining why you're carrying an umbrella, or why plans need to change. The んだ makes it shared information, not a weather report. そんなに変かわるんだ、すごい。 So it changes that much — amazing. Literal: That-much changes [realization], amazing. Context: The input sentence. Someone has shown the speaker a before-and-after photo, or described a transformation. The speaker processes the scale of change aloud (変かわるんだ), then reacts emotionally (すごい).

docs.title: In Real Life
docs.text:
In Real Life

Situation: ミカ is looking at a photo of her friend ユイ from six months ago. Yui has lost weight, changed her hairstyle, and looks completely different — but Mika hadn't seen her during the transition, only the result.

よかったね。そんなに変かわるんだ、すごい。

*"That's great. So you change that much — amazing."*

Why 〜んだ fits here: 変かわる without んだ would be a flat observation: "You change that much." With んだ, it becomes a moment of cognitive adjustment — Mika is updating her mental model of Yui in real time. The んだ says "I'm processing this as we speak."

What makes this natural: The two-beat structure is essential. First beat: そんなに変かわるんだ (realization, spoken aloud). Second beat: すごい (emotional reaction, now justified by the realization). Without the comma and follow-up, 変かわるんだ sounds incomplete — like the speaker was cut off. The pattern demands completion.

**The よかったね that precedes:** This is a separate evaluative comment (likely responding to Yui mentioning she feels better or achieved a goal). It sets up the emotional frame — the speaker is already in a supportive, appreciative mode when the 〜んだ realization arrives.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
〜んだ is built from two layers: の (nominalizer) + だ (copula), with の contracting to ん in casual speech. This creates a "nominalized statement + copula" structure — literally "It is [the case that] X." But the pragmatic function goes far beyond literal grammar.

Layer 1 — Nominalization (の): The の turns the preceding clause into a noun-like concept. 変かわる (changes) becomes 変かわるの (the fact/situation of changing). This abstraction is what allows the speaker to comment on the statement as a whole, rather than just asserting it.

Layer 2 — Copula (だ): The だ treats the nominalized statement as a situation to be recognized. It says "this is the case" — not as raw fact, but as shared understanding. The combination creates a frame: "The situation is that X, [and I'm pointing this out for us both]."

Layer 3 — Realization mode: In the input sentence, the んだ doesn't explain a cause. It marks a discovery. The speaker thought they understood the scale of change; new evidence has updated that understanding. The んだ is the audible click of comprehension.

そんなに変かわる。 You change that much. Flat assertion — could be accusatory, observational, or simply stated. そんなに変かわるんだ。 So you change that much [I'm realizing this now]. Cognitive processing — the speaker's understanding has just shifted. The んだ invites the listener to witness the realization. そんなに変かわるんだ、すごい。 So you change that much — amazing. Two-beat structure: realization (んだ) triggers emotional response (すごい). The comma is the breath between cognitive shift and feeling.

docs.title: Real Speech vs. Textbook Form
docs.text:
Textbooks teach 〜のだ as "giving explanations" — answering why something happened. In real speech, 〜んだ is more often a social tool for sharing mental states: surprise, realization, confirmation, soft insistence, or emotional processing.

Form Example Note Textbook (causal explanation) 電車でんしゃが遅おくれたんだ。 "The train was late [that's why I'm late]" — classic explanatory usage Real speech (realization) そんなに変かわるんだ、すごい。 "So it changes that much — wow" — processing new information aloud Real speech (soft insistence) そうなんだよ。 "That's how it is [I'm telling you]" — gentle insistence, not harsh correction Real speech (emotional grounding) 寂さびしいんだ。 "I'm lonely [and that's the situation I'm in]" — vulnerable sharing Real speech (rhetorical question) どうして来こないんだ？ "Why aren't you coming [I'm genuinely trying to understand]" — んだ softens the question

docs.title: Function in Context
docs.text:
• そんなに (to that extent) scopes 変かわる — it establishes the scale of change as surprising. Without そんなに, 変かわるんだ would be mild realization; with it, the realization carries weight.
  • 変わるんだ is the core realization utterance. The る (dictionary form) + んだ pattern is standard for ongoing/general truths being recognized — if this were a specific past event, it would be 変わったんだ.
  • The comma after んだ is prosodic — it marks the breath between cognitive processing and emotional release. In casual speech this pause is often longer than the comma suggests; the speaker is genuinely absorbing the information.
  • すごい is the emotional payoff. It stands alone as an exclamation, receiving its justification from the preceding んだ clause. This is a common pattern: 〜んだ、[emotional reaction].
  • Without んだ: そんなに変かわる、すごい would sound like two disconnected observations — "You change that much. Amazing." With んだ, the second clause flows from the first: "The fact that you change that much [is what makes this] amazing."
一人ひとりで来きたんだ、偉えらいね。 You came alone [I just realized this] — impressive. Literal: Alone came [realization], impressive, right? Context: Parent to child, or friend to friend. The んだ marks the moment of recognition; 偉いね is the praise that follows.

docs.title: Construction
docs.text:
Form Result What Changed Verb (plain present) 行いくんだ Dictionary form + んだ Verb (plain past) 行いったんだ Past plain form + んだ i-Adjective (plain) 高たかいんだ i-adjective plain + んだ な-Adjective 静しずかなんだ だ → な before んだ (same rule as のに) Noun 学生がくせいなんだ だ → な before んだ Noun (past) 学生がくせいだったんだ だった + んだ for past state

The な rule

な before んだ is required for nouns and 名詞めいし and な-adjectives — it is not optional. Saying 学生がくせいんだ is ungrammatical. This is the same な that appears in なのに and なので.

Formal equivalent

In formal speech, んだ becomes のです or んです (polite contraction). Never use んだ in formal writing or polite conversation with strangers — it sounds inappropriately casual.

Register Form Example Casual 〜んだ 変かわるんだ Polite spoken 〜んです 変かわるんです Formal written 〜のだ 変かわるのだ Formal polite 〜のです 変かわるのです

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
English speakers process surprises internally and then report the conclusion: "Wow, you changed a lot!" Japanese speakers often process aloud, sharing the cognitive moment itself: 変かわるんだ、すごい. The んだ is not just grammar — it's a social invitation to witness the speaker's realization.

This reflects a broader Japanese conversational preference for shared cognitive experience over reported conclusions. The んだ makes the listener a participant in the mental event, not just a recipient of its result.

  • Processing aloud as social bonding. By verbalizing the realization (変かわるんだ), the speaker makes their inner cognitive shift visible and shared. The listener is not just told a fact — they are invited to experience the surprise alongside the speaker.
  • Emotional reactions need grounding. In Japanese, bare exclamations (すごい！） can feel abrupt or performative. Preceding them with 〜んだ (変かわるんだ、すごい) grounds the emotion in a freshly established fact, making the reaction feel authentic rather than rehearsed.
  • Softening assertion through nominalization. 変かわる is a claim. 変かわるんだ is a discovered situation. The んだ removes the speaker's authority and replaces it with shared witness — "This is what we're both seeing, right?"

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text:
Grammar Point Core Meaning Emotional / Pragmatic Nuance Trigger Context JLPT 〜んだ "The situation is that X" / "So X is the case" Realization, explanation, soft insistence, emotional grounding Processing new information; sharing cognitive shifts; softening assertions N4 〜のだ (formal) Same as 〜んだ More explicit, written-style explanation; less emotional processing Written explanations, formal presentations N4 〜よ "I tell you / you know" Direct assertion, emphasis, slight insistence Adding weight to a claim the listener might doubt N4 〜ね "Right? / Isn't it?" Seeking agreement, softening, shared confirmation Checking listener alignment; gentle assertions N5 〜んだよ "The thing is, X [I'm telling you]" Explanation + insistence combined Gentle correction or persistent explanation N4 〜んだね "So X is the case, right?" Explanation + confirmation seeking Processing aloud while checking listener understanding N4 〜だろう "Probably X / I suppose X" Speculation, hedging, tentative conclusion Uncertain situations; avoiding overcommitment N4 〜ってば "I said X [why don't you get it]" Frustrated insistence — repetitive When listener isn't accepting what speaker says N3

The closest lookalike is 〜よ. Both add emphasis, but よ asserts ("Listen to me") while んだ discovers ("Look at this"). 変かわるよ sounds like "It changes, okay? Believe me." 変かわるんだ sounds like "So that's how much it changes — I'm seeing it now."

docs.title: 〜よ (direct assertion)
docs.text:
The emphatic alternative. Same informational content, but よ pushes the fact toward the listener rather than sharing a discovery with them.

Example そんなに変かわるよ、すごい。 English "It changes that much, you know — amazing." Intent The speaker already knew this and is asserting it; the amazement feels slightly performative vs. original よ removes the cognitive processing layer. The speaker sounds like they're showing off knowledge rather than experiencing surprise.

When to use this instead

Use よ when you're confirming something the listener might doubt, or when you have prior knowledge you want to emphasize. Use んだ when the realization is fresh and shared.

docs.title: 〜ね (confirmation seeking)
docs.text:
Softens the statement by seeking agreement. Less about realization, more about mutual recognition.

Example そんなに変かわるね、すごい。 English "You change that much, right — amazing." Intent Assumes both speaker and listener already recognize this; seeks confirmation rather than processing discovery vs. original ね skips the cognitive shift. It says "We both see this, don't we?" rather than "I'm just now seeing how big this is."

docs.title: 〜の (feminine softening, casual)
docs.text:
In very casual speech (often feminine-associated), の can replace んだ as a soft explanatory marker. Functionally similar, socially coded.

Example そんなに変かわるの、すごい。 English "So you change that much — amazing." Intent Same realization structure, softer and more intimate register vs. original の is slightly more personal and less explanatory than んだ. It sounds like sharing a secret thought rather than processing aloud.

Learner trap — register mismatch

Using の in masculine-coded speech or formal contexts sounds affected or childish. んだ is the safer default for casual mixed-gender speech.

docs.title: 〜わ (feminine assertion)
docs.text:
A more assertive feminine variant. Less common in modern speech but still heard.

Example そんなに変かわるわ、すごい。 English "It certainly changes that much — amazing." Intent Feminine assertiveness — the speaker is impressed and wants to state it clearly vs. original わ is a statement of personal reaction; んだ is a shared cognitive event.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
The realization function — processing new information aloud and reacting to it — must be preserved across register shifts. The formality changes the packaging, not the cognitive event.

Casual Basic Polite Extra Polite

よかったね。そんなに変かわるんだ、すごい。

"That's great. So you change that much — amazing."

Shift: Input sentence as-is. Casual register — んだ contraction, comma pause, standalone すごい as emotional reaction.

よかったですね。そんなに変かわるんです、すごいです。

"That's wonderful. So you change that much — it's amazing."

Shift: んだ → んです; すごい → すごいです. The realization structure is preserved but delivered with polite distance. The comma pause remains.

よかったですね。そんなに変かわられるのですね、素晴すばらしいです。

"That is wonderful indeed. So you change to such a degree — it is remarkable."

Shift: 変かわる → 変かわられる (respectful form); のですね adds formal confirmation; すごい → 素晴すばらしいです (elevated lexical choice). The cognitive processing is now ceremonial — appropriate for commenting on a superior's transformation.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: Step Pattern What It Adds Example N5 — Foundation 〜だ／〜です Plain copula — stating facts 猫ねこだ。"It's a cat." N5 — Foundation 〜よ／〜ね Sentence-final particles — emphasis and confirmation 猫ねこだよ／猫ねこだね。 N4 — Foundation 〜の (nominalizer) Turns clauses into noun-like concepts 行いくの。"The act of going." N4 — Core ← You are here 〜んだ (explanatory) Nominalization + copula + casual contraction; frames statement as explanation or realization そんなに変かわるんだ。"So it changes that much." N4 — Extended 〜んだよ／〜んだね んだ combined with よ (insistence) or ね (confirmation) 知しってるんだよ。"I do know, okay?" N3 — Extended 〜んじゃない Negative explanatory — "It's not that…" 嫌きらいなんじゃない。"It's not that I dislike it." N2 — Awareness 〜んだった (retroactive realization) Past form — "So it was that…" そうだったんだった。"So that's how it was."

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
一人ひとりで来きたんだ、偉えらいね。 You came alone [I just realized] — impressive. Literal: Alone came [realization], impressive, right?

~Context: Parent to child who arrived at an event solo. The んだ marks the moment of noticing; 偉えらいね is the praise that naturally follows.~

毎日まいにち練習れんしゅうしてるんだ、凄すごいね。 So you practice every day — that's amazing. Literal: Every day practicing [realization], amazing, right?

~Context: Friend discovering another friend's dedication. The 凄すごいね evaluates it; んだ processes the new information.~

日本にほんに来きたばかりなんだ、大変たいへんだね。 So you just got to Japan [I see now] — that must be tough. Literal: Japan came-just-now [realization], tough, right?

~Context: Expressing empathy upon learning someone's situation. The んだ turns the fact into a shared understanding that justifies the sympathy.~

そうなんだ。 I see / So that's how it is. Literal: So [it is] that way [realization].

~Context: The universal Japanese response to new information. そうなんだ is the sound of mental files being updated. Said alone, it completes a conversation turn.~

好すきなんだ、本気ほんきで。 So you really do like [them/it] — seriously. Literal: Like [realization], seriously.

~Context: Processing someone's true feelings that were previously unclear. The んだ marks the moment of clarity; 本気ほんきで adds weight to the realization.~

docs.title: 1. Using 〜んだ as a universal explanation
docs.text:
Learners often apply 〜んだ to every sentence, thinking it makes their Japanese more natural. But んだ carries specific pragmatic weight — overuse sounds like you're constantly explaining yourself or processing aloud.

Overused

今日きょうは月曜日げつようびなんだ。私わたしは学生がくせいなんだ。

"Today is Monday [explanatory]. I'm a student [explanatory]." — Sounds like you're anxiously justifying your existence. Bare facts don't need んだ.

Appropriate usage

今日きょうは月曜日げつようびだ。だから授業じゅぎょうがあるんだ。

"Today is Monday. That's why I have class [explanation]." — んだ justifies the relevance of the second clause.

docs.title: 2. Confusing 〜んだ with questions
docs.text:
In questions, 〜んだ seeks explanation. Learners sometimes use it as a plain question marker, which can sound demanding.

Socially pushy

何なにを食たべるんだ？

"What are you going to eat [explain yourself]?" — Without context, this sounds like "What the hell are you eating?" The んだ adds pressure.

Natural question

何なにを食たべる？

"What are you going to eat?" — Plain question, neutral tone. Or add context: 何なにを食たべたいんだ？"What do you want to eat [I'm trying to understand your preference]?"

docs.title: 3. Using casual 〜んだ in formal writing
docs.text:
Register crash

〜んだ is strictly casual spoken. Using it in essays, business emails, or presentations is inappropriate.

Avoid Use instead 重要じゅうようなんだ。 重要じゅうようなのである。／重要じゅうようである。

In formal contexts, use のだ or drop the nominalization entirely.

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text:
Collocation Meaning そうなんだ "I see / So that's how it is" — the universal realization response そうだったんだ "So that's how it was" — retroactive processing of past information 〜なんだ、すごい "[Realization], amazing" — the canonical two-beat praise pattern 〜なんだ、大変たいへんだね "[Realization], that must be hard" — empathy pattern 〜なんだ、偉えらいね "[Realization], impressive" — praise for effort or achievement 知しらなかったんだ "I didn't know that [but now I do]" — newly learned information 好すきなんだ "So you do like [them/it]" — processing confirmed feelings 寂さびしいんだ "I'm lonely [and that's my situation]" — vulnerable emotional sharing

~These collocations are worth learning as complete pragmatic units. The 〜なんだ、[reaction] pattern especially is a foundational rhythm of casual Japanese conversation.~

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• 〜んだ can stand alone as a response. If someone tells you something surprising, responding with just そうなんだ completes the conversational turn. It says "I have received and processed your information." The lack of follow-up is not rude — it's a valid social move.
  • The comma is prosodic, not just grammatical. In 変かわるんだ、すごい, the comma represents a genuine pause where the speaker's cognition catches up with their emotion. In fast speech this pause may be minimal; in emotional speech it can stretch. Writing it without the comma (変かわるんだすごい) looks wrong because it erases the breath.
  • 〜んだ softens contradiction. When correcting someone gently, 違ちがうんだ ("That's not quite right [let me explain]") is softer than 違ちがうよ ("Wrong!"). The んだ frames the correction as a shared discovery rather than an opposing claim.
  • Feminine の vs. masculine んだ is not absolute. While の is stereotypically feminine and んだ masculine, modern usage is more fluid. Women use んだ constantly in casual speech; men using の in very intimate contexts is not unheard of. The "rule" is more about register than gender.
  • 〜んだ can create emotional distance from oneself. Saying 寂さびしいんだ ("I'm lonely [and that's the situation]") is slightly more bearable than 寂さびしい ("I'm lonely") because the んだ frames the loneliness as an observable fact rather than a raw feeling. This self-objectification is a subtle emotional survival strategy.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N4 Recognize 〜んだ in speech; understand it as explanatory/realization marker; produce basic patterns correctly; avoid overuse in plain factual statements Developing — N4→N3 Deploy the two-beat structure naturally (〜んだ、[reaction]); distinguish んだ from よ and ね based on pragmatic intent; begin using んだ for soft insistence and emotional sharing Automatic — N3→N2 〜んだ arrives as a cognitive signal — you hear it and immediately register "this speaker is processing something." The two-beat pattern feels as natural as breathing. Mastery You control the emotional temperature with precision — dropping んだ for clinical distance, adding it for warmth, using の for intimacy, and knowing when んだよ would sound pushy rather than explanatory.

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 〜のだ (formal explanatory) — the full, uncontracted form; used in writing and formal speech
  • 〜んです (polite explanatory) — the polite contraction; safe for most spoken contexts
  • 〜の (feminine casual explanatory) — intimate register alternative to んだ

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 〜よ (assertion) — pushes information toward listener; んだ shares processing with listener
  • 〜ね (confirmation) — seeks agreement; んだ marks realization
  • 〜だろう (speculation) — uncertain conclusion; んだ treats the statement as established fact
  • 〜ってば (frustrated insistence) — repetitive and annoyed; んだ is explanatory and inviting

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• の (nominalizer) (N4) — turns clauses into noun-like concepts
  • だ (copula) (N5) — the "is" that frames nominalized statements
  • Casual contraction rules (N4) — の + だ → んだ in spoken Japanese

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 〜んだった (N2) — retroactive realization with surprise or regret
  • 〜んじゃない (N3) — negative explanatory; "It's not that…"
  • 〜んじゃなかった (N2) — negative retroactive realization; "I shouldn't have…"

docs.title: Packaging Your Own Feeling or Wish as a Soft Inner Voice — N3 Grammar
1. Overview & Nuance
docs.text:
なーって (and its shorter variant なって) is a casual spoken structure used to package your own feelings, wishes, or desires as a soft internal quotation — rather than stating them directly, you let the listener hear your inner voice at a comfortable, low-pressure distance.

The core sentence for this guide:

今こん週末しゅうまつ、トモエのところに、遊あそびに行いけたら、嬉うれしいなーって思おもってるんだけど。 "I've kind of been hoping I could go hang out at Tomoe's place this weekend, if that works out."

The speaker doesn't say "I want to go." They say "I've been inwardly going, 'that would be so nice…'" — a meaningfully different social move.

Core Rule

Use なーって to package your own feeling or wish as a soft internal quotation. You're not announcing it directly — you're letting the listener hear your inner voice at a safe, low-pressure distance.

docs.title: How it differs from related structures
docs.text:
Form Core Function Direction たい States a desire directly as a fact Speaker → listener (direct) と思おもう States a view or opinion Speaker → listener (assertive) なーって思おもう Reports a wish as an inner voice Speaker's inner world → shared gently かなって思おもう Reports genuine uncertainty / wondering Speaker's inner world → open-ended

A simple way to think about it: たい is "I want to go." なーって思おもってる is "I've been sitting here quietly thinking it'd be so nice if I could go." The desire is identical — the social packaging is entirely different.

Why this matters in Japanese communication

Japanese casual speech — especially between close friends and partners — strongly favors framing desires as inner-voice reports rather than direct requests. なーって思おもってるんだけど signals "I'm sharing this with you, not putting it on you." The listener can respond, affirm, or suggest an alternative without awkwardness. This indirect softness is not hesitation — it is skilled social calibration.

docs.title: 2a. What なーって is built from
docs.text:
なーって is a two-part structure:

  1. な — a sentence-final particle expressing a soft personal feeling, wish, or emotional color (distinct from the prohibitive な)
  2. って — the casual quotation marker, equivalent to と in quotation structures

Together they mean: "[inner feeling/wish]…" — is what I've been thinking/feeling.

The elongated なー (vs. plain な) is not decorative. It signals a held, slightly longing emotional state — warmer and more wistful. Shortening it to なって makes the same sentence slightly more matter-of-fact. Native speakers adjust this micro-signal unconsciously.

docs.title: 2b. Attachment rules by word type
docs.text:
Word Type Base Form + なーって Note Verb — plain non-past 行いく 行いくなーって Direct attachment to plain form Verb — plain past / potential past 行いけた 行いけたなーって Very common with potential past forms Verb — masu-form — ❌ Not used なーって attaches to plain forms only Verb — ない-form 行いかない 行いかないなーって Expresses resigned or reluctant inner feeling Verb — する／くる する／くる するなーって／くるなーって Direct attachment to dictionary form i-Adjective 嬉うれしい 嬉うれしいなーって い retained — the exact form in the target sentence i-Adjective — ない-form 嬉うれしくない 嬉うれしくないなーって Attach directly after くない na-Adjective 好すきだ 好すきだなーって だ is retained — unlike some patterns, it is not dropped here Noun 夢ゆめだ 夢ゆめだなーって だ required before なーって

The masu-form trap

なーって only attaches to plain forms. A very common learner error is treating it like a sentence-ender that works with any conjugation.

❌ 行いきますなーって思おもってる。 ✅ 行いけたら嬉うれしいなーって思おもってる。

docs.title: 3. The Three Hedging Layers
docs.text:
The target sentence is more sophisticated than it first appears. It stacks three hedging mechanisms deliberately:

Layer Element Function ① 行いけたら Conditional softener — "if it works out" ② 嬉うれしいなーって Inner-voice framing — packages the wish as a private feeling ③ 思おもってるんだけど Ongoing (ている) + explanatory (ん) + open hanger (だけど) — invites a response

Together these turn a private wish into a gentle social bid. The speaker is not just reporting an inner state — they are opening a door. This is sophisticated, natural casual intimacy, not evasiveness.

Learn this as a complete pragmatic package

〜なーって思おもってるんだけど is one of the most common ways Japanese adults make indirect proposals or invitations in casual speech. Learning it as a fixed social chunk is more useful than analyzing it piece by piece every time.

docs.title: Level 1 — Simple Inner Feeling
docs.text:
Example 1 · Self-reflection

最近さいきん、もっと運動うんどうしなきゃなーって思おもってる。 "Lately I've been feeling like I really need to work out more." Self-reflection shared as inner voice — not a complaint or announcement to the listener.

Example 2 · Wistful trailing off (なーって without 思おもう)

もうちょっとだけ話はなせたら良よかったなーって。 "I kind of wish we could've talked a little longer." Said after parting. Trailing なーって without 思おもう is natural when the wistfulness speaks for itself — the unfinished sentence is part of the emotional effect.

docs.title: Level 2 — Sharing a Private Thought
docs.text:
Example 3 · Long-held thought surfacing

そろそろ引ひっ越こしたいなーって思おもってたんだよね。 "I'd been thinking it might be time to move soon, you know." The んだよね ending invites shared acknowledgment — the speaker is drawing the listener into the thought, not just reporting it.

Example 4 · Tentative life decision

転職てんしょくしようかなーって思おもってて、今いまいろいろ調しらべてるところ。 "I've been kind of thinking about changing jobs, so I'm looking into things right now." しようかなーって signals the idea is still forming — invites the friend into the thinking process rather than announcing a conclusion.

docs.title: Level 3 — Indirect Proposal / Soft Invitation (the target sentence)
docs.text:
Example 5 · Soft invitation with どう？

ハルも一緒いっしょに来これたら楽たのしいなーって思おもってたんだけど、どう？ "I was kind of hoping you could come too — what do you think?" どう？ at the end makes the implicit invitation explicit without pressure. Classic soft-proposal structure.

Example 6 · The target sentence

今こん週末しゅうまつ、トモエのところに遊あそびに行いけたら嬉うれしいなーって思おもってるんだけど。 "I've kind of been hoping I could go hang out at Tomoe's place this weekend, if that works out." All three hedging layers active. The speaker floats a wish, frames it as inner speech, and hangs the sentence open for the listener to respond naturally.

docs.title: 5. Nuance Comparison — なーって vs. Similar Structures
docs.text: Grammar Point Core Meaning Emotional Nuance Best Used When JLPT なーって（思おもう） Inner soft wish reported as internal quote Wistful, warm, low-pressure Floating a wish or hope to a close listener N3 なって（思おもう） Same structure, shorter vowel Slightly more neutral / less wistful Same as above; な vs なー is emotional coloring only N3 かなって（思おもう） Genuine wondering / self-questioning More uncertain; speaker isn't sure themselves Open-ended wondering — not just softening a known wish N3 と思おもう Speaker thinks / believes X Direct and assertive; presenting a view Stating an opinion, assessment, or prediction N4 と思おもってる Speaker is currently / ongoing thinking X More direct than なって; less inner-voice feel Sharing a current view or plan — less hedged N4 〜ものだと思おもっていた Had been thinking X was the expected state Retrospective; assumption that turned out wrong Reflecting on a past belief with mild surprise N2

docs.title: 6. The Mistake Monitor
docs.text:
Pitfall 1 — Dropping な and treating って as equivalent

Learners often drop な thinking it's optional filler. It is not — な is what transforms the content from a neutral thought into an emotional inner utterance.

  • With な: 行いけたら嬉うれしいなーって思おもってる。→ "I've been feeling like it'd be so nice if I could go." (soft, wistful, inner-voice)
  • Without な: 行いけたら嬉うれしいって思おもってる。→ "I think it'd make me happy if I could go." (neutral, analytical, matter-of-fact)

Pitfall 2 — Defaulting to たい when なーって is more appropriate

たい directly states a desire as a fact. In casual intimate speech, this can feel slightly blunt where なーって would be warmer and more natural.

❌ (slightly blunt) 遊あそびに行いきたい。→ "I want to go hang out." ✅ (soft, social) 遊あそびに行いけたら嬉うれしいなーって思おもってる。→ "I've been hoping it'd work out to go hang out."

The desire is identical. The difference is how much social space you give the other person.

Pitfall 3 — Using なーって alone without 思おもう in writing

Trailing なーって without 思おもう works naturally in spoken Japanese when intonation carries the unfinished feeling. In writing, it can read as ambiguous or incomplete.

Ambiguous in writing: 行いけたら嬉うれしいなーって。 Clear: 行いけたら嬉うれしいなーって思おもってる。

なーって vs. かなって — the closest pair

Both are inner-voice quotations, but they answer different questions.

  • なーって: The speaker already knows what they feel and is sharing it softly. → 行いけたら嬉うれしいなーって ("It'd be so nice if I could go…")
  • かなって: The speaker is genuinely wondering and hasn't settled on a feeling yet. → 行いけたらいいかなって ("I wonder if it'd be good if I could go…")

If the speaker wants to go, use なーって. If they're not sure whether they want to, use かなって.

docs.title: 7. Formality Ladder
docs.text: Register Japanese Natural English What Changed Casual ⟦タメ口ぐち⟧ 今こん週末しゅうまつ、トモエんちに遊あそびに行いけたら嬉うれしいなーって思おもってるんだけど。 "I've kind of been hoping I could head over to Tomoe's place this weekend." のところに → んちに (natural casual contraction). Core warmth unchanged. Basic Polite ⟦丁寧ていねい語ご⟧ 今こん週末しゅうまつ、トモエさんのところに遊あそびに行いけたら嬉うれしいなって思おもっているんですが。 "I've been thinking it would be really nice if I could go visit Tomoe this weekend." なー → な (elongation is casual-only); 思おもってるんだけど → 思おもっているんですが. Soft indirect-proposal function fully preserved — ですが mirrors だけど as a polite open-ended hanger. Formal ⟦N2 awareness⟧ 今こん週末しゅうまつ、トモエさんのところに伺うかがえればと思おもっているのですが。 "I was thinking that, if possible, I might stop by Tomoe's place this weekend." 伺うかがえる is humble keigo for visiting — raises register significantly. 〜ければと思おもっている replaces the inner-voice quotation entirely. No longer feels like shared inner speech — feels like a considered proposal.

docs.title: 8. Practice Drill
docs.text:
1. Translate to Japanese using なーって思おもってる: "I've been thinking it'd be nice if we could meet up soon." (casual, to a close friend)
  2. Convert from direct to inner-voice: 「一緒いっしょに住すみたい。」→ Reframe using なーって思おもってる to make it softer and less direct.
  3. Translate: "I've kind of been wondering if I should quit." (Use しようかなーって to capture genuine uncertainty)
  4. Spot and fix the error: 早はやく会あいたいですなーって思おもってる。
  5. Choose the right form: Your friend asks if you want to come to a party. You're not sure you want to go, but you're considering it. Do you use なーって or かなって? Write a natural response.

docs.title: 9. Answer Key
docs.text:
Click to reveal answers

Question 1 そろそろ会あえたら嬉うれしいなーって思おもってる。 Conditional 会あえたら (potential past) + 嬉うれしいなーって + 思おもってる. そろそろ ("soon / about time") sets the soft time frame naturally.

Question 2 ❌ (direct) 一緒いっしょに住すみたい。 ✅ (inner-voice) 一緒いっしょに住すめたら嬉うれしいなーって思おもってる。 The desire is identical. The inner-voice framing gives the listener more social space — less "I'm asking you for something," more "I'm sharing a feeling with you."

Question 3 やめようかなーって思おもってる。 Use かなって here, not なーって — the speaker is genuinely uncertain (wondering whether to quit), not softening a known wish. しようかなーって is the key form: the か signals the open wondering.

Question 4 ❌ 早はやく会あいたいですなーって思おもってる。 ✅ 早はやく会あいたいなーって思おもってる。 Error: なーって attaches to plain forms only. です is a polite copula — it is incompatible with this structure. Drop です entirely; たい is already a plain-form adjective that attaches directly.

Question 5 Use かなって — you are genuinely unsure, so the wondering form is correct. 行いけたらいいかなって思おもってるんだけど、まだわからない。 "I've been kind of wondering if it'd be good if I could go — I'm still not sure." If you already knew you wanted to go and were just softening the way you said it, なーって would be the right choice instead.

docs.title: 〜のこと — The Person Themselves / What Concerns X
〜のこと — The Person Themselves / What Concerns X
タキナのこと、もっと大事だいじにすればよかった…
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — Mid N3

のこと at its core is an N4 pattern — nominalizing a person or situation with の and adding こと for abstraction. But the emotional, interpersonal usage — referring to someone as a whole person, with all their feelings, history, and significance bundled together — is a mid-N3 pragmatic layer that textbooks rarely teach explicitly.

Tier Pattern Description Example N4 — Foundation 〜のこと (factual) Nominalization — "the fact that X" or "the matter of X" 彼かれが来こないのことは問題もんだいだ。"The fact that he's not coming is a problem." N4 — Foundation 〜のことが好すき "I like [everything about] X" — のこと bundles the person beyond surface traits 彼かれのことが好すき。"I like him [as a person]." N3 — Core ← You are here 〜のこと + emotional verb のこと marks the person as the holistic object of emotion, regret, care, or responsibility タキナのこと、もっと大事だいじにすればよかった。 N3 — Extended 〜のことばかり "Only thinking about X" — のこと as obsessive focus 彼かれのことばかり考かんがえてる。"I can't stop thinking about him." N2 — Awareness 〜のことだから "Because it's X [I know them well]" — のこと marks intimate knowledge タキナのことだから、きっと怒おこってないよ。"It's Takina we're talking about — I'm sure she's not mad."

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 〜のこと turns a person's name (or a situation) into a holistic object — not just the person, but everything that concerns them: their feelings, their wellbeing, their significance to the speaker.
  • When attached to a person's name and followed by an emotional verb (大事だいじにする、思おもう、心配しんぱいする、 etc.), it signals that the speaker is treating the person as a complete, irreducible whole — not as a task, a role, or a set of traits.
  • In the input sentence, タキナのこと is the object of 大事だいじにする — but the のこと layer transforms it from "value Takina" (surface-level) to "value everything that Takina is and means to me" (emotional depth).
  • This is the grammar of interpersonal weight. Native speakers use のこと automatically when the relationship matters; omitting it can sound coldly transactional.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Use 〜のこと when you want to refer to someone (or something) not as a discrete entity but as a bundled whole — their entire being, their feelings, their situation, everything that concerns them. The こと adds abstraction and scope; the の tethers it to the specific referent.

彼かれのことが好すきだ。 I like him [not just his looks or behavior — him as a whole person]. Literal: His matters/self [object] like-is. Context: The classic confession. Saying 彼かれが好すきだ is grammatically fine but sounds like "I like [the fact of] him" — distant and analytical. 彼かれのことが好すきだ is what you say when you're emotionally invested in the entire person. タキナのこと、もっと大事だいじにすればよかった…ほんとにごめん。 I should have valued Takina more… I'm truly sorry. Literal: Takina's matters/self, more value-do-if-was-good… truly sorry. Context: The input sentence. Spoken in regret — the speaker realizes they failed to honor the full weight of who Takina is. The trailing ほんとにごめん is raw apology without mitigation.

docs.title: In Real Life
docs.text:
In Real Life

Situation: 千束せんぞく (Chisato) is speaking to a close friend after a falling-out with タキナ (Takina). The conflict has been resolved, but Chisato is reflecting on her own behavior during the fight. She was distracted by work, took Takina's patience for granted, and said something careless. Now, alone with her thoughts, she's admitting fault.

タキナのこと、もっと大事だいじにすればよかった…ほんとにごめん。

"I should have valued Takina more… I'm truly sorry."

Why のこと fits here: Without のこと, タキナをもっと大事だいじにすればよかった would mean "I should have treated Takina more carefully" — still valid, but focused on actions toward a person. With のこと, the scope expands: Takina's feelings, her presence, her history with the speaker, her unspoken needs — all of it becomes the object that was neglected. The regret is total, not transactional.

What makes this natural: The sentence is self-directed — Chisato isn't apologizing to Takina here (that would require face-to-face mitigation strategies). She's confessing to a third party, or to herself, acknowledging the full emotional weight of her failure. のこと is what makes this feel like genuine emotional reckoning rather than performative regret.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
のこと is a nominalization pattern built from の (genitive/linking particle) + こと (nominalizer — "thing, matter, fact"). Together they create an abstract noun phrase that bundles everything associated with the preceding noun.
