This is less direct than English "must" — it approaches necessity through fear of negative outcome rather than assertion of positive duty. This indirectness is characteristically Japanese.

docs.title: 1. Using ultra-casual forms in formal contexts
docs.text:
Register crash

明日あしたまでに出ださなきゃ。

"I gotta submit by tomorrow." — In a workplace email to a superior, this sounds childish and disrespectful.

Appropriate

明日あしたまでに提出ていしゅつしなければなりません。

"Submission is required by tomorrow." — Formal, appropriate, clear.

docs.title: 2. Confusing obligation with prohibition
docs.text:
Opposite meaning

入はいらなきゃ。

"I must not enter" [wrong — this means "I must enter"]*

Correct

入はいらなきゃ。

"I gotta enter." — Positive obligation through double negative.

入はいってはいけない。

"You must not enter." — Prohibition through te-form + negative permission.

docs.title: 3. Overusing なければなりません in casual speech
docs.text:
Social stiffness

友達ともだちに「一緒いっしょに行いかなければなりません」

"To a friend: 'We must go together.'" — Sounds like a military order or legal requirement.

Appropriate

一緒いっしょに行いかないと。

"We gotta go together, or…" — Casual, friendly, appropriately hanging.

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text: Collocation Meaning 〜しなきゃだよね "We gotta do it, right?" — shared obligation with confirmation 〜しないといけませんね "We must do it, mustn't we?" — polite reminder 〜しなきゃいけない "I gotta do it" — standard casual obligation 〜しないとだめ "If not, no good" — explicit consequence 〜しなくちゃ "I gotta do it [cute]" — feminine/childish 〜しなければ "If I don't do it…" — formal conditional, often hanging 〜ずにはいられない "Cannot help doing" — emotional compulsion 〜しなきゃ困こまる "If I don't do it, I'll be in trouble" — personal vulnerability as request 〜しないと間まに合あわない "If not, won't make it" — explicit time pressure 〜しないと怒おこられる "If not, will be scolded" — social consequence fear

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N4 Produce なければならない correctly; recognize なきゃ, ないと in speech; understand the double-negative logic; avoid register mismatches Developing — N4→N3 Deploy なきゃ/ないと naturally in casual speech; use ないといけません for polite reminders; calibrate なければなりません for formal contexts; recognize なくちゃ gender coding Automatic — N3→N2 The full spectrum arrives as intuition — you feel the difference between なきゃ (panicked), ないと (explanatory), ないといけません (gentle), and なければなりません (official). The hanging consequence feels natural. Mastery You control the obligation temperature with precision — using なきゃだよね for bonding, ないと for warnings, ないといけませんね for polite adult reminders, and なければなりません for authority. You also recognize when someone uses obligation grammar to express vulnerability or need.

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 〜てはいけない — Prohibition; "must not"
  • 〜なくてはいけない — Formal variant of obligation
  • 〜ずにはいられない — Emotional compulsion; "cannot help but"

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 〜てもいい — Permission; "may do" — opposite of obligation
  • 〜なくてもいい — Negative permission; "don't have to" — obligation release
  • 〜べきだ — Strong obligation/duty; "ought to" — more moral than social
  • 〜なければよかった — Past counterfactual regret; "should have"

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• 〜ない (N5) — Negative form
  • 〜ば conditional (N4) — なければ
  • 〜と conditional (N4) — ないと
  • 〜ては (N4) — なくては
  • 〜くれる / 〜もらう (N4) — Benefactive frame (in 〜てくれないと困こまる patterns)

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 〜ずにはいられない (N2) — Emotional compulsion
  • 〜なければよかった (N3) — Regret; "should have done"
  • 〜なくてはならなかった (N3) — Past obligation
  • 〜ないとだめだった (N3) — Past failed obligation

docs.title: Model
docs.text: Kimi 2.6 Instant

docs.title: 〜っぱなし — Leaving Something in an Ongoing State
Reference Sentence: 〜っぱなし — "Left Doing" / "Left in the State of Doing"
Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 〜っぱなし attaches to the masu-stem of a verb and means "to leave something in an ongoing state" — the action continues (or its result persists) without the attention, intervention, or closure that would normally be expected.
  • It carries a strong nuance of neglect, forgetfulness, or failure to complete the proper cycle of an action. Something is on, open, running, or undone when it should have been stopped, closed, or finished.
  • In the reference sentence — テレビが付つけっぱなしです — the TV has been left on (it is in the "turned-on" state, and someone should have turned it off but didn't).
  • っぱなし is distinct from 〜たまま, which neutrally describes a state persisting, and from 〜ている, which simply states an ongoing action or resultant state without the judgment of neglect.

docs.title: Core Meaning — "Left in an Inappropriate State"
docs.text:
Core Rule

Use 〜っぱなし when you want to say that an action or its result continues without being properly closed or attended to — usually because someone forgot, neglected, or failed to complete the expected follow-up.

テレビが付つけっぱなしです。 The TV has been left on. Literal: TV turned-on-left-alone is. Someone should have turned it off; the state of being on is continuing unattended.

docs.title: The Sentence
docs.text: テレビが付つけっぱなしです。

docs.title: Natural Translation
docs.text: "The TV has been left on." / "The TV is still on (and shouldn't be)."

docs.title: Component Breakdown
docs.text: Component Form Function テレビ Noun Television が Subject particle Marks the TV as the thing in the state 付つけ Masu-stem of 付つける (to turn on) The base to which っぱなし attaches っぱなし Suffix "Left in the state of doing" — adds neglect nuance です Polite copula Makes the sentence polite

docs.title: Why Not 〜ている or 〜たまま?
docs.text: Form Sentence Nuance 〜ている テレビが付ついている。 "The TV is on." (neutral state; no judgment) 〜たまま テレビが付つけたままだ。 "The TV is on (and was left that way)." (persistent state; can be neutral or neglectful depending on context) 〜っぱなし テレビが付つけっぱなしだ。 "The TV is left on (and someone should have turned it off)." (strong neglect nuance)

docs.title: The Grammar
docs.text:
Verb (masu-stem) + っぱなし

Remove ます from the polite form to get the stem, then attach っぱなし directly. っぱなし functions as a noun (it can be followed by だ/です). It comes from the verb 放はなす (はなす, to let go / to release), with the same root as 放はなし飼がい (free-range) — the idea is that the action has been "released" and left to run.

Verb Masu-stem + っぱなし Meaning 付つける (to turn on) 付つけ 付つけっぱなし Left on 開あける (to open) 開あけ 開あけっぱなし Left open 出だす (to take out) 出だし 出だしっぱなし Left out (not put away) 書かく (to write) 書かき 書かきっぱなし Left half-written; left as-is without finishing 言いう (to say) 言いい 言いいっぱなし Left as just words; said and then abandoned without follow-up 立たつ (to stand) 立たち 立たちっぱなし Left standing (without sitting) 座すわる (to sit) 座すわり 座すわりっぱなし Left sitting (without moving)

Only attaches to the masu-stem

You cannot attach っぱなし to the dictionary form, te-form, or any other conjugation. Only the masu-stem (the part before ます) is used.

docs.title: The Neglect Nuance — The Core of っぱなし
docs.text:
っぱなし always implies that something should have been done but wasn't — the state is not just continuing; it's continuing inappropriately. This is what distinguishes it from neutral state descriptions.

Sentence Nuance 窓まどが開あいている。 The window is open. (No judgment — maybe it's open for fresh air.) 窓まどが開あけっぱなしだ。 The window has been left open. (Someone should have closed it — it's cold, it's insecure, etc.) 扇風機せんぷうきが回まわっている。 The fan is running. (Neutral.) 扇風機せんぷうきが付つけっぱなしだ。 The fan has been left on. (Someone forgot to turn it off; waste of electricity.)

The speaker using っぱなし is criticising the situation, even if mildly. It's not just observation — it's observation with a "this shouldn't be the case" judgment.

docs.title: 〜っぱなし vs. 〜たまま — A Critical Distinction
docs.text:
Both describe a state continuing, but the nuance differs sharply.

〜っぱなし 〜たまま Nuance Neglect, oversight, inappropriateness — the state should have been resolved. Persistence — the state simply hasn't changed yet; neutral or context-dependent. Judgment Inherently critical or regretful. Can be neutral, practical, or sometimes critical if context adds it. Typical use Complaints, noticing forgotten things. Describing posture, position, or unchanged situation. Example 電気でんきが付つけっぱなしだ。 "The light has been left on." (Wasteful; someone forgot.) 電気でんきが付ついたままだ。 "The light is still on." (Just stating; maybe it's intentional.) Example 靴くつを脱ぬぎっぱなしだ。 "The shoes have been left lying around." (Tidy them up!) 靴くつを脱ぬいだままだ。 "The shoes are still off." (Maybe the person is still inside; no criticism implied.)

Rule of thumb

If you're annoyed that something hasn't been put right, use っぱなし. If you're simply describing that a state hasn't changed, use まま.

docs.title: Extended Examples — っぱなし in Real Life
Household Forgetfulness
docs.text: Japanese English 冷蔵庫れいぞうこが開あけっぱなしだよ。 The fridge has been left open. Someone didn't close it properly; food will spoil. 部屋へやの電気でんきが付つけっぱなしだった。 The room light had been left on. I found it on when it shouldn't have been. 水みずを出だしっぱなしにしないで。 Don't leave the water running. Turn it off when you're done. お菓子かしを食たべっぱなしで片付かたづけてない。 You left the snacks out after eating — didn't tidy up. The eating is done; the remains are still there.

docs.title: Undone Actions / Half-Finished States
docs.text: Japanese English 書類しょるいを書かきっぱなしで提出ていしゅつしていない。 I left the documents half-written and haven't submitted them. The writing was interrupted; the task is incomplete. 彼かれは言いいっぱなしで行動こうどうしない。 He just talks and never follows through. The words are "left out there" without action. 仕事しごとをやりっぱなしにして帰かえった。 He left work half-done and went home. Tasks were started but not finished or put away.

docs.title: Personal State — Standing, Sitting, Wearing
docs.text: Japanese English 電車でんしゃで一時間いちじかんも立たちっぱなしだった。 I was left standing on the train for a whole hour. No seat; standing continued without relief. 一日中いちにちじゅう座すわりっぱなしで腰こしが痛いたい。 I've been sitting all day and my back hurts. The sitting continued unbroken, to a negative degree. 靴くつを履はきっぱなしで家いえに上あがらないで。 Don't come into the house with your shoes still on. The shoes should have been removed at the entrance.

docs.title: 1. Using っぱなし for neutral or positive continuation
docs.text:
っぱなし is inherently negative — it implies neglect or something left undone. Using it for a neutral or intentional state sounds accusatory or odd.

Incorrect nuance

音楽おんがくをかけっぱなしで勉強べんきょうするのが好すきです。

~"I like studying with the music left on." Sounds like you're complaining about the music being on, not enjoying it.~

More natural

音楽おんがくをかけたまま勉強べんきょうするのが好すきです。

~"I like studying with the music playing." まま is neutral; no neglect nuance.~

docs.title: 2. Attaching っぱなし to the wrong form
docs.text:
っぱなし only attaches to the masu-stem. Using the dictionary form, te-form, or past tense is incorrect.

Incorrect

付つけるっぱなし ❌ 付つけったっぱなし ❌

Correct

付つけっぱなし ✅ (masu-stem of 付つける)

docs.title: 3. Confusing っぱなし with 〜たまま for all contexts
docs.text:
Though they overlap, っぱなし adds judgment. Using it when simply describing a persistent state without criticism can make the speaker sound unfairly harsh.

Overly critical

彼かれは若わかいままだ。 → 彼かれは若わかいっぱなしだ ❌

~"He remains young." っぱなし with i-adjectives isn't standard; "left young" makes no sense. まま is correct.~

Correct

彼かれは若わかいままだ。

docs.title: Quick Reference Table
docs.text: Pattern Meaning Example Masu-stem + っぱなし Left doing / left in a state (neglect) 付つけっぱなし "left on" Masu-stem + っぱなし + だ／です Polite/Negative statement about the state 付つけっぱなしです 〜っぱなしにする To leave something in that state (deliberate or causal) 開あけっぱなしにする "leave it open" 〜っぱなしにしないで Don't leave it in that state 出だしっぱなしにしないで "Don't leave it out"

docs.title: Similar Patterns
docs.text:
• 〜たまま — Persistent state, neutral. "The window is still open (as it was)." No neglect nuance.
  • 〜ている — Ongoing action or resultant state. "The TV is on." Pure state description.
  • 〜ずにいる — "To remain without doing." Negative action remaining undone, but without the "left" judgment.
  • 〜放題ほうだい（ほうだい） — "All you can / as much as you like." Different nuance — freedom, not neglect. 食たべ放題ほうだい, 言いいたい放題ほうだい.

docs.title: Contrast With
docs.text: • 〜っぱなし vs. 〜たまま — Neglect vs. neutral persistence. See the comparison section above.

docs.title: 〜っぱなし — Left On / Left Unfinished
テレビが付つけっぱなしです。
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — Mid N3

The core concept of 〜っぱなし is N4-accessible — a suffix indicating a state left unchanged. But the pragmatic layer — the mild reproach, the sense of negligence, the implication that someone should have fixed this — is solidly N3. The form also requires comfort with verb stem manipulation that sits at the N3 boundary.

Tier Pattern Description Example N4 — Foundation 〜たまま "Left as is" — state continuation, neutral or descriptive 窓まどを開あけたまま寝ねた。"Slept with the window open." N4 — Foundation 〜ている Ongoing state — progressive or resultative 電気でんきがついている。"The light is on." N3 — Core ← You are here 〜っぱなし State left deliberately or negligently unchanged — carries mild reproach or awareness of incompletion テレビてれびが付つけっぱなしです。 N3 — Extended 〜っぱなし + social reproach Using っぱなし to indirectly criticize someone's carelessness 靴くつを脱ぬぎっぱなしにしないで。"Don't leave your shoes kicked off [everywhere]." N2 — Awareness 〜っぱなしの + noun modification Embedded in noun phrases for compound description 開あけっぱなしの扉とびら。"A door left standing open."

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 〜っぱなし attaches to the stem of a transitive verb to indicate that the resulting state has been left unchanged — typically through negligence, forgetfulness, or deliberate inaction.
  • Unlike 〜たまま (neutral description), 〜っぱなし carries a subtle emotional charge: someone should have returned things to normal, and didn't. The state feels suspended, waiting for resolution.
  • In the input sentence, 付つけっぱなし describes a TV left on — not merely "the TV is on" (付ついている), but "the TV was turned on and left that way." The implication: someone should have turned it off.
  • The form is common in household complaints, self-recrimination, and observations of carelessness. It turns a static state into a small narrative of human failing.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Use 〜っぱなし when you want to describe a state that resulted from a transitive action and was then left unchanged — with the implication that the responsible party failed to complete the natural follow-up action (turning off, closing, putting away, etc.).

窓まどを開あけっぱなしにして出でかけた。 I went out leaving the window open [and shouldn't have]. Literal: Window open-left-as-is doing went-out. Context: Self-recrimination or explanation to someone who noticed. The っぱなし marks the negligence; the して出でかけた adds the compounding error of leaving. テレビてれびが付つけっぱなしです。 The TV has been left on [someone should have turned it off]. Literal: TV turned-on-left-as-is is. Context: The input sentence. Entering a room and noticing the TV still running. The です softens the observation into a polite report, but the っぱなし carries the unspoken "again?" or "really?"

docs.title: In Real Life
docs.text:
In Real Life

Situation: ユキ comes home from work. Her roommate ハル was supposed to be home all day. Yuki enters the living room and finds the TV blaring, the lights on, and no one in the room. She texts Haru later that evening.

テレビが付つけっぱなしです。

"The TV's been left on."

Why 〜っぱなし fits here: Yuki could have said テレビがついています ("The TV is on") — a neutral observation. Or テレビを消けさなかったの？ ("Didn't you turn off the TV?") — direct confrontation. 付つけっぱなしです strikes a middle path: it reports the state while implying negligence, without accusing directly. The です makes it a polite observation; the っぱなし makes it a gentle reproach.

What makes this natural: In shared living situations, っぱなし is the grammar of domestic friction — soft enough to avoid open conflict, clear enough to communicate that something is wrong. The implication is always "you (or someone) started this and didn't finish."

The unspoken continuation: A native speaker hearing this immediately supplies the missing action: 消けしてほしかった ("I wish you'd turned it off"). The っぱなし leaves the resolution hanging, which is precisely its power — it describes an incomplete story.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
〜っぱなし is a suffix that attaches to the stem of transitive verbs (verbs that take a direct object and cause a change of state). It describes the resultant state of that verb being left as-is, typically because the agent failed to perform the natural restoring action.

Layer 1 — Verb stem: The suffix attaches to the stem form (ren'yōkei), not the te-form or plain form. This creates a compound word that functions as a noun or adjectival noun.

Layer 2 — っぱなし (the frozen state): The etymology is debated, but ぱなし likely derives from 放はなす (hanasu, "to release, to leave alone"). The sense is "released into a state and abandoned." The initial っ is a phonological glue that connects tightly to the stem.

Layer 3 — Implied negligence: Unlike 〜たまま which simply describes a concurrent state, っぱなし implies a deviation from expected behavior. Something was activated or changed, and the responsible party walked away without restoring the default state.

電気でんきをつけっぱなし The light left on The turning-on happened; the turning-off did not. Someone's action created an ongoing state that now needs correction. 電気でんきがついている The light is on Neutral state description. No implication about how it got that way or who is responsible.

docs.title: Real Speech vs. Textbook Form
docs.text:
Textbooks often present 〜っぱなし as "leaving something on/unfinished." In real speech, it's a social tool — the grammar of noticing negligence without naming the negligent party.

Form Example Note Textbook (neutral) 窓まどを開あけっぱなしにする。 "To leave the window open" — grammatical but stripped of social context Real speech (reproach) また靴くつを脱ぬぎっぱなし？ "Shoes kicked off again?" — the っぱなし + また (again) compounds the criticism Real speech (self-recrimination) 冷蔵庫れいぞうこを開あけっぱなしにしちゃった。 "I left the fridge open [careless me]" — ちゃった adds regret to the negligence Real speech (polite report) テレビてれびが付つけっぱなしです。 The input sentence — polite observation with embedded criticism

docs.title: Function in Context
docs.text:
• テレビが marks the TV as the grammatical subject — but semantically, the TV is the patient, not the agent. It was acted upon (付つける) and left in that acted-upon state.
  • 付つけっぱなし is the compound: stem of 付つける (to turn on) + っぱなし. The verb 付つける is transitive (requires an agent), which is mandatory for っぱなし — intransitive states don't take this suffix.
  • です closes the sentence politely. In a more casual or frustrated context, this might be だよね or even just っぱなし with rising intonation: 付つけっぱなし？
  • The absence of an explicit agent (who left it on) is functional — the speaker avoids direct accusation. In a household context, the responsible party is usually obvious from context, so naming them would be confrontational.
  • Without っぱなし: テレビがついています is a neutral observation. With っぱなし: the TV becomes evidence of someone's incomplete action — a small domestic crime scene.
本ほんを広ひろげっぱなしで寝ねちゃった。 I fell asleep with the book spread open [face-down, pages strained, a bibliophile's nightmare]. Literal: Book spread-open-left-as-is slept [regrettable]. Context: Self-recrimination. The っぱなし marks the book as victim of the sleeper's negligence; ちゃった compounds the regret.

docs.title: Construction
docs.text:
Verb Type Dictionary Form Stem + っぱなし Note Godan — く 書かく 書かき 書かきっぱなし "Left written" — unfinished writing Godan — ぐ 脱ぬぐ 脱ぬぎ 脱ぬぎっぱなし "Left kicked off" — shoes, clothes Godan — す 出だす 出だし 出だしっぱなし "Left out" — things not put away Godan — つ 立たつ 立たち 立たちっぱなし "Left standing" — standing idle, or left upright Godan — ぬ 死しぬ — — Intransitive — cannot take っぱなし Godan — ぶ 呼よぶ 呼よび 呼よびっぱなし "Left called" — phone off-hook, name unretracted Godan — む 読よむ 読よみ 読よみっぱなし "Left unread" — started but not finished Godan — る 帰かえる 帰かえり 帰かえりっぱなし "Left as returned" — doesn't occur naturally Ichidan 食たべる 食たべ 食たべっぱなし "Left eating" — started eating, never stopped Irregular — する する し しっぱなし "Left as done" — unfinished business Irregular — くる くる き きっぱなし "Left as came" — doesn't occur naturally

Transitivity requirement

〜っぱなし requires a transitive verb — one that takes a direct object and changes the state of something. Intransitive verbs (起おきる, 死しぬ, 来くる, 行いく) cannot take っぱなし because there is no agent who "left" the state unresolved. Saying 起おきっぱなし is ungrammatical.

The stem form

Learners sometimes try to attach っぱなし to the te-form or plain form. The correct attachment is the stem (ren'yōkei — the form used before ます). 付つける → 付つけっぱなし, not 付つけてっぱなし or 付つくっぱなし.

docs.title: Noun Modification
docs.text:
〜っぱなし can modify nouns directly, creating compound descriptions of neglected states.

Pattern Example Meaning 〜っぱなしの + Noun 開あけっぱなしの扉とびら "A door left standing open" 〜っぱなしで + Verb 靴くつを脱ぬぎっぱなしで寝ねた "Slept with shoes kicked off" 〜っぱなしだ (predicative) 窓まどが開あけっぱなしだ "The window is left open"

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
Japanese domestic and social harmony depends heavily on restoring default states. Shoes are removed at the genkan and arranged. Lights are turned off when leaving rooms. Doors are closed. These are not merely practical actions — they are social signals that one respects shared space and doesn't burden others with cleanup.

〜っぱなし is the grammar of failed restoration. It marks a state where the social contract of "leave things as you found them" has been broken. The reproach is rarely explicit because the grammar itself carries the accusation.

  • Default states as social obligation. In Japanese spatial culture, every object has a proper state. A TV is off when not watched. A door is closed when not passed through. っぱなし describes a state that violates this expectation — and the violation is social, not merely physical.
  • Indirect reproach as face-saving. By describing the state (付つけっぱなし) rather than accusing the person (あなたが消けさなかった), the speaker protects both parties' face. The TV becomes the subject; the human fault is implied but unspoken.
  • Self-recrimination through objectification. When used for oneself (開あけっぱなしにしちゃった), the speaker treats their own negligence as an observable external fact. This creates emotional distance — the mistake becomes a situation to be fixed rather than a character flaw to be agonized over.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text:
Grammar Point Core Meaning Emotional / Pragmatic Nuance Trigger Context JLPT 〜っぱなし State left unchanged after transitive action Mild reproach, negligence, incompleteness; agent failed to restore default Household observations; shared space complaints; self-recrimination N3 〜たまま State left unchanged Neutral, descriptive; no implication of fault Concurrent states, accidental situations, literary description N4 〜ている Ongoing state or progressive action Neutral; simply describes current condition General state descriptions, continuous actions N5 〜ておく Do something in advance / leave prepared Proactive preparation; the opposite of negligence Preparing for future convenience N4 〜ずにいる Continue not doing something Deliberate abstention; maintained inaction Choosing not to act over time N3 〜たきり Did once and never since Abandoned habit or one-time action Something started and then dropped N2

The closest lookalike is 〜たまま. The key difference is agency and reproach: たまま describes a state that happens to persist (窓まどが開ひらいたまま — the window happened to stay open); っぱなし describes a state that someone caused and failed to resolve (窓まどを開あけっぱなし — someone opened it and left it that way). The difference is the difference between weather and negligence.

docs.title: 〜たまま (neutral state continuation)
docs.text:
The descriptive alternative. Same physical state, none of the social judgment.

Example テレビてれびがついたままです。 English "The TV is on [and has remained so]." Intent Neutral observation — might be describing a state you want to preserve, or simply noting a fact vs. original Removes the implication that someone should have turned it off. Safe when you don't want to assign blame or when the state is intentional.

When to use this instead

Use たまま when the ongoing state is neutral, accidental, or desired. Use っぱなし when the state resulted from someone's action and implies their failure to complete the natural follow-up.

docs.title: 〜ている (simple state description)
docs.text:
The stripped-down alternative. No narrative, no implication — just the fact.

Example テレビてれびがついています。 English "The TV is on." Intent Pure description — no backstory, no blame vs. original ついている doesn't tell you how it got that way or whether it should be different. It's the weather report version of the observation.

docs.title: 〜ておく (deliberate preparation)
docs.text:
The proactive opposite. Where っぱなし is negligence, ておく is foresight.

Example テレビてれびはつけておいてください。 English "Please leave the TV on [for a reason]." Intent Deliberate state maintenance — the opposite of negligence vs. original ておく justifies the ongoing state; っぱなし criticizes it. They are moral opposites.

docs.title: 〜ずにいる (deliberate inaction)
docs.text:
When someone chooses to maintain a state of not-doing.

Example 何なにも言いわずにいる。 English "Remaining without saying anything." Intent Sustained silence as a choice — not negligence but deliberate restraint vs. original ずにいる describes active maintenance of inaction; っぱなし describes passive failure to complete action.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
The reproach function — noting negligence without direct accusation — should be preserved across register shifts. The formality changes the packaging, not the social judgment.

Casual Basic Polite Extra Polite

テレビてれび、付つけっぱなしじゃん。

"The TV's totally been left on."

Shift: Input sentence with casual bluntness. じゃん (じゃないか) adds emphatic observation — "Can you believe this?" The です is dropped; the reproach is foregrounded.

テレビてれびが付つけっぱなしです。

"The TV has been left on."

Shift: Input sentence as-is. Polite observation with embedded criticism. The です maintains social distance while the っぱなし delivers the message.

テレビてれびの電源でんげんが入はいったままになっております。

"The television remains powered on."

Shift: 付つけっぱなし → 電源でんげんが入はいったまま (formal periphrasis); です → なっております (humble progressive). The reproach is completely erased — this is now a neutral status report appropriate for hotel staff or service contexts. The っぱなし emotional charge is incompatible with formal register.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: Step Pattern What It Adds Example N5 — Foundation 〜ている Ongoing state or progressive action 電気でんきがついている。"The light is on." N4 — Foundation 〜たまま State left unchanged, neutral 窓まどを開あけたまま寝ねた。"Slept with the window open." N4 — Foundation Verb stem + 〜っぱなし The compound form itself 靴くつを脱ぬぎっぱなし。"Shoes kicked off and left." N3 — Core ← You are here 〜っぱなし + social reproach Negligence implication; indirect criticism of incomplete action テレビてれびが付つけっぱなしです。 N3 — Extended 〜っぱなしの + noun Noun modification for compound description 開あけっぱなしの扉とびら。"A door left open." N2 — Awareness 〜っぱなしで + verb Embedded in longer clauses 本ほんを広ひろげっぱなしで寝ねた。"Fell asleep with the book open."

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
窓まどを開あけっぱなしにして出でかけた。 I went out leaving the window open [regrettable]. Literal: Window open-left-as-is doing went-out.

~Context: Self-recrimination upon returning home to a cold room or discovering the oversight later. The にして adds the compounding error of leaving while in that state.~

靴くつを脱ぬぎっぱなしにしないで。 Don't leave your shoes kicked off [everywhere]. Literal: Shoes kick-off-left-as-is don't-do.

~Context: Parent or housemate to someone who repeatedly fails to arrange their shoes properly at the genkan. The っぱなし + negative command is a standard domestic reproach pattern.~

冷蔵庫れいぞうこを開あけっぱなしにしちゃった。 I left the fridge open [careless me]. Literal: Refrigerator open-left-as-is did [regrettable].

~Context: Self-recrimination upon discovering spoiled food or an overworked compressor. The ちゃった compounds the negligence with regret.~

本ほんを広ひろげっぱなしで寝ねちゃった。 I fell asleep with the book spread open [pages now ruined]. Literal: Book spread-open-left-as-is slept [regrettable].

~Context: The morning-after discovery. The っぱなし marks the book as victim; the で connects the state to the sleeping; ちゃった adds the regret.~

電気でんきをつけっぱなしで一晩ひとばん中じゅう。 The light was left on all night long. Literal: Electricity turned-on-left-as-is whole-night.

~Context: Discovering the waste in the morning. The っぱなし marks the negligence; the 一ひと晩ばん中ちゅう compounds the duration and implied waste.~

話はなしっぱなしで食たべるのを忘わすれた。 I talked and talked and forgot to eat [the food went cold]. Literal: Talk-left-as-is eating forgot.

~Context: Social overindulgence. The っぱなし here describes an action (talking) that continued unchecked and displaced another necessary action (eating).~

docs.title: 1. Using 〜っぱなし with intransitive verbs
docs.text:
The most common structural error. Learners apply っぱなし to verbs that don't take objects, producing ungrammatical forms.

Incorrect

電気でんきがつい[っぱなし]{.mistake}

~つく is intransitive — it describes a state that occurs, not an action performed by an agent. You cannot "leave" an intransitive state in the っぱなし sense.~

Correct

電気でんきをつけっぱなし

~つける is transitive — someone turned it on and left it that way. The agent's incomplete action is what っぱなし describes.~

docs.title: 2. Attaching to wrong verb form
docs.text:
Learners sometimes attach っぱなし to the te-form or dictionary form instead of the stem.

Incorrect

開あけてっぱなし

~Attaching to te-form is ungrammatical. The correct base is the stem.~

Correct

開あけっぱなし

~Stem form (ren'yōkei) + っぱなし. 開あける → 開あけ + っぱなし.~

docs.title: 3. Overusing 〜っぱなし for neutral descriptions
docs.text:
In contexts where no negligence is implied, っぱなし sounds judgmental or bizarre.

Register mismatch

山やまが見みえっぱなしです。

"The mountain remains visible." — 見みえる is intransitive, and even if it were transitive, there's no negligence in a mountain being visible. Sounds like you're criticizing the mountain for existing.

Appropriate usage

写真しゃしんを広ひろげっぱなしです。

"The photos are left spread out." — Someone spread them and didn't put them away. Negligence applies.

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text:
Collocation Meaning つけっぱなし "Left turned on" — lights, TV, appliances; the canonical household negligence 開あけっぱなし "Left open" — windows, doors, refrigerators, books, laptops 脱ぬぎっぱなし "Left kicked off" — shoes at genkan, clothes on floor 出だしっぱなし "Left out" — things not put away, drawers not closed 話はなしっぱなし "Left talking" — talked incessantly, neglected other duties 広ひろげっぱなし "Left spread open" — books, maps, newspapers; pages damaged しっぱなし (from する) "Left undone / left as is" — general unfinished business 〜っぱなしにする "To leave [something] in an unchanged state" — the deliberate act of neglecting 〜っぱなしで + verb "While leaving [something] as is, [do something else]" — compounding the negligence

~These collocations are worth learning as complete units. つけっぱなし and 開あけっぱなし especially are daily-use household vocabulary.~

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• 〜っぱなし can describe chronic habits. いつも靴くつを脱ぬぎっぱなし ("always leaving shoes kicked off") uses っぱなし not for a single incident but for a repeated pattern. The suffix stretches from momentary negligence to character trait.
  • The absence of agent is functional, not accidental. In テレビが付つけっぱなしです, the TV is the subject because naming the human would be confrontational. This grammatical sleight-of-hand is a core Japanese politeness strategy: make the object the subject, hide the human fault in the suffix.
  • 〜っぱなし implies a ghost action. The turning-off (or closing, or putting-away) that didn't happen haunts the sentence. Native speakers automatically supply this missing action when they hear っぱなし — the sentence is incomplete without this phantom verb.
  • Self-directed っぱなし is emotional management. Saying 開あけっぱなしにしちゃった ("I left it open, careless me") packages the negligence as an external fact, which reduces self-blame. The grammar becomes a coping mechanism.
  • 〜っぱなし in formal register is nearly impossible. The emotional charge of negligence is too informal for keigo. Formal Japanese replaces it with neutral periphrasis (電源でんげんが入はいったまま) or simply avoids describing the state altogether.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N3 Recognize 〜っぱなし in household contexts; understand the negligence implication; produce correctly with transitive verb stems; distinguish from 〜たまま and 〜ている Developing — N3→N2 Deploy っぱなし naturally in indirect reproach; use noun-modification form (開あけっぱなしの扉とびら); understand when formal register forces replacement with neutral alternatives Automatic — N2+ っぱなし registers immediately as "someone didn't finish what they started" — you hear the ghost action before conscious analysis; the reproach is felt, not decoded Mastery You control the social temperature precisely — using っぱなし for gentle domestic friction, たまま for neutral description, and knowing when to drop both for formal distance. You also recognize when someone uses っぱなし to criticize you without naming you.

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 〜たまま — State left unchanged; neutral, descriptive
  • 〜ている — Ongoing state or progressive action
  • 〜ておく — Deliberate preparation; proactive state maintenance

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 〜ている — Simple state description; no implication of negligence or incomplete action
  • 〜てある — Deliberately maintained state; the opposite of negligence
  • 〜ずにいる — Deliberate maintained inaction; choice rather than failure

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• Verb stems (N4) — the ren'yōkei form required for suffix attachment
  • Transitive vs. intransitive verbs (N4) — understanding why つける takes っぱなし but つく does not
  • 〜たまま (N4) — the neutral state-continuation pattern that っぱなし emotionally charges

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 〜っぱなしの + noun (N3) — Noun modification for compound descriptions
  • 〜っぱなしで + verb (N2) — Embedded in longer clauses with compounding meaning
  • 〜放題ほうだい (N3) — "Doing X without restriction" — visually similar but semantically different (食たべ放題ほうだい vs. 食たべっぱなし)

docs.title: 〜か & 〜かどうか — Embedding a Question in a Larger Statement
Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • Japanese can embed a full question inside a larger sentence using the particle か (for both yes/no and wh-questions) or かどうか (exclusively for yes/no questions).
  • かどうか explicitly signals "whether or not" — it turns a yes/no alternative into a noun-like clause that can serve as the object, subject, or topic of a sentence.
  • In the reference sentence — 週末しゅうまつ、旅行りょこうに行いくかどうか決きめましょう — the embedded question "whether or not we go on a trip this weekend" is the thing being decided.
  • When embedding, the copula だ is dropped before か. Plain forms are used; politeness is expressed on the main-clause verb.
  • Embedded questions commonly appear with verbs of decision (決きめる), knowledge (知しっている, わかる), inquiry (調しらべる, 聞きく), and communication (教おしえる, 伝つたえる).

docs.title: The Core Distinction — Direct vs. Embedded Questions
docs.text:
Two Ways a Question Appears

A question can be the main sentence itself (direct) or a piece of a larger sentence (embedded).

Type Function Example Direct question The sentence is the question. Ends with か and rising intonation. 週末しゅうまつ、旅行りょこうに行いきますか？ "Will you go on a trip this weekend?" Embedded question The question is a component inside a statement, command, or another question. The main sentence does something with that question — decides it, knows it, checks it. 週末しゅうまつ、旅行りょこうに行いくかどうか決きめましょう。 "Let's decide whether to go on a trip this weekend."

In the reference sentence, the speaker is not asking a direct question. They are proposing that the group decide a particular question. That question — "go or not go" — is packaged as the object of 決きめる.

docs.title: Yes/No Embedded Questions — かどうか & か
docs.text:
A yes/no question is embedded in one of two ways:

Pattern Structure Nuance Plain form + かどうか 行いくかどうか Explicitly presents the yes/no alternative: "whether or not [X]." This is the standard, unambiguous choice. Plain form + か 行いくか Implies the yes/no alternative without spelling out the "or not." Slightly more compact, but can sometimes be ambiguous with wh-questions if context isn't clear.

Both are grammatical, but かどうか is more common and safer for yes/no embeddings, especially in formal or careful speech.

docs.title: Formation — かどうか
docs.text:
Verb / Adjective / Noun Plain Form + かどうか Verb (affirmative) 行いく 行いくかどうか Verb (negative) 行いかない 行いかないかどうか i-adjective 高たかい 高たかいかどうか na-adjective 便利べんり∅ 便利べんりかどうか Noun 休やすみ∅ 休やすみかどうか

だ is dropped before かどうか

For na-adjectives and nouns, the copula だ is never used before か or かどうか.

  • 便利べんりだかどうか ❌
  • 便利べんりかどうか ✅
  • 休やすみだかどうか ❌
  • 休やすみかどうか ✅

docs.title: The Reference Sentence — かどうか
docs.text:
週末しゅうまつ、旅行りょこうに行いくかどうか決きめましょう。

Component Meaning 週末しゅうまつ "Weekend" — time context 旅行りょこうに "On a trip" — destination and purpose 行いく "Go" — plain present, the action being questioned かどうか "Whether or not" — turns 行いく into an embedded yes/no question 決きめましょう "Let's decide" — the main verb that takes the embedded question as its object

The embedded clause 旅行りょこうに行いくかどうか functions as a single noun-like unit. It is the thing being decided. You can think of it as: "Let's decide [the question of whether we'll go on a trip this weekend]."

docs.title: Wh- Embedded Questions — か Only
docs.text:
Wh-questions (who, what, where, when, why, how) are embedded with か alone. かどうか is not used — the wh-word already specifies the unknown.

Wh-word + plain clause + か Meaning 誰だれが来くるか Who will come いつ出発しゅっぱつするか When (we) will depart 何なにを食たべるか What (we) will eat どこに泊とまるか Where (we) will stay どうやって行いくか How (we) will go なぜ遅おくれたか Why (someone) was late 彼かれがどこに住すんでいるか知しっていますか？ Do you know where he lives? The embedded question is "where he lives." The main question is "do you know?"

docs.title: The Copula Drop — だ Before か
docs.text:
When embedding a question that ends in a noun or na-adjective, the copula だ is systematically dropped before か and かどうか. This is a hard grammatical rule, not a stylistic choice.

Plain Statement Embedded Question (だ→∅) Meaning これは本ほんだ。 これが本ほん∅かどうか調しらべる。 Check whether this is a book. あの店みせは有名ゆうめいだ。 あの店みせが有名ゆうめい∅かどうか知しっている？ Do you know whether that shop is famous? 明日あしたは休やすみだ。 明日あしたが休やすみ∅かどうか確認かくにんする。 Confirm whether tomorrow is a day off.

Why this happens

The copula だ evolved from である, and in embedded contexts, the attributive form of the copula (である/な) is used historically. The だ is a sentence-final form and is incompatible with subordination markers like か. The underlying structure is closer to 〜であるか, but in modern Japanese the ある is also dropped, leaving just the bare noun/adjective stem + か.

docs.title: Particles After Embedded Questions
docs.text:
The embedded question functions as a noun-like element. It can be followed by case particles like を, が, に, で, と, or by the topic marker は. However, the accusative を and nominative が are often omitted in natural speech, especially in casual register.

Particle Function Example を (often omitted) Marks the embedded question as the direct object of verbs like 決きめる, 調しらべる, 聞きく, 確認かくにんする 行いくかどうか(を)決きめる。 が (often omitted) Marks the embedded question as the subject, typically with verbs like わかる, 決きまる, 問題もんだいだ 誰だれが来くるか(が)重要じゅうようだ。 は Topic-marks the embedded question for contrast or emphasis 行いくかどうかはまだ決きめていない。 によって "Depending on" — the embedded question is the factor 天気てんきがいいかどうかによって決きめよう。 週末しゅうまつ、旅行りょこうに行いくかどうかを決きめましょう。 Same meaning as the reference sentence, with the を explicitly marking the object. Slightly more formal.

In the reference sentence, を is omitted. This is the most natural pattern in everyday speech.

docs.title: Verb Types That Take Embedded Questions
docs.text:
Certain verb families routinely take embedded questions as their complements. Learning these as collocations helps with natural production.

Category Verbs Example Decision / Determination 決きめる, 決きまる, 決断けつだんする, 判断はんだんする 行いくかどうか決きめる Knowledge / Understanding 知しっている, わかる, 理解りかいする, 把握はあくする 誰だれが来くるか知しっている Investigation / Confirmation 調しらべる, 確認かくにんする, 検査けんさする, 調査ちょうさする 本当ほんとうかどうか調しらべる Inquiry 聞きく, 尋たずねる, 質問しつもんする, 伺うかがう どこにいるか聞きく Memory 覚おぼえている, 思おもい出だす どこに置おいたか覚おぼえている Communication 教おしえる, 伝つたえる, 話はなす, 報告ほうこくする, 説明せつめいする いつ着つくか教おしえる Relevance / Importance 問題もんだいだ, 重要じゅうようだ, 関係かんけいある, 次第しだいだ 誰だれがやるかが問題もんだいだ 彼女かのじょがこのことを知しっているかどうか確認かくにんして。 Check whether she knows about this. 確認かくにんする is an investigation verb. The embedded yes/no question is its object.

docs.title: Politeness and Register in Embedded Clauses
docs.text:
The embedded clause is typically in plain form, even when the main clause is polite. The politeness level is marked on the main-clause verb only.

Main Clause Embedded Clause Correct/Incorrect Polite Polite ❌ 行いきますかどうか決きめましょう Polite Plain ✅ 行いくかどうか決きめましょう Polite Plain (negative) ✅ 行いかないかどうか決きめましょう

Do not use です・ます inside the embedded question

The embedded clause is grammatically subordinate. Politeness markers like です and ます appear only in the main clause (the outermost verb). Using them inside the embedded clause is a common learner error.

  • 彼かれが来くるかどうか知しっていますか？ ✅
  • 彼かれが来きますかどうか知しっていますか？ ❌

However, in very formal written Japanese (e.g., academic papers, formal reports), である may appear inside embedded questions: 〜であるかどうか. This is a different register and not the norm for everyday business or conversational Japanese.

docs.title: かどうか vs. か — Nuance and Preference
docs.text:
For yes/no embedded questions, both かどうか and bare か are grammatical. The choice is a matter of clarity, register, and rhythm.

かどうか か Clarity Unambiguous — explicitly means "whether or not." Can be ambiguous, especially if a wh-word could be implied. Formality Slightly more formal or careful. Preferred in writing and business. Slightly more casual or spoken. Common in fast conversation. Emphasis Emphasises the binary alternative. Neutral; the "or not" is implied from context. With negative embedded 行いかないかどうか → explicit: "whether or not [I] don't go" 行いかないか → could sound like "the question of not going" without the alternative 彼かれが来くるかどうか知しらない。 I don't know whether he'll come (or not). 彼かれが来くるか知しらない。 I don't know whether he'll come. (Same meaning, slightly shorter, common in speech.)

When to prefer かどうか

  • In any written communication (emails, reports, messages to superiors).
  • When the yes/no alternative is the key point you're deliberating.
  • After negative predicates (行いかないかどうか) for clarity.
  • When learning: it's safer and clearer.

When bare か is fine: - Fast casual conversation with clear context. - When the verb itself already implies a binary (決きめる, 確認かくにんする) and the alternative is obvious.

docs.title: かどうか with the Negative — "Whether or Not I Don't…"
docs.text:
Sometimes the embedded question itself is negative — the speaker is unsure whether the negative state is the case.

彼かれが来こないかどうか確認かくにんして。 Check whether he's not coming. The question is not "will he come?" but "is it the case that he won't come?" The negative is inside the embedded question.

This is distinct from the positive embedded question. The nuance: the speaker suspects he might not come and wants confirmation of that suspicion.

docs.title: Embedded Questions as Topics — Using は and も
docs.text:
The embedded question can be made the topic of the sentence with は, typically when discussing the status of the decision or knowledge itself.

行いくかどうかはまだ決きめていない。 As for whether I'll go or not — I haven't decided yet. 誰だれがやるかは後あとで話はなし合あおう。 As for who will do it — let's discuss that later. 成功せいこうするかどうかは努力次第どりょくしだいだ。 Whether we succeed or not depends on effort.

docs.title: Embedded Questions in the Subject Position — が
docs.text:
When the embedded question is the grammatical subject, が is the particle used, though it is often omitted in speech. This occurs especially with predicates like わかる, 問題もんだいだ, 重要じゅうようだ, 決きまる (intransitive "to be decided").

いつ始はじまるか(が)まだわからない。 I still don't know when it starts. 誰だれがリーダーか(が)問題もんだいだ。 Who the leader is — that's the issue.

docs.title: Direct Quote と vs. Embedded Question か
docs.text:
A common point of confusion: と marks a direct or indirect quote (content of speech or thought), while か marks an embedded question. They are not interchangeable.

と — Quote か — Embedded Question 彼かれは「行いく」と言いった。 彼かれが行いくか聞きいた。 He said, "I'll go." I asked whether he would go. Marks the actual words or content of communication. Marks a question being asked, decided, checked, etc.

Some verbs (聞きく, 言いう, 伝つたえる) can take either, with a difference in meaning:

彼かれに行いくか聞きいた。 I asked him whether he would go. (The question "will you go?" was posed to him.) 彼かれに行いくと聞きいた。 I heard from someone that he would go. (The information "he will go" came from him or through him.)

docs.title: 1. Keeping だ before か／かどうか
docs.text:
Incorrect

彼かれが学生がくせいだかどうか知しらない。

~The copula だ must be dropped before かどうか.~

Correct

彼かれが学生がくせい∅かどうか知しらない。

~Nouns and na-adjectives attach directly to かどうか without だ.~

docs.title: 2. Using かどうか with wh-words
docs.text:
Incorrect

どこに行いくかどうか決きめよう。

~かどうか is only for yes/no questions. どこ already fills the unknown slot.~

Correct

どこに行いくか決きめよう。

~Wh-questions use か alone.~

docs.title: 3. Using polite forms inside the embedded clause
docs.text:
Incorrect

彼かれが来きますかどうか知しっていますか？

~Polite ます does not belong inside the subordinate clause.~

Correct

彼かれが来くるかどうか知しっていますか？

~Plain form in the embedded clause; politeness only on the main verb 知しっています.~

docs.title: 4. Confusing と and か with verbs like 聞きく
docs.text:
Incorrect

彼かれに来くると聞きいた (intending "I asked him whether he's coming")

~With と, 聞きく means "I heard that he's coming" — the inquiry meaning is lost.~

Correct

彼かれに来くるか聞きいた。 (I asked whether he's coming.)

~か marks the question being asked.~

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• かどうか literally means "or not." The どう corresponds to "how / in what way" and か is the question marker. Together they form "whether or not." The etymology helps remember why it only works for binary questions.
  • The particle を after embedded questions is dropped roughly 80% of the time in spoken Japanese. Including it sounds slightly more formal or emphatic. Learners who always include を can sound stiff; learners who never include it may struggle in formal writing where it is expected.
  • When the main verb is 決きまっている (it has been decided), the embedded question takes the subject marker が. 行いくかどうかがもう決きまっている。 "Whether we go or not has already been decided." The intransitive verb 決きまる assigns the が role.
  • Embedded questions can nest indefinitely. 誰だれがいつどこで何なんをしたかどうかを調しらべる — "to investigate the matter of who did what, when, and where." Stylistically heavy but grammatically possible.
  • The boundary between bare か and かどうか is blurring in casual speech. Some speakers use か for yes/no embeddings more freely than textbooks suggest. Context almost always disambiguates.

docs.title: Extended Examples — かどうか & か in Action
docs.text: Japanese English 明日あしたのパーティーに行いくかどうかまだ迷まよってる。 I'm still undecided about whether to go to tomorrow's party. 迷まよう (to hesitate / be undecided) takes the embedded question as its object. 彼かれが本当ほんとうのことを言いっているかどうかわからない。 I don't know whether he's telling the truth. わかる (to understand/know) is intransitive. The embedded question is the subject of understanding. このボタンを押おせば動うごくかどうか試ためしてみよう。 Let's try (pressing this button) to see whether it works. 試ためす (to test/try) takes the embedded question as the thing being tested. 電車でんしゃが時間通じかんどおりに来くるか心配しんぱいだ。 I'm worried about whether the train will come on time. 心配しんぱいだ (to be worried) takes an embedded question as the source of worry. 誰だれが責任者せきにんしゃか確認かくにんしておきます。 I'll confirm who is the person in charge. Wh-question with noun predicate (責任せきにん者しゃ). Note: だ is absent before か.

docs.title: Quick Reference Table
docs.text: Question Type Structure Example Yes/No (explicit) Plain form + かどうか 行いくかどうか Yes/No (bare) Plain form + か 行いくか Wh-question Wh-word + plain clause + か 誰だれが行いくか Noun / na-adj predicate Stem + か／かどうか (no だ) 休やすみかどうか Object particle を (often omitted) 〜かどうか(を)決きめる Subject particle が (often omitted) 〜かどうか(が)わかる Topic は 〜かどうかは未定みていだ

docs.title: Related Constructions
docs.text:
• 〜という — indirect quotation and hearsay; often paired with embedded questions for clarifying content
  • 〜かどうか vs. 〜か — further nuance in yes/no embeddings (this page)
  • 疑問ぎもん詞し (wh-words) — 何なん, 誰だれ, どこ, いつ, なぜ, どう, どうして, どの, etc.
  • 〜てみる — trying something to see the result; commonly combined with embedded questions (動うごくかどうか試ためしてみる)

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• Plain form (N5) — the base for all embedded clauses
  • Direct questions with か (N5) — the source structure that gets embedded
  • と (quotation) (N4) — the contrast with embedded questions

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 〜かと思おもう — "I wonder if…" (embedded question + 思おもう)
  • 〜かも — "might" (derived from か + も)
  • 〜か何なんか — "or something" (embedded question with indefinite reference)

docs.title: 〜か / 〜かどうか — Embedding a Question in a Larger Statement
彼かれが来くるかどうか、分わからない。
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — Upper N4 / Lower N3

The basic pattern of embedding a plain question with 〜か is N4. But the full system — distinguishing 〜か from 〜かどうか, knowing when particles drop, and controlling the scope of uncertainty — is solidly lower N3. Native speakers make these choices automatically; learners often stumble over whether to include どうか or whether が should stay.

Tier Pattern Description Example N5 — Foundation Plain questions Standalone questions with か 彼かれは来きますか？ N4 — Foundation 〜か (embedded) Embedding a question as a noun clause 彼かれが来くるか分わからない。 N4 — Core ← You are here 〜かどうか Explicit yes/no embedding — "whether or not" 彼かれが来くるかどうか分わからない。 N3 — Extended Particle dropping が/は often omitted in embedded clauses 彼かれ来くるかどうか… (very casual) N3 — Extended Double embedding 〜か〜か + どうか in lists 行いくか行いかないか、分わからない。 N2 — Awareness 〜かのように "As if…" — hypothetical embedding 知しっているかのように話はなす。

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 〜か turns a question into a noun-like clause that can be inserted into a larger sentence — as the object of 知しる, 分わかる, 聞きく, 確認かくにんする, etc.
  • 〜かどうか explicitly embeds a yes/no question, marking both alternatives: "whether [X] or not." It is clearer, more explicit, and often preferred in formal contexts.
  • With question words (何なん, 誰だれ, どこ, etc.), only 〜か is used — never 〜かどうか. 何なんを食たべるかどうか知しらない is ungrammatical because "what" is not a yes/no question.
  • With yes/no questions, both 〜か and 〜かどうか work, but the nuance differs: 〜か is neutral and compact; 〜かどうか is explicit about the uncertainty between two outcomes.
  • Particles が and は are often dropped in embedded clauses, especially in casual speech. 彼かれが来くるか becomes 彼かれ来くるか in fast conversation.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Use 〜か to embed any question (wh- or yes/no) into a larger statement. Use 〜かどうか when you want to explicitly mark that a yes/no question is being embedded — emphasizing the uncertainty between the positive and negative outcome.

彼かれが来くるか分わからない。 I don't know if he'll come [embedded yes/no]. Literal: He come-question don't-know. Context: Neutral embedding. The か alone carries the yes/no ambiguity. 彼かれが来くるかどうか分わからない。 I don't know whether he'll come or not [explicit yes/no]. Literal: He come-question-or-not don't-know. Context: The どうか makes the two alternatives explicit. Slightly more formal and clearer than か alone. 何なんが起おきたか知しりたい。 I want to know what happened [embedded wh-question]. Literal: What happened-question want-to-know. Context: Wh-question embedding. Only か is possible here — かどうか cannot follow 何なん. 日本にっぽん語ごが話はなせるかどうか聞きいてください。 Please ask whether [they] can speak Japanese or not. Literal: Japanese speak-can-question-or-not ask-please. Context: Explicit yes/no embedding in a request. The かどうか makes the binary nature of the inquiry clear.

docs.title: In Real Life
docs.text:
In Real Life

Situation: ユイ is organizing a weekend picnic. She needs to know if her friend ケン is coming because she has to buy food. She texts another friend who might know.

ケンが来くるかどうか、分わからない？

"Do you know whether Ken is coming or not?"

Why かどうか fits here: Yui could have said ケンが来くるか分わからない — grammatically fine, but slightly less explicit. The かどうか makes the binary nature of the uncertainty clear: he either comes or he doesn't, and she needs to know which. The comma pause after かどうか creates a small space of suspense before the main question.

What the どうか adds: It transforms a vague "if" into a clear "whether or not." This is especially useful when the listener needs to understand that both outcomes matter — not just the positive one.

What makes this natural: In planning contexts, かどうか is extremely common because plans require binary decisions. The explicitness prevents misunderstanding.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Embedding a question in Japanese is structurally simple but pragmatically layered. The question clause is nominalized by か (and optionally どうか), then placed in a grammatical slot that normally takes a noun — typically as the object of a verb like 知しる, 分わかる, 聞きく, 確認かくにんする, 決きめる, or 考かんがえる.

Layer 1 — The embedded clause: Take a plain-form question (not the polite 〜ますか form) and replace the question mark with か. 彼かれは来きますか？ → 彼かれが来くるか.

Layer 2 — The explicit marker (optional): For yes/no questions, add どうか after か to make both alternatives explicit: 彼かれが来くるかどうか.

Layer 3 — The main clause: The embedded clause functions as the object (or sometimes subject) of the main predicate. 彼かれが来くるかどうか + 分わからない.

彼かれが来くるか Whether he comes [compact] The か alone carries the uncertainty. The listener understands it's a yes/no question from context. 彼かれが来くるかどうか Whether he comes or not [explicit] The どうか spells out the negative alternative. More formal, more precise, more planning-friendly.

docs.title: Real Speech vs. Textbook Form
docs.text:
Textbooks often present 〜か and 〜かどうか as interchangeable for yes/no questions. In real speech, the choice is meaningful: 〜か is neutral and compact; 〜かどうか is explicit and often carries a sense of "I need a definitive answer."

Form Example Note Textbook (neutral) 彼かれが来くるか分わからない。 Standard example — grammatically correct, fully natural Textbook (explicit) 彼かれが来くるかどうか分わからない。 Standard example — grammatically correct, fully natural Real speech (wh-embedded) 何なんが起おきたか知しりたい。 Wh-question — only か possible Real speech (particle drop) 彼かれ来くるかどうか… Casual particle drop — が omitted Real speech (double form) 行いくか行いかないか、決きめていない。 Both alternatives stated explicitly

docs.title: Function in Context
docs.text:
• ケンが来くるかどうか is the embedded clause functioning as the object of 分わからない. The comma after かどうか is optional but common — it marks the boundary between the embedded question and the main predicate.
  • 分わからない is the main predicate — "don't know." It requires a noun-like object: "I don't know [the answer to this question]."
  • The どうか explicitly marks the negative alternative: "come or not-come." Without it, the negative alternative is implied but not stated.
  • The question clause uses plain form (来くる), not polite form (来きます). Embedding 〜ますか is ungrammatical: ケンが来きますか分わからない is wrong.
  • Without かどうか: ケンが来くるか分わからない is softer and more compact. It doesn't emphasize the binary nature as strongly. In casual speech, this is often preferred.
これが正ただしいか確認かくにんしてください。 Please check whether this is correct. Literal: This correct-question confirm-please. Context: 〜か alone is sufficient because the context makes the yes/no nature obvious. Adding どうか would be redundant but not wrong.

docs.title: Construction
docs.text:
Question Type Embedded Form Example かどうか Possible? Yes/no (plain) verb-plain + か 来くるか Optional Yes/no (explicit) verb-plain + かどうか 来くるかどうか Yes Yes/no (negative explicit) verb-plain + か + verb-nai + か 来くるか来こないか Yes (literary) Wh-question (何なん) 何なん + predicate + か 何なんを食たべるか No Wh-question (誰だれ) 誰だれ + predicate + か 誰だれが来くるか No Wh-question (どこ) どこ + predicate + か どこに行いくか No Wh-question (いつ) いつ + predicate + か いつ帰かえるか No Wh-question (どれ) どれ + predicate + か どれがいいか No Wh-question (なぜ) なぜ + predicate + か なぜ怒おこったか No

かどうか is only for yes/no

かどうか cannot follow question words (何なん, 誰だれ, どこ, etc.) because those are not yes/no questions. 何なんを食たべるかどうか知しらない is ungrammatical.

Plain form required

The embedded clause must use plain form, not polite 〜ます form. 来きますか分わからない is ungrammatical. The politeness of the main sentence is carried by the main verb: 来くるか分わかりません (polite).

docs.title: Particle Dropping in Embedded Clauses
docs.text:
In embedded questions, が and は are frequently dropped — especially in casual speech.

Full Casual / Fast Speech 彼かれが来くるか 彼かれ来くるか 雨あめが降ふるかどうか 雨あめ降ふるかどうか 彼女かのじょが何なんを言いったか 彼女かのじょ何なん言いったか

The drop is optional

Omitting particles is casual but not ungrammatical in standard Japanese. In formal writing, particles are usually retained.

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
Japanese is a language that nominalizes freely. Where English uses "whether" or "if" as conjunctions, Japanese turns the entire question into a noun-like block and drops it into a sentence slot. This reflects a broader pattern: Japanese prefers clause nominalization over conjunction chaining.

The かどうか pattern also reveals a cultural preference for explicit binary framing. In planning, decision-making, and formal contexts, Japanese speakers often want to mark both alternatives clearly — not just "if yes" but "whether yes or no." This prevents the ambiguity of English "if," which can mean both "whether" and "in the event that."

  • Questions as objects. In Japanese, a question is not just an utterance act — it can be a grammatical object, a thing to be known, decided, or checked. 来くるか is "the matter of whether [someone] comes." This reification is natural in Japanese but takes adjustment for English speakers.
  • Binary explicitness as clarity. かどうか marks both poles of the uncertainty. In contexts where miscommunication would be costly (plans, medical questions, legal matters), this explicitness is preferred.
  • Particle drop as intimacy. Dropping が in embedded questions signals casualness and trust. The listener can parse the clause without grammatical scaffolding. This is a subtle intimacy marker.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text:
Grammar Point Core Meaning Explicitness / Nuance Trigger Context JLPT 〜か (embedded) "Whether…" / "if…" [embedded] Neutral, compact; leaves alternatives implied General embedding; wh-questions; casual yes/no N4 〜かどうか "Whether… or not" Explicit binary; both alternatives marked Yes/no questions where clarity matters; formal contexts N4 〜か〜ないか "Whether… or not" [literary] Both alternatives stated in full Written Japanese; emphatic; literary N3 〜かのように "As if…" Hypothetical embedding; pretend knowledge Describing behavior that assumes something N2 〜かと思おもった "I wondered if…" Past self-questioning; relief or surprise Reacting to something that resolved an uncertainty N3 〜かもしれない "Maybe…" Possibility, not embedding Expressing uncertainty directly (not as object) N4

The closest lookalike is 〜かもしれない. But かもしれない is a sentence-ending possibility marker (彼かれが来くるかもしれない — "He might come"), not an embedded clause. The grammatical function is completely different.

docs.title: 〜か (compact embedding)
docs.text:
The minimal form. Same function, less explicit about the binary.

Example 彼かれが来くるか分わからない。 English "I don't know if he'll come." Intent Neutral, compact, sufficient for most contexts vs. かどうか Less explicit. In casual speech, often preferred. Can feel slightly less formal or less urgent.

When to use this instead

Use 〜か alone when the yes/no nature is obvious from context, when speaking casually, or when brevity matters. It is never wrong for yes/no embedding.

docs.title: 〜か〜ないか (literary double form)
docs.text:
States both alternatives explicitly with full verbs.

Example 行いくか行いかないか、決きめていない。 English "I haven't decided whether to go or not." Intent Literary, emphatic, written style vs. かどうか More formal and weighty than かどうか. Common in novels, essays, and dramatic speech. Sounds heavy in casual conversation.

docs.title: 〜かもしれない (possibility marker)
docs.text:
Not an embedding pattern — a sentence-ending expression of possibility.

Example 彼かれが来くるかもしれない。 English "He might come." Intent Direct expression of uncertainty vs. embedded か This is a main-clause pattern, not an embedded clause. It cannot function as the object of 知しる or 分わかる.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
The explicitness of かどうか makes it slightly more formal than か alone, but both are acceptable across most registers.

Casual Casual (explicit) Basic Polite Extra Polite

ケン来くるか分わからない。

"Don't know if Ken's coming."

Shift: Particle drop (が omitted), か alone. Ultra-casual, text-message friendly.

ケンが来くるかどうか、知しってる？

"Do you know whether Ken is coming or not?"

Shift: かどうか adds explicitness even in casual speech. The comma pause is common. The 知しってる is casual.

ケンさんが来くるかどうか、分わかりますか。

"Do you know whether Ken is coming or not?"

Shift: Standard polite. The かどうか is appropriate here. The main verb 分わかります carries the politeness.

ケン様さまがご出席しゅっせきされるかどうか、ご確認かくにんいただけますでしょうか。

"Might we ask you to confirm whether Ken will be in attendance or not?"

Shift: Full keigo. 来くる → ご出席しゅっせきされる (respectful); 確認かくにんする → ご確認かくにんいただけますでしょうか (humble request). The かどうか is retained even in ultra-formal contexts — it is not casual.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: Step Pattern What It Adds Example N5 — Foundation Plain questions with か Standalone question formation 彼かれは来きますか？ N5 — Foundation Question words (何なん, 誰だれ, etc.) Wh-question formation 何なんを食たべますか？ N4 — Core ← You are here 〜か (embedded) Turns questions into nominal clauses 彼かれが来くるか分わからない。 N4 — Core 〜かどうか Explicit yes/no embedding 彼かれが来くるかどうか分わからない。 N3 — Extended Particle dropping Casual omission of が/は in embedded clauses 彼かれ来くるかどうか… N3 — Extended 〜か〜ないか Literary double-form 行いくか行いかないか。 N2 — Awareness 〜かのように Hypothetical "as if" embedding 知しっているかのように話はなす。

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: 何なんが起おきたか知しりたい。 I want to know what happened. Literal: What happened-question want-to-know. Context: Wh-question embedding. Only か is possible. 何なんが起おきたかどうか would be ungrammatical. 誰だれが来くるか、まだ知しらせてない。 I haven't been told who's coming yet. Literal: Who come-question, yet inform-not-done. Context: Wh-question as object of passive communication. The comma marks the embedded clause boundary. これが正ただしいか確認かくにんしてください。 Please check whether this is correct. Literal: This correct-question confirm-please. Context: Yes/no embedding with か alone. The context makes the binary nature obvious, so どうか is unnecessary. 日本にっぽん語ごが話はなせるかどうか、聞きいてください。 Please ask whether they can speak Japanese or not. Literal: Japanese speak-can-question-or-not, ask-please. Context: Explicit yes/no embedding in an instruction. The かどうか makes the binary inquiry clear. 行いくか行いかないか、決きめていない。 I haven't decided whether to go or not. Literal: Go-question go-not-question, decide-not-doing. Context: Literary double form. Both alternatives stated explicitly. More emphatic than 行いくかどうか. 彼かれが怒おこっているかのように、黙だまっていた。 He remained silent as if he were angry. Literal: He angry-is-question-as, silent-was. Context: N2 pattern — hypothetical embedding. The かのように creates a simile of unconfirmed state. 明日あすまでに終おわるかどうか、怪あやしい。 It's doubtful whether it'll be finished by tomorrow. Literal: Tomorrow-by finish-question-or-not, doubtful. Context: かどうか with adjectival predicate (怪あやしい). The embedded clause functions as the subject.

docs.title: 1. Using かどうか with wh-questions
docs.text:
Ungrammatical

何なんを食たべるかどうか知しらない。

何なん is a wh-word, not a yes/no question. かどうか cannot follow wh-questions. Use 何なんを食たべるか知しらない.

Correct

何なんを食たべるか知しらない。

"I don't know what [they] will eat." — Wh-embedding with か only.

docs.title: 2. Using polite form inside the embedded clause
docs.text:
Ungrammatical

彼かれが来きますか分わからない。

Embedded clauses must use plain form. 来きますか is a standalone polite question, not an embeddable clause.

Correct

彼かれが来くるか分わからない。

"I don't know if he'll come." — Plain form inside the embedding; politeness is carried by the main verb.

docs.title: 3. Confusing embedded か with sentence-ending かもしれない
docs.text:
Structural error

彼かれが来くるか知しらないかもしれない。

This tries to embed 知しらないかもしれない ("might not know"), which is possible but means something different. If the intent is "I don't know if he might come," the structure is wrong.

Correct (for intended meaning)

彼かれが来くるかどうか分わからない。

"I don't know whether he'll come or not." — Clear embedding.

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text:
Collocation Meaning 〜か分わからない "Don't know whether…" — the most common embedding pattern 〜かどうか分わからない "Don't know whether… or not" — explicit uncertainty 〜か知しりたい "Want to know whether/what…" — curiosity embedding 〜か聞きく "Ask whether…" — inquiry embedding 〜か確認かくにんする "Check whether…" — verification embedding 〜か決きめる "Decide whether…" — decision embedding 〜か考かんがえる "Think about whether…" — deliberation embedding 〜かどうか気きになる "Care about whether… or not" — emotional investment 〜かどうか関係かんけいない "Doesn't matter whether… or not" — dismissal 〜かのように "As if…" — hypothetical resemblance (N2)

These collocations are worth learning as complete units. 〜か分わからない and 〜かどうか分わからない are daily-use patterns.

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• 〜か can embed statements that aren't literally questions. 彼かれが来くるかどうか is not a direct question — it's a nominalized clause. But it retains the semantic shape of a question: uncertainty, alternatives, information gap.
  • The comma after かどうか is prosodic. It represents a breath between the uncertainty and the main predicate. In fast speech it may disappear; in careful speech it marks the clause boundary clearly.
  • 〜かどうか can be used sarcastically. 行いくかどうか、知しったことじゃない ("Whether I go or not is none of your business") uses the explicit binary to create distance and finality.
  • Double embedding with lists: 行いくか留とまるか、決きめていない ("I haven't decided whether to go or stay") embeds two alternatives with two か markers. This is standard for multiple-choice embedding.
  • 〜か can embed exclamations rhetorically. 何なんて言いったか忘わすれた ("I forgot what [you] said") embeds a question the speaker once knew. The か marks the information as previously questioned, now lost.
  • The plain-form requirement is absolute. Even in ultra-formal keigo, the embedded clause remains plain: ご出席しゅっせきされるかどうか (not ご出席しゅっせきされますかどうか). The politeness is always on the main predicate, never inside the embedding.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N4 Produce 〜か and 〜かどうか correctly; distinguish wh-embedding from yes/no embedding; use plain form inside embedded clauses; avoid かどうか with question words Developing — N4→N3 Drop particles naturally in casual embedded clauses; choose between か and かどうか based on explicitness needs; use double-form 〜か〜ないか in writing Automatic — N3→N2 Embedded questions arrive as single units — you no longer parse them as "question + か." The choice between か and かどうか becomes intuitive. Mastery You deploy the full embedding spectrum: か (compact), かどうか (explicit), か〜ないか (literary), かのように (hypothetical). You know when particle dropping sounds natural and when it sounds sloppy. You never put ます inside an embedded clause.

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 〜か (question particle) — standalone question formation
  • 〜かどうか — explicit yes/no embedding
  • 〜か〜ないか — literary double-form embedding

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 〜かもしれない — Possibility marker (sentence-ending, not embedding)
  • 〜かと思おもう — "I think that maybe…" — embedding with tentative main verb
  • 〜かのように — "As if…" — hypothetical resemblance (N2)

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• Plain verb forms (N5) — required for embedded clauses
  • Question formation (N5) — the か question system
  • Nominalization (N4) — treating clauses as noun-like entities
  • Particle dropping (N4) — casual omission of が/は

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 〜かのように (N2) — Hypothetical embedding; "as if"
  • 〜かと思おもった (N3) — "I wondered if…"; past self-questioning
  • 〜か否いなか (N2) — Literary written form of かどうか
  • 〜かどうかに関かかわらず (N2) — "Regardless of whether… or not"

docs.title: 1. Overview & Nuance
docs.text:
An embedded question is a question tucked inside a larger sentence, functioning as a noun phrase — the subject or object of the main verb. In English, we do this with words like "whether" or "if":

  • "I don't know whether she will come." (whether-clause is the object of "know")
  • "Let's decide where to eat." (where-clause is the object of "decide")
  • "I'm wondering if the meeting is tomorrow." (if-clause is the object of "wondering")

Japanese handles this with two closely related patterns:

Pattern A — Wh-Question Embedded

Plain-form clause containing 何なに/誰だれ/どこ/いつ/etc. + か + main verb

Pattern B — Yes/No Question Embedded

Plain-form clause + かどうか + main verb

The difference is exactly the same as in English: 〜か embeds wh-questions (what / who / where / when / why / how), while 〜かどうか embeds yes/no questions (whether or not).

Why this pattern is worth mastering

Embedded questions are one of the most common structures in natural Japanese — you will hear them constantly in sentences like「どこに行いくか決きめた?」("Did you decide where to go?") or「彼かれが来くるかどうか分わからない。」("I don't know whether he's coming.") Mastering them unlocks a big chunk of natural conversation.

docs.title: 2a. The Plain-Form Requirement
docs.text:
The clause inside the embedded question must use plain form, not polite form. This is the biggest mistake learners make — they try to tuck 〜ます forms into embedded questions.

Part of Speech Form Inside Embedded Q Example Verbs Plain form (dictionary / plain past / plain negative) 行いく、行いった、行いかない い-adjectives Plain form (unchanged) 高たかい、高たかかった、高たかくない な-adjectives Drop だ before か / かどうか 元気げんき (not 元気げんきだ) Nouns Drop だ before か / かどうか 学生がくせい (not 学生がくせいだ)

The 'dropping だ' rule

This is the single most common grammatical trip-up in this pattern. When a na-adjective or noun comes before か or かどうか, you drop the だ:

✅ 彼かれが学生がくせいかどうか知しりません。 ❌ 彼かれが学生がくせいだかどうか知しりません。

However, for past tense or negative, the copula conjugation stays: 学生がくせいだったかどうか / 学生がくせいじゃないかどうか are both correct.

docs.title: 2b. Pattern A — 〜か (Embedded Wh-Questions)
docs.text:
Use 〜か when the embedded question contains a question word: 何なに、誰だれ、どこ、いつ、どう、どうして、どの、どれ、いくら、いくつ, etc.

Original Question Embedded Version 何なにを食たべますか。 何なにを食たべるか決きめましょう。 どこに住すんでいますか。 どこに住すんでいるか教おしえてください。 誰だれが来きましたか。 誰だれが来きたか分わかりません。 いつ始はじまりますか。 いつ始はじまるか聞ききましょう。

docs.title: 2c. Pattern B — 〜かどうか (Embedded Yes/No Questions)
docs.text:
Use 〜かどうか when the embedded question is a yes/no question — one with no question word. The どうか part literally means "or how (i.e., or not)," so かどうか = "whether or not."

Original Question Embedded Version 彼かれは来きますか。 彼かれが来くるかどうか分わかりません。 この店みせは開あいていますか。 この店みせが開あいているかどうか確たしかめます。 これで正ただしいですか。 これで正ただしいかどうか教おしえてください。 彼かれは元気げんきですか。 彼かれが元気げんきかどうかわかりません。

The particle shift: は → が

Notice that は often becomes が when a question gets embedded. This is because the embedded clause is no longer the "topic" of the whole sentence — it's a subordinate clause, and subordinate clauses strongly prefer が for marking their subjects. Not a strict rule, but a strong tendency to internalize.

docs.title: 2d. Main-Verb Pairings
docs.text:
Embedded questions are always the object or subject of some main verb. These are the verbs you'll see 90% of the time:

Main Verb Meaning Use Case 分わかる / 知しる to understand / know Expressing lack of knowledge 決きめる to decide Making a decision about something open 聞きく to ask / hear Inquiring or being told 教おしえる to tell / teach Requesting information 考かんがえる to think / consider Deliberating 覚おぼえている / 忘わすれる to remember / forget Memory 調しらべる / 確たしかめる to check / verify Investigation 心配しんぱいする to worry Expressing anxiety about uncertainty

docs.title: Level 1 — Basic Daily Use
docs.text:
Example 1 · Making Plans

週末しゅうまつ、旅行りょこうに行いくかどうか決きめましょう。 "Let's decide whether or not we'll go on a trip this weekend."

Yes/no question (go or not go), so かどうか. The main verb 決きめる takes the embedded question as its object.

Example 2 · Asking for Directions

駅えきがどこにあるか教おしえてください。 "Please tell me where the station is."

Wh-question (どこ), so plain か. Note が marking the subject inside the embedded clause.

Example 3 · Forgetting Something

彼かれの名前なまえが何なんだったか忘わすれました。 "I forgot what his name was."

Past tense inside the embedded clause: 何なんだった uses the past form of the copula. The だ isn't dropped here because it's だった (past), not だ (present).

docs.title: Level 2 — Expressing Uncertainty
docs.text:
Example 4 · Checking Availability

その映画えいががまだ上映じょうえいしているかどうか調しらべてみます。 "I'll check whether that movie is still showing."

Example 5 · Self-Doubt

この仕事しごとが自分じぶんに合あっているかどうかわかりません。 "I don't know whether this job suits me."

Example 6 · Polite Inquiry

この料理りょうりに肉にくが入はいっているかどうか教おしえていただけますか。 "Could you tell me whether this dish contains meat?"

docs.title: Level 3 — Business & Formal Usage
docs.text:
Example 7 · Business Email

来週らいしゅうの会議かいぎにご出席しゅっせきいただけるかどうか、お知しらせください。 "Please let us know whether you'll be able to attend next week's meeting."

Standard scheduling phrasing for business correspondence.

Example 8 · Project Decision

このプロジェクトを続つづけるかどうか、来月らいげつまでに決定けっていする必要ひつようがあります。 "We need to decide by next month whether to continue this project."

Example 9 · Nested Question

なぜ彼かれが辞やめたか誰だれも知しらないようです。 "It seems no one knows why he quit."

The embedded question (なぜ彼かれが辞やめたか) is the object of 知しる, wrapped in 〜ようです (it seems).

docs.title: Level 4 — Advanced Nuance
docs.text:
Example 10 · Hedged Opinion

この方法ほうほうが正ただしいかどうかは別べつとして、まずやってみましょう。 "Setting aside whether this method is correct, let's first give it a try."

The fixed phrase 〜かどうかは別べつとして ("setting aside whether...") is an elegant way to acknowledge uncertainty while pushing forward.

docs.title: 4. 〜か vs. 〜かどうか — Choosing the Right One
docs.text:
The choice is entirely determined by whether the embedded clause contains a question word:

Does the Embedded Clause Have a Question Word? Use English Parallel ✅ Yes — 何なに、誰だれ、どこ、いつ、etc. 〜か what / who / where / when / why / how ❌ No — just a yes/no question 〜かどうか whether (or not) / if

docs.title: Side-by-Side Comparison
docs.text: Meaning Japanese Pattern "I don't know where he went." 彼かれがどこに行いったかわかりません。 〜か (wh) "I don't know whether he went." 彼かれが行いったかどうかわかりません。 〜かどうか (y/n) "Please tell me when the meeting starts." 会議かいぎがいつ始はじまるか教おしえてください。 〜か (wh) "Please tell me whether the meeting is starting." 会議かいぎが始はじまるかどうか教おしえてください。 〜かどうか (y/n) "Let's decide what to eat." 何なにを食たべるか決きめましょう。 〜か (wh) "Let's decide whether to eat dinner out." 外食がいしょくするかどうか決きめましょう。 〜かどうか (y/n)

docs.title: 5. The Mistake Monitor
docs.text:
Pitfall 1 — Using Polite Form Inside the Embedded Clause

The clause inside 〜か or 〜かどうか must be in plain form, even if the main verb is polite.

❌ 彼かれが来きますかどうかわかりません。 ✅ 彼かれが来くるかどうかわかりません。

The politeness level is set by the main verb (わかりません), not the embedded clause. The embedded clause is subordinate and always uses plain form.

Pitfall 2 — Forgetting to Drop だ

With na-adjectives and nouns in present affirmative, drop the だ before か or かどうか:

❌ 彼かれが学生がくせいだかどうか… ✅ 彼かれが学生がくせいかどうか…

❌ その町まちが静しずかだかどうか… ✅ その町まちが静しずかかどうか…

This rule applies only to present affirmative. Past tense (だった) and negative (じゃない / ではない) forms keep their shape.

Pitfall 3 — Using 〜かどうか with a Question Word

The どうか part means "or not" — it's meaningless when there's already a question word like "what" or "where."

❌ 何なにを食たべるかどうか決きめましょう。 (nonsensical — "Let's decide whether to eat what") ✅ 何なにを食たべるか決きめましょう。 ("Let's decide what to eat")

Mental check: wh-question → use か. Whether/if → use かどうか.

Stylistic Tip — Dropping the Final か in Casual Speech

In casual speech, the final か in an embedded wh-question is sometimes dropped with verbs like 知しっている / 分わかる:

Formal: 彼かれがどこに住すんでいるか、知しってる? Casual: 彼かれがどこに住すんでる、知しってる?

The か-dropped version sounds rough. For N3 study and any formal register, keep the か.

Nuance — 〜かどうか vs. 〜か〜ないか

There's an alternative form that spells out the "or not" explicitly:

行いくかどうか分わからない ≈ 行いくか行いかないか分わからない

"I don't know whether I'll go or not."

The 〜か〜ないか version is slightly more emphatic about the binary choice. Both are natural; かどうか is more common and compact.

docs.title: 6. The Comparison Table
docs.text: Scenario N4: Direct Question N3: Embedded Question Nuance Difference Knowing 何時なんじですか？ "What time is it?" 何時なんじか知しっていますか。 "Do you know what time it is?" Embedded is much softer — better for strangers or superiors. Checking 雨あめが降ふりますか。 "Will it rain?" 雨あめが降ふるかどうか確たしかめます。 "I will check if it will rain." Turns the question into an object of investigation. Deciding 結婚けっこんしますか？ "Will you marry?" 結婚けっこんするか考かんがえています。 "I'm thinking about whether to marry." Allows you to express internal process and deliberation.

docs.title: 7. Practice Drill
docs.text:
1. Convert: 「会議かいぎは何時なんじに始はじまりますか？」→ "Please tell me what time the meeting starts."
  2. Combine: この答こたえは正ただしいですか。+ 確たしかめてください。→ One sentence using 〜か or 〜かどうか.
  3. Translate: "I haven't decided yet whether I'll study abroad next year."
  4. Translate: "Do you remember where we put the keys?"
  5. Spot and fix the error: 彼かれが元気げんきだかどうか心配しんぱいしています。

docs.title: 8. Answer Key
docs.text:
Click to reveal answers

Question 1 会議かいぎが何時なんじに始はじまるか教おしえてください。 Wh-question (何時なんじ), so use 〜か. Note the particle shift from は to が: the embedded clause is subordinate and takes が for its subject. The polite form 始はじまります becomes plain 始はじまる.

Question 2 この答こたえが正ただしいかどうか確たしかめてください。 Yes/no question (is it correct?), so use 〜かどうか. 正ただしい is an i-adjective, so it attaches directly with no だ issue.

Question 3 来年らいねん留学りゅうがくするかどうか、まだ決きめていません。 Yes/no question (will study abroad or not), so 〜かどうか. 決きめていません (have not decided) is the natural form — not 決きめません (won't decide), because the issue is ongoing indecision, not refusal.

Question 4 鍵かぎをどこに置おいたか覚おぼえていますか。 Wh-question (どこ), so 〜か. Past tense 置おいた preserved inside the embedded clause.

Question 5 ❌ 彼かれが元気げんきだかどうか心配しんぱいしています。 ✅ 彼かれが元気げんきかどうか心配しんぱいしています。 元気げんき is a na-adjective; the だ must be dropped before かどうか in present affirmative form. Compare to past tense: 元気げんきだったかどうか is fine because だった is past-tense copula, not present だ.

docs.title: お/ご〜ください — Respectful Request (尊敬語)
敬けい意いあるあ依い頼 — お/ご〜下ください
ご覧らん下ください。 / お座すわり下ください。
JLPT Levelレベル
docs.text:
JLPT Levelレベル — Upper N4 / Lower N3

The pattern itself is N4 — a core 尊敬語そんけいご (sonkeigoそんけいご / respectful language) structure. But the calibration of when to use it versus neutral 〜て下ください or directive 〜なさいなさい, and the wago/kango prefix distinction, push it into lower-N3 territory for active production. Service-industry fluency depends on this pattern.

Tier Pattern Description Example N5 — Foundation 〜て下ください Neutral request — "please do X" 座すわって下ください。 N4 — Foundation 〜なさいなさい Directive — "do X" (authority to subordinate) 座すわりなさいなさい。 N4 — Core ← You are here お/ご〜下ください Respectful request — elevates the listener's action お座すわり下ください。 N4 — Foundation お/ご〜するする Humble — lowers speaker's action お座すわりしますします。 N3 — Extended お/ご〜いただけますか Humble request for listener's action お座すわりいただけますか。 N2 — Awareness お/ご〜になられる Double keigo (often avoided) お座すわりになられる。

docs.title: Quickクイック Summaryサマリー
docs.text:
At a glance

  • お/ご〜下ください is a 尊敬語そんけいご (sonkeigoそんけいご) pattern: it elevates the listener's action while politely requesting it. It does not mean "please" in a casual sense — it means "I humbly ask that you, who are above me, do this elevated action."
  • お attaches to 和語わご (wagoわご / native Japaneseじゃぱにーず) verb stems: 座すわる → お座すわり下ください, 休やすむ → お休やすみ下ください.
  • ご attaches to 漢語かんご (kangoかんご / Sinoしの-Japaneseじゃぱにーず) verb stems or nouns: 利用りようする → ご利用りよう下ください, 覧らんする → ご覧らん下ください.
