Growth narrative

Situation: ユイ is catching up with a friend she hasn't seen in a year. Last year, she was struggling with Japanese; now she's conversational.

去年きょねんは全然ぜんぜん話はなせなかったけど、今いまは日常会話にちじょうかいわくらい話はなせるようになったよ。

"Last year I couldn't speak at all, but now I've become able to handle daily conversation."

Why ようになった fits here: The sentence explicitly contrasts past inability (全然ぜんぜん話はなせなかった) with present ability. The ようになった is the bridge — it narrates the transformation. Without it, 話はなせるよ would be a flat statement of current ability; with it, the ability is framed as earned growth.

What the よ adds: The sentence-final よ marks this as shareable news — something the speaker is pleased to report. Combined with ようになった, it creates a small celebration of progress.

Habit formation

Situation: ケンタ is explaining to his doctor how his lifestyle has changed.

医者いしゃさんに言いわれたから、野菜やさいを食たべるようになったんです。

"Because the doctor told me to, I've come to eat vegetables."

Why ようになった fits here: The vegetables are now part of Kenta's diet — a change from before. The ようになった captures that this wasn't always true. The んです adds explanatory grounding — he's answering an implied "Why?" or "How come?"

Natural change

Situation: ハルコ is talking about her aging father.

父ちちは最近さいきん、夜よる中なかに目めが覚さめるようになった。

"My father has recently come to wake up in the middle of the night."

Why ようになった fits here: The waking is new, observed, and not deliberately caused. The ようになる pattern accommodates natural, even unwanted, changes — not just earned abilities.

docs.title: Function in Context
docs.text:
• 去年きょねんは全然ぜんぜん話はなせなかった establishes the "before" state — complete inability. The けど (but) sets up the contrast.
  • 今いまは marks the "after" state — present time frame.
  • 日常にちじょう会話かいわくらい scopes the ability — "daily conversation level." The くらい softens the claim, making it modest.
  • 話はなせるようになった is the core transformation. 話はなせる (potential) + ように (nominalization) + なった (past change).
  • よ closes the sentence as shareable news. The speaker is not just stating ability; they're reporting growth.
子供こどもの頃ころは苦手にがてだったけど、今いまは納豆なっとうが食たべられるようになった。 I used to be bad with it as a kid, but now I've become able to eat natto. Literal: Child time disliked-was but, now natto eat-can way became. Context: The classic Japanese "acquired taste" narrative. The ようになった marks the palate's maturation.

docs.title: Construction
docs.text:
Preceding Form Structure Example Potential verb (できる) [potential] + ようになる 泳およげるようになる Plain verb [plain] + ようになる 分わかるようになる Negative plain [nai-form] + ようになる 行いかないようになる Adjective (i) [stem] + く + ようになる 強つよくなるようになる Adjective (na) [stem] + に + ようになる 静しずかになるようになる Noun + の [noun] + のようになる 大人おとなのようになる

The なる vs. する distinction

〜ようになる (intransitive) describes natural or observed change. 〜ようにする (transitive) describes deliberate effort. Do not confuse them.

Intransitive (observed) Transitive (deliberate) 早はやく起おきるようになった 早はやく起おきるようにした "I've come to wake up early" "I made an effort to wake up early" Change observed, result-focused Effort made, process-focused

docs.title: The Full Spectrum of よう
docs.text:
The よう particle serves multiple functions across Japanese grammar. Understanding how 〜ように/〜ようになる fits into this spectrum prevents confusion.

Pattern Function Example 〜ようにする Volitional effort 毎日まいにち運動うんどうするようにする。"I'll try to exercise every day." 〜ようにしている Sustained effort 毎日まいにち運動うんどうするようにしている。"I make it a point to exercise every day." 〜ようになる Change over time 毎日まいにち運動うんどうするようになった。"I've come to exercise every day." 〜ようにしてくれる Request for accommodation 静しずかにするようにしてくれる？"Can you try to be quiet?" 〜ように言ゆう Indirect instruction 早はやく来こいように言いった。"I told him to come early." 〜ように見みえる Appearance 分わかるように見みえる。"It looks like [you] understand." 〜ようになっている Designed state 自動じどうで止とまるようになっている。"It's set up to stop automatically."

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
English talks about ability with can/could — static, present-oriented. Japanese talks about ability with 〜ようになる — narrative, change-oriented. This reflects a broader cultural preference for process over state, emergence over possession.

When a Japanese speaker says 泳およげるようになった, they are not just saying "I can swim." They are saying "There was a process, and now swimming is part of me." The grammar demands a story.

  • Growth as narrative. 〜ようになる requires a before and after. Even when unstated, the listener supplies it. The pattern activates a temporal frame automatically.
  • Effort as invisible. Unlike 〜ようにする which foregrounds effort, 〜ようになる backgrounds it. The change simply emerges. This is humbler — the speaker doesn't claim credit for their growth, they merely observe it.
  • Naturalness as virtue. Changes described with 〜ようになる often feel inevitable, organic, even welcomed. The pattern carries a subtle positive valence — things coming into their proper state.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text:
Grammar Point Core Meaning Emotional / Pragmatic Nuance Trigger Context JLPT 〜ようになる Change over time — ability/state emerges Narrative of growth; natural emergence; humble observation Describing acquired abilities, habits, or natural changes N4 〜ようにする Volitional effort — "try to make it so" Active willpower; self-discipline; resolution Setting intentions; making plans; self-improvement goals N4 〜ようにしている Sustained effort — maintaining practice Ongoing discipline; established routine Describing current maintained habits N3 〜できる Direct potential — current ability Flat statement; no narrative Answering ability questions; neutral capability N5 〜られる (passive/potential) Potential (formal) or passive More formal than できる; can be ambiguous Written Japanese; formal speech N4 〜始はじめる Begin to do Active start; deliberate initiation Starting new actions N4 〜出だす Start to do (sudden) Sudden emergence; often emotional or physical Sudden changes in state N4 〜てくる Change approaching speaker Gradual approach; "has come to be" Changes noticed over time, approaching present N4

The closest lookalike is 〜始はじめる. Both describe change over time, but 〜始はじめる emphasizes the starting point ("I started swimming"), while 〜ようになる emphasizes the resulting state ("I've become able to swim"). 〜始はじめる is about action; 〜ようになる is about capacity.

docs.title: 〜できる (direct potential)
docs.text:
The flat statement of ability. No narrative, no change.

Example 日本語にほんごが話はなせる。 English "I can speak Japanese." Intent Neutral capability statement vs. ようになる Removes the growth story. Appropriate when the "before" state is irrelevant or unknown.

When to use this instead

Use できる when you simply want to state current ability without framing it as achievement. Use ようになる when the journey matters.

docs.title: 〜ようにする (deliberate effort)
docs.text:
The active counterpart. Emphasizes willpower over result.

Example 毎日まいにち日本語にほんごを話はなすようにする。 English "I'll try to speak Japanese every day." Intent Setting an intention; making a plan vs. ようになる ようにする is about effort; ようになる is about result. They often appear in sequence: first you ようにする, then you ようになる.

docs.title: 〜てくる (change approaching)
docs.text:
Describes change noticed over time, approaching the present.

Example 最近さいきん、日本語にほんごが話はなせてくる。 English "Recently, I've been becoming able to speak Japanese." Intent Change approaching the speaker; ongoing emergence vs. ようになる 〜てくる emphasizes the approach toward the speaker (temporal proximity); 〜ようになる emphasizes the state change itself. Both can describe the same phenomenon with slightly different framing.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
The change-of-state meaning is preserved across registers, but the packaging shifts.

Casual Basic Polite Extra Polite

去年きょねんは全然ぜんぜん話はなせなかったけど、今いまは日常会話にちじょうかいわくらい話はなせるようになったよ。

"Last year I couldn't speak at all, but now I've become able to handle daily conversation."

Shift: Input example. Casual, narrative, celebratory. The よ marks shareable news.

去年きょねんは全然ぜんぜん話はなせませんでしたが、今いまは日常会話にちじょうかいわくらい話はなせるようになりました。

"Last year I could not speak at all, but now I have become able to handle daily conversation."

Shift: Past negative 話はなせなかった → 話はなせませんでした; なった → なりました. The narrative structure is identical; the politeness is elevated.

昨年さくねんは全くまったく話はなすことができませんでしたが、現在げんざいは日常会話にちじょうかいわレベルまで話はなせるようになっております。

"Last year I was entirely unable to speak, but at present I have come to be able to speak at a daily conversation level."

Shift: 去年きょねん → 昨年さくねん; 全然ぜんぜん → 全まったく; 今いま → 現在げんざい; なった → なっております (humble progressive). The core pattern 〜ようになる is preserved even in ultra-formal register.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: Step Pattern What It Adds Example N5 — Foundation 〜できる Direct potential — static ability 泳およげる。"I can swim." N5 — Foundation 〜なる Intransitive change — "become" 強つよくなる。"Become strong." N4 — Foundation 〜ようにする Volitional effort 毎日まいにち泳およぐようにする。"I'll try to swim every day." N4 — Core ← You are here 〜ようになる Observed change — ability/state emergence 泳およげるようになった。"I've become able to swim." N3 — Extended 〜ようにしている Sustained effort 毎日まいにち泳およぐようにしている。"I make it a point to swim every day." N3 — Extended 〜ようにしてくれる Request for accommodation 静しずかにするようにしてくれる？ N2 — Awareness 〜ようになっている Designed state 自動じどうで止とまるようになっている。"It's set up to stop automatically."

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
子供こどもの頃ころは苦手にがてだったけど、今いまは納豆なっとうが食たべられるようになった。 I used to be bad with it as a kid, but now I've become able to eat natto. Literal: Child time disliked-was but, now natto eat-can way became.

~Context: The classic Japanese palate-development narrative. The ようになった marks maturation without claiming effort — the change simply happened.~

毎日まいにち練習れんしゅうしたから、漢字かんじが読よめるようになった。 Because I practiced every day, I've become able to read kanji. Literal: Every day practiced because, kanji read-can way became.

~Context: Explicit effort → result. The から makes the causal chain explicit, but the ようになった still frames the result as gradual emergence.~

父ちちは最近さいきん、夜よる中なかに目めが覚さめるようになった。 My father has recently come to wake up in the middle of the night. Literal: Father recently, night middle eye wake way became.

~Context: Natural, possibly unwanted change. The ようになる accommodates changes that aren't achievements — simply observed shifts.~

日本にほんに来きてから、刺身さしみが食たべられるようになった。 Since coming to Japan, I've become able to eat sashimi. Literal: Japan came-after-from, sashimi eat-can way became.

~Context: Environmental influence. The change is attributed to context (being in Japan), not personal effort. The ようになる is neutral about cause.~

無理むりやり早はやく起おきるようにしてたら、自然しぜんに早はやく起おきるようになった。 I forced myself to wake up early for a while, and then I naturally came to wake up early. Literal: Forcibly early wake-up way doing-was, naturally early wake-up way became.

~Context: The full arc — deliberate effort (ようにしてた) leading to natural change (ようになった). This is the grammar of habit formation.~

大人おとなのようになったね。 You've become like an adult, haven't you. Literal: Adult way became, right?

~Context: Metaphorical change. The よう here is the "like/similar to" meaning rather than pure nominalization. The pattern overlaps with simile.~

docs.title: 1. Confusing 〜ようにする and 〜ようになる
docs.text:
The most common N4 error. Learners produce 泳およぐようにした when they mean 泳およげるようになった, or vice versa.

Wrong pattern

毎日まいにち泳およいで、泳およげるようにした。

"I swam every day and tried to make it so I could swim." — Bizarre. The effort and result are conflated.

Correct

毎日まいにち泳およぐようにしてたら、泳およげるようになった。

"I tried to swim every day, and then I became able to swim." — Effort (ようにする) leads to result (ようになる).

docs.title: 2. Using 〜ようになる for sudden changes
docs.text:
Pragmatic mismatch

事故じこで足あしが動うごかないようになった。

"Due to an accident, my legs became unable to move." — Grammatically possible, but 動うごかなくなった is more natural for sudden traumatic change. 〜ようになる prefers gradual emergence.

Better

事故じこで足あしが動うごかなくなった。

"My legs stopped moving due to an accident." — Direct state change, more appropriate for sudden events.

docs.title: 3. Overusing 〜ようになる for current ability
docs.text:
Narrative overkill

私わたしは英語えいごが話はなせるようになっている。

"I have become able to speak English [and remain so]." — Grammatically odd. 〜ようになっている is for designed states, not personal abilities. Use 話はなせる or 話はなせます.

Correct

私わたしは英語えいごが話はなせます。

"I can speak English." — Simple, appropriate.

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text:
Collocation Meaning 話はなせるようになる "Become able to speak" — language acquisition narrative 食たべられるようになる "Become able to eat" — palate development, allergy recovery 分わかるようになる "Come to understand" — comprehension emerging 読よめるようになる "Become able to read" — literacy development 聞きこえるようになる "Become able to hear" — hearing recovery, attention shift 見みえるようになる "Become visible / become able to see" — clarity emerging 起おきるようになる "Come to wake up [at a certain time]" — habit formation 寝ねられるようになる "Become able to sleep" — insomnia recovery 大人おとなのようになる "Become like an adult" — maturation (simile) 毎日まいにち〜するようになる "Come to do ~ every day" — habit formation

~These collocations are worth learning as complete units. The pattern is highly productive for any ability or habit that develops over time.~

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• 〜ようになる can describe loss of ability. 泳およげなくなった ("became unable to swim") uses the negative potential + なる. The pattern accommodates decline as well as growth — though the valence is usually negative when describing loss.
  • The "before" state is often ellipsed. Japanese speakers frequently say 泳およげるようになった without explicitly stating 前まえは泳およげなかった. The pattern itself activates the "before" frame — listeners automatically supply it.
  • 〜ようになる + たい is aspirational grammar. なりたい ("want to become") + ようになる creates the goal-state construction: 話はなせるようになりたい. This is more humble and process-oriented than 話はなしたい ("want to speak").
  • Simile よう and nominalization よう are related but distinct. 大人おとなのようになる ("become like an adult") uses simile よう; 泳およげるようになる uses nominalization よう. The grammar looks identical but the semantics differ. Context disambiguates.
  • 〜ようになっている is a hidden N2 gem. It describes designed or expected states: 自動じどうで止とまるようになっている ("It's set up to stop automatically"). The ている adds "is currently in the state of being designed to…" This is common in technical descriptions and instructions.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N4 Produce 〜ようになる correctly with potential verbs; distinguish from 〜ようにする; narrate simple ability acquisition Developing — N4→N3 Deploy naturally in growth narratives; use with plain verbs for habit formation; recognize 〜ようにしている as sustained effort counterpart Automatic — N3→N2 The pattern becomes a single conceptual unit — "emergence." You hear ようになった and immediately register a before/after frame without conscious parsing. Mastery You control the full よう spectrum: ようにする (effort), ようになる (emergence), ようにしている (sustained practice), ようにしてくれる (request), ようになっている (designed state). You know when できる is flat and when ようになる is narrative. You also recognize simile よう vs. nominalization よう instantly.

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 〜ようにする — Volitional effort; "try to make it so"
  • 〜ようにしている — Sustained effort; maintaining a practice
  • 〜ようになっている — Designed state; "is set up to"

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 〜ようにする — Deliberate effort vs. observed change
  • 〜できる — Static potential vs. narrative emergence
  • 〜始はじめる — Active beginning vs. gradual emergence
  • 〜てくる — Change approaching speaker vs. state change
  • 〜になる — Simple becoming vs. manner/state becoming

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• 〜なる (N5) — Intransitive change
  • 〜ように (N4) — Manner/nominalization
  • Potential forms (N4) — 泳およげる, 話はなせる, 読よめる
  • Plain verb forms (N5) — For habit formation usage

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 〜ようにしてくれる (N3) — Requesting accommodation
  • 〜ように言いう (N3) — Indirect reported commands
  • 〜ようになっている (N2) — Designed/expected states
  • 〜ようとする (N3) — Attemptive; "try to [do]"
  • 〜ようとしない (N3) — Refusal; "not try to [do]"

docs.title: Particles
docs.text:
助

Particles

The small words that carry structural weight — location, means, scope, addition, and emphasis.

で

で — Location, Means, Scope, and Limit

Four distinct roles of で and how to tell them apart in context

› も

も — Additive, Emphatic, Scalar, and Universal

How も extends beyond "also" into emphasis, extremes, and universal scope

›

docs.title: で — Location, Means, Scope, and Limit
The Particle で — Action, Means, and Domain
From "by bus" to "in the world" to "that's everything"
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — N5 Foundation through N3 Extended

The instrumental and locational で is N5 — among the first particles learned. But the scope/totalizing use (範囲はんい・限界げんかい) — superlatives, sums, and boundaries — is often undertaught until N4, where it suddenly appears everywhere in descriptions, comparisons, and accounting. Native speakers feel these as one unified particle; learners often fragment them.

Tier Pattern Description Example N5 — Foundation Means / instrument Tool, method, vehicle バスで行いく。 N5 — Foundation Location of action Place where an action occurs 図書としょ館かんで勉強べんきょうする。 N4 — Core ← You are here Scope / totalizing Domain of evaluation, sum total, boundary 日本にっぽんで一番いちばん高たかい。全部ぜんぶで千せん円えん。 N4 — Foundation Temporal limit Time within which something completes 一いち時間じかんで終おわる。 N4 — Foundation Material Substance something is made from 紙かみで作つくる。 N4 — Foundation Cause / reason Reason for a result or state 病気びょうきで休やすむ。 N3 — Extended State / manner Condition in which an action occurs 一人ひとりで行いく。裸はだかで寝ねる。 N3 — Extended Company Acting as a group 家族かぞくで旅行りょこうする。

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • で marks the context in which an action happens — whether that context is a physical place (図書としょ館かんで), a tool (ペンで), a domain of evaluation (日本にっぽんで一番いちばん), a time limit (三みっ日かで), or a total sum (全部ぜんぶで).
  • The core metaphor unifying all uses: で draws a boundary around the relevant field — the field of action, the field of comparison, the field of resources, or the field of time.
  • The scope/totalizing use is the focus: で marks the domain within which a statement applies (日本にっぽんで一番いちばん) or the sum total that a number represents (合計ごうけいで千せん円えん). English splits these into "in," "for," "by," and "with"; Japanese sees them as one particle.
  • The boundary between "location of action" (図書としょ館かんで) and "scope" (日本にっぽんで一番いちばん) can feel blurry because both mark a field — but the key distinction is whether the field is a stage for action or a frame for evaluation.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Use で to mark the field, means, or boundary within which an action, evaluation, or state is contained. If you can rephrase it as "within the field of X," "by means of X," or "at the limit of X," で is likely the particle.

バスで行いく。 I'll go by bus [means — the bus is the vehicle/method]. The bus is the means of movement. It defines how the going happens. 図書としょ館かんで勉強べんきょうする。 I study at the library [location of action — the library is the stage]. The library is the field where studying occurs. It defines where the action happens. 日本にっぽんで一番いちばん高たかい山やま。 The tallest mountain in Japan [scope — Japan is the domain of evaluation]. Japan is the field within which "tallest" is measured. It defines the comparison set. 全部ぜんぶで千せん円えんです。 It's 1000 yen in total [totalizing — the sum is contained within this amount]. "Everything" is the scope; 1000 yen is the total within that scope. 一いち時間じかんで終おわる。 It finishes in one hour / within one hour [temporal limit — the hour is the boundary]. One hour is the container of time. It defines the limit. 病気びょうきで学校がっこうを休やすんだ。 I missed school due to illness [cause — illness is the reason]. Illness is the causal field. It defines why the absence happened.

docs.title: 1. Means / Instrument (手段しゅだん・方法ほうほう)
docs.text:
The most famous use. で marks the tool, vehicle, method, or medium used to perform an action.

Pattern Example Vehicle 電車でんしゃで通勤つうきんする Tool 包丁ほうちょうで切きる Method 日本にっぽん語ごで話はなす Material (means sense) 手てで触さわる Language / medium メールで連絡れんらくする

Means で is active

The means is something the agent actively uses. It is not the material something is made of (that's a different use), nor the company (that's another use).

docs.title: 2. Location of Action (動作どうさの場所ばしょ)
docs.text:
で marks the place where an action occurs — as opposed to に, which marks the place where something exists or the target of movement.

Pattern Example Action stage 教室きょうしつで授業じゅぎょうを受うける Event venue 公園こうえんでコンサートがある Work location 会社かいしゃで働はたらく Eating location レストランで食たべる

で vs に for location

This is the classic N5 struggle. See the dedicated section below.

docs.title: 3. Scope / Domain (範囲はんい) — THE FOCUS
docs.text:
で marks the field within which an evaluation, comparison, or ranking applies. This is the "scope" use.

Sub-type Pattern Example Superlative domain 〜で一番いちばん クラスで一番いちばん頭あたまがいい Comparative domain 〜で〜の中なかで 日本にっぽんで有名ゆうめいな料理りょうり Group evaluation 〜で チームで最さい年少ねんしょう Exhaustive scope これで / それで これで全部ぜんぶです

The particle says: "Within the boundary of this set, the following evaluation holds."

世界せかいで二に番ばん目めに大おおきい。 The second largest in the world. The world is the evaluation set. で contains the superlative. うちの会社かいしゃで一番いちばん若わかい。 The youngest in our company. The company is the comparison group.

docs.title: 4. Totalizing / Summation (合計ごうけい・総計そうけい)
docs.text:
A specialized scope use. で marks the total sum or complete set.

Pattern Example Total sum 全部ぜんぶで千せん円えん Per-unit 一人ひとりで五百ごひゃく円えん Aggregate 合計ごうけいで十じゅう人にん Exhaustive これで終おわり

Totalizing で as 'in total'

English uses "in total," "altogether," or "for" in these contexts. Japanese uses で because the total amount is the field that contains all the individual items.

家族かぞく全部ぜんぶで五ご人にんです。 There are five people in the family altogether. The family is the set; five is the total within it. これで十分じゅうぶんです。 This is enough [this covers the requirement]. "This" marks the boundary of sufficiency. It contains everything needed.

docs.title: 5. Temporal Limit (期限きげん・時間じかん的てき限界げんかい)
docs.text:
で marks the time within which something is completed or the deadline/boundary of an action.

Pattern Example Completion time 三みっ日かでできる Duration limit 一いち時間じかんで終おわる Deadline 今日きょうで締しめ切きり Point of change 来月らいげつで三さん年ねん目め

Temporal で vs time に

三さん時じに終おわる = ends at 3:00 (point in time). 三さん時間じかんで終おわる = ends within 3 hours (duration limit). で marks the container of time; に marks the point on the timeline.

docs.title: 6. Material (材料ざいりょう)
docs.text:
で marks the raw material something is made from. This overlaps with means but is distinct: material is consumed or transformed, not just used.

Pattern Example Raw material 木きで作つくる Ingredient 小麦こむぎ粉こなで作つくるパン Resource 水みずで発電はつでんする

Material で vs から

See the comparison section below.

docs.title: 7. Cause / Reason (原因げんいん・理由りゆう)
docs.text:
で marks the cause of a result, especially natural causes, reasons for absence, or causal conditions.

Pattern Example Natural cause 地震じしんで建物たてものが倒たおれた Reason for absence 風邪かぜで休やすんだ Causal condition 不ふ注意ちゅういで怪我けがをした

Cause で vs から / ので

で is more compact and often implies direct causation. から is explanatory; ので is softer and more polite.

docs.title: 8. State / Manner / Company (様態ようたい・同行どうこう)
docs.text:
で marks the condition or group configuration in which an action occurs.

Pattern Example Alone 一人ひとりで行いく As a group 家族かぞくで旅行りょこうする In a state 無料むりょうで入場にゅうじょうする Bare/naked state 裸はだかで走はしる

一人ひとりで as scope and means

一人ひとりで can mean "by oneself" (means) or "alone" (state). The underlying logic is the same: one person is the field containing the action.

docs.title: The Scope/Totalizing Deep Dive
docs.text: This is the use most learners encounter late and understand incompletely. It deserves focused treatment.

docs.title: Evaluative Scope: Superlatives and Rankings
docs.text:
When you evaluate something as "the most X" or "the best Y," you need a domain. で marks that domain.

Domain Evaluation Full Phrase 日本にっぽん 一番いちばん高たかい 日本にっぽんで一番いちばん高たかいビル クラス 一番いちばん背せが高たかい クラスで一番いちばん背せが高たかい 世界せかい 二に番ばん目めに大おおきい 世界せかいで二に番ばん目めに大おおきい国くに チーム 最さい年少ねんしょう チームで最さい年少ねんしょうの選手せんしゅ うち 一番いちばん古ふるい うちで一番いちばん古ふるい車くるま

English 'in' = Japanese で

English uses "in" for these: "the tallest in the class." Japanese uses で, not に. This is because the class is the field of evaluation, not a physical location where something exists. に marks existence; で marks evaluative domains.

クラスで一番いちばん頭あたまがいい。 The smartest in the class. で marks the class as the comparison set. に would imply the intelligence exists inside the classroom as a physical location — wrong metaphor.

docs.title: Totalizing Scope: Sums and Aggregates
docs.text:
で marks the total amount that contains all individual parts.

Pattern Example Meaning 全部ぜんぶで 全部ぜんぶで千せん円えん 1000 yen altogether 合計ごうけいで 合計ごうけいで五ご人にん 5 people total 一人ひとりで 一人ひとりで三百さんびゃく円えん 300 yen per person / 300 yen for one person 合あわせてで (rare) — 総額そうがくで 総額そうがくで百ひゃく万まん円えん 1 million yen total これで これで全部ぜんぶです That's everything

これで as scope closure

これで終おわり doesn't just mean "It ends with this" (means). It means "This covers the scope — we are done." The で marks the boundary of completion.

これで十分じゅうぶんだ。 This is enough [this covers all requirements]. The scope of "enough" is defined by "this." で contains the sufficiency.

docs.title: Limiting Scope: Deadlines and Boundaries
docs.text:
で can mark the point at which something stops or the boundary is reached.

Pattern Example Meaning Time boundary 今日きょうで辞やめる Quit as of today Duration boundary 一いち週間しゅうかんで終おわる Finish within a week Final item これで最後さいご This is the last one Limit ここでやめる Stop here 来月らいげつで三さん年ねん目めになる。 It will become the third year as of next month. Next month is the boundary. The state of "third year" begins at the edge of that month.

docs.title: How It Works: The Field Metaphor
docs.text:
All uses of で can be unified under a single spatial metaphor: the particle draws a circle around a field, and the action or evaluation happens inside that circle.

Use Field Type Metaphor Means Method field "Within the method of [bus], movement occurs" Location Physical field "Within the space of [library], studying occurs" Scope Evaluative field "Within the set of [Japan], this is the tallest" Total Sum field "Within the total of [1000 yen], everything is contained" Time limit Temporal field "Within the boundary of [1 hour], completion occurs" Cause Causal field "Within the condition of [illness], absence occurs" State Conditional field "Within the state of [alone], action occurs"

This is why a single particle covers such apparently different concepts. Japanese treats them as one grammatical gesture: containment within a relevant boundary.

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
English slices the world finely: "by bus" (means), "at the library" (location), "in Japan" (scope), "in one hour" (time), "from paper" (material), "due to illness" (cause). Japanese sees a pattern: all of these are fields that contain the action.

The Japanese speaker doesn't ask "What kind of relationship is this?" but rather "What is the containing field?" If the library contains the studying, it's で. If the class contains the comparison, it's で. If one hour contains the completion, it's で.

  • Containment over relationship. English particles often mark grammatical relationships (agent, patient, instrument). Japanese particles often mark containers (space, time, domain, means). で is the ultimate container particle.
  • Scope as natural evaluation. English needs "in" or "of" for superlatives (tallest in the world). Japanese uses the same particle it uses for buses and libraries. The world is just a bigger container.
  • Total as closed field. 全部ぜんぶで千せん円えん treats the total as a closed circle: everything fits inside 1000 yen. The particle closes the set.

docs.title: で vs に (Location)
docs.text:
The most important distinction for learners.

Particle Function Example English に Existence / target / destination 図書としょ館かんにある It is in the library に Target of movement 図書としょ館かんに行いく Go to the library に Time point 三さん時じに行いく Go at 3:00 で Action stage 図書としょ館かんで勉強べんきょうする Study at the library で Scope 図書としょ館かんで一番いちばん静しずか The quietest in the library

The test

If you can insert "exist" and it makes sense, use に: 図書としょ館かんに本ほんがある (books exist in the library). If you can insert "do something" and it makes sense, use で: 図書としょ館かんで勉強べんきょうする (study at the library).

レストランで食たべる。 Eat at the restaurant [action stage]. レストランにある。 It is in the restaurant [existence].

docs.title: で vs から (Material)
docs.text:
Both can mean "made from," but the nuance differs.

Particle Nuance Example で Material is obvious / processed 紙かみで作つくった箱ばこ (a box made of paper — paper is the material) から Material is transformed / origin 葡萄ぶどうから作つくったワイン (wine made from grapes — transformation) で General material 木きで作つくる から Raw material with change 牛乳ぎゅうにゅうからチーズを作つくる

The distinction is blurry

In practice, で and から overlap for material. で is more common for everyday objects; から is more common for derived products.

docs.title: で vs から / ので (Cause)
docs.text: Particle Register / Nuance Example で Compact, direct cause 病気びょうきで休やすむ から Explanatory, logical 病気びょうきだから休やすむ ので Soft, polite 病気びょうきなので休やすみます ために Formal, objective 病気びょうきのために休やすむ

docs.title: で vs も (Scope/Additive)
docs.text:
Particle Function Example で Scope of evaluation 日本にっぽんで一番いちばん も Additive inclusion 日本にっぽんも行いく

Don't confuse these

日本にっぽんで行いく = "Go in Japan" or "Go by Japan" (nonsense). 日本にっぽんも行いく = "I'll go to Japan too."

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
で is neutral and appears at every register. Formality shifts come from the main predicate, not from the particle.

Casual Basic Polite Extra Polite

バスで行いく。全部ぜんぶで千せん円えんだよ。

"I'll go by bus. It's 1000 yen total."

Shift: Plain form. で is unchanged.

バスで参まいります。全部ぜんぶで千せん円えんです。

"I will go by bus. It is 1000 yen in total."

Shift: です／ます. で remains で.

電車でんしゃでお伺うかがいいたします。合計ごうけいで千せん円えんでございます。

"I will visit by train. The total is 1000 yen."

Shift: Keigo verbs. で remains で. The particle is register-invariant.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: Step Pattern What It Adds Example N5 — Foundation に (location/target) Existence, destination, time points 東京とうきょうにある。三さん時じに。 N5 — Foundation を (object) Direct object 本ほんを読よむ。 N5 — Core ← You are here で (means/location) Action stage, instrument, method 図書としょ館かんで。ペンで。 N4 — Extended で (scope/total) Evaluative domain, sum total 日本にっぽんで一番いちばん。全部ぜんぶで。 N4 — Extended で (temporal limit) Completion boundary 一いち時間じかんで終おわる。 N4 — Extended で (cause) Direct reason 病気びょうきで休やすむ。 N3 — Extended で (state/company) Condition, group configuration 一人ひとりで。家族かぞくで。 N3 — Extended で (material) Raw substance 紙かみで作つくる。

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: 日本にっぽんで一番いちばん有名ゆうめいな山やまは富士ふじ山やまです。 The most famous mountain in Japan is Mt. Fuji. Scope: Japan is the evaluative field. 全部ぜんぶで千せん円えんです。 It's 1000 yen in total. Totalizing: 1000 yen contains the full sum. 一人ひとりでできる。 I can do it alone / by myself. State/scope: One person is the containing field. 三みっ日かで終おわる。 It will be finished in three days. Temporal limit: Three days is the boundary. 地震じしんで建物たてものが倒たおれた。 Buildings collapsed due to the earthquake. Cause: The earthquake is the causal field. これで終おわりです。 This is the end / That covers it. Limiting scope: "This" marks the boundary of completion. クラスで一番いちばん背せが高たかい。 The tallest in the class. Evaluative scope: The class is the comparison set. 家族かぞくで旅行りょこうする。 To travel as a family. Company/state: The family is the containing group. 紙かみで作つくった。 I made it from paper. Material: Paper is the substance. 無料むりょうで入場にゅうじょうできる。 You can enter for free. State: "Free" is the condition containing the action.

docs.title: 1. Using に for evaluative scope
docs.text:
English transfer error

日本にっぽんに一番いちばん高たかい山やま。

~に marks existence or direction. For evaluative scope ("in Japan"), で is required.~

Correct

日本にっぽんで一番いちばん高たかい山やま。

docs.title: 2. Confusing temporal point (に) with temporal limit (で)
docs.text:
Time particle error

三さん時間じかんに終おわる。

~三さん時じに = at 3:00 (point, に). 三さん時間じかんで = within 3 hours (duration limit, で).~

Correct

三さん時間じかんで終おわる。

docs.title: 3. Using で for existence location
docs.text:
Existence error

図書としょ館かんで本ほんがある。

~Existence uses に: 図書としょ館かんに本ほんがある. で marks where an action happens, not where something exists.~

Correct

図書としょ館かんに本ほんがある。

docs.title: 4. Confusing scope で with topic は
docs.text:
Redundancy

日本にっぽんでは一番いちばん高たかい。

~Adding は is possible (contrastive topic: "in Japan [as opposed to elsewhere]"), but 日本にっぽんで一番いちばん高たかい is the neutral form. Don't overuse は.~

Correct

日本にっぽんで一番いちばん高たかい。

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text:
Collocation Meaning 〜で一番いちばん "The most ~ in [domain]" — superlative scope 〜で有名ゆうめい "Famous in [domain]" — evaluative scope 全部ぜんぶで "In total" — totalizing 合計ごうけいで "In total / altogether" — totalizing 一人ひとりで "Alone / by oneself" — state/scope 家族かぞくで "As a family" — company これで "With this / that's everything" — limiting scope 三みっ日かで "In three days" — temporal limit 一いち時間じかんで "In one hour" — temporal limit 今日きょうで "As of today" — boundary バスで "By bus" — means ペンで "With a pen" — means 日本にっぽん語ごで "In Japanese" — means/medium 紙かみで "From paper" — material 病気びょうきで "Due to illness" — cause 不ふ注意ちゅういで "Due to carelessness" — cause 無料むりょうで "For free" — state 裸はだかで "Naked / bare" — state ゆっくりで "Slowly / at a leisurely pace" — manner

~These collocations are worth learning as units. 〜で一番いちばん and 全部ぜんぶで are especially high-frequency.~

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• で can stack with other particles in compound forms. でも (even if), までも (even until), からも (even from). These are not the particle で alone, but で participates in the compound.
  • 一人ひとりで has two readings. It can mean "by oneself" (means: using only one person) or "alone" (state: in the condition of solitude). Context disambiguates, but the underlying container metaphor is the same.
  • これで can mean "that's why" in some contexts. When used as a sentence opener (これで分わかった), it means "Now I get it" — the で marks the current moment as the field of understanding. This is related to scope: "within this, comprehension occurs."
  • で can mark the price of a transaction. 千せん円えんで買かった ("bought it for 1000 yen"). This is a specialized scope/totalizing use — the 1000 yen is the field containing the transaction.
  • 全部ぜんぶで vs 全部ぜんぶが. 全部ぜんぶで千せん円えん = "1000 yen total." 全部ぜんぶが千せん円えん = "Everything is 1000 yen [each]." The particle choice completely changes the math.
  • で can be used for "at the age of." 二十歳はたちで結婚けっこんした ("Got married at 20"). This is a temporal boundary use — 20 is the field containing the marriage event.
  • The "company" use is culturally significant. 家族かぞくで旅行りょこうする doesn't just mean "travel with family" — it means "travel as a family unit." The で marks the family as the containing social field. This is why 一人ひとりで can sound lonely in Japanese: it explicitly marks the absence of a containing group.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N5 Use で correctly for means and location of action; distinguish で from に for location; avoid using に for evaluative scope Developing — N5→N4 Deploy scope/totalizing で naturally (〜で一番いちばん, 全部ぜんぶで, これで終おわり); use temporal limit で correctly (三さん時間じかんで); handle cause で without overusing から Automatic — N4→N3 The container metaphor becomes intuitive. You feel why で works for buses, libraries, Japan, sums, and time limits. You no longer fragment で into "different particles." Mastery You control the full で spectrum and know exactly when it competes with に (existence), から (material/cause), も (additive), and は (topic). You also hear the subtle differences between これで終おわり (scope closure) and これで終おわる (means completion).

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• に — Location of existence, target, time point
  • を — Direct object
  • から — Origin, raw material, cause
  • まで — Until, extent

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• に (location) — existence vs. action
  • から (material/cause) — transformation vs. direct cause
  • ので (conjunction) — soft cause, different word
  • でも (conjunction) — "but," unrelated to particle で
  • も (additive) — inclusion vs. scope

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• N5 particle foundation — understanding case marking
  • Verb transitivity — knowing whether an action needs a stage
  • Adjective superlatives — 一番いちばん, 最もっとも, etc.
  • Counters and numbers — for totalizing use

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• でも (particle) — "even if / even [noun]" — conditional extreme
  • から (material) — derived products and transformation
  • ので (conjunction) — soft causal connection
  • ては / では — conditional negative (contraction of て + は)

docs.title: The Particle も — Also, Even, and Everything In Between
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — N5 Foundation through N3 Extended

The additive も is N5 — one of the first particles learners encounter. But the scalar, emphatic, conditional, and negative-polarity uses spread across N4 and N3, forming a dense web that native speakers navigate effortlessly and learners often underuse.

Tier Pattern Description Example N5 — Foundation Noun + も Additive — "also / too" 私わたしも行いく。 N5 — Foundation AもBも Listing — "both A and B" 猫ねこも犬いぬも好すき。 N4 — Foundation Quantity + も Scalar — "as much as / a whole / not even" 三さん時間じかんも待まった。 N4 — Foundation Wh-word + も + negative Negative polarity — "nothing / nobody / nowhere" 何なんも食たべない。 N4 — Core ← You are here 〜ても / 〜でも Conditional — "even if / even though" 高たかくても買かう。 N3 — Extended Noun + でも Extreme — "even [noun]" 子供こどもでもできる。 N3 — Extended 〜もしない / 〜もある Minimal / maximal — "not even / there is also" 一いち度ども行いかない。 N2 — Awareness 〜も〜ば〜も Correlative — "both… and… / neither… nor…" 行いくも行いかないも自由じゆうだ。

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • も is a multipurpose particle that replaces は, が, or を on a noun to add it to a set. 私わたしは行いく → 私わたしも行いく ("I too will go").
  • Beyond simple addition, も scales: attached to quantities it means "as much as / a whole" (三さん時間じかんも); attached to minimal quantities with negative verbs it means "not even" (千せん円えんもない).
  • With wh-words (何なん, 誰だれ, どこ) and negative verbs, も creates universal negatives: 何なんも食たべない = "I eat nothing."
  • With the te-form (〜ても / 〜でも), も creates conditional extremes: "even if / even though."
  • With bare nouns in extreme contexts, も means "even": 子供こどもでもできる = "Even a child can do it."
  • The core logic unifying all uses: も marks an item as at the edge of a set — either adding to the set (additive), pushing the boundary of the set (scalar), or exhausting the set entirely (universal / negative polarity).

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Use も when you want to mark an item as belonging to a set in a way that emphasizes its inclusion, its extremity, or its exhaustiveness. The particle absorbs and replaces whatever case particle (は, が, を) would normally mark that item.

私わたしも行いく。 I will go too [additive — I am added to the set of goers]. も replaces が or は. The speaker joins an existing group. 三さん時間じかんも待まった。 I waited a whole three hours [scalar — the quantity is emphasized as large]. も attaches to the quantity. The three hours are marked as notable, burdensome, or impressive. 何なんも食たべない。 I eat nothing [negative polarity — the wh-word + も exhausts the set of possible objects]. も attaches to 何なん in a negative context, creating universal negation. 高たかくても買かう。 Even if it's expensive, I'll buy it [conditional extreme]. も attaches to the te-form. The condition (expensive) is marked as extreme but insufficient to prevent the action. 子供こどもでもできる。 Even a child can do it [extreme — the noun is at the low end of capability]. も attaches to a noun directly (or to the copula contraction でも). The child represents the minimal expected capability.

docs.title: 1. Additive も — "Also / Too / As Well"
docs.text:
The foundational use. も replaces は, が, or を on a noun to include it in a set already under discussion.

Pattern Example Meaning Noun + も 私わたしも。 Me too. Noun + も + predicate 彼かれも来きた。 He came too. Noun + も + particle + predicate 東京とうきょうにも行いった。 I went to Tokyo too. Noun + も + noun + も 猫ねこも犬いぬも好すき。 I like both cats and dogs.

も absorbs particles

You cannot double-mark. 私わたしはも and 私わたしもは are both ungrammatical. も replaces the topic/subject/object marker entirely. For other particles (に, で, から, etc.), も attaches after them: 東京とうきょうにも, 学校がっこうでも.

彼かれにも言いった。 I told him too [に + も]. The に marks the recipient; the も adds him to the set of people told. 昨日きのうも雨あめだった。 It rained yesterday too [temporal + も]. も attaches to time expressions to add them to a temporal set.

docs.title: 2. Scalar も — "As Much As / A Whole / Not Even"
docs.text:
When も attaches to quantities, it pushes them to the edge of expectation. The direction (large or small) depends on context and polarity.

Large / Surprising: | 千せん円えんもした。 | It cost as much as 1000 yen [surprisingly expensive]. | |---|---| | 三さん時間じかんも待まった。 | I waited a whole three hours [surprisingly long]. | | 十じゅう人にんも来きた。 | As many as ten people came [surprisingly many]. |

Small / Dismissive (with negative or downplaying predicates): | 千せん円えんもない。 | I don't even have 1000 yen [dismally little]. | |---|---| | 三さん時間じかんも寝ねていない。 | I haven't even slept three hours [insufficient]. | | 一人ひとりもいなかった。 | There wasn't even one person [universal negative]. |

The scalar flip

The same quantity + も can mean "as much as" (positive, surprising) or "not even" (negative, dismissive). Context and the main predicate determine which reading applies.

docs.title: 3. Wh-word + も — Universal and Negative Polarity
docs.text:
When も attaches to question words (wh-words), it creates universal or exhaustive reference. With negative predicates, this becomes universal negation.

Wh-word + Negative Meaning + Affirmative Meaning 何なんも 何なんも食たべない eat nothing (rare/non-standard) — 誰だれも 誰だれも来こなかった nobody came (rare) — どこも どこも行いかない go nowhere どこも素敵すてきだ everywhere is lovely いつも (rare with neg) — いつも忙いそがしい always busy どれも どれも好すきじゃない none of them liked どれも美味おいしい all of them delicious いくらも いくらもない not much at all いくらもある there is plenty

いつも is lexicalized

いつも means "always" and is almost always affirmative. It does not follow the negative polarity pattern. For "never," use 一いち度ども〜ない or 全然ぜんぜん〜ない.

何なんも with affirmative verbs is non-standard

何なんも食たべた is ungrammatical in standard Japanese. For "I ate something," use 何なんか食たべた. For "I ate everything," use 全部ぜんぶ食たべた or 何なんでも食たべた.

docs.title: 4. Conditional ても / でも — "Even If / Even Though"
docs.text:
も attaches to the te-form of verbs, i-adjectives, and the de-form of nouns/na-adjectives to create extreme conditions.

Type Form Example Meaning Verb (te-form) 〜ても 行いっても無駄むだだ Even if [you] go, it's useless i-Adjective 〜くても 高たかくても買かう Even if expensive, [I] buy na-Adjective 〜でも 静しずかでも聞きこえる Even if quiet, [I] can hear Noun 〜でも 雨あめでも出でかける Even if rain / even rain, [I] go out Negative verb 〜なくても 行いかなくてもいい Even if [you] don't go, it's fine

でも has three faces

  1. Conditional even: 雨あめでも出でかける (even if rain)
  2. Extreme even: 子供こどもでもできる (even a child)
  3. Sentence-initial but: でも、高たかいよ (but, it's expensive) — this is a different word entirely, a contraction of それでも.

docs.title: 5. Extreme も — "Even [Noun]"
docs.text:
When も attaches directly to nouns (or noun+copula) without a conditional te/de, it marks the noun as an extreme or unexpected member of a category.

子供こどもも知しっている。 Even children know [surprisingly basic]. 彼女かのじょも来くるって。 Even she's coming [surprisingly, she too]. 百ひゃく円えんもあれば足たりる。 Even 100 yen would be enough [surprisingly little].

Extreme も vs. Additive も

The line is blurry. 彼女かのじょも来くる can mean "She's coming too" (additive) or "Even she's coming" (surprising extreme). Intonation and context disambiguate. Falling intonation = additive; rising or emphatic intonation = extreme.

docs.title: 6. Minimal / Maximal も — 〜もしない / 〜もある
docs.text:
も marks the edge of verbal action — either the absence of even minimal action, or the presence of additional action.

一いち度ども行いったことがない。 I've never gone even once [minimal]. 驚おどろくこともある。 There are also surprising things [maximal/additive]. 何なんもしない。 Do nothing [universal negative]. 笑わらうこともできない。 Can't even laugh [extreme inability].

docs.title: In Real Life
docs.text:
In Real Life

Situation: ユイ and ケン are planning a weekend outing. They've been discussing restaurants.

> ユイ：寿司すしも食たべたいな。 > ケン：私わたしも。でも、高たかいよ。 > ユイ：千せん円えんもしないよ。ランチなら。 > ケン：子供こどもでも行いける？ > ユイ：誰だれでも行いける。何なんも心配しんぱいしないで。

Yui: "I want to eat sushi too." Ken: "Me too. But it's expensive." Yui: "It won't even cost 1000 yen. If it's lunch." Ken: "Can even children go?" Yui: "Anyone can go. Don't worry about anything."

The も density: This short exchange contains five distinct も functions: 1. 寿司すしも — additive (sushi too, in addition to other foods) 2. 私わたしも — additive (me too) 3. 千せん円えんもしない — scalar negative (not even 1000 yen) 4. 子供こどもでも — extreme conditional (even if [you're] a child / even a child) 5. 誰だれでも — extreme universal (anyone, no matter who) 6. 何なんも — negative polarity (nothing)

What makes this natural: Native speakers switch between も functions unconsciously. The particle is a Swiss Army knife that scales from simple addition to universal quantification. The density in this exchange is completely normal.

docs.title: The Particle Replacement Rule
docs.text:
も does not stack with は, が, or を. It replaces them. For other particles, it stacks after them.

Normal Particle + も Example は (topic) も (replaces) 私わたしは → 私わたしも が (subject) も (replaces) 彼かれが → 彼かれも を (object) も (replaces) 本ほんを → 本ほんも に (direction) にも 東京とうきょうに → 東京とうきょうにも で (location/means) でも 学校がっこうで → 学校がっこうでも から (origin) からも 日本にっぽんから → 日本にっぽんからも へ (direction) へも 家いえへ → 家いえへも と (with) とも 友達ともだちと → 友達ともだちとも まで (until) までも 夜よるまで → 夜よるまでも

でも is overloaded

学校がっこうでも can mean: 1. "At school too" (で + も, additive) 2. "Even at school" (で + も, extreme) 3. "Even if at school" (conditional, though rare with concrete nouns)

Context resolves the ambiguity.

docs.title: Scalar も with Numbers
docs.text:
The scalar function has precise pragmatic rules:

Pattern Reading Example Number + counter + も + affirmative "As many as / a whole" 三さん時間じかんも待まった Number + counter + も + negative "Not even" 三さん時間じかんも寝ねていない 一いち + counter + も + negative "Not even one" 一いち度ども行いかない 少すこしも + negative "Not even a little" 少すこしも分わからない ちっとも + negative "Not at all" ちっとも知しらない

The number one is special

一いち度ども, 一人ひとりも, 一ひとつも with negative verbs create the strongest universal negation. They mean "not even once / not even one person / not even one thing."

docs.title: Wh-word + も Grammar
docs.text:
Wh-word Negative Form Meaning Affirmative Form Meaning 何なんも 何なんも〜ない nothing / not anything (non-standard) — 誰だれも 誰だれも〜ない nobody / not anyone 誰だれもが知しる everyone knows (literary) どこも どこも〜ない nowhere / not anywhere どこも賑にぎやか everywhere is lively いつも (rare) — いつも〜 always どれも どれも〜ない none どれも〜 all of them いくらも いくらも〜ない not much いくらも〜 plenty

何なんか vs. 何なんも

何なんか (something) is the affirmative counterpart to 何なんも (nothing). They are not interchangeable: - 何なんか食たべた (I ate something) - 何なんも食たべなかった (I ate nothing)

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
English tends to add information through new subjects and verbs: "I went. She went too. He also went." Japanese tends to add information through particle manipulation on a stable predicate: 私わたしが行いった。彼女かのじょも行いった。彼かれも行いった。 The predicate stays; the participants accumulate.

This reflects a broader Japanese preference for stable frames with modular participants. も is the perfect tool for this — it allows the sentence skeleton to remain while new elements are clipped on.

The scalar uses reveal another preference: quantities as emotional events. 三さん時間じかんも待まった doesn't just state the duration; it evaluates it. The も turns a measurement into an experience. English requires separate words ("a whole three hours," "as much as three hours") to achieve this; Japanese builds it into the particle.

  • Inclusion as social harmony. Additive も is the grammar of solidarity. Saying 私わたしも is not just information — it's alignment. You are joining the other's frame.
  • Extremity as rhetoric. Scalar も pushes quantities to the edge of believability. It makes numbers feel large, small, shocking, or pitiful.
  • Exhaustion as negation. Wh-word + も + negative doesn't just deny — it empties the universe of possibilities. 何なんもない is stronger than ない because it denies the existence of any possible thing.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text:
Grammar Point Core Meaning Emotional / Pragmatic Nuance Trigger Context JLPT も (additive) Also / too Solidarity, inclusion, joining a set Adding oneself or others to an established group N5 も (scalar) As much as / not even Emphasis on quantity as surprising or insufficient Quantities with emotional loading N4 も (wh-negative) Nothing / nobody / nowhere Universal negation; emptiness Negative sentences with wh-words N4 ても / でも Even if / even though Extreme condition insufficient to prevent action Conditional sentences pushing boundaries N4 Noun + でも Even [noun] Extreme member of a category Surprising capability or inclusion N3 さえ / すら Even More extreme than も; often negative or literary "Not even" with stronger dismissal; literary N3 だけ Only Exclusionary; limits a set "Nothing but" contexts N4 しか〜ない Only [negative frame] "Nothing except"; more emphatic than だけ Negative exclusivity N4 でも (sentence-initial) But Contrastive conjunction (different from particle も) Changing topic, pushing back N5

も vs. さえ: 子供こどもでもできる means "Even a child can do it" (surprising but neutral). 子供こどもさえできる means "Even a child can do it" (dismissive of adult difficulty, or emphasizing how easy it is). さえ is sharper and more evaluative.

も vs. だけ: 私わたしも行いく ("I too will go" — inclusive) vs. 私わたしだけ行いく ("Only I will go" — exclusive). They are semantic opposites but structurally similar in replacing particles.

docs.title: だけ (only — exclusive)
docs.text:
The opposite of additive も. Limits rather than expands.

Example 私わたしだけ行いく。 English "Only I will go." Intent Exclusion — others are not going vs. も も adds to a set; だけ subtracts from it.

docs.title: さえ (even — extreme)
docs.text:
More emphatic and often more critical than scalar も.

Example 子供こどもさえ知しっている。 English "Even children know [so why don't you?]" Intent Sharp emphasis; can carry reproach vs. も さえ sounds more pointed. も sounds more neutral or inclusive.

docs.title: しか〜ない (only — negative frame)
docs.text:
Exclusive, like だけ, but grammatically negative.

Example 私わたししか行いかない。 English "Only I will go / Nobody but me is going." Intent Strong exclusion with negative framing vs. も も is positive/inclusive addition; しか〜ない is negative/exclusive limitation.

docs.title: ても vs. とても (completely different)
docs.text:
Visual similarity trap

高たかくても (even if expensive) and とても高たかい (very expensive) share ても/とても but are unrelated. Do not confuse them.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
も is neutral and appears across all registers. The formality shifts come from the main predicate, not from も itself.

Casual Basic Polite Extra Polite

私わたしも行いく。千せん円えんもしないし。

"I'm going too. And it won't even cost 1000 yen."

Shift: Plain form, additive + scalar. The し adds "and what's more."

私わたしも行いきます。千せん円えんもいたしません。

"I will go as well. It will not even amount to 1000 yen."

Shift: です／ます on the main verbs. も itself is unchanged. しない → いたしません (humble negative of する).

私わたしどもも参さんります。いかなる場合ばあいでも対応たいおういたします。

"We too will attend. We will respond in any and all circumstances."

Shift: 私わたしどもも (humble plural + additive も). いかなる場合ばあいでも (literary/extreme conditional + も). も survives even in the most formal registers.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: Step Pattern What It Adds Example N5 — Foundation Noun + も Additive — "also / too" 私わたしも行いく。 N5 — Foundation AもBも Listing — "both A and B" 猫ねこも犬いぬも好すき。 N4 — Foundation Quantity + も Scalar — "as much as / not even" 三さん時間じかんも待まった。 N4 — Foundation Wh-word + も + negative Negative polarity 何なんも食たべない。 N4 — Core ← You are here 〜ても / 〜でも Conditional extreme — "even if" 高たかくても買かう。 N3 — Extended Noun + でも Extreme — "even [noun]" 子供こどもでもできる。 N3 — Extended 〜もしない Minimal — "not even" 一いち度ども行いかない。 N2 — Awareness 〜も〜ば〜も Correlative — "both… and…" 行いくも行いかないも自由じゆうだ。

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: 彼女かのじょも来くるって。 I heard she's coming too [additive, possibly surprising]. Context: Adding her to the set of attendees. The って marks hearsay. 三さん時間じかんも待まったのに、来こなかった。 I waited a whole three hours, and yet he didn't come [scalar + のに]. Context: Scalar も emphasizes the wasted duration; のに adds the frustrated contrast. 誰だれも知しらない。 Nobody knows [universal negative]. Context: Complete absence of knowers. Stronger than 知しらない人ひとがいない (double negative). 高たかくても買かう。 Even if it's expensive, I'll buy it [conditional extreme]. Context: The expense is acknowledged as extreme but insufficient to stop the purchase. 子供こどもでもできる。 Even a child can do it [extreme noun]. Context: The child represents the lower boundary of capability. The task is therefore easy. どこも素敵すてきだった。 Everywhere was lovely [universal affirmative]. Context: どこも with affirmative verb means "everywhere." With negative it would mean "nowhere." 何なんも心配しんぱいない。 There's nothing to worry about [universal negative]. Context: Reassurance through total negation. The も exhausts the set of worries. 一いち度ども行いったことがない。 I've never gone even once [minimal scalar]. Context: 一いち度ども with negative creates the strongest "never." The minimal quantity is denied. 百ひゃく円えんもあれば足たりる。 Even 100 yen would be enough [extreme minimal]. Context: The small amount is marked as sufficient — surprisingly little is needed. 寿司すしも天てんぷらも食たべた。 I ate both sushi and tempura [listing]. Context: Multiple items, each marked with も. The predicate applies to the entire set.

docs.title: 1. Double-marking with は or が
docs.text:
Incorrect

私わたしはも行いく。

~も replaces は/が. Stacking them is ungrammatical.~

Correct

私わたしも行いく。

docs.title: 2. Using 何なんも with affirmative verbs
docs.text:
Incorrect

何なんも食たべた。

~何なんも requires a negative predicate in standard Japanese. For affirmative "something," use 何なんか. For affirmative "everything," use 全部ぜんぶ or 何なんでも.~

Correct

何なんか食たべた。 (I ate something.) 何なんも食たべなかった。 (I ate nothing.)

docs.title: 3. Confusing sentence-initial でも with conditional でも
docs.text:
Ambiguous misread

でも、高たかいよ。

~This is the conjunction "but," unrelated to the particle も. It comes from それでも.~

Understanding

雨あめでも出でかける = "Even if it rains, I'll go out" (conditional particle でも). でも、高たかいよ = "But it's expensive" (sentence-initial conjunction).

docs.title: 4. Using いつも with negative verbs for "never"
docs.text:
Pragmatically odd

いつも行いかない。

~This means "I always don't go" (habitual non-attendance), not "I never go." For "never," use 一いち度ども行いかない or 全然ぜんぜん行いかない.~

Correct

一いち度ども行いかない。 (I never go — not even once.)

docs.title: 5. Assuming scalar も always means "a lot"
docs.text:
Context error

千せん円えんもある。 = "I have as much as 1000 yen" [surprisingly much]. 千せん円えんもない。 = "I don't even have 1000 yen" [dismally little].

~The same scalar も flips meaning based on predicate polarity. Learners sometimes miss the flip.~

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text:
Collocation Meaning 私わたしも "Me too" — the universal additive 〜も〜も "Both… and…" — listing pattern 何なんも〜ない "Not… anything" — universal negative 誰だれも〜ない "Nobody…" — universal negative どこも〜ない "Nowhere…" — universal negative 一いち度ども〜ない "Never…" — minimal scalar negative 少すこしも〜ない "Not even a little…" — scalar negative ちっとも〜ない "Not at all…" — emphatic scalar negative いくらも〜ない "Not much…" — scalar negative 千せん円えんもする "Costs as much as 1000 yen" — large scalar 三さん時間じかんも "A whole three hours" — temporal scalar 子供こどもでも "Even a child" — extreme capability 誰だれでも "Anyone" — universal extreme 何なんでも "Anything / everything" — universal affirmative いつも "Always" — lexicalized temporal universal どこでも "Anywhere / everywhere" — locative universal 〜てもいい "It's okay even if…" — permission pattern 〜てもかまわない "I don't mind even if…" — permissive conditional 〜なくてもいい "Don't have to…" — negative permission 〜てもしょうがない "Even if… it's no use" — resigned conditional 〜ても無駄むだだ "Even if… it's useless" — futility

~These collocations are worth learning as complete units. 何なんも〜ない, 〜てもいい, and 誰だれでも are especially high-frequency.~

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• も can stack on multiple items in a single predicate. 猫ねこも犬いぬも鳥とりも好すき is perfectly natural — three も-marked items with one predicate. English would need "I like cats, dogs, and birds" or "I like cats and dogs and even birds."
  • も replaces を, but the object reading remains. When you say 本ほんも読よむ, the も tells you the book is added to a set, but the underlying grammatical role (object) is unchanged. This is why も is sometimes called a "case-marking particle" rather than a "sentence particle."
  • Scalar も with time can be nostalgic or burdensome. 三さん時間じかんも待まった sounds long and burdensome. 三さん時間じかんも話はなした sounds long and possibly intimate (we talked for a whole three hours). The emotional valence depends on the verb.
  • 何なんでも and 誰だれでも are affirmative universals. Unlike 何なんも/誰だれも which require negatives, 何なんでも食たべる means "I eat anything" and 誰だれでも来きていい means "Anyone may come." The でも here is the conditional/extreme particle, not the conjunction.
  • いつも is the lexicalized exception. It behaves like an adverb meaning "always" and does not require negative polarity. It is essentially a different word that happens to share the spelling.
  • 〜てもいい is a permission pattern with hidden complexity. 行いってもいい ("You may go") uses も to mark the action as extreme — "Even if you go, it's fine." Over time this has grammaticalized into the standard permission pattern. But the scalar origin is still audible: the speaker is saying "Going is at the edge of acceptable actions, and I'm okay with it."
  • 〜もしない with positive verbs means "not even." 笑わらいもしない ("doesn't even laugh") is stronger than 笑わらわない ("doesn't laugh"). The も exhausts the set of possible reactions.
  • The "both… and…" pattern (AもBも) implies exhaustiveness. When you say 猫ねこも犬いぬも好すき, you imply these are the relevant categories. Adding 鳥とりも changes the implied set. The listener unconsciously tracks what も marks as "the complete relevant group."

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N5 Use additive も correctly (私わたしも, AもBも); avoid double-marking with は/が; recognize いつも as "always" Developing — N5→N4 Deploy scalar も with quantities (三さん時間じかんも, 千せん円えんも); master 何なんも/誰だれも/どこも + negative; use 〜ても/〜でも for conditional extremes Automatic — N4→N3 Distinguish additive も from scalar も from extreme も in real time; use 子供こどもでも naturally; understand 〜てもいい as permission; produce 一いち度ども〜ない without thinking Mastery You control the full も spectrum — adding items, scaling quantities, exhausting universes, and pushing conditional boundaries. You know when さえ would be too sharp, when だけ would be too exclusive, and when も is exactly right. You also hear the scalar origin in てもいい permission and the lexicalized drift in いつも.

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• は (topic marker) — the particle も often replaces
  • が (subject marker) — the particle も often replaces
  • を (object marker) — the particle も often replaces
  • に / で / から — particles that stack with も

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• でも (sentence-initial) — "but" — unrelated conjunction
  • さえ / すら — "even" — more extreme and often sharper than も
  • だけ — "only" — exclusive opposite of additive も
  • しか〜ない — "only" — negative-frame exclusive
  • とても — "very" — adverb, unrelated to conditional ても

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• は・が・を (N5) — the core particles も replaces
  • て-form (N4) — required for conditional も
  • Wh-words (N5) — 何なん, 誰だれ, どこ, etc.
  • Negative verbs (N5) — required for universal negative も

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• さえ / すら (N3) — sharper extremes
  • 〜も〜ば〜も (N2) — correlative "both… and… / neither… nor…"
  • 〜ても仕方しかたがない (N3) — "even if… it can't be helped"
  • 〜なくてもいい (N4) — negative permission; "don't have to"
  • 何なんでも (N4) — affirmative universal; "anything / everything"

docs.title: なる + ている — The Resultative State of Becoming
〜ていてくれる — Sustained Action for My Benefit
N3
docs.text: Verb Forms and Conjugation Chains

docs.title: なる + ている — The Resultative State of Becoming
なる as a Change Verb — The Full Picture
〜くなる / 〜になる + ている — "Has Become" and "Is Now"
Relationship to Main Page
docs.text:
This page extends: 〜ている for Resultant States

This supplementary page deepens the treatment of なる as a change verb introduced in the main resultative 〜ている page. Where the main page covers the broader distinction between progressive and resultative 〜ている, this page focuses exclusively on なる — its grammar, its combinations with different word classes, and the specific resultative meanings that arise when 〜ている attaches to it.

Read the main page first if you haven't already. This page assumes you understand the core distinction between progressive 〜ている (action verbs) and resultative 〜ている (change verbs).

docs.title: Why なる Deserves Its Own Page
docs.text:
なる is the single most productive change verb in Japanese. It combines with:

  • Every i-adjective (暗くらくなる, 暖あたたかくなる, 大おおきくなる…)
  • Every na-adjective (静しずかになる, 元気げんきになる, 有名ゆうめいになる…)
  • Every noun (医者いしゃになる, 春はるになる, 問題もんだいになる…)
  • Many verb forms (〜ことになる, 〜ようになる, 〜なくなる…)

Each of these combinations can take 〜ている to describe the resulting state of that becoming. The pattern is infinitely productive and appears constantly in everyday speech. Mastering なる + ている unlocks a huge portion of natural Japanese expression.

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • なる means "to become" — it describes a change from one state to another.
  • 〜くなる attaches to i-adjectives (adverbial 〜く form + なる).
  • 〜になる attaches to na-adjectives and nouns (に particle + なる).
  • When 〜ている attaches to any なる construction, the meaning is resultative: "has become X and is now in that state."
  • The contracted form 〜てる is standard in casual speech: 暗くらくなってる, 医者いしゃになってる, etc.
  • 〜ていた describes a past resulting state: "had become X / was X (at that past reference time)."

docs.title: The Grammar — How なる Combines
Base Patterns
docs.text: Source Word Change Form Meaning 〜ている Form Resultative Meaning i-adjective 〜くなる To become [adj] 〜くなっている Has become [adj] / is [adj] na-adjective 〜になる To become [adj] 〜になっている Has become [adj] / is [adj] Noun 〜になる To become [noun] 〜になっている Has become [noun] / is now [noun]

docs.title: i-adjectives → 〜くなる
docs.text:
Remove the final い and add く + なる.

i-adjective Stem + く + なる + ている Meaning 暗くらい (dark) 暗あんく 暗くらくなる 暗くらくなっている Has become dark / is dark 暖あたたかい (warm) 暖あたたかく 暖あたたかくなる 暖あたたかくなっている Has warmed up / is warm 大おおきい (big) 大おおきく 大おおきくなる 大おおきくなっている Has grown / is big (now) 良よい (good) 良りょうく 良よくなる 良よくなっている Has improved / is better 寒さむい (cold) 寒かんく 寒さむくなる 寒さむくなっている Has gotten cold / is cold 早はやい (early/fast) 早はやく 早はやくなる 早はやくなっている Has gotten earlier/faster

docs.title: na-adjectives → 〜になる
docs.text:
Remove だ (or use the base stem) and add に + なる.

na-adjective Stem + に + なる + ている Meaning 静しずかだ (quiet) 静しずかに 静しずかになる 静しずかになっている Has become quiet / is quiet 元気げんきだ (well/energetic) 元気げんきに 元気げんきになる 元気げんきになっている Has recovered / is well 有名ゆうめいだ (famous) 有名ゆうめいに 有名ゆうめいになる 有名ゆうめいになっている Has become famous / is famous 好すきだ (liked) 好すきに 好すきになる 好すきになっている Has come to like / is in love 便利べんりだ (convenient) 便利べんりに 便利べんりになる 便利べんりになっている Has become convenient / is convenient 大変たいへんだ (tough) 大変たいへんに 大変たいへんになる 大変たいへんになっている Has gotten tough / is difficult

Remember to drop だ

The dictionary form of na-adjectives typically ends in だ (静しずかだ, 元気げんきだ), but になる attaches to the stem without だ. 静しずかだ → 静しずかになる, not 静しずかだになる.

docs.title: Nouns → 〜になる
docs.text:
Nouns use the same に + なる pattern as na-adjectives.

Noun + に + なる + ている Meaning 医者いしゃ (doctor) 医者いしゃになる 医者いしゃになっている Has become a doctor / is now a doctor 春はる (spring) 春はるになる 春はるになっている Has become spring / it's now spring 夜よる (night) 夜よるになる 夜よるになっている Has become night / it's night now 問題もんだい (problem) 問題もんだいになる 問題もんだいになっている Has become a problem / is an issue 大人おとな (adult) 大人おとなになる 大人おとなになっている Has grown up / is an adult now 話題わだい (hot topic) 話題わだいになる 話題わだいになっている Has become a trending topic / is being talked about

docs.title: The Meaning Spectrum — なる vs. なっている
docs.text: The core distinction is temporal: the change itself vs. the post-change state. But the nuance shifts slightly depending on the word class.

docs.title: i-adjectives: State Arrival
docs.text:
〜くなる is the transition. 〜くなっている is the arrival in that state, with the state persisting.

Form Example Meaning 暗くらくなる もうすぐ暗くなる。 "It will get dark soon." The darkening is about to happen. 暗くらくなっている もう暗くなっている。 "It's already dark." The darkening happened; the darkness is here. 暗くらくなっていた 帰る頃ころには暗くらくなっていた。 "By the time I went home, it had gotten dark." Past reference time; darkness was already in effect.

docs.title: na-adjectives: Condition Onset vs. Condition in Effect
docs.text: Form Example Meaning 静しずかになる 夜は静かになる。 "It gets quiet at night." General statement about the change. 静しずかになっている もう静かになっている。 "It's already quiet." The quietness is the current state. 静しずかになっていた 図書館はもう静かになっていた。 "The library had already gotten quiet." Past state.

docs.title: Nouns: Identity Change vs. Current Identity
docs.text:
With nouns, 〜になっている often carries a nuance of "has ended up being" — the change may be unexpected, noteworthy, or the result of a process.

Form Example Meaning 医者いしゃになる 将来医者になりたい。 "I want to become a doctor in the future." The aspiration. 医者いしゃになっている 気づいたら医者になっていた。 "Before I knew it, I'd become a doctor." The result; maybe unexpected or just a reflection. 問題もんだいになる それが問題になるかも。 "That might become a problem." The change might occur. 問題もんだいになっている それはもう問題になっている。 "That's already become a problem / That is already an issue." The problematic state is in effect.

話題わだいになっている — a fixed expression

話題わだいになっている is a standard phrase meaning "has become a topic of conversation / is being talked about / is trending." It almost always appears in the 〜ている form because it describes a current state of being in the public conversation. 話題わだいになる exists but is less common — it would describe the moment something starts trending, which is harder to pinpoint.

docs.title: The Temporal Logic — Visualised
docs.text:
Every なる + ている sentence implies the same timeline structure: Timeline: ──────────────────────────────────────────→

[State A] ──[Change]──→ [State B persists] ──→ ↑ ↑ ↑ (before) なる なっている (the (the post-change transition) state, ongoing)

  • 前まえに (before the change): The world was in State A (明あかるい / 静しずかじゃない / 学生がくせい, etc.).
  • なる (the change): The transition from A to B occurs at a moment or over an interval.
  • なっている (the post-change state): State B is now the reality. It began at the moment of change and continues.

When you use なっている, you are placing the listener's attention after the change, in the world where State B is already the condition.

docs.title: Extended Examples — なる + ている in Context
i-adjective + なっている
docs.text: Japanese English 暖あたたかくなってるから、コートいらないかも。 It's warmed up, so you might not need a coat. The warming already happened. The current state is warm. 彼かれ、前まえより大おおきくなってるね。 He's gotten bigger than before, hasn't he? The growth happened over time; the new larger state is what the speaker observes now. 最近さいきん、物価ぶっかが高たかくなってる。 Prices have been getting higher lately. The change is ongoing and the resulting high-price state is in effect. 前まえより日本語にほんごが上手じょうずくなってる！ Your Japanese has gotten better than before! Not "is getting better" — the improvement is observed as an accomplished state.

docs.title: na-adjective + なっている
docs.text: Japanese English 薬くすりが効きいて、楽らくになってる。 The medicine kicked in — I'm feeling better. 楽らくだ (comfortable / at ease) → 楽らくになる (to become comfortable) → 楽らくになっている (is in a comfortable state). 町まちがきれいになってるね。掃除そうじしたのかな。 The town has gotten clean, hasn't it? I wonder if someone cleaned. The change (cleaning) happened; the speaker observes the resulting clean state. 彼女かのじょ、最近さいきんきれいになってるよね。 She's gotten prettier lately, hasn't she? きれいになっている — the speaker observes a current state that is the result of a change. 説明せつめいを聞きいて、事情じじょうが複雑ふくざつになっていることがわかった。 After hearing the explanation, I understood that the situation had become complicated. The complication happened before the understanding. なっている marks the state at the time of discovery.

docs.title: Noun + なっている
docs.text: Japanese English 気きがついたら外そとは暗くらくなっていて、もう夜よるになっていた。 Before I realised, it had gotten dark outside — it had already become night. 夜よるになっていた — past resultative. At the moment of noticing, night was already the state in effect. 彼かれはいつの間まにか有名人ゆうめいじんになっている。 He's become a celebrity before anyone knew it. The change is complete; the celebrity state is current. その店みせはもう閉店へいてんになっているよ。 That shop has already closed down / is already out of business. 閉店へいてんになる (to close down, of a business) — the change happened; the closed state is in effect. 気候変動きこうへんどうはもう深刻しんこくな問題もんだいになっている。 Climate change has already become a serious problem. The transition to "serious problem" status has occurred; that status is current.

docs.title: Special Patterns — なる with Verb Phrases
docs.text: なる also combines with verb-derived structures, creating idiomatic patterns that frequently appear with 〜ている.

docs.title: 〜ようになる — "Come to Do / Reach the Point of Doing"
docs.text:
Describes a change in ability, habit, or circumstance — reaching a state where something happens.

Form Example Meaning 〜ようになる 日本語にほんごが話はなせるようになる To reach the point of being able to speak Japanese 〜ようになっている 日本語にほんごが話はなせるようになっている Has reached the point of being able to speak Japanese / can now speak Japanese 最近さいきん、早はやく起おきられるようになっている。 Lately, I've gotten to the point where I can wake up early. The change (from can't to can) has occurred; the ability state is current.

docs.title: 〜ことになる — "It Has Been Decided That / Things Have Worked Out So That"
docs.text:
Describes a decision or outcome — often external, beyond the speaker's control.

Form Example Meaning 〜ことになる 来月らいげつ転勤てんきんすることになる It is decided that I will be transferred next month 〜ことになっている 来月らいげつ転勤てんきんすることになっている It has been decided that I'm being transferred next month / The arrangement is that I'll be transferred 会議かいぎは来週らいしゅうの月曜げつように行おこなわれることになっている。 It has been decided that the meeting will be held next Monday. The decision was made earlier. The arrangement is the current state. 〜ことになっている is the standard way to state an existing arrangement.

docs.title: 〜なくなる — "To Stop Doing / To No Longer Be"
docs.text:
The negative form of なる constructions. Describes the transition into a state of absence or cessation.

Source Change 〜ている Meaning i-adj: 寒さむい 寒さむくなる (become cold) 寒さむくなっている Is cold Negative: 寒さむくない 寒さむくなくなる (stop being cold) 寒さむくなくなっている Has stopped being cold / is no longer cold Verb: 食たべる 食たべなくなる (stop eating) 食たべなくなっている Has stopped eating / no longer eats 彼かれは最近さいきんお酒さけを飲のまなくなっている。 He's stopped drinking alcohol recently. The change (cessation) happened; the non-drinking state persists.

docs.title: なっていた — Past Resultative State
docs.text:
The past form 〜ていた places the resulting state at a past reference time — the world at that past moment already had the change completed.

Form Temporal Meaning Example なっている Change completed; state in effect now もう暗くなっている。 "It's already dark now." なっていた Change completed before a past reference time; state was in effect then 着いたときにはもう暗くなっていた。 "When I arrived, it was already dark." Japanese English 気きがついたときには、もう手遅ておくれになっていた。 By the time I noticed, it had already become too late. The becoming-too-late happened before the noticing. なっていた places the "too late" state in the past, prior to the noticing event. 彼かれが着ついたとき、パーティーはもう終おわっていた。 When he arrived, the party had already ended. (Not a なる sentence, but the same resultative logic: the ending preceded the arrival. 終おわっている is resultative; 終おわっていた is past resultative.)

docs.title: Real Speech vs. Textbook Form
docs.text:
In casual spoken Japanese, 〜ている regularly contracts. This applies to all なる constructions.

Full Form Contracted Meaning 暗くらくなっている 暗くらくなってる Has become dark / is dark 静しずかになっている 静しずかになってる Has become quiet / is quiet 医者いしゃになっている 医者いしゃになってる Has become a doctor / is a doctor 〜ようになっている 〜ようになってる Has come to be the case that… 〜ことになっている 〜ことになってる It has been arranged that…

The contraction is the default in casual conversation. The full form sounds slightly more deliberate, explanatory, or emphatic. Learners should recognise both and produce the contracted form in casual settings.

docs.title: 1. Using plain form when the state is in effect
docs.text:
Incorrect

もう暗くらくなるから、電気でんきをつけよう。

~"It's going to get dark soon, so let's turn on the lights." — 暗くらくなる is the change about to happen. But if it's already dark…~

Correct for current state

もう暗くらくなってるから、電気でんきをつけよう。

~"It's already dark, so let's turn on the lights." — 暗くらくなっている describes the current state.~

docs.title: 2. Using になっている for future arrangements that aren't 〜ことになっている
docs.text:
Incorrect

明日あしたは雨あめになっている。

~"Tomorrow has become rain / is in a state of rain." Weird. The change hasn't happened yet; you're making a prediction, not describing a current state.~

Correct

明日あしたは雨あめになるだろう / なりそうだ。

~"Tomorrow will probably become rain / looks like it'll rain." Use なる (change form) for future predictions, not なっている.~

Exception: 〜ことになっている

明日あす雨あめが降ふることになっている is fine — it means "it has been arranged / it is forecast that it will rain tomorrow." The ことになっている is an existing arrangement or determination; the rain itself is not yet a state.

docs.title: 3. Confusing 〜くなる and 〜になる
docs.text:
Incorrect

暖あたたかいになる。

~暖あたたかい is an i-adjective. It takes 〜くなる, not 〜になる.~

Correct

暖あたたかくなる。

~i-adjective adverbial form is 〜く. 暖あたたかい → 暖あたたかく + なる.~

Incorrect

静しずかくなる。

~静しずか is a na-adjective. It takes 〜になる, not 〜くなる.~

Correct

静しずかになる。

~na-adjective uses に + なる. 静しずか + に + なる.~

docs.title: Japanese Mindset — State Orientation
docs.text:
Japanese has a strong preference for describing the world in terms of current states that resulted from prior changes. The なる + ている pattern is one of the most natural expressions of this tendency.

An English speaker might say "It's dark," framing the darkness as a simple property of the present. A Japanese speaker saying 暗くらくなっている embeds the same observation in a timeline — the darkness is the outcome of a change. Both describe the same reality, but the Japanese version carries an implicit awareness of how things got this way.

This becomes especially relevant with nouns and social statuses. 医者いしゃになっている ("has become a doctor / is now a doctor") frames the profession as the result of a process — study, training, qualification. The identity is not just a label; it's an outcome. Similarly, 問題もんだいになっている ("has become a problem") frames the problem status as something that arose — it wasn't always there; it came into being.

  • Change is acknowledged, even when only the state matters. The なる in なっている quietly references the transition, even when the speaker's focus is on the present.
  • States are temporary, even when they seem permanent. 医者いしゃになっている implies a before (not a doctor) and potentially an after (retirement, career change). The 〜ている keeps the state anchored in time rather than treating it as an eternal property.
  • なっている is the natural choice for observations. When you notice something has changed — the room is quiet, the lights are on, someone has grown — なっている is the form that registers the change as completed and the new state as the reality.

docs.title: Quick Reference — All なる + ている Patterns
docs.text: Source Change Form Resultative Form Meaning i-adj 〜くなる 〜くなっている Has become [adj] / is [adj] na-adj 〜になる 〜になっている Has become [adj] / is [adj] Noun 〜になる 〜になっている Has become [noun] / is now [noun] Verb (ability) 〜ようになる 〜ようになっている Has come to be able to / now does Verb (arrangement) 〜ことになる 〜ことになっている Has been decided that / is arranged that Negative (i-adj) 〜くなくなる 〜くなくなっている Has stopped being [adj] Negative (verb) 〜なくなる 〜なくなっている Has stopped doing / no longer does Past state — 〜くなっていた / 〜になっていた Had become / was (at a past time) Casual contraction — 〜てる All of the above, in casual speech

docs.title: Main Page
docs.text: • 〜ている for Resultant States — the core distinction between progressive and resultative 〜ている

docs.title: Related Patterns
docs.text:
• 〜てある — resultative with an agent: "has been deliberately put in X state" (contrast with spontaneous なっている)
  • 〜ことになる vs. 〜ことにする — external decision vs. personal decision
  • 〜ようになる vs. 〜ようにする — natural change vs. deliberate effort

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• i-adjective 〜く form (N5) — the adverbial form that feeds 〜くなる
  • na-adjective + に (N5) — the adverbial form that feeds 〜になる
  • なる (N5) — the base change verb
  • 〜ている (N4) — the aspect form that creates the resultative meaning

docs.title: 〜ている - Resultative Change
docs.text: The change (暗くらくなる) occurs before the reference time (着つくころ). The 〜ている form — 暗くらくなってる — expresses that at the reference time, the post-change state is the reality. The speaker is making a prediction about what state will be in effect at a future reference point.

docs.title: Change Verbs — The Core Set
docs.text:
Verbs that take the resultative 〜ている meaning typically describe instantaneous changes — things that happen at a moment, not over a duration. Once the change occurs, the new state continues.

Verb Meaning (Change) 〜ている Meaning (Resultative State) 死しぬ To die 死しんでいる Is dead (not "is dying") 結婚けっこんする To get married 結婚けっこんしている Is married (not "is getting married") 開あく To open 開あいている Is open (not "is opening") 閉しまる To close 閉しまっている Is closed (not "is closing") 落おちる To fall 落おちている Has fallen / is on the ground 消きえる To disappear 消きえている Has disappeared / is gone 汚よごれる To get dirty 汚よごれている Is dirty (not "is getting dirty") 乾かわく To dry 乾かわいている Is dry (not "is drying") 沸わく To boil 沸わいている Is boiling / has boiled (the water is hot)

The literal translation trap

English speakers often want to translate 死しんでいる as "is dying." That is almost always wrong. "Is dying" describes an ongoing process — the person is still alive, approaching death. 死しんでいる means the person is already dead — the change is complete, the state is in effect. The English "is dead" captures it; "has died" also works. "Is dying" does not.

docs.title: Adjective + なる as a Change Verb
docs.text:
The pattern 〜くなる (i-adjective adverbial form + なる) and 〜になる (na-adjective/noun + になる) function exactly like change verbs. The change is the transition into the state; 〜ている describes being in that state.

Base Meaning Change Form 〜ている Resultative Meaning 暗くらい Dark 暗くらくなる 暗くらくなっている Is dark (has become dark) 暖あたたかい Warm 暖あたたかくなる 暖あたたかくなっている Is warm (has warmed up) 静しずかだ Quiet 静しずかになる 静しずかになっている Is quiet (has become quiet) 元気げんきだ Well / energetic 元気げんきになる 元気げんきになっている Is well (has recovered)

docs.title: Progressive vs. Resultative — Side-by-Side
docs.text:
The same 〜ている form means completely different things depending on the verb type. Here is the contrast:

Verb Verb Type 〜ている Meaning 食たべる Action 食たべている Is eating (action in progress) 走はしる Action 走はしっている Is running (action in progress) 死しぬ Change 死しんでいる Is dead (state resulting from completed change) 開あく Change 開あいている Is open (state resulting from completed change) 着きる Change (punctual) 着きている Is wearing (state resulting from having put on) 知しる Change (punctual) 知しっている Knows (state resulting from having learned)

着きている and 知しっている are resultative — don't read them as progressive

Two especially common traps: - 着きている means "is wearing" (the state after putting clothes on), not "is putting on clothes" (which would be 着きている in some contexts, but usually 着替きがえている or similar). - 知しっている means "knows" (the state after learning), not "is knowing."
