The verb 知しる means "to come to know / to learn" — a punctual change. 知しっている is the post-change state: "to have learned and therefore know."

docs.title: Examples — Resultative 〜ている in Context
docs.text: Japanese English 窓まどが開あいているから、ちょっと寒さむいね。 The window is open, so it's a bit cold, isn't it. Literal: Window opened-state-is because, a-little cold, right? ~Context: The opening happened earlier. The speaker finds the resulting open state and comments on it.~ 彼かれはもう結婚けっこんしているよ。 He's already married. Literal: He already married-state-is. ~Context: The wedding happened in the past. The married state continues.~ 道みちが混こんでるから、少すこし遅おくれるかも。 The roads are congested, so I might be a bit late. Literal: Road congested-state-is because, a-little late-might. ~Context: 混こむ (to become congested) is a change. 混こんでいる describes the post-change congested state.~ もう暗くらくなってるから、気きをつけて帰かえってね。 It's already dark out, so be careful going home. Literal: Already dark-become-state-is because, careful go-home. ~Context: Same pattern as the reference sentence. The darkening happened earlier; the darkness is the present state.~ 財布さいふが落おちてるよ。 Your wallet has fallen — it's on the ground. Literal: Wallet fallen-state-is, you-know. ~Context: The falling happened earlier. The speaker finds the wallet in its fallen state.~

docs.title: The Contraction — 〜ている → 〜てる
docs.text:
In casual spoken Japanese, 〜ている regularly contracts to 〜てる. This is not slang or substandard — it is the default pronunciation in everyday conversation.

Full Form Contracted Meaning 暗くらくなっている 暗くらくなってる Has become dark / is dark 開あいている 開あいてる Is open 結婚けっこんしている 結婚けっこんしてる Is married 知しっている 知しってる Knows

The contraction involves dropping the い after the te-form. For godan verbs, the te-form ending contracts:

  • 〜んでいる → 〜んでる （死しんでいる → 死しんでる）
  • 〜っている → 〜ってる （入はいっている → 入はいってる）
  • 〜いている → 〜いてる （開あいている → 開あいてる）

The reference sentence uses the contracted form

着つくころには暗くらくなってるかも。The 暗くらくなってる is the contracted form of 暗くらくなっている. In casual speech, the full form 暗くらくなっている would sound slightly stiff or deliberate. The contraction is the natural choice between friends, family, or in relaxed self-talk.

docs.title: 1. Reading resultative 〜ている as progressive
docs.text:
The single most common error. Learners apply the "is doing" meaning to every 〜ている they see, including change verbs where that reading makes no sense.

Incorrect reading

もう死しんでいる。

~"He is dying." — Wrong. The person is already dead. "Is dying" would describe an ongoing process; 死しんでいる describes a completed state.~

Correct reading

"He is dead." / "He has died."

~The change (death) occurred. The state (being dead) is in effect. Resultative 〜ている.~

docs.title: 2. Using plain form where 〜ている is needed
docs.text:
Using the dictionary form of a change verb when you mean the resulting state.

Incorrect

もう暗くらくなるから、帰かえろう。

~"It's going to get dark soon, so let's go home." — 暗くらくなる describes the change about to happen. Grammatically fine on its own, but...~

Correct (if the intended meaning is state)

もう暗くらくなってるから、帰かえろう。

~"It's already dark, so let's go home." — 暗くらくなっている describes the current state. The darkness is already here.~

docs.title: 3. Using 〜ている with stative verbs
docs.text:
Verbs like ある (to exist), いる (to exist, animate), and できる (to be able) describe states, not actions or changes. They rarely appear with 〜ている because the state is already built into the verb.

Ungrammatical

そこにあっている。

~"It is existing there." — ある is already a state. Adding 〜ている is redundant and ungrammatical.~

Correct

そこにある。

~"It is there." Plain form for stative verbs.~

docs.title: Japanese Mindset — States Over Actions
docs.text:
Japanese has a strong preference for describing the world in terms of states rather than actions. The resultative 〜ている is one of the clearest expressions of this tendency.

In English, "It's dark" is a simple adjective construction — the darkness is a property, not an outcome. In Japanese, 暗くらくなっている embeds the same observation in a temporal logic: it became dark, and that darkness is now the state of the world. The change is acknowledged even when only the state matters.

This is not just grammar — it reflects a deeper cognitive framing. Where English often foregrounds the present condition, Japanese frequently frames the condition as the result of a prior change. The 〜ている form is the grammatical tool that makes this framing possible.

  • State as outcome. 結婚けっこんしている means "is married" — the state is the outcome of a wedding. 死しんでいる means "is dead" — the state is the outcome of dying. The change is always implied, even when not mentioned.
  • Temporal precision. The distinction between 暗くらくなる (the change) and 暗くらくなっている (the state) lets Japanese speakers place events precisely in time relative to each other. In the reference sentence, this distinction is critical: the darkening precedes the arrival. The 〜ている is what establishes that sequence.
  • Continuation of state. The 〜ている in both progressive and resultative uses shares a core meaning: the situation described by the verb stem has continuing relevance. For action verbs, the action continues. For change verbs, the resulting state continues. The form is unified around this idea of ongoing validity.

docs.title: Distinguishing Progressive from Resultative — A Practical Guide
docs.text:
When you encounter an unfamiliar verb with 〜ている, ask:

  1. Is the verb an action that takes time? (食たべる、走はしる、読よむ、書かく) → Progressive. "Is doing."
  2. Is the verb a change that happens at a moment? (死しぬ、開ひらく、落おちる、結婚けっこんする) → Resultative. "Has become / is in the resulting state."
  3. Is the verb an adjective + なる? (暗くらくなる、暖あたたかくなる、静しずかになる) → Resultative. "Has become / is in that state."
  4. Is the verb already a state? (ある、いる、できる) → Plain form is used; 〜ている is usually absent or means something else.
Test Progressive Resultative "Is [verb]ing" makes sense in English? Yes No (or sounds weird) "Has [verb]ed" or "is [adjective]" fits better? No Yes The verb describes a process or a moment? Process (takes time) Moment (instantaneous change)

docs.title: Same Grammar Family
docs.text:
• 〜ている (progressive) — "is doing" for action verbs
  • 〜てある — resultative with an agent implied; "has been done deliberately" (contrast with 〜ている for change verbs)
  • 〜ていた — past state; "was in the state of having become X"
  • 〜てしまう — regrettable or complete action; often combines with 〜ている for "has unfortunately ended up in X state"

docs.title: Related Patterns
docs.text:
• 〜くなる / 〜になる — the change form that feeds into resultative 〜ている
  • 〜ことになる — "it has been decided that" — a resultative use of なる
  • 〜ているところだ — "is just in the middle of doing" — emphasises the progressive over the resultative reading

docs.title: Contrast With
docs.text:
• Plain form vs. 〜ている for change verbs — the core distinction this page explains
  • 〜た (past) vs. 〜ている — past describes the change event; 〜ている describes the resulting state. 死しんだ = "died" (the event). 死しんでいる = "is dead" (the state).

docs.title: 〜ていてくれる — Sustained Action for My Benefit
〜ていてくれる — Sustained Action for My Benefit
帰かえりが…遅おそくなっても、待まっていてくれる？
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — Mid N3

The individual components (〜てくれる, 〜ている) are N4 grammar. But the layered combination — te-form of progressive + te-form of giving — creates a sustained-benefit construction that sits at the N3 boundary. The pragmatic nuance of "will you keep doing this for me over time?" is rarely taught explicitly at N4.

Tier Pattern Description Example N4 — Foundation 〜てくれる Someone does something for me (giving frame) 待まってくれる？"Will you wait for me?" N4 — Foundation 〜ている Progressive / ongoing state 待まっている。"I am waiting." N3 — Core ← You are here 〜ていてくれる Sustained action performed for my benefit — "keep doing X for me" 待まっていてくれる？"Will you keep waiting for me?" N3 — Extended 〜ていてくれる + conditional Sustained benefit under hypothetical or counterfactual conditions 遅おそくなっても、待まっていてくれる？ N2 — Awareness 〜ていてくれた / 〜ていてくれなかった Past sustained benefit realized or denied いつも応援おうえんしていてくれた。"You were always supporting me."

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 〜ていてくれる is a double-te construction: 待まつ → 待まって (te-form) → 待まっている (progressive) → 待まっていて (te-form of progressive) → 待まっていてくれる (giving frame applied to sustained action).
  • The core question: Why not just 待まってくれる？ Because 待まってくれる asks "Will you wait [the action]?" while 待まっていてくれる asks "Will you keep waiting [sustained over time]?" The difference is between a discrete favor and an ongoing commitment.
  • In the input sentence, the 〜ていて layer is essential because the condition (帰かえりが遅おそくなっても, "even if I'm late") implies an extended duration. A single moment of waiting isn't enough — the listener must sustain the action through uncertainty.
  • The form carries emotional weight: it asks someone to maintain a state of care on your behalf while you are absent or delayed. This is vulnerability packaged in grammar.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Use 〜ていてくれる when you want to ask (or express gratitude for) a sustained, ongoing action performed for your benefit — not a single moment of help, but a continued state maintained on your behalf.

待まってくれる？ Will you wait [for me]? Literal: Wait [te-form] give [question]? Context: Asking someone to perform the action of waiting. The waiting is a discrete event — perhaps brief, perhaps until you arrive. The focus is on the action itself. 待まっていてくれる？ Will you keep waiting [for me]? / Will you be waiting [for me]? Literal: Wait [progressive] [te-form] give [question]? Context: Asking someone to maintain a state of waiting over time. The waiting is sustained — through delay, through uncertainty, through your absence. The focus is on the continued state. 帰かえりが…遅おそくなっても、待まっていてくれる？ Even if I'm… late getting back, will you keep waiting for me? Literal: Return [nominalized]… late become-even-if, wait [sustained] [te-form] give [question]? Context: The input sentence. The 遅おそくなっても establishes a hypothetical extended duration. The 〜ていて layer is the grammatical response to that duration — it asks for sustained care through uncertain time.

docs.title: Why Not Just 待まってくれる？
docs.text:
This is the heart of the grammar point. The difference between 待まってくれる and 待まっていてくれる is not merely "more polite" or "longer form" — it is a fundamentally different request with different emotional and temporal geometry.

待まってくれる 待まっていてくれる Temporal scope Discrete, bounded Sustained, open-ended Action type Single performance Continued state Implied condition None — just do this Often paired with "even if…" or "while…" Emotional weight Gratitude for a favor Vulnerability, trust, dependence Speaker's position Beneficiary of an action Beneficiary of someone's sustained attention Listener's burden Perform an action Maintain a state on speaker's behalf

docs.title: The Temporal Dimension
docs.text:
待まってくれる asks for a point: the moment of waiting. 待まっていてくれる asks for a line: the duration of waiting.

駅えきで待まってくれる？ Will you wait at the station [for me to arrive]? The waiting has a clear endpoint: your arrival. Once you arrive, the waiting is complete. 遅おそくなるかもしれないけど、待まっていてくれる？ I might be late, but will you keep waiting [through the uncertainty]? The waiting has no clear endpoint. The listener must sustain the state through unknown duration. The 〜ていて is the grammatical acknowledgment of that burden.

docs.title: The Emotional Dimension
docs.text:
待まってくれる is a request for help. 待まっていてくれる is a request for emotional presence — asking someone to hold space for you while you are absent or delayed.

The input sentence dramatizes this: 帰かえりが…遅おそくなっても (the ellipsis before 遅おそく suggests hesitation, apology, vulnerability) frames the speaker as unreliable, as someone who might fail to return on time. In this context, asking 待まってくれる would be inadequate — it asks for a simple favor without acknowledging the strain. 待まっていてくれる asks for sustained commitment despite that strain.

docs.title: In Real Life
docs.text:
In Real Life

Situation: ハルカ is texting her partner ユウタ. She's at a work dinner that's running long. The last train is approaching, and she doesn't know when she'll be able to leave. She types carefully, aware that she's asking for patience she may not deserve.

帰かえりが…遅おそくなっても、待まっていてくれる？

"Even if I'm… late getting back, will you keep waiting for me?"

Why 〜ていてくれる fits here: Haruka could have said 待まってくれる — shorter, simpler, less emotionally exposed. But that would frame the waiting as a simple favor: "Will you wait?" The 遅おそくなっても condition makes that framing insufficient — she's not asking for a moment of patience; she's asking for sustained patience through uncertainty.

What the 〜ていて layer adds: It transforms the request from "perform an action for me" to "maintain a state for me." The progressive 〜ている encodes ongoingness; the second て connects that ongoingness to the giving frame くれる. The result is a request for emotional continuity — "keep holding space for me even while I'm failing to return."

What the ellipsis suggests: 帰かえりが…遅おそくなっても — the pause before 遅おそく is the sound of someone hesitating to admit fault. The speaker knows she's imposing. The 〜ていてくれる form is her grammatical apology — it acknowledges the burden by making the request itself heavier.

What makes this natural: The combination of conditional (ても), progressive (ている), and giving (くれる) creates a three-layer emotional architecture: [even if this happens] + [keep doing this] + [for my benefit]. This is how Japanese packages complex emotional requests — not through single words, but through grammatical layering.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
〜ていてくれる is a double-te construction built from two te-forms stacked sequentially. Understanding the stacking is essential to producing and parsing the form.

Layer 1 — 待まつ → 待まって (first te-form): The verb 待まつ (to wait) takes its te-form 待まって. This is the standard connective form.

Layer 2 — 待まって → 待まっている (progressive): The first te-form connects to いる, creating the progressive aspect. 待まっている = "is waiting / keeps waiting." This encodes ongoingness and duration.

Layer 3 — 待まっている → 待まっていて (second te-form): The progressive form 待まっている takes its own te-form: 待まっていて. This is the connective form of the progressive, allowing it to connect to another verb.

Layer 4 — 待まっていて → 待まっていてくれる (giving frame): The second te-form connects to くれる, the giving verb that indicates action performed for the speaker's benefit. The full stack: "waiting [ongoing] [connective] give [to me]."

Step Form Function Base verb 待まつ "to wait" Te-form 1 待まって Connective; enables progressive Progressive 待まっている "is waiting" — encodes duration Te-form 2 待まっていて Connective of progressive; enables giving frame Giving frame 待まっていてくれる "keeps waiting for me" — sustained benefit

The double-te is not optional

Learners sometimes try to skip the second te-form: 待まっているくれる. This is ungrammatical — the giving frame くれる requires the te-form connective. The progressive 待まっている cannot directly attach to くれる; it must first take its own te-form 待まっていて.

docs.title: Real Speech vs. Textbook Form
docs.text:
Textbooks teach 〜てくれる and 〜ている separately. Real speech stacks them constantly — not just for waiting, but for any sustained benefit.

Form Example Note Textbook (separate) 待まってくれる？ + 待まっている Taught as distinct patterns; learners must assemble them mentally Real speech (stacked) 待まっていてくれる？ The natural form for sustained requests Real speech (gratitude) いつも応援おうえんしていてくれてありがとう "Thanks for always supporting me [sustained]" — stacked in gratitude expressions Real speech (past) 待まっていてくれた "You kept waiting for me [and you did]" — past realization of sustained benefit Real speech (negative) 待まっていてくれなかった "You didn't keep waiting for me" — past denial of sustained benefit

docs.title: Function in Context
docs.text:
• 帰かえりが nominalizes the verb 帰かえる (to return) — "the returning / my return." This creates a noun phrase that can be modified by the following condition.
  • …遅おそくなっても — the ellipsis suggests hesitation, the 遅おそくなって is the te-form of 遅おそくなる (to become late), and も adds "even if." The full condition: "even if [my return] becomes late." This establishes the hypothetical extended duration that demands sustained waiting.
  • 待まっていてくれる is the core request. The 待まっていて is the te-form of the progressive; the くれる is the giving frame. Together: "will you keep waiting [sustained] for me?"
  • The question mark (or rising intonation) softens the request into a question, but the くれる already implies the speaker is the beneficiary — it's a request that assumes a relationship of care.
  • Without 〜ていて: 帰かえりが遅おそくなっても、待まってくれる？ would ask "Even if I'm late, will you wait?" — a simpler request that doesn't acknowledge the duration of the delay. The listener might wait briefly and then leave. With 〜ていて: the speaker asks for sustained presence through the entire uncertain period.
  • Without くれる: 帰かえりが遅おそくなっても、待まっていて？ would be grammatically incomplete — the te-form 待まっていて requires connection to something (ください, くれる, もらえる). The くれる provides the benefactive frame that makes the request personal and relational.
仕事しごとで忙いそがしくても、覚おぼえていてくれる？ Even if work gets busy, will you keep me in mind [sustained attention]? Literal: Work-by busy even-if, remember [sustained] [te-form] give [question]? Context: Asking someone to maintain emotional memory through their own difficult periods. The 覚おぼえていて layer is essential — "keep holding this in your mind" rather than "remember this fact."

docs.title: Construction
docs.text:
Pattern Formation Example Godan verb stem + いてくれる 待まつ → 待まって → 待まっている → 待まっていてくれる Ichidan verb drop る + いてくれる 食たべる → 食たべて → 食たべている → 食たべていてくれる する して → していてくれる 勉強べんきょうする → 勉強べんきょうして → 勉強べんきょうしていてくれる くる きて → きていてくれる 手伝てつだってくる → 手伝てつだってきて → 手伝てつだってきていてくれる

The いる contraction trap

In very casual speech, 〜ている contracts to 〜てる: 待まってる. The double-te then becomes 待まっててくれる (with いる fully dropped). This is common but slightly more casual than 待まっていてくれる.

Register Form Example Standard casual 〜ていてくれる 待まっていてくれる？ Very casual (contracted) 〜ててくれる 待まっててくれる？ Polite 〜ていてくれますか 待まっていてくれますか？ Formal request 〜ていてください 待まっていてください

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
Japanese relational grammar encodes sustained attention as a form of care. Asking someone to 待まっていてくれる is not merely asking for a practical favor — it is asking them to maintain an emotional state on your behalf while you are absent or failing.

This reflects the broader cultural value of 思おもい遣やり (omoi-yari / empathetic consideration) — but with a twist. The speaker is not performing consideration; they are requesting it. This creates vulnerability, and the grammar must package that vulnerability without making it burdensome.

  • Sustained states as relational glue. In Japanese, relationships are maintained through ongoing small acts of attention, not grand declarations. 待まっていてくれる asks for one such act — but sustained, through time. The grammar recognizes that brief moments are insufficient for real care.
  • The progressive as emotional commitment. 〜ている is not merely grammatical progressive aspect; in this construction, it becomes a promise of continuity. "I will keep this state alive for you."
  • Conditional vulnerability. The 〜ても condition (遅おそくなっても) exposes the speaker's potential failure — lateness, unreliability, imperfection. Asking for sustained care despite this exposure is a specific kind of intimacy. The grammar must be heavy enough to match the emotional weight.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text:
Grammar Point Core Meaning Emotional / Pragmatic Nuance Trigger Context JLPT 〜ていてくれる Sustained action for my benefit Ongoing commitment through duration; vulnerability; trust Asking for continued presence through delay or uncertainty N3 〜てくれる Single action for my benefit Discrete favor; gratitude for help Asking for or thanking for a specific action N4 〜ていてくれる vs. 〜てくれる Duration vs. moment The difference between "do this" and "keep doing this" Any context where time or uncertainty extends the need N3 〜ていてくれる vs. 〜ていてもらえる Humble vs. polite request くれる assumes intimacy; もらえる adds distance もらえますか is safer with strangers; くれる assumes closeness N4 〜ていてくれた Past sustained benefit Gratitude for realized commitment Thanking someone who did keep waiting/supporting N3 〜ていてくれなかった Past sustained benefit denied Disappointment; failed expectation When someone didn't maintain the expected state N3 〜ていてくれてありがとう Gratitude for sustained benefit Deep thanks for ongoing care Expressions of gratitude for sustained support N3

The closest lookalike is 〜てくれる. The key difference is temporal scope and emotional weight: 待まってくれる is a light request for a moment's patience; 待まっていてくれる is a heavier request for sustained presence through uncertainty. The progressive layer transforms a favor into a commitment.

docs.title: 〜てくれる (simple giving frame)
docs.text:
The lighter alternative. Same benefactive direction, none of the sustained-duration weight.

Example 待まってくれる？ English "Will you wait for me?" Intent Simple request for a discrete action vs. original Removes the duration layer. Appropriate when the waiting is brief, certain, or low-stakes. Sounds lighter and less emotionally demanding.

When to use this instead

Use 〜てくれる when the action is brief, the timeline is clear, or you want to minimize the emotional weight of your request. Use 〜ていてくれる when duration, uncertainty, or sustained presence is the point.

docs.title: 〜ていてくれますか (polite sustained request)
docs.text:
Adds ます distance to the sustained request. Appropriate when intimacy is present but formality is required.

Example 待まっていてくれますか？ English "Will you keep waiting for me [polite]?" Intent Same sustained request, delivered with polite distance vs. original The くれる → くれます shift adds social distance. The sustained duration is still requested, but the relationship is less intimate or the context requires formality.

docs.title: 〜ていてください (formal request)
docs.text:
Removes the giving frame entirely, replacing it with direct request.

Example 待まっていてください。 English "Please keep waiting." Intent Direct instruction or request; no benefactive intimacy vs. original ください is a command/request pattern, not a giving frame. It doesn't assume the listener's care; it asks for compliance. Appropriate for service contexts, less appropriate for romantic or close relationships.

docs.title: 〜ていてもらえる？ (humble sustained request)
docs.text:
Shifts the giving frame to receiving frame, adding humility.

Example 待まっていてもらえる？ English "Can I receive the favor of your keeping waiting?" Intent Humble request — the speaker frames themselves as receiving benefit rather than assuming it vs. original もらえる is more formal and less intimate than くれる. It acknowledges the listener's agency more explicitly. Appropriate when the relationship is asymmetrical or the favor is significant.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
The sustained-benefit meaning must be preserved across register shifts, though the intimacy level changes.

Casual Casual (contracted) Basic Polite Extra Polite

帰かえりが…遅おそくなっても、待まっていてくれる？

"Even if I'm… late getting back, will you keep waiting for me?"

Shift: Input sentence as-is. Casual, intimate, slightly vulnerable. The ellipsis and the くれる assume closeness.

帰かえり遅おそくなっても、待まっててくれる？

"Even if I'm late back, will you keep waiting?"

Shift: 待まっていて → 待まってて (いる dropped). Very casual, common in texting and fast speech. The contraction signals intimacy and urgency.

帰かえりが遅おそくなっても、待まっていてくれますか？

"Even if I am late returning, will you keep waiting for me?"

Shift: くれる → くれますか. The sustained request is preserved but delivered with polite distance. The ellipsis is removed; the hesitation must be packaged more formally.

帰かえりが遅おそくなる場合ばあいでも、待まっていていただけますでしょうか。

"Even in the event that my return is delayed, might we receive the favor of your continued waiting?"

Shift: 遅おそくなっても → 遅おそくなる場合ばあいでも (formal conditional); くれる → いただけますでしょうか (humble receiving frame). The emotional vulnerability is buried under layers of formality. The core meaning — sustained waiting — is preserved but the personal intimacy is erased.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: Step Pattern What It Adds Example N5 — Foundation 〜て Te-form connective 待まって。"Wait [connective]." N4 — Foundation 〜ている Progressive aspect — ongoing state 待まっている。"Is waiting." N4 — Foundation 〜てくれる Giving frame — action for my benefit 待まってくれる。"Waits for me." N3 — Core ← You are here 〜ていてくれる Sustained action for my benefit — progressive + giving stacked 待まっていてくれる？"Will you keep waiting for me?" N3 — Extended 〜ていてくれた Past sustained benefit realized 待まっていてくれた。"You kept waiting for me." N3 — Extended 〜ていてくれてありがとう Gratitude for sustained benefit いつも応援おうえんしていてくれてありがとう。"Thanks for always supporting me." N2 — Awareness 〜ていてくれなかった Past sustained benefit denied 待まっていてくれなかった。"You didn't keep waiting for me."

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
仕事しごとで忙いそがしくても、覚おぼえていてくれる？ Even if work gets busy, will you keep me in mind? Literal: Work-by busy even-if, remember [sustained] [te-form] give [question]?

Context: Asking a partner to maintain emotional memory through their own demanding periods. The 覚おぼえていて layer is essential — "keep holding this in your mind" rather than "remember this fact."

遠とおくにいても、応援おうえんしていてくれる？ Even if I'm far away, will you keep supporting me? Literal: Far being even-if, support [sustained] [te-form] give [question]?

Context: Before a long-distance separation. The していて layer asks for sustained action across spatial and temporal distance.

寂さびしくても、我慢がまんしていてくれる？ Even if you get lonely, will you keep being patient [for me]? Literal: Lonely even-if, endure [sustained] [te-form] give [question]?

Context: Asking someone to maintain a difficult emotional state on your behalf. The burden is explicitly acknowledged.

いつも信しんじていてくれてありがとう。 Thank you for always keeping believing in me. Literal: Always believe [sustained] [te-form] give [te-form] thanks.

Context: Expression of deep gratitude. The いつも (always) + ていて (sustained) + くれて (giving) creates a triple layer of appreciation for ongoing commitment.

待まっていてくれて、ありがとう。 Thanks for keeping waiting for me. Literal: Wait [sustained] [te-form] give [te-form], thanks.

Context: Arriving late and finding someone still there. The ていてくれて thanks them for the sustained state they maintained.

待まっていてくれなかった。 You didn't keep waiting for me. Literal: Wait [sustained] [te-form] give-past-negative.

Context: Disappointment upon finding someone gone. The ていて layer makes the disappointment deeper — they didn't just fail to wait; they failed to sustain the state.

docs.title: 1. Omitting the second te-form
docs.text:
Learners sometimes try to connect the progressive directly to くれる without the intermediate te-form.

Ungrammatical

待まっているくれる？

The progressive 待まっている cannot directly attach to くれる. The te-form 待まっていて is required as connective.

Correct

待まっていてくれる？

Te-form of progressive (待まっていて) + くれる. The double-te is mandatory.

docs.title: 2. Using 〜ていてくれる for brief actions
docs.text:
The sustained aspect is semantic, not just grammatical. Using it for momentary actions sounds bizarre.

Pragmatically odd

窓まどを開あけていてくれる？

"Will you keep opening the window for me?" — Opening a window is a momentary action. The sustained progressive doesn't fit unless you're asking someone to repeatedly open it or keep it open.

Appropriate

窓まどを開あけてくれる？

"Will you open the window for me?" — Simple request for discrete action.

docs.title: 3. Confusing 〜ていてくれる with 〜てくれる in emotional weight
docs.text:
Using the simple form when the sustained form is emotionally required creates a mismatch — like asking for a Band-Aid when you need stitches.

Emotionally insufficient

帰かえりが遅おそくなっても、待まってくれる？

"Even if I'm late, will you wait?" — Grammatically fine, but emotionally light. Doesn't acknowledge the duration or strain of waiting through uncertainty.

Emotionally complete

帰かえりが…遅おそくなっても、待まっていてくれる？

"Even if I'm… late getting back, will you keep waiting for me?" — The ていて layer matches the emotional weight of the condition. The ellipsis and the sustained form together create vulnerability.

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text:
Collocation Meaning 待まっていてくれる？ "Will you keep waiting for me?" — the canonical sustained-patience request 覚おぼえていてくれる？ "Will you keep me in mind?" — sustained memory/emotional attention 応援おうえんしていてくれる？ "Will you keep supporting me?" — sustained encouragement 信しんじていてくれる？ "Will you keep believing in me?" — sustained trust 我慢がまんしていてくれる？ "Will you keep being patient?" — sustained endurance 愛あいしていてくれる？ "Will you keep loving me?" — sustained affection 〜ていてくれてありがとう "Thanks for keeping [doing X] for me" — gratitude for sustained benefit 〜ていてくれた "[You] kept [doing X] for me" — past realization of sustained care 〜ていてくれなかった "Didn't keep [doing X] for me" — past disappointment

These collocations are worth learning as complete emotional units. The pattern is highly productive for any verb where sustained presence or attention matters.

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• 〜ていてくれる can stand alone as a fragment. In texting or casual speech, 待まっていて？ with くれる implied by context is common. The giving frame is so strongly associated with this construction that it can be ellipsed.
  • The progressive 〜ていて implies the listener's current state. When you ask 待まっていてくれる？, you imply that the listener is already waiting or that waiting is the natural state you want them to maintain. This is different from 待まってくれる？, which asks them to start waiting.
  • 〜ていてくれる is psychologically heavier than it appears grammatically. The double-te makes the form longer, which Japanese often associates with heavier emotional weight. The length itself signals: "This request matters enough to require more grammar."
  • The 〜ても condition is the natural partner. Sustained requests almost always appear with 〜ても (even if), 〜たら (if), or 〜間まに (while) — some temporal or conditional frame that justifies the need for sustained action. Bare 待まっていてくれる？ without such framing sounds slightly incomplete, like a request without context.
  • Gratitude for sustained benefit is deeply valued. いつも待まっていてくれてありがとう is more emotionally resonant than 待まってくれてありがとう because it acknowledges not just the action but the duration and commitment. In Japanese relationships, sustained attention is the highest currency.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N3 Recognize 〜ていてくれる in speech and writing; understand the double-te construction; produce correctly with common verbs; distinguish from simple 〜てくれる in emotional weight Developing — N3→N2 Deploy naturally in requests for sustained presence or attention; use くれた / くれなかった for past evaluation; combine naturally with 〜ても conditions Automatic — N2+ The form arrives as a single unit — "sustained benefit request." You no longer parse the double-te analytically. You feel the difference between 待まってくれる (light) and 待まっていてくれる (heavy) intuitively. Mastery You control the full spectrum: てくれる (discrete), ていてくれる (sustained), ていてくれますか (polite sustained), ていてください (formal sustained request), ていてもらえる (humble sustained). You know exactly how much emotional weight each carries and when to use which.

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 〜てくれる — Giving frame; action for my benefit (discrete)
  • 〜ている — Progressive aspect; ongoing state
  • 〜てくれてありがとう — Gratitude for discrete benefit
  • 〜ていてくれてありがとう — Gratitude for sustained benefit

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 〜てくれる — Simple giving frame; lacks duration layer
  • 〜てもらえる — Receiving frame; humble alternative to くれる
  • 〜てください — Direct request; no benefactive intimacy
  • 〜ているだけ — "Just doing"; no giving frame, no benefit direction

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• 〜て (N5) — Te-form connective
  • 〜ている (N4) — Progressive aspect
  • 〜てくれる (N4) — Giving frame
  • 〜ても (N4) — Conditional "even if"

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 〜ていてくれた (N3) — Past sustained benefit realized
  • 〜ていてくれなかった (N3) — Past sustained benefit denied
  • 〜ていてくれてありがとう (N3) — Gratitude for sustained benefit
  • 〜ていてもらえる (N4) — Humble sustained request
  • 〜ていてほしい (N3) — "I want you to keep [doing X]" — desire rather than request

docs.title: Test 1
docs.text: TEST PAGE FOR DIFFERENT AI MODELS

docs.title: 〜ちゃいなよ — Casual Encouragement to Go Ahead and Finish / Do It
〜ちゃいなよ — “Go ahead and do it already / just do it”
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — Lower N3

The pieces are familiar earlier — てしまう, casual contraction ちゃう, and なよ — but the combined spoken pattern 〜ちゃいなよ is best treated as Lower N3 because its real difficulty is tone: friendly, pushy, caring, teasing, or mildly impatient depending on context.

Tier Pattern Description Example N4 — Foundation 〜てしまう Completion / “end up doing” 食たべてしまう。"I eat it all / end up eating it." N4 — Spoken 〜ちゃう Casual contraction of 〜てしまう 食たべちゃう。"I’ll eat it / I end up eating it." N3 — Core ← You are here 〜ちゃいなよ Friendly push: “go ahead and do it already” 先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。"Go ahead and get in first." N3 — Extended 〜ちゃえば？ / 〜ちゃったら？ Softer suggestion variants 先さきに入はいっちゃえば？"Why don’t you just go in first?"

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 〜ちゃいなよ is a casual spoken pattern meaning “go ahead and do it,” “just do it already,” or “you might as well do it.”
  • It comes from 〜てしまう → 〜ちゃう, but here the main nuance is usually completion / moving forward, not regret.
  • It often sounds caring or encouraging when used with close people.
  • It can sound pushy if used with strangers, superiors, or people you are not close to.
  • In the example sentence, 入はいっちゃいなよ means “go ahead and get in already” — the speaker is warmly encouraging the listener to take the bath first.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Attach 〜ちゃいなよ to a verb to casually encourage someone to go ahead and do the action, usually because the speaker thinks it is fine, convenient, or better to get it done now.

先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。 Go ahead and get in first. Literal: Go ahead and enter first already.

~Context: Friendly encouragement. The speaker is not ordering harshly; they are making the action feel easy and acceptable.~

お風呂ふろ準備じゅんびできてるから、もう先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。 The bath is ready, so go ahead and get in first. Literal: Since the bath preparation is done, already go ahead and enter first.

~Context: The input sentence. The speaker has prepared the bath and is gently pushing the listener to use it first. The tone is close, casual, and considerate.~

docs.title: In Real Life
docs.text:
In Real Life

Situation: One person is staying over. The host has already prepared the bath and wants the guest or partner to relax first.

お風呂ふろ準備じゅんびできてるから、もう先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。

"The bath is ready, so go ahead and get in first."

Why this form fits here: 入はいっちゃいなよ makes the suggestion feel casual and warm. The speaker is not just saying "you may enter" or "please enter"; they are lightly nudging the listener to stop hesitating and take the bath.

What makes this natural: もう adds “at this point / already,” 先さきに sets up “before me / first,” and ちゃいなよ makes the action feel like something the listener can simply go ahead and do. This is close-relationship speech, not polite service speech.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: The pattern is built from three layers:

docs.title: てしまう
docs.text:
• completion
  • “do it fully”
  • sometimes regret, but not always

docs.title: ちゃう
docs.text:
• casual contraction of てしまう
  • very common in spoken Japanese

docs.title: ちゃいなよ
docs.text:
• casual encouraging command / suggestion
  • “go ahead and do it”
  • often used when the speaker thinks the listener is hesitating unnecessarily
Layer Form Function Base verb 入はいる enter / get in て-form 入はいって connective form Contracted てしまう 入はいっちゃう go ahead and get in / end up getting in Suggestive push 入はいっちゃいなよ go ahead and get in already

docs.title: Why It Does Not Mean Regret Here
docs.text:
In textbooks, てしまう is often taught as “unfortunately did X.” That is only one use. In casual speech, ちゃう often means the action is completed, done decisively, or moved forward.

Sentence Natural Meaning Regret? 先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。 Go ahead and get in first. No 全部ぜんぶ食たべちゃった。 I ate it all. Maybe 財布さいふ忘わすれちゃった。 I forgot my wallet. Yes

Key point

ちゃう does not automatically mean regret. It often means completion, decisiveness, or “it happened / got done.” The emotional meaning comes from the verb and situation.

docs.title: Function in Context
docs.text:
• お風呂ふろ準備じゅんびできてるから gives the reason: the bath is already ready.
  • もう adds a light “now / already / at this point” feeling.
  • 先さきに means “before me / first.”
  • 入はいっちゃいなよ is the friendly push: “go ahead and get in.”
  • よ adds warmth and makes the suggestion feel directed at the listener.
Segment Role お風呂ふろ準備じゅんびできてるから reason / setup もう “already / now” 先さきに first / before me 入はいっちゃいなよ go ahead and get in

docs.title: Construction
docs.text:
Base Verb て-form ちゃう Form ちゃいなよ Form 入はいる 入はいって 入はいっちゃう 入はいっちゃいなよ 食たべる 食たべて 食たべちゃう 食たべちゃいなよ 飲のむ 飲のんで 飲のんじゃう 飲のんじゃいなよ 帰かえる 帰かえって 帰かえっちゃう 帰かえっちゃいなよ 見みる 見みて 見みちゃう 見みちゃいなよ する して しちゃう しちゃいなよ 来くる 来きて 来きちゃう 来きちゃいなよ

ちゃう vs じゃう

If the て-form ends in で, the contraction becomes じゃう, not ちゃう. 飲のんで → 飲のんじゃう → 飲のんじゃいなよ.

docs.title: What Is 〜いなよ?
docs.text:
The いなよ part comes from a casual command-like pattern related to なさい, but it is much softer and more colloquial than a direct command in many friendly contexts.

Form Feeling 入はいって simple request / command 入はいりなさい parent / teacher command 入はいりなよ casual suggestion / light command 入はいっちゃいなよ go ahead and get in already; friendly push

Not for everyone

〜ちゃいなよ assumes closeness. It is natural with friends, partners, family, or someone you are caring for casually. It can sound too casual or bossy with strangers, seniors, customers, or teachers.

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
English often separates permission, advice, and command very clearly: “You can go in,” “You should go in,” “Go in.” Japanese often uses softer spoken packaging to place the action inside the relationship. 〜ちゃいなよ does not simply tell someone what to do — it says, “It’s okay, the situation is ready, so just go ahead.”

The key is that the speaker is reducing hesitation. In the bath sentence, the listener may be waiting politely, unsure whether to go first, or not wanting to impose. 入はいっちゃいなよ removes that hesitation and makes the action socially easy.

  • Social easing: The pattern helps the listener move forward without feeling selfish.
  • Completion framing: ちゃう packages the action as something to just get done.
  • Relationship signal: The speaker sounds close enough to nudge the listener casually.
  • Care through pushiness: The form can feel caring because the speaker is actively clearing the listener’s hesitation.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text: Pattern Core Meaning Emotional / Pragmatic Nuance Example JLPT 〜て Do X Plain request or command depending on tone 先さきに入はいって。"Go in first." N5 〜ていいよ You may do X Permission; softer and less pushy 先さきに入はいっていいよ。"You can go in first." N5 〜なよ Do X / you should X Casual suggestion; can sound slightly directive 先さきに入はいりなよ。"You should go in first." N4 〜ちゃいなよ Go ahead and do X Friendly push toward completion; “just do it already” 先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。"Go ahead and get in first." N3 〜ちゃえば？ Why not just do X? Suggestion framed as an option 先さきに入はいっちゃえば？"Why don’t you just go in first?" N3 〜てしまいなさい Go ahead and do X Formal/written or parent-like full form; stronger 先さきに入はいってしまいなさい。"Go ahead and get in first." N3

docs.title: Micro Contrast
docs.text: Sentence Natural English Feeling 先さきに入はいっていいよ。 You can go in first. permission 先さきに入はいりなよ。 You should go in first. suggestion / light command 先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。 Go ahead and get in first already. friendly push / completion 先さきに入はいっちゃえば？ Why don’t you just go in first? softer option

docs.title: 〜ていいよ
docs.text:
A softer permission-based alternative. Use this when you want to avoid sounding pushy.

Example もう先さきに入はいっていいよ。 English "You can go in first now." Intent Permission, not a push vs. original Softer but less warm/active. It allows the action but does not strongly encourage it.

docs.title: 〜なよ
docs.text:
A direct casual suggestion. It keeps the friendly tone but loses the “go ahead and finish/do it” nuance from ちゃう.

Example もう先さきに入はいりなよ。 English "Go in first already." Intent Casual suggestion / light command vs. original More direct. Less sense of “the situation is ready, so just go ahead.”

docs.title: 〜ちゃえば？
docs.text:
A softer, option-like version. It sounds more like “why don’t you just…?”

Example もう先さきに入はいっちゃえば？ English "Why don’t you just go in first?" Intent Suggestion framed as an option vs. original Softer and less directive. Good when you do not want to sound like you are pushing.

Choosing between them

Use 〜ていいよ when giving permission, 〜なよ when casually recommending, 〜ちゃいなよ when warmly pushing someone to just go ahead and do it.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
Casual Basic Polite Extra Polite

お風呂ふろ準備じゅんびできてるから、もう先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。

"The bath is ready, so go ahead and get in first."

Shift: Input sentence as-is. Natural close-relationship speech. Friendly, lightly pushy, caring.

お風呂ふろの準備じゅんびができていますので、よかったら先さきに入はいってください。

"The bath is ready, so please feel free to go in first if you'd like."

Shift: 〜ちゃいなよ is removed because it is too casual. よかったら and ください preserve the considerate intent without the casual push.

お風呂ふろの準備じゅんびができておりますので、よろしければお先さきにお入はいりください。

"The bath is ready, so please go ahead and use it first if you would like."

Shift: More formal and hosted. Natural in a polite household, lodging, or service-like context. The warmth becomes courtesy rather than intimacy.

No true humble equivalent

〜ちゃいなよ is directed at someone else, so there is no direct humble version. Humble speech describes your own actions toward someone above you; here, the speaker is inviting the listener to act.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: Step Pattern What It Adds Example N5 て-form Connects verbs; basis for requests 入はいって N4 〜てしまう Completion / regret / finality 入はいってしまう N4 Spoken 〜ちゃう Casual contraction of 〜てしまう 入はいっちゃう N3 — Core ← You are here 〜ちゃいなよ Friendly push to go ahead and do it 入はいっちゃいなよ N3 Extended 〜ちゃえば？ Softer suggestion / option 入はいっちゃえば？ N2 Awareness 〜てしまいなさい Full, formalized version; stronger 入はいってしまいなさい

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
冷さめちゃうから、先さきに食たべちゃいなよ。 It’ll get cold, so go ahead and eat first. Literal: Because it will end up getting cold, go ahead and eat first.

~Context: Close friend or partner. Caring push. The speaker is removing hesitation.~

疲つかれてるなら、もう寝ねちゃいなよ。 If you're tired, just go to bed already. Literal: If you're tired, go ahead and sleep already.

~Context: Caring but slightly directive. Natural with someone close.~

気きになるなら、買かっちゃいなよ。 If you want it, just buy it already. Literal: If it bothers/interests you, go ahead and buy it.

~Context: Friend encouraging someone who keeps hesitating. Light, casual push.~

時間じかんあるうちに、先さきにやっちゃいなよ。 While you still have time, go ahead and get it done first. Literal: While there is time, go ahead and do it first.

~Context: Practical advice. Completion nuance is strong.~

読よみたいなら、もう読よんじゃいなよ。 If you want to read it, just go ahead and read it. Literal: If you want to read it, go ahead and read it already.

~Context: Friendly encouragement. んじゃいなよ appears because 読よむ → 読よんで → 読よんじゃう.~

docs.title: 1. Treating ちゃう as always regret
docs.text:
Incorrect assumption

入はいっちゃいなよ = "Unfortunately go in."

~This is not how the sentence works. Here ちゃう expresses completion / going ahead, not regret.~

Correct understanding

入はいっちゃいなよ = "Go ahead and get in."

~The speaker is encouraging action, not expressing regret.~

docs.title: 2. Using it with people you are not close to
docs.text:
Too casual

先生せんせい、もう先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。

~Too casual and too directive toward a teacher. It sounds socially inappropriate unless the relationship is extremely unusual.~

Safer polite version

先生せんせい、よろしければお先さきにお入はいりください。

~Polite and respectful. The invitation remains, but the casual push disappears.~

docs.title: 3. Forgetting じゃう after で-ending て-forms
docs.text:
Incorrect

飲のんちゃいなよ。

~飲のむ → 飲のんで, so the contraction is じゃう, not ちゃう.~

Correct

飲のんじゃいなよ。

~で → じゃう. 読よむ also works this way: 読よんで → 読よんじゃう → 読よんじゃいなよ.~

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text:
Common Chunk Meaning Note 食たべちゃいなよ Go ahead and eat it Caring / practical 寝ねちゃいなよ Just go to bed Caring, sometimes slightly parental 買かっちゃいなよ Just buy it already Encouraging someone hesitating やっちゃいなよ Just get it done Common, practical, direct 読よんじゃいなよ Just read it already で → じゃう contraction 使つかっちゃいなよ Go ahead and use it Permission + encouragement

~These are best learned as spoken chunks. The social meaning comes from the whole phrase, not just the grammar pieces.~

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• ちゃいなよ contains a mild push. It is not neutral permission. The speaker is actively encouraging the listener to stop waiting, stop worrying, or just move forward.
  • It often carries care. With close people, this pattern can sound warm because the speaker is removing the listener’s hesitation.
  • It can sound parental. 寝ねちゃいなよ or 食たべちゃいなよ can sound like someone gently taking care of another person.
  • The regret meaning of ちゃう is not automatic. In this pattern, completion and decisiveness are usually more important than regret.
  • The relationship matters. The same phrase can sound affectionate with a partner, friendly with a friend, and rude with a stranger.
  • よ is doing social work. It makes the suggestion feel addressed and supportive rather than cold or abrupt.

docs.title: Coming Soon
docs.text:
〜ちゃえば？

A softer suggestion pattern meaning “why don’t you just…?” It is often less pushy than 〜ちゃいなよ and useful when you want to make the action feel like the listener’s choice.

〜ちゃった

A very high-frequency spoken contraction of 〜てしまった. It can mean regret, completion, accident, or emotional confession depending on context.

〜なよ

A casual suggestion / light command pattern. It is simpler than 〜ちゃいなよ but can sound more direct because it lacks the “go ahead and finish/do it” nuance from ちゃう.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — Lower N3 You can recognize 〜ちゃいなよ as a casual spoken suggestion and understand that it usually means “go ahead and do it,” not regret Developing — N3 You can choose between 〜ていいよ, 〜なよ, 〜ちゃいなよ, and 〜ちゃえば？ based on how pushy or soft you want to sound Automatic — N3→N2 You feel the social temperature instantly: caring, teasing, pushy, parental, or inappropriate depending on relationship Mastery You can use 〜ちゃいなよ naturally to reduce hesitation without sounding bossy, and you can avoid it when the relationship does not support that casual push

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 〜ちゃう — casual contraction of 〜てしまう
  • 〜ちゃった — completed / regretted / ended up doing
  • 〜ちゃえば？ — softer “why don’t you just…?” suggestion
  • 〜なよ — casual suggestion / light command

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 〜ていいよ — permission, not active encouragement
  • 〜たほうがいい — stronger advice
  • 〜なさい — command, often parent/teacher tone
  • 〜てください — polite request or instruction

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• て-form
  • 〜てしまう
  • 〜ちゃう
  • Casual sentence-ending よ

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 〜ちゃえばいい
  • 〜ちゃったほうがいい
  • 〜ちゃってもいい
  • 〜ちゃえばよかった

docs.title: 〜ていけばいいから — Reassurance of Sufficient Ongoing Action
JLPT Level
docs.text:
Claude Sonnet 4.6 · 2026·04·28 〜ていけばいいから — Just Keep Going, That's Enough Mid N3

The input sentence represents extended N3 usage — the core pattern 〜ばいい is N4, but the full stack of 〜ていく + 〜ばいい + sentence-final から functioning as a soft reassurance anchor is a mid-N3 pragmatic package.

N4 — Foundation 〜ばいい marks a single sufficient condition: "all you need to do is X." 聞きけばいい。 "All you have to do is ask." N4 — Foundation 〜ていく encodes action continuing forward into the future: te-form + いく. 覚おぼえていく。 "To keep learning going forward." N3 — Core 〜ていけばいい combines both: "just keep doing X as you go — that's enough." 少すこしずつ覚おぼえていけばいい。 "Just pick it up little by little." N3 — Extended ← You are here 〜ていけばいいから adds sentence-final から as a soft reassurance close: "…and that's enough, so don't worry." 自じ分ぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから。 "Just go at your own pace — that's enough." N2 — Awareness 〜ていけばよいだろう: same logic in a formal or written register. No conversational warmth. [N2 — awareness only] 段だん階かいを踏ふんで進すすんでいけばよいだろう。 "It would be best to proceed step by step."

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
• 〜ていけばいいから reassures someone that steadily continuing an action is all that's needed — not a one-time fix, not perfection, just ongoing forward movement.
  • The ていく layer reframes the task as a journey already in progress. The から at the end closes the statement warmly: "this is sufficient — so relax."
  • Most commonly used in supportive speech between close friends, partners, family, or mentors. Pairs naturally with a line that removes a burden first.
  • Learn this as a complete pragmatic package, not just its grammar parts — the three layers work together as a single social move.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text: Core Rule: Use 〜ていけばいい（から） to reassure someone that they just need to keep doing X as an ongoing process — not a single action, not perfection, just steady forward movement — and that's genuinely enough. JapaneseEnglish 無む理りしなくていいよ。自じ分ぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから。 You don't need to push yourself. Just go at your own pace — that's all you need.Literal: Pushing yourself not-necessary. At own pace if-you-keep-doing good, because. The input sentence. Two-beat structure: remove pressure first, then offer a sustainable direction. 少すこしずつ慣なれていけばいいから、焦あせらないで。 Just get used to it gradually — don't rush.Literal: Little by little if-you-keep-getting-used-to-it good, so don't hurry. A mentor reassuring someone anxious about adapting to something new.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
This pattern is built from three layers that each carry distinct meaning. Understanding what each layer contributes is the key to using it naturally.

Layer 1 — 〜ていく: The te-form of a verb plus いく (to go) creates a sense of forward movement continuing into the future. It frames the action not as something to complete but as an ongoing process — the emphasis shifts from "achieve X" to "keep going toward X." Without this layer, the pattern would suggest a single sufficient action. With it, the meaning becomes "keep going, over time."

Layer 2 — 〜ばいい: The ba-conditional plus いい marks the condition as sufficient. It doesn't say "you must" or "you should" — it says "this alone is enough." Combined with ていく, the sufficiency applies to the ongoing process itself, not a single outcome. The bar has been set at "just keep going," and that bar is declared reachable.

Layer 3 — sentence-final から: In casual speech, から at the end of a sentence doesn't always introduce a cause-and-effect chain. Here it functions as a soft close — "…and that's why it's fine / so don't worry." The clause it "explains" is implied: the listener doesn't need to worry or rush. This use of から is one of the most common reassurance moves in casual Japanese conversation.

JapaneseEnglish ゆっくり覚おぼえていけばいいんだから、急いそがなくていいよ。 You can learn it slowly — there's really no need to rush.Literal: Slowly if-you-keep-learning good it-is, so hurrying not-necessary. The んだ variant adds a grounding tone — the speaker is making a gentle logical claim, not just offering comfort.

docs.title: Real Speech vs. Textbook Form
docs.text:
The textbook form is 〜ていけばよい. In real speech, よい contracts to いい in virtually all casual contexts — 〜ていけばいい is what you will hear and what you should produce. A further reduction exists in fast speech: ていく compresses to てく, making the pattern sound like 〜てけばいい. This is natural between close speakers but sounds too casual for most supportive advice contexts.

JapaneseEnglish やっていけばいい Natural spoken — "Just keep doing it, that's enough"Literal: If-you-keep-doing good. The form you will hear and use in casual conversation. やっていけばよい Textbook / written — grammatically correct but stiff in casual speechLiteral: If-you-keep-doing good (formal). Appears in written guidance, formal advice, N2-level patterns. Avoid in casual conversation.

docs.title: Function in Context
docs.text:
• The sentence operates as a two-beat comfort structure: 無む理りしなくていいよ removes the pressure first, then 自じ分ぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから replaces it with a sustainable alternative. Jumping straight to advice without removing the burden first would feel tone-deaf in most casual contexts.
  • 〜ていく is the critical upgrade over plain やればいい. Without it, the sentence suggests a single action to complete. With it, the doing is reframed as a continuous journey — emphasis on gradual progress, not a destination.
  • 自じ分ぶんのペースで personalizes the frame: the standard isn't external or comparative, it belongs to the listener. No comparison to others is implied or expected.
  • Sentence-final から closes the utterance as emotionally complete. The listener does not need to respond with a counterargument — the speaker is signaling the reassurance is self-sufficient.
  • Without から: やっていけばいいよ is warm advice. With から: the conversation about worry is finished, and the listener can exhale.
JapaneseEnglish 自じ分ぶんのペースでやればいいから。 Just do it at your own pace.Literal: At own pace if-you-do good, because. Without ていく — sounds like one action to complete, not an ongoing journey. The reassurance is weaker. 自じ分ぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから。 Just keep going at your own pace — that's all you need.Literal: At own pace if-you-keep-doing good, because. With ていく — frames it as a sustainable ongoing process. The emotional reassurance is complete.

docs.title: Construction
docs.text:
FormResult 行いく (godan) 行いけばいい く→け (godan e-stem) + ば + いい 食たべる (ichidan) 食たべればいい drop る + れば + いい する／くる (irregular) すればいい／くればいい する→すれ、くる→くれ + ば + いい Verb + ていく (ongoing action) やっていけばいい te-form + いく → ba-form of いく (いけ) + ばいい. This is the exact structure of the input sentence. ない-form (negative) 行いかなければいい ない→なければ. Casual contraction: なきゃいい i-Adjective: 安やすい 安やすければいい い→ければ. "As long as it's cheap." な-Adjective: 元げん気きだ 元げん気きならいい だ→なら. Also: 元げん気きであればいい Noun: 今きょう日ひだ 今きょう日ひならいい だ dropped before なら. Same pattern as な-adjective.

Note: Masu-form cannot attach to this pattern — ba-conditional applies to plain forms only. Past tense + ばいい is also unnatural for permission or sufficiency contexts.

docs.title: Variations
docs.text:
Adding んだ before から: やっていけばいいんだから adds a mild explanatory or emphatic nuance — the speaker is grounding the reassurance in a logical claim rather than just offering comfort.

ゆっくり覚おぼえていけばいいんだから、急いそがなくていいよ。 Natural English: "You can learn it slowly — there's really no need to rush." Shift: んだ adds a grounding tone — the speaker makes a gentle logical claim, not just emotional comfort. Gains: Slightly more insistent, warmer than bare から. Loses: The lighter, airy feel of the bare から version.

Dropping から entirely: やっていけばいいよ or やっていけばいい are both natural and more direct — the soft reassurance close is removed, leaving clean practical advice. Still warm, but less emotionally cushioned.

自じ分ぶんのペースでやっていけばいいよ。 Natural English: "Just keep going at your own pace." Shift: から removed — now practical advice rather than a complete emotional close. Gains: Slightly more direct; works well mid-conversation without feeling final. Loses: The "you can stop worrying now" signal that から provides.

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
In English, reassurance often takes the form of direct assertions: "You'll be fine," "You've got this," "Don't worry." The speaker claims a positive outcome or quality about the listener. Japanese casual reassurance tends to work differently — rather than asserting an outcome, the speaker reframes the situation itself, replacing an overwhelming frame with a manageable one.

やっていけばいいから doesn't claim the listener will succeed. It redefines what success requires: not speed, not perfection, not keeping up with others — just continuing to move forward. The speaker shrinks the task from "achieve X" to "just keep going." This is a fundamentally different communicative strategy: the goal is not to increase the listener's confidence but to reduce the height of the bar.

  • Burden removal before direction. The full sentence structure — remove pressure first, then offer the replacement frame — reflects a core Japanese conversational norm. Advice without first acknowledging the burden can feel dismissive. The sequencing is a social skill, not just grammar.
  • Sufficiency over achievement. いい here means "sufficient, acceptable, fine" — not "good" in an evaluative sense. The speaker is not praising; they are lowering the bar to a sustainable level. This is a different communicative move than encouragement.
  • から as an emotional close. In English, "because" always requires a following clause. In Japanese, sentence-final から signals the preceding statement is its own sufficient reason — the speaker is closing an argument, not opening one. Learning to hear から this way is essential for natural comprehension of casual speech.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text:
Grammar Point Core Meaning Emotional / Pragmatic Nuance Trigger Context JLPT 〜ていけばいい（から） "Just keep doing X — that's enough" Warm, reassuring; frames task as sustainable ongoing journey Comforting someone overwhelmed; encouraging gradual progress N3 〜ばいい "All you need to do is X" Practical, solution-focused; less emotional warmth Giving practical permission or a simple solution to a problem N4 〜ばいいだけ "All you have to do is just X" Emphatic simplification; can feel dismissive if used carelessly Cutting through overthinking; minimizing complexity N3 〜てもいい "It's okay to do X / you may do X" Explicit permission; about individual allowance, not forward process Granting permission for something the listener was hesitant about N4 〜なくていい（よ） "You don't have to do X" Obligation release; pairs naturally as a two-beat comfort opener Removing an expectation the listener is carrying unnecessarily N4 〜ていけばよいだろう "It would be advisable to proceed by doing X" Formal, detached; no conversational warmth Written advice, formal guidance [N2 — awareness only] N2

The closest lookalike is plain 〜ばいい. The key difference is temporal framing: 〜ばいい points to a single sufficient action, while 〜ていけばいい reframes the entire situation as a continuing process. When someone is overwhelmed by the scale of a task, this distinction carries real emotional weight — the ていく layer is what transforms practical advice into genuine reassurance.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
The emotional core — removing pressure and offering a sustainable frame — must be preserved across all register shifts. Do not flatten the warmth into a neutral statement.

Casual 無む理りすんなって。自じ分ぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから。 Natural English: "Don't push yourself, seriously. Just go at your own pace — that's all you need." Shift: 無む理りしなくていいよ → 無む理りすんなって adds slightly more emphatic, almost exasperated warmth. The reassurance half is identical to the input sentence. Basic Polite 無む理りしなくていいですよ。自じ分ぶんのペースでやっていけばいいですから。 Natural English: "You really don't need to push yourself. Just going at your own pace is perfectly fine." Shift: いいよ→いいですよ; いいから→いいですから. Warmth and reassurance fully preserved — ですから carries the same soft close as casual から. Extra Polite 無む理りをなさらなくて構かまいません。ご自じ分ぶんのペースで進すすんでいただければ十じゅう分ぶんです。 Natural English: "Please don't feel the need to push yourself. Taking things at your own pace is entirely sufficient." Shift: なさらなくて構かまいません uses respectful negative permission. 進すすんでいただければ十じゅう分ぶんです replaces the warm colloquial いいから with a formal sufficiency statement. Emotional intimacy is reduced — sounds considered and respectful rather than close and warm.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: N4 — Foundation        〜ばいい — the ba-conditional marks a sufficient condition. "If X is met, that's enough." The foundation this pattern builds from.       聞きけばいい。 "All you have to do is ask." N4 — Foundation        〜ていく — te-form + いく encodes action continuing forward into the future. Without this layer, 〜ていけばいい is unreadable.       少すこしずつ覚おぼえていく。 "To keep learning little by little." N3 — Core        〜ていけばいい — combines ていく + ばいい: "just keep doing X as you go — that's enough." Reframes a task as a sustainable ongoing journey.       少すこしずつ慣なれていけばいい。 "Just ease into it gradually — that's all you need." N3 — Extended ← You are here        〜ていけばいいから — adds sentence-final から as a warm emotional close. The statement becomes a complete reassurance, not just advice.       自じ分ぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから。 "Just go at your own pace — that's enough." N3 — Extended        〜ていけばよかった — the same structure applied to a past counterfactual: "I should have kept doing X." Regret form of this pattern.       もっと早はやくから続つづけていけばよかった。 "I should have kept it up from earlier." N2 — Awareness        〜ていかなければならない — the obligatory form: "must keep doing X going forward." Significantly heavier register, built on the same ていく foundation. [N2 — awareness only]       努ど力りょくし続つづけていかなければならない。 "We must keep making an effort going forward."

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: JapaneseEnglish 最さい初しょは難むずかしくて当とう然ぜんだから、少すこしずつ覚おぼえていけばいいよ。 It's completely normal for it to feel hard at first — just pick it up bit by bit.Literal: At first being-difficult is natural, so little by little if-you-keep-learning good. A senior colleague reassuring a new starter who feels overwhelmed in a new role. 全ぜん部ぶいっぺんにやろうとしなくていい。今きょう日ひできることだけやっていけばいいから。 You don't have to try to do everything at once. Just do what you can today — that's enough.Literal: All at once try-to-do not-necessary. Today can-do things only if-you-keep-doing good, because. A close friend talking to someone burned out and spreading themselves too thin. みんなと同おなじペースじゃなくていいんだよ。自じ分ぶんのやり方かたで進すすんでいけばいいから、気きにしないで。 You don't have to keep up with everyone else. Just move forward in your own way — don't worry about it.Literal: Same pace as everyone not-necessary. Own way if-you-keep-going good, so don't worry. An older sibling reassuring a younger one who feels behind their peers. まだ慣なれなくて当とう然ぜんだよ。ゆっくり慣なれていけばいいんだから、焦あせらないで。 It makes total sense you're not used to it yet. Take your time getting comfortable — don't rush.Literal: Still not-accustomed is natural. Slowly if-you-keep-getting-used-to-it good it-is, so don't hurry. One partner reassuring the other anxious about adjusting to living together for the first time. 完かん璧ぺきにやろうとしなくていい。今いまのままで続つづけていけばいいから、信しんじてみて。 You don't have to try to be perfect. Just keep going as you are — trust yourself.Literal: Perfectly try-to-do not-necessary. As-you-are-now if-you-keep-continuing good, so try to trust. A coach or close friend speaking to someone spiraling with self-doubt before a performance or exam.

docs.title: Mistake 1: Dropping ていく and using やればいいから
docs.text:
Learners often drop the ていく layer and use plain やればいいから, thinking it means the same thing. This loses the "ongoing forward process" nuance — the sentence sounds like a one-time action to complete rather than a sustainable journey, which significantly weakens the reassurance.

JapaneseEnglish 自じ分ぶんのペースでやればいいから。 Just do it at your own pace.Literal: At own pace if-you-do good, because. Sounds like a one-time action. The reassurance that ongoing gradual progress is sufficient is missing.

Adding ていく reframes the entire situation as a continuing journey. The listener is not being asked to complete a task — they are being told that staying on the path they are already on is enough.

JapaneseEnglish 自じ分ぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから。 Just keep going at your own pace — that's all you need.Literal: At own pace if-you-keep-doing good, because. ていく makes the action ongoing and forward-moving. The emotional reassurance is now complete.

docs.title: Mistake 2: Misreading sentence-final から as causal
docs.text:
Learners who parse から strictly as "because" will expect a following result clause that never comes, or will misread the sentence as incomplete. Sentence-final から in reassurance contexts is a soft emotional close — the statement is its own sufficient explanation.

JapaneseEnglish やっていけばいいから… [何なにかが続つづく？] Because you just need to keep doing it… [something follows?]Literal: If-you-keep-doing good, because… [?] Wrong reading. Treating から as a causal connector expecting a result clause that doesn't exist.

Sentence-final から marks the preceding statement as a complete, self-sufficient reason. The implied clause is: "so you don't need to worry." No continuation is needed or expected.

JapaneseEnglish やっていけばいいから。 Just keep doing it — that's all there is to it.Literal: If-you-keep-doing good, because [that's sufficient]. から closes the statement warmly and completely. Hear it as "…and that's the reason / so don't worry."

docs.title: When NOT to Use It
docs.text:
When giving neutral practical instructions. This pattern has a warm, intimate tone built in. Using it for a neutral practical instruction sounds over-familiar or oddly emotional for the context.

JapaneseEnglish このボタンを押おしていけばいいから。 Just keep pressing this button.Literal: This button if-you-keep-pressing good, because. The warm reassurance tone doesn't fit a neutral technical instruction. Sounds odd out of context.

Plain ばいい without ていく is the natural choice for practical neutral instructions — it marks sufficiency without the emotional warmth.

JapaneseEnglish このボタンを押おせばいい。 All you need to do is press this button.Literal: This button if-you-press good. Plain ばいい suits neutral practical instructions. No emotional warmth, no ongoing-process framing — just a simple sufficient condition.

When the listener needs concrete steps, not reframing. If someone genuinely needs specific guidance on how to do something, offering やっていけばいい without specifics can feel vague or evasive. Reserve this pattern for situations where the task is already defined and the barrier is psychological, not informational.

docs.title: Don't Confuse With
docs.text:
〜ていけない (prohibition): Both contain いけ, but the sources are completely different. 〜ていけばいい = te-form + いく → ba-form + いい (permission/sufficiency). 〜てはいけない = te-form + は + いけない (cannot go = prohibited). The ば vs. は split is the key — one leads to permission, the other to prohibition.

JapaneseEnglish 自じ分ぶんのペースでやっていけばいい。 You just need to do it at your own pace.Literal: Own pace if-you-keep-doing good. — Permission / sufficiency. 〜ていけばいい: te-form + いく + ばいい. Encouragement — the action is marked as sufficient. 無む理りをしてはいけない。 You must not push yourself too hard.Literal: Pushing-too-hard doing-is-prohibited. — Prohibition. 〜てはいけない: te-form + は + いけない. Prohibition — the action is marked as not permitted. 〜ばいい vs. 〜たらいい is an equally important distinction frequently blurred at N3 level. 〜ばいい marks a sufficient condition: "if this is met, that's enough." 〜たらいい imagines a completed outcome: "once/when X happens, that would be good" — often with a wishful or mildly frustrated undertone. In most casual contexts they overlap, but the underlying logic differs and becomes audible at the edges.

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text:
Certain words appear so frequently alongside 〜ていけばいいから that they function as near-fixed chunks worth learning together.

自じ分ぶんのペースで — "at your own pace." The most common collocate. Both expressions are purpose-built for each other: 自じ分ぶんのペースで establishes the personal standard, and やっていけばいい marks ongoing movement at that standard as sufficient.

JapaneseEnglish 自じ分ぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから、焦あせらないで。 Just go at your own pace — don't rush.Literal: At own pace if-you-keep-doing good, so don't hurry. The canonical collocation. Learn this chunk whole.

少すこしずつ — "little by little." Pairs with 〜ていけばいい to frame progress as small, gradual, and acceptable. Both communicate that the pace of progress is not the problem.

ゆっくり — "slowly, without rushing." Similar to 少すこしずつ but emphasizes pace over increment size. Often used when the task is less granular — adapting, recovering, building a relationship.

焦あせらなくていい / 急いそがなくていい — "no need to rush / hurry." These appear just before or after the pattern, completing the reassurance frame: [remove time pressure] + [reframe as sufficient ongoing process].

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• 〜ていく carries genuine directionality. いく (to go) physically encodes forward movement through time. The listener is being told the journey they are already on is heading somewhere, and continuing on it is all that's required. This spatial metaphor is active, not decorative — it's doing real pragmatic work.
  • Sentence-final から shifts with intonation. With falling intonation it closes the statement warmly — "and that's why it's fine." With flat or rising intonation it shades toward impatience — "because I said so." In writing, context carries the tonal weight; in the input sentence, 無む理りしなくていいよ makes the warm intent unmistakable.
  • The two-beat comfort structure is a learnable social script. [Remove burden] + [offer sustainable frame] appears across casual Japanese supportive speech. Jumping straight to やっていけばいい without first acknowledging the burden can feel tone-deaf. Sequence matters as much as grammar here.
  • 自じ分ぶんのペースで signals non-comparison. This near-fixed expression carries a cultural subtext: you are not being evaluated against anyone else. Combined with やっていけばいいから, the full sentence tells the listener not just what to do but how to measure themselves — by their own standard, not an external one.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text:
• What you can do now: Recognize this pattern reliably in speech and writing, understand its reassurance function, and produce it correctly in controlled contexts — especially the common chunk 自じ分ぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから. The grammar mechanics are within N3 reach.
  • What still takes effort: Deploying the full two-beat structure naturally in real time — knowing when to lead with burden removal before the reframe. Parsing sentence-final から as a soft close rather than a causal connector also requires deliberate attention at this stage; the instinct to look for a following clause is strong.
  • What comes later: Through extended listening exposure, the pattern stops feeling like assembled grammar and arrives as a single emotional unit. The intonation signal on sentence-final から becomes automatic rather than analytical. That shift happens through immersion, not study.

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 〜ばいい — All you need to do is X (foundational pattern this builds from)
  • 〜てもいい — Permission: it's okay to do X
  • 〜なくていい — Obligation release: you don't have to do X

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 〜たらいい — Wished-for outcome; imagining a completed result vs. marking a sufficient condition
  • 〜ていけない — Prohibition; visually similar but grammatically and semantically opposite

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• 〜ばいい (N4) — Sufficient condition: "all you need to do is X"
  • 〜ていく (N4) — Ongoing directional action: "keep doing X going forward"
  • Sentence-final から (N4–N3) — Soft explanatory close in casual speech

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 〜ていけばよかった — Regret form: "I should have kept doing X"
  • 〜ていかなければならない — Obligation form: "must keep doing X" [N2 — awareness only]
  • 〜ていけるかどうか — Uncertainty about ongoing capacity: "whether I can keep going"

docs.title: 〜ていけばいいから — Reassurance of Sufficient Ongoing Action
〜ていけばいいから — Just Keep Going, That's Enough
docs.text:
JLPT Level — Mid N3

The core pattern 〜ばいい is N4, but the full stack of 〜ていく + 〜ばいい + sentence-final から functioning as a soft reassurance anchor is a mid-N3 pragmatic package.

Tier Pattern Description Example N4 — Foundation 〜ばいい Marks a single sufficient condition: "all you need to do is X." 聞きけばいい。"All you have to do is ask." N4 — Foundation 〜ていく Te-form + いく encodes action continuing forward into the future. 覚おぼえていく。"To keep learning going forward." N3 — Core 〜ていけばいい Combines both: "just keep doing X as you go — that's enough." 少すこしずつ覚おぼえていけばいい。"Just pick it up little by little." N3 — Extended ← You are here 〜ていけばいいから Adds sentence-final から as a soft reassurance close. 自分じぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから。"Just go at your own pace — that's enough." N2 — Awareness 〜ていけばよいだろう Same logic in a formal or written register. No conversational warmth. 段階だんかいを踏ふんで進すすんでいけばよいだろう。"It would be best to proceed step by step."

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 〜ていけばいいから reassures someone that steadily continuing an action is all that's needed — not a one-time fix, not perfection, just ongoing forward movement.
  • The ていく layer reframes the task as a journey already in progress. The から at the end closes the statement warmly: "this is sufficient — so relax."
  • Most commonly used in supportive speech between close friends, partners, family, or mentors. Pairs naturally with a line that removes a burden first.
  • Learn this as a complete pragmatic package — the three layers work together as a single social move.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Use 〜ていけばいい（から） to reassure someone that they just need to keep doing X as an ongoing process — not a single action, not perfection, just steady forward movement — and that's genuinely enough.

Japanese English 無理むりしなくていいよ。自分じぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから。 You don't need to push yourself. Just go at your own pace — that's all you need. Literal: Pushing yourself not-necessary. At own pace if-you-keep-doing good, because. The input sentence. Two-beat structure: remove pressure first, then offer a sustainable direction. 少すこしずつ慣なれていけばいいから、焦あせらないで。 Just get used to it gradually — don't rush. Literal: Little by little if-you-keep-getting-used-to-it good, so don't hurry. A mentor reassuring someone anxious about adapting to something new.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
This pattern is built from three layers that each carry distinct meaning.

Layer 1 — 〜ていく: The te-form of a verb plus いく (to go) creates a sense of forward movement continuing into the future. It frames the action not as something to complete but as an ongoing process — the emphasis shifts from "achieve X" to "keep going toward X." Without this layer, the pattern would suggest a single sufficient action. With it, the meaning becomes "keep going, over time."

Layer 2 — 〜ばいい: The ba-conditional plus いい marks the condition as sufficient. It doesn't say "you must" or "you should" — it says "this alone is enough." Combined with ていく, the sufficiency applies to the ongoing process itself, not a single outcome.

Layer 3 — sentence-final から: In casual speech, から at the end of a sentence doesn't always introduce a cause-and-effect chain. Here it functions as a soft close — "…and that's why it's fine / so don't worry." This use of から is one of the most common reassurance moves in casual Japanese conversation.

Japanese English ゆっくり覚おぼえていけばいいんだから、急いそがなくていいよ。 You can learn it slowly — there's really no need to rush. Literal: Slowly if-you-keep-learning good it-is, so hurrying not-necessary. The んだ variant adds a grounding tone — the speaker makes a gentle logical claim, not just offers comfort.

docs.title: Real Speech vs. Textbook Form
docs.text:
The textbook form is 〜ていけばよい. In real speech, よい contracts to いい in virtually all casual contexts — 〜ていけばいい is what you will hear and what you should produce. A further reduction exists in fast speech: ていく compresses to てく, making the pattern sound like 〜てけばいい. This is natural between close speakers but sounds too casual for most supportive advice contexts.

Form Japanese Note Natural spoken やっていけばいい What you will hear and use in casual conversation Textbook / written やっていけばよい Grammatically correct but stiff in casual speech Fast speech reduction やってけばいい Natural between close speakers; too casual for advice contexts

docs.title: Function in Context
docs.text:
• The sentence operates as a two-beat comfort structure: 無理むりしなくていいよ removes the pressure first, then 自分じぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから replaces it with a sustainable alternative.
  • 〜ていく is the critical upgrade over plain やればいい. Without it, the sentence suggests a single action to complete. With it, the doing is reframed as a continuous journey.
  • 自分じぶんのペースで personalizes the frame: the standard belongs to the listener, not an external measure. No comparison to others is implied.
  • Sentence-final から closes the utterance as emotionally complete — the speaker signals the reassurance is self-sufficient.
  • Without から: やっていけばいいよ is warm advice. With から: the conversation about worry is finished.
Japanese English 自分じぶんのペースでやればいいから。 Just do it at your own pace. Without ていく — sounds like one action to complete. The reassurance is weaker. 自分じぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから。 Just keep going at your own pace — that's all you need. With ていく — frames it as a sustainable ongoing process. The emotional reassurance is complete.

docs.title: Construction
docs.text:
Form Result What Changed 行いく (godan) 行いけばいい く→け (godan e-stem) ＋ ば ＋ いい 食たべる (ichidan) 食たべればいい Drop る ＋ れば ＋ いい する／くる (irregular) すればいい／くればいい する→すれ、くる→くれ ＋ ば ＋ いい Verb ＋ ていく (ongoing) やっていけばいい Te-form ＋ いく → ba-form of いく (いけ) ＋ ばいい ない-form (negative) 行いかなければいい ない→なければ. Casual: なきゃいい i-adj: 安やすい 安やすければいい い→ければ な-adj: 元気げんきだ 元気げんきならいい だ→なら Noun: 今日きょうだ 今日きょうならいい だ dropped before なら

Forms that don't attach

Masu-form cannot attach to this pattern — ba-conditional applies to plain forms only. Past tense + ばいい is also unnatural for permission or sufficiency contexts.

docs.title: Variations
docs.text:
Adding んだ before からDropping から entirely

やっていけばいいんだから adds a mild explanatory or emphatic nuance — the speaker grounds the reassurance in a logical claim rather than just offering comfort.

Japanese English ゆっくり覚おぼえていけばいいんだから、急いそがなくていいよ。 You can learn it slowly — there's really no need to rush. Slightly warmer and more insistent than the bare から version. The speaker makes a gentle claim.

Gains: More insistent, grounds the reassurance logically. Loses: The lighter, airy feel of the bare から version.

やっていけばいいよ or やっていけばいい are both natural and more direct — the soft reassurance close is removed, leaving clean practical advice. Still warm, but less emotionally cushioned.

Japanese English 自分じぶんのペースでやっていけばいいよ。 Just keep going at your own pace. Practical advice without the emotional close. Works well mid-conversation.

Gains: More direct; doesn't signal finality. Loses: The "you can stop worrying now" signal that から provides.

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
In English, reassurance often takes the form of direct assertions: "You'll be fine," "You've got this," "Don't worry." The speaker claims a positive outcome or quality about the listener. Japanese casual reassurance tends to work differently — rather than asserting an outcome, the speaker reframes the situation itself, replacing an overwhelming frame with a manageable one.

やっていけばいいから doesn't claim the listener will succeed. It redefines what success requires: not speed, not perfection, not keeping up with others — just continuing to move forward. The speaker shrinks the task from "achieve X" to "just keep going."

  • Burden removal before direction. The full sentence structure — remove pressure first, then offer the replacement frame — reflects a core Japanese conversational norm. Advice without first acknowledging the burden can feel dismissive.
  • Sufficiency over achievement. いい here means "sufficient, acceptable, fine" — not "good" in an evaluative sense. The speaker is not praising; they are lowering the bar to a sustainable level.
  • から as an emotional close. In English, "because" always requires a following clause. In Japanese, sentence-final から signals the preceding statement is its own sufficient reason — the speaker is closing an argument, not opening one.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text:
