Grammar Point Core Meaning Emotional / Pragmatic Nuance Trigger Context JLPT 〜ていけばいい（から） "Just keep doing X — that's enough" Warm, reassuring; frames task as sustainable ongoing journey Comforting someone overwhelmed; encouraging gradual progress N3 〜ばいい "All you need to do is X" Practical, solution-focused; less emotional warmth Giving practical permission or a simple solution N4 〜ばいいだけ "All you have to do is just X" Emphatic simplification; can feel dismissive if used carelessly Cutting through overthinking N3 〜てもいい "It's okay to do X / you may do X" Explicit permission; about individual allowance, not forward process Granting permission the listener was hesitant about N4 〜なくていい（よ） "You don't have to do X" Obligation release; pairs naturally as a two-beat comfort opener Removing an expectation the listener carries unnecessarily N4 〜ていけばよいだろう "It would be advisable to proceed by doing X" Formal, detached; no conversational warmth Written advice, formal guidance [N2 — awareness only] N2

The closest lookalike is plain 〜ばいい. The key difference is temporal framing: 〜ばいい points to a single sufficient action, while 〜ていけばいい reframes the entire situation as a continuing process. When someone is overwhelmed by the scale of a task, this distinction carries real emotional weight — the ていく layer is what transforms practical advice into genuine reassurance.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
The emotional core — removing pressure and offering a sustainable frame — must be preserved across all register shifts.

Casual Basic Polite Extra Polite

無理むりすんなって。自分じぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから。

"Don't push yourself, seriously. Just go at your own pace — that's all you need."

Shift: 無理むりすんなって adds slightly more emphatic, almost exasperated warmth. The reassurance half is identical to the input sentence.

無理むりしなくていいですよ。自分じぶんのペースでやっていけばいいですから。

"You really don't need to push yourself. Just going at your own pace is perfectly fine."

Shift: いいよ→いいですよ; いいから→いいですから. Warmth fully preserved — ですから carries the same soft close as casual から.

無理むりをなさらなくて構かまいません。ご自分じぶんのペースで進すすんでいただければ十分じゅうぶんです。

"Please don't feel the need to push yourself. Taking things at your own pace is entirely sufficient."

Shift: Keigo replaces the warm colloquial tone with respectful distance. なさらなくて構かまいません uses respectful negative permission; 十分じゅうぶんです is a formal sufficiency statement.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: Step Pattern What It Adds Example N4 — Foundation 〜ばいい Marks a single sufficient condition 聞きけばいい。"All you have to do is ask." N4 — Foundation 〜ていく Encodes action continuing forward in time 少すこしずつ覚おぼえていく。"To keep learning little by little." N3 — Core 〜ていけばいい Combines both: sufficient ongoing process 少すこしずつ慣なれていけばいい。"Just ease into it gradually." N3 — Extended ← You are here 〜ていけばいいから Adds sentence-final から as emotional close 自分じぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから。"Just go at your own pace — that's enough." N3 — Extended 〜ていけばよかった Past counterfactual — "I should have kept doing X" もっと早はやくから続つづけていけばよかった。"I should have kept it up from earlier." N2 — Awareness 〜ていかなければならない Obligation form: "must keep doing X going forward" 努力どりょくし続つづけていかなければならない。"We must keep making an effort."

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: Japanese English 最初さいしょは難むずかしくて当然とうぜんだから、少すこしずつ覚おぼえていけばいいよ。 It's completely normal for it to feel hard at first — just pick it up bit by bit. Literal: At first being-difficult is natural, so little by little if-you-keep-learning good. A senior colleague reassuring a new starter overwhelmed in a new role. 全部ぜんぶいっぺんにやろうとしなくていい。今日きょうできることだけやっていけばいいから。 You don't have to try to do everything at once. Just do what you can today — that's enough. Literal: All at once try-to-do not-necessary. Today can-do things only if-you-keep-doing good, because. A close friend talking to someone burned out and spreading themselves too thin. みんなと同おなじペースじゃなくていいんだよ。自分じぶんのやり方かたで進すすんでいけばいいから、気きにしないで。 You don't have to keep up with everyone else. Just move forward in your own way — don't worry about it. Literal: Same pace as everyone not-necessary. Own way if-you-keep-going good, so don't worry. An older sibling reassuring a younger one who feels behind their peers. まだ慣なれなくて当然とうぜんだよ。ゆっくり慣なれていけばいいんだから、焦あせらないで。 It makes total sense you're not used to it yet. Take your time — don't rush. Literal: Still not-accustomed is natural. Slowly if-you-keep-getting-used-to-it good it-is, so don't hurry. One partner reassuring the other anxious about adjusting to living together for the first time. 完璧かんぺきにやろうとしなくていい。今いまのままで続つづけていけばいいから、信しんじてみて。 You don't have to try to be perfect. Just keep going as you are — trust yourself. Literal: Perfectly try-to-do not-necessary. As-you-are-now if-you-keep-continuing good, so try to trust. A coach speaking to someone spiraling with self-doubt before a performance.

docs.title: Dropping ていく
docs.text:
Learners often drop the ていく layer and use plain やればいいから. This loses the "ongoing forward process" nuance — the sentence sounds like a one-time action to complete rather than a sustainable journey.

Incorrect

自分じぶんのペースでやればいいから。

"Just do it at your own pace." — Sounds like one action to complete. The reassurance that ongoing gradual progress is sufficient is missing.

Correct

自分じぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから。

"Just keep going at your own pace — that's all you need." — ていく makes the action ongoing and forward-moving. The emotional reassurance is now complete.

docs.title: Misreading sentence-final から as causal
docs.text:
Learners who parse から strictly as "because" expect a following clause that never comes. Sentence-final から in reassurance contexts is a soft emotional close — the statement is its own sufficient explanation.

Incorrect reading

やっていけばいいから… [何なんかが続つづく？]

"Because you just need to keep doing it… [something follows?]" — Wrong. Treating から as a causal connector expecting a result clause.

Correct reading

やっていけばいいから。

"Just keep doing it — that's all there is to it." — から closes the statement warmly. Hear it as "…and that's the reason / so don't worry."

docs.title: When NOT to Use It
docs.text:
Neutral practical instructions

This pattern has warm, intimate tone built in. Using it for a neutral practical instruction sounds over-familiar.

Avoid Use instead このボタンを押おしていけばいいから。"Just keep pressing this button." このボタンを押おせばいい。"All you need to do is press this button."

Reserve 〜ていけばいいから for situations where the task is already defined and the barrier is psychological, not informational.

docs.title: Don't Confuse With
docs.text:
〜ていけない (prohibition)

Both contain いけ, but the sources are completely different.

  • 〜ていけばいい = te-form ＋ いく → ba-form ＋ いい (permission/sufficiency)
  • 〜てはいけない = te-form ＋ は ＋ いけない (prohibition)
Japanese Meaning 自分じぶんのペースでやっていけばいい。 "You just need to do it at your own pace." — Encouragement 無理むりをしてはいけない。 "You must not push yourself too hard." — Prohibition

〜ばいい vs. 〜たらいい

Frequently blurred at N3 level.

  • 〜ばいい marks a sufficient condition: "if this is met, that's enough"
  • 〜たらいい imagines a completed outcome: "once/when X happens, that would be good" — often with a wishful or mildly frustrated undertone

In most casual contexts they overlap, but the underlying logic differs and becomes audible at the edges.

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text:
Certain words appear so frequently alongside 〜ていけばいいから that they form near-fixed chunks worth learning together.

Collocate Meaning Why it pairs naturally 自分じぶんのペースで "at your own pace" Establishes a personal standard; やっていけばいい marks movement at that standard as sufficient 少すこしずつ "little by little" Explicitly frames progress as small and gradual — both parts say the pace is not the problem ゆっくり "slowly, without rushing" Emphasizes pace over increment size; used when the task is less granular 焦あせらなくていい／急いそがなくていい "no need to rush / hurry" Completes the reassurance frame: [remove time pressure] ＋ [reframe as sufficient ongoing process] Japanese English 自分じぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから、焦あせらないで。 Just go at your own pace — don't rush. The canonical collocation. Learn this chunk whole.

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• 〜ていく carries genuine directionality. いく (to go) physically encodes forward movement through time. The listener is being told the journey they are already on is heading somewhere, and continuing on it is all that's required. This spatial metaphor is active, not decorative.
  • Sentence-final から shifts with intonation. With falling intonation it closes the statement warmly — "and that's why it's fine." With flat or rising intonation it shades toward impatience — "because I said so." In writing, context carries the tonal weight.
  • The two-beat comfort structure is a learnable social script. [Remove burden] ＋ [offer sustainable frame] appears across casual Japanese supportive speech. Jumping straight to やっていけばいい without first acknowledging the burden can feel tone-deaf.
  • 自分じぶんのペースで signals non-comparison. This near-fixed expression carries a cultural subtext: you are not being evaluated against anyone else. Combined with やっていけばいいから, the full sentence tells the listener not just what to do but how to measure themselves — by their own standard, not an external one.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text:
• What you can do now: Recognize this pattern reliably in speech and writing, understand its reassurance function, and produce it correctly in controlled contexts — especially the common chunk 自分じぶんのペースでやっていけばいいから. The grammar mechanics are within N3 reach.
  • What still takes effort: Deploying the full two-beat structure naturally in real time — knowing when to lead with burden removal before the reframe. Parsing sentence-final から as a soft close rather than a causal connector also requires deliberate attention at this stage.
  • What comes later: Through extended listening exposure, the pattern stops feeling like assembled grammar and arrives as a single emotional unit. The intonation signal on sentence-final から becomes automatic rather than analytical. That shift happens through immersion, not study.

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 〜ばいい — All you need to do is X (foundational pattern this builds from)
  • 〜てもいい — Permission: it's okay to do X
  • 〜なくていい — Obligation release: you don't have to do X

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 〜たらいい — Wished-for outcome; imagining a completed result vs. marking a sufficient condition
  • 〜ていけない — Prohibition; visually similar but grammatically and semantically opposite

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• 〜ばいい (N4) — Sufficient condition: "all you need to do is X"
  • 〜ていく (N4) — Ongoing directional action: "keep doing X going forward"
  • Sentence-final から (N4–N3) — Soft explanatory close in casual speech

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 〜ていけばよかった — Regret form: "I should have kept doing X"
  • 〜ていかなければならない — Obligation form: "must keep doing X" [N2 — awareness only]
  • 〜ていけるかどうか — Uncertainty about ongoing capacity: "whether I can keep going"

docs.title: 〜ちゃいなよ — Casual Urging to Just Go Ahead and Do It
〜ちゃいなよ — Just Go Ahead and [Do It]
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — Lower N3

The grammatical components (てしまう contraction + なさい contraction) are N4, but the combined form as a single casual urging chunk — and the ability to deploy it with natural warmth and timing — sits at lower N3.

Tier Pattern Description Example N4 — Foundation 〜てしまう／〜ちゃう Regrettable or complete action; things happening on their own or against one's will. 食たべちゃった。"I (accidentally) ate it." N4 — Foundation 〜なさい Polite but firm command — parent to child, teacher to student. 食たべなさい。"Eat up." N3 — Core ← You are here 〜ちゃいなよ Merges ちゃう + なさい into a warm, casual urging: "Just go ahead and do it — why hold back?" 先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。"Go on ahead and get in." N3 — Extended 〜ちゃいな Bare form without よ — slightly more matter-of-fact, less coaxing. 早はやく食たべちゃいな。"Just eat already." N2 — Awareness 〜てしまいなさい Uncontracted form. Rare in casual speech; sounds formal or written alongside a command. さっさと片付かたづけてしまいなさい。"Finish cleaning up at once."

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 〜ちゃいなよ is a warm, casual urging — it tells someone to just go ahead and do something they might be hesitating about, with a tone that is encouraging, not commanding.
  • It's a contraction of 〜てしまいなさい (or 〜ちゃいなさい) — the urging version of てしまう.
  • The 〜よ at the end softens it further into a coaxing suggestion among friends, family, or close colleagues.
  • Common in domestic settings, between partners, close friends, or anyone in a relaxed in-group dynamic.
  • The core nuance is removing hesitation: the speaker sees the listener holding back and nudges them forward.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Use 〜ちゃいなよ when you want to urge someone to just go ahead and do something — typically something they already want to do or should do, but are hesitating over. The tone is warm, casual, and encouraging, not a sharp command.

先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。 Go on ahead and get in. Literal: Ahead enter-and-finish — why don't you?

~Context: The input sentence. The bath is ready; one person is urging the other to go ahead without waiting for them. The hesitation being removed is the listener's restraint — they were probably waiting.~

いいから食たべちゃいなよ。冷さめる前まえに。 Just go ahead and eat, really. Before it gets cold. Literal: Never-mind, eat-and-finish, you know. Cold-become before.

~Context: Someone is waiting out of politeness. The speaker releases them from that obligation with warmth.~

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
This pattern is a triple contraction with a pragmatic overlay — each layer carries a piece of the final meaning.

Layer 1 — てしまう → ちゃう: The auxiliary verb てしまう marks completion or an action running its course — often with a nuance of something happening naturally, unintentionally, or unresisted. Contracted to ちゃう in casual speech. Here, it frames the action not as a formal decision but as something to just let happen.

Layer 2 — なさい → な: なさい is a soft imperative — firmer than てください, softer than 命令めいれい形けい. It's the standard parent-to-child or teacher-to-student command form. Contracted to な, it loses some of its parental weight and becomes a casual, in-group urging.

Layer 3 — よ: The sentence-final particle よ adds coaxing warmth. Without it, 〜ちゃいな is a straightforward urging. With it, the speaker is leaning in — "c'mon, just do it, it's fine."

Combined: ちゃう (natural completion) + な (casual command) + よ (coaxing) = "Just go ahead and let it happen — what are you waiting for?"

遠慮えんりょしないで入はいっちゃいなよ。 Don't hold back — just go on in. Literal: Restraint don't-do, enter-and-finish-why-don't-you. The full social move: remove the hesitation word (遠慮えんりょ), then supply the urging form.

docs.title: Real Speech vs. Textbook Form
docs.text:
This pattern is almost never taught in full form because it lives entirely in casual spoken Japanese. The textbook equivalent 〜てしまいなさい sounds stiff and parental by comparison.

Form Example Note Natural spoken (urging) 入はいっちゃいなよ What you will hear between family and close friends Bare spoken (matter-of-fact) 入はいっちゃいな Slightly less coaxing; still casual and warm Textbook / parental 入はいってしまいなさい Stiff; sounds like a mother telling a child to get in the bath Written / formal 入はいってしまってください Grammatical but pragmatically odd — てしまう is casual; ください is polite. Rarely combined.

docs.title: Function in Context
docs.text:
In the input sentence — お風呂ふろ準備じゅんびできてるから、もう先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ — the speaker sets up a reason (the bath is ready) and then issues the urging (just go ahead and get in). The から clause provides the permission structure; ちゃいなよ delivers the push.

  • お風呂ふろ準備じゅんびできてるから establishes that there is no obstacle — the bath is ready. This removes any practical reason to wait.
  • もう adds "already / at this point" — reinforcing that the moment for waiting has passed.
  • 先さきに explicitly frames the action as going ahead — the listener is being urged to act independently.
  • 入はいっちゃいなよ wraps the urging in warmth. The speaker isn't ordering the listener to get in — they're telling them to stop holding back and just do what they probably already want to do.

Without ちゃいなよ: 先さきに入はいって would be a simple "go in first." The urging and the warmth are missing.

docs.title: Construction
docs.text:
Step Action Result 1. Dictionary form Start with the verb 入はいる 2. Te-form Convert to te-form 入はいって 3. Add しまう (casual: ちゃう) Attach completion auxiliary 入はいっちゃう 4. Replace う with いな（さい） Imperative form of ちゃう 入はいっちゃいな 5. Add よ for warmth Coaxing sentence-final particle 入はいっちゃいなよ Verb Type Dictionary Form ちゃいなよ Form Godan (u) 言いう 言いっちゃいなよ Godan (m) 飲のむ 飲のんじゃいなよ Godan (k) 書かく 書かいちゃいなよ Godan (g) 急いそぐ 急いそいじゃいなよ Godan (r) やる やっちゃいなよ Ichidan 食たべる 食たべちゃいなよ Ichidan 寝ねる 寝ねちゃいなよ Irregular: する する しちゃいなよ Irregular: くる くる きちゃいなよ

The な is not the negative な

Learners sometimes confuse this な (contracted なさい, soft imperative) with the negative command な (食たべるな = "don't eat!"). 入はいっちゃいなよ means "go ahead and get in" — it's an urging, not a prohibition. Context and intonation disambiguate: urging な is warm and often followed by よ; prohibition な is sharp and sentence-final.

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
This pattern is built on a very specific social move: noticing someone's restraint and releasing them from it. The speaker observes the listener holding back — out of politeness, hesitation, or self-consciousness — and issues a warm nudge to remove that barrier.

In the bath sentence, the listener was almost certainly waiting for the speaker before getting in. The speaker frames the action as already completable (てしまう) and the waiting as unnecessary (もう先さきに). The urging isn't about the bath — it's about the social hesitation.

  • Hesitation is recognized before it's addressed. The speaker doesn't just say "get in the bath." They first establish that everything is ready (お風呂ふろ準備じゅんびできてるから), removing practical objections, and then address the unspoken social hesitation.
  • Completing, not doing. てしまう frames the action as something to finish up and be done with — not a heavy task, just a thing to get through. This makes the urging feel lighter.
  • In-group warmth. This form is almost never used toward out-group members. Using it signals closeness — the speaker feels comfortable enough to nudge the listener. Using it toward a superior would be presumptuous and rude.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text: Grammar Point Core Meaning Emotional / Pragmatic Nuance Trigger Context JLPT 〜ちゃいなよ "Just go ahead and do it" Warm, coaxing urgency — removes hesitation; mildly affectionate Noticing someone holding back; in-group encouragement N3 〜なさい "Do it" (firm) Parental, teacherly — firm but not harsh Authority figure to subordinate; parent to child N4 〜て／〜で "Do it" (casual request) Neutral casual request or instruction Everyday requests between equals N5 〜たら？ "Why don't you do it?" Suggestion, not urging — the listener decides Offering an option without emotional investment N4 〜ば？ "How about doing it?" Even lighter than たら? — hypothetical framing Suggesting an option neutrally N4 〜ちゃいな "Just do it" (flat) Same urging minus the coaxing よ Slightly more matter-of-fact; still warm N3 〜てしまいなさい "Finish doing it" Formal/parental command + completion Mother to child; stiff in most other contexts N4–N3

docs.title: 〜たら？（suggestion, not urging）
docs.text:
The neutral alternative. Offers an option with no emotional loading. The listener may or may not take it.

Example 先さきに入はいったら？ English "Why don't you go in first?" Intent Neutral suggestion — the speaker is offering an idea, not nudging vs. original 〜たら maintains the listener's hesitation as valid. ちゃいなよ overrides it warmly.

When to use this instead

Use 〜たら when you want to float an option without pressure. If someone might genuinely prefer to wait, 〜たら respects that. ちゃいなよ assumes they're holding back unnecessarily.

docs.title: 〜ば？（hypothetical suggestion）
docs.text:
Even lighter and less committal than 〜たら. Purely hypothetical — "if you were to do X…"

Example 先さきに入はいれば？ English "How about going in first?" Intent Light, hypothetical — no expectation attached vs. original Much cooler than ちゃいなよ. No warmth, no urgency — just a thought.

docs.title: 〜なさい（parental command）
docs.text:
The source form. Can be warm in the right context (parent to child) but carries authority the speaker may not have or want to claim.

Example 先さきに入はいりなさい。 English "Go in first." Intent Instruction with mild authority vs. original Sounds like a parent instructing a child. Between adults, なさい can sound condescending unless used ironically or affectionately. ちゃいなよ removes the authority and replaces it with warmth.

Learner trap — なさい to a superior

Using なさい toward a superior is rude. This form assumes the speaker has the right to instruct. ちゃいなよ is equally impossible toward superiors — it assumes in-group closeness. Know your relationship before using either.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
This pattern is inherently casual. Politeness markers would contradict the "just go ahead" warmth and create a register clash.

Casual Basic Polite Extra Polite

先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。

"Go on ahead and get in."

Register: The canonical form. Warm, in-group, coaxing. Exactly right for the bath context between family or close friends.

先さきに入はいってしまってください。

"Please go ahead and get in."

Shift: ちゃいなよ→てしまってください. Grammatically correct but pragmatically odd — てしまう is casual; ください is polite. The combination is stiff and rarely used. The warmth of the urging is lost.

先さきにお入はいりください。

"Please go in first."

Shift: The urging form is abandoned entirely. A standard polite request replaces it. Appropriate for a hotel staff member speaking to a guest, but the warmth and the "just go ahead" nuance are gone.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: Step Pattern What It Adds Example N5 — Foundation Te-form Core connective form 入はいって。"Enter / go in." N4 — Foundation 〜てしまう／〜ちゃう Completion; things running their course naturally or regrettably 入はいっちゃった。"I ended up going in." N4 — Foundation 〜なさい Soft imperative; parental/teacherly command 入はいりなさい。"Get in." N3 — Core ← You are here 〜ちゃいなよ Merged casual urging — "just go ahead and do it, what are you waiting for?" 先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。"Go on ahead and get in." N3 — Extended 〜ちゃいな Same urging minus よ — flatter delivery, still warm 早はやく食たべちゃいな。"Just eat already." N2 — Awareness 〜てしまいなさい Uncontracted form — rare in natural speech; stiff/parental 片付かたづけてしまいなさい。"Finish tidying up."

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: Japanese English 遠慮えんりょしないで、食たべちゃいなよ。 Don't hold back — just dig in. Literal: Restraint don't-do, eat-and-finish-why-don't-you. ~Context: Dinner with close friends. One person is hesitating to take food. The host releases them warmly.~ もう遅おそいし、帰かえっちゃいなよ。明日あしたも早はやいんでしょ？ It's already late — just head home. You've got an early start tomorrow, right? Literal: Already late, go-home-and-finish-why-don't-you. Tomorrow also early-isn't-it? ~Context: A friend noticing someone is tired but lingering out of politeness. The speaker supplies the reason and the urging together.~ 気きになるなら聞きいちゃいなよ。悩なやんでるよりそのほうが早はやいって。 If it's bothering you, just go ahead and ask. Better than worrying about it — quicker that way. Literal: If-it-bothers-you, ask-and-finish-why-don't-you. Worrying than that-direction faster, I'm-saying. ~Context: A friend hesitating to ask someone a question. The speaker frames asking as something to just get done.~ 疲つかれてるんだから、寝ねちゃいなよ。私わたしは後あとで片付かたづけるから。 You're exhausted — just go to bed. I'll tidy up afterwards. Literal: Tired you-are, sleep-and-finish-why-don't-you. I later tidy-up, because. ~Context: A partner seeing the other fighting sleep on the sofa. The から clause removes the responsibility that's keeping them awake.~ いいから言いっちゃいなよ。私わたしは味方みかただから。 Just go ahead and say it, really. I'm on your side. Literal: Never-mind, say-and-finish-why-don't-you. I ally am, because. ~Context: Someone is struggling to confess something. The speaker creates safety and urges them forward.~

docs.title: 1. Confusing な with the negative command marker
docs.text:
Learners who know 食たべるな as "don't eat" may misread 食たべちゃいなよ as a prohibition. It's the opposite — it's an urging.

Incorrect reading

食たべちゃいなよ = "Don't eat!"

~Wrong. This is なさい→な (soft imperative), not the prohibition な. The よ makes the urging tone unambiguous.~

Correct reading

食たべちゃいなよ = "Just go ahead and eat!"

~The な is from なさい, contracted. The よ adds coaxing warmth. Context between close friends or family.~

docs.title: 2. Using it toward superiors or out-group members
docs.text:
ちゃいなよ is strongly in-group. Used toward a boss, a customer, or someone you're not close with, it sounds presumptuous, overfamiliar, or outright rude.

Socially inappropriate

部長ぶちょう、先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。

~Using ちゃいなよ toward a department manager is a level violation. The form assumes in-group closeness that doesn't exist.~

More appropriate

部長ぶちょう、先さきにお入はいりください。

~Standard polite request. Respects the social distance while conveying the same practical message.~

docs.title: 3. When NOT to Use It
docs.text:
Formal settings

This pattern belongs in kitchens, living rooms, and casual izakaya — not in meetings, ceremonies, or formal written communication. There is no formal equivalent that preserves the warmth and urging.

When you're not sure about closeness

If you're uncertain whether the relationship permits in-group urging, default to 〜たら or a standard polite request. Overestimating closeness with ちゃいなよ can make people uncomfortable.

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text: Collocation Meaning いいから + 〜ちゃいなよ "Never mind all that — just go ahead and [do it]" — dismisses objections before the urging 遠慮えんりょしないで + 〜ちゃいなよ "Don't hold back — just [do it]" — removes the hesitation explicitly before nudging もう + 〜ちゃいなよ "Just [do it] already" — adds a sense of "the waiting has gone on long enough" 先さきに + 〜ちゃいなよ "Go ahead and [do it] first" — common in domestic contexts with bathing, eating, leaving 早はやく + 〜ちゃいな "Hurry up and just [do it]" — slightly more impatient; な without よ feels more directive Japanese English いいから食たべちゃいなよ。冷さめちゃうから。 Just eat, really. It's gonna get cold. The canonical collocation: dismiss hesitation, supply the urging, add the practical reason.

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• The てしまう layer is doing real work. It's not just a filler — it frames the action as finishing something up naturally, which makes the urging feel lighter than a direct command. "Just go ahead and get in" feels different from "Get in" — the completion framing reduces the weight of the action.
  • よ is what makes it coaxing, not ordering. Without よ, 〜ちゃいな is a straightforward urging. With よ, the speaker is leaning into the listener's world — "I'm telling you this for your own good." The よ signals that the speaker cares about the listener's hesitation and wants to help them past it.
  • The pattern only works when the listener already wants to do the thing. You can't use ちゃいなよ to urge someone toward an action they genuinely don't want. It nudges past hesitation; it doesn't overcome resistance.
  • から is the natural lead-in. In many natural examples, a から clause establishes the reason or permission first — お風呂ふろ準備じゅんびできてるから, 疲つかれてるんだから. The structure is: [reason/permission] + [urging]. Jumping straight to the urging without the reason can feel abrupt.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N3 Recognize the form in speech and drama; understand that な here is なさい contraction, not prohibition; parse ちゃう + な + よ as a unit; produce it in controlled practice with close friends who understand you're learning Developing — N3→N2 Deploy it naturally in the right domestic moments; feel the warmth difference between ちゃいなよ, たら？, and plain て-form; know when the relationship supports it Automatic — N2+ The form arrives as a single emotional chunk — you don't assemble it from parts; you also know exactly when it would be too warm and default to a cooler form Mastery You use it not just for urging but for the social move it encodes — noticing hesitation and releasing someone from it, with just the right amount of affectionate push

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 〜ちゃう／〜じゃう — casual contraction of てしまう; the completion layer
  • 〜なさい — soft imperative; the source of the な contraction
  • 〜ちゃいな — same urging minus よ; slightly flatter delivery

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 〜するな（negative command） — "don't do X"; な is a different な entirely
  • 〜て／〜で（casual request） — neutral instruction, no urging warmth
  • 〜たら？（suggestion） — lighter, leaves the decision to the listener

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• てしまう／ちゃう (N4) — completion auxiliary; the first layer
  • なさい (N4) — soft command; the second layer
  • Sentence-final よ (N5) — coaxing particle; the warmth layer

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 〜てしまいなさい — uncontracted form; rare in speech but useful to recognise in written or parental contexts
  • Other なさい contractions (e.g., 食たべな→食しょくえな in some dialects)
  • Dialogic urging patterns in drama and casual conversation — 〜ば？, 〜たら？, 〜といいよ

docs.title: 行いけばいいから
docs.text:
N3-Lower Focus: Advice & Reassurance Casual 行いけばいいから。 Polite 行いけばいいですから。 Conditionals Advice Quick Jumps
  • Core Meaning & Nuance
  • Grammar Structure
  • Sentence Examples
  • Tokyo 2026: Real Speech
This grammar point is a combination of the conditional **〜ば**, the adjective **いい** (good/fine), and the explanatory particle **から**. It is a very common way to offer advice that lowers the pressure on the listener, suggesting that a single, simple action is sufficient to solve their problem.

docs.title: 核かく心こころの意い味み
docs.text: ### "You just need to..." / "It's fine as long as..." The "core" of this expression is reassurance. By adding **から** (because/since) at the end, the speaker isn't just giving a command; they are providing a *reason* for the listener to stop worrying.  * **Simplifying the Solution:** It implies that while the situation might seem complex, the solution is actually quite simple. * **Gentle Encouragement:** Because of the conditional **〜ば**, it sounds less forceful than saying "Do this." It's more like saying, "If you just do this, everything will be fine, you know?"  !!! tip "Learner Tip"     In English, we often say "Just [do X]." In Japanese, ending with **から** provides that "don't worry" nuance that "just" carries in English advice.

docs.title: 接せつ続ぞく
docs.text: This uses the **Conditional (~eba) Form** of the verb.  | 動どう詞し (辞じ書しょ形けい) | 接せつ続ぞく | 結けっ果か | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 行いく | → 行いけば | 行いけばいいから | | 言いう | → 言いえば | 言いえばいいから | | 見みる | → 見みれば | 見みればいいから |   動どう詞し（ば形けい） + いい + から Verb (Conditional) + Good + Because

docs.title: 例れい文ぶん
docs.text: 無む理りしなくていいよ。自じ分ぶんのペースでやって行いけばいいから。 Don't overdo it. You just need to keep going at your own pace. Unreasonableness not doing is okay. Because if you do and go at your own pace, it is good. 困こまったときは、いつでも連絡れんらくすればいいからね。 Whenever you're in trouble, just reach out to me, okay? When troubled, because if you do contact anytime, it is good. 全部ぜんぶ覚おぼえなくてもいいよ。必要ひつようなところだけ見みればいいから。 You don't have to memorize everything. You just need to look at the necessary parts. Even if you don't remember all, it's fine. Because if you see only necessary parts, it is good.

docs.title: 現代げんだいの言いい方かた
docs.text: ### Tokyo 2026: The "Tara" Shift While **〜ばいいから** is perfectly correct and very common, in modern casual Tokyo speech, the **〜たら** conditional is often preferred because it feels slightly softer and more conversational.   Textbook 聞きけばいいから。 You just need to ask. (Standard) Modern Spoken 聞きいたらいいから。 You can just ask. (More natural/soft)   ### Contextual Softeners In very casual settings, you will often hear **〜ばいいからさ** or **〜ばいいからね**. Adding these sentence-ending particles (**さ** or **ね**) reinforces the "I'm on your side" feeling of the advice.

docs.title: 注ちゅう意い点てん
docs.text: ### Tone Check: Avoid Being Dismissive Be careful when using this with superiors. Because **〜ばいいから** simplifies a solution, using it with a boss or teacher might sound like you are "dumbing down" their problem or being dismissive of the seriousness of the situation.   * **Safe:** To a friend stressed about a test. * **Risky:** To a teacher asking for a complex report (unless you use Extra Polite forms).

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: * 〜たらいい (Conditional Advice) * 〜だけでいい (Just ... is enough) * 〜ばいいのに (I wish you would... / Regret) * 〜もの / 〜もん (Explanatory Particle - Casual)

docs.title: 1. The noun and its modifier
docs.text:
The noun is 仕事しごと. Everything before it — 終おわらせないとマズい — is a single modifier that describes what kind of 仕事しごと this is. After the noun, がある tells you what the sentence actually says about it ("it exists / I have it"), and の adds the explanatory/softening frame.

So the skeleton is:

⟦big modifier⟧ + 仕事しごと + がある + の

docs.title: 2. Internal structure of the modifier, layer by layer
docs.text:
① 終おわらせる — the causative/transitive form of 終おわる. "To finish (something) / to make (something) end." This establishes the action.

② 終おわらせない — negative form. "Not finish it."

③ 終おわらせないと — the と here is the conditional と ("if"). 終おわらせないと = "if I don't finish it." This と sets up a consequence — something bad follows.

④ 終おわらせないとマズい — マズい is the consequence: "it's bad / I'm in trouble." So the full clause means "if I don't finish it, it's bad." But critically, this clause doesn't end with a period — it lands directly on a noun, which means it's functioning as an adjective.

The entire thing — "if-not-finished-then-bad" — becomes a single property of 仕事しごと.

docs.title: 3. Processing order: Japanese listener vs. English speaker
docs.text:
Japanese listener hears it forward, in this order:

終おわらせないと → "if I don't finish something..." (what?) マズい → "...it'll be bad" (okay, but what are we talking about?) 仕事しごと → "WORK." (ah — that's the thing) がある → "...exists / I have some" の → "...that's my situation"

The noun arrives after the listener has already absorbed the full conditional evaluation. When 仕事しごと lands, it retroactively collects everything before it. The listener was building a description, waiting for the thing being described.

English speaker builds it the opposite way:

"There's work" → noun first "that I have to finish" → relative clause unpacks condition after "or I'm in trouble" → consequence comes last

English reveals the noun, then explains why it matters. Japanese explains why something matters, then reveals what it is.

docs.title: 4. Why English can't do this
docs.text:
English noun modifiers are structurally limited. Before a noun, you can stack adjectives ("urgent, unfinished work") but you can't embed a conditional clause there. You'd never say:

"I have if-I-don't-finish-it-it's-bad work"

English requires a relative clause — a post-noun structure introduced by "that" or "which" — to attach complex logic to a noun. Japanese doesn't need a relative pronoun at all. Any clause that ends in a noun-modifiable form (dictionary verb, い-adjective, な + noun, の + noun) can sit directly before the noun it describes. No connector word, no structural workaround. The position alone does the job.

This is why Japanese noun-modifying clauses can get very long and dense — there's no grammatical ceiling on what you can pack in there.

docs.title: 5. More examples of the same pattern
docs.text:
Conditional inside a noun modifier:

食たべないと体からだに悪わるいもの "stuff that's bad for you if you don't eat it" (e.g., medicine, vegetables in a parent's logic)

The conditional 食たべないと体からだに悪わるい modifies もの the same way — the whole "if you don't eat it, it's bad for your body" clause sits before the noun.

Multi-layer predicate modifying a noun:

昨日きのう買かったのに全然ぜんぜん使つかってない服ふく "clothes that I bought yesterday but haven't used at all"

Here the modifier contains a concessive (のに), a time reference, and a negative progressive — all packed before 服ふく. English needs "clothes that I bought yesterday but haven't worn at all" — two relative clause verbs joined by a conjunction. Japanese just stacks the whole narrative before the noun.

docs.title: 6. The recognition skill to build
docs.text:
The practical takeaway: when you're reading or listening to Japanese and you hit a string of verbs, adjectives, and conditionals that don't seem to have a subject or a sentence-ending — don't panic. You're probably inside a noun-modifying clause, and the noun hasn't arrived yet. Everything you're hearing is building a description. Wait for the noun to land, and the whole thing will snap into place.

The signal to watch for: a clause that could be a sentence but doesn't end like one — no final particle, no です/ます, no period-feeling — and instead flows directly into a noun. That's your modifier. The more comfortable you get with holding that information in suspension and letting the noun collect it, the more natural long Japanese sentences will feel.

docs.title: Sentence: 終おわらせないとマズい仕事しごとがあるの
1. Identifying the Modified Noun and the Modifying Clause
docs.text:
• Head noun (the thing being described): 仕事しごと
  • Full modifying clause: 終おわらせないとマズい
  • Complete sentence core: [終おわらせないとマズい] + 仕事しごと + があるの

Natural meaning: "There's a job that I have to finish." Literal gloss: "A [if-I-don't-finish-it-bad] job exists."

The modifying clause sits before the noun and acts like a giant adjective. Everything inside that clause describes the noun before you even hear the noun itself.

docs.title: 2. Layer-by-Layer Breakdown of the Modifying Clause
Layer 1 — The Action: 終おわらせない
docs.text:
• 終おわる (intransitive: to end / be finished)
  • Causative: 終おわらせる = "to bring to completion" / "to finish [something]"
  • Negative: 終おわらせない = "not finish [it]"

> N3 Note: The noun 仕事しごと is understood here as the object. Inside the clause, the full thought is 仕事しごとを終おわらせない. The noun is "extracted" and placed after the clause so it can be modified.

docs.title: Layer 2 — The Conditional Link: と
docs.text:
• Attaches to the negative verb to create a condition.
  • 〜ないと = "if [I] don't…"
  • This is the "natural consequence" conditional: the result (マズい) follows automatically.

docs.title: Layer 3 — The Evaluation: マズい
docs.text:
• Colloquial i-adjective meaning "bad," "in trouble," or "not good."
  • 〜ないとマズい = "if I don't…, it's bad" → effectively expresses obligation.
  • In formal Japanese, this would be 〜なければならない or 〜ないといけない.

docs.title: Layer 4 — Attachment to the Noun
docs.text:
• The entire block [終おわらせないとマズい] is placed directly in front of 仕事しごと.
  • Japanese allows whole clauses to modify nouns this way without any relative pronoun (no "that," "which," or "who").

docs.title: 3. Processing Order: Japanese Listener vs. English Speaker
How a Japanese Listener Receives the Information
docs.text:
1. 終おわらせないとマズい… — hears a condition and a negative evaluation, holds it in working memory.
  2. …仕事しごと — discovers what the condition applies to.
  3. …があるの — learns the sentence is existential ("there is") and that the の adds an explanatory, justifying tone.

Result: The listener assembles meaning backwards: "There's some X, and X is something where if I don't finish it, it's bad."

docs.title: How an English Speaker Would Structure the Same Idea
docs.text:
1. "There is…" — existential frame first.
  2. "…a job…" — the head noun is delivered early.
  3. "…that I have to finish." — the condition and evaluation come after the noun in a relative clause.

Key difference: Japanese is head-final in noun phrases. The description piles up before the noun. English is head-initial: the noun comes first, then the description follows.

docs.title: 4. Why English Cannot Front-Load a Conditional Inside a Noun Phrase
docs.text:
English grammar does not allow a conditional to sit in front of a noun and modify it directly. The following is ungrammatical:

  • * "An if-I-don't-finish-it-bad job exists."
  • * "There is a must-finish-or-else job."

docs.title: English Structural Workaround: The Relative Clause
docs.text:
English solves this by placing the conditional logic after the noun, wrapped in a relative clause:

Japanese (Head-Final) English (Head-Initial Workaround) [if-not-finish-bad] + job a job + [that I have to finish] [行いかないとまずい] + 約束やくそく an appointment + [that I must attend]

English converts the embedded conditional obligation into: - A modal verb ("must," "have to," "need to") - Inside a relative clause ("that…," "which…")

This is the fundamental structural gap between the two languages: Japanese can embed an entire conditional sentence in front of a noun; English must split the information and append it.

docs.title: Example A — Conditional Obligation
docs.text:
行いかないとまずい約束やくそくがある

  • Modifying clause: 行いかないとまずい
  • Head noun: 約束やくそく
  • Meaning: "There's an appointment that I have to go to."
  • Structure: [if-not-go-bad] + appointment + exists

docs.title: Example B — Multi-Layer Predicate (Conditional + Result)
docs.text:
読よまないと意味いみが分わからない文章ぶんしょうがある

  • Modifying clause: 読よまないと意味いみが分わからない
  • Head noun: 文章ぶんしょう
  • Meaning: "There's a sentence that you won't understand the meaning of unless you read it."
  • Layer breakdown:
  • 読よまない — "don't read [it]"
  • と — "if"
  • 意味いみが分わからない — "the meaning won't be understood"
  • Structure: Two predicates linked by a conditional, all placed before the noun.

docs.title: The "Gap" Inside the Clause
docs.text:
In the modifying clause, the head noun 仕事しごと occupies a grammatical slot that is not spoken aloud:

  • Deep structure: 仕事しごとを終おわらせないとマズい
  • Surface structure: [___を終おわらせないとマズい] 仕事しごと

This empty slot is called a gap. Japanese noun-modifying clauses are gap relatives: the noun is understood to belong inside the clause even though it appears on the outside. Recognizing where the gap is (object, subject, location, etc.) is a core N3 skill.

docs.title: 〜ないとマズい as a Colloquial Obligation Pattern
docs.text:
At N3, you should recognize that casual speech often replaces formal obligation patterns with evaluative adjectives:

Casual / Spoken Formal / Written 〜ないとマズい 〜なければならない 〜ないとヤバい 〜ないといけない 〜ないと困こまる 〜ないと困こまります

When you see 〜ないと + evaluative adjective modifying a noun, the speaker is expressing necessity or urgency.

docs.title: The Explanatory の
docs.text: The sentence ends in があるの, not just がある. The final の (or ん) serves as an explanatory particle. It frames the sentence as background information, a justification, or soft new information. It makes the statement feel like an answer to an unspoken question: "Why do you look busy?" → "There's a job I have to finish."

docs.title: 7. Recognition Drill for Future Input
docs.text:
When reading or listening, look for this signature pattern:

> [Clause containing a conditional] + [Noun] + [Predicate]

Specifically: 1. A verb in negative form (〜ない) 2. Followed by と, たら, or ば 3. Followed by a consequence (adjective, verb, or noun + だ) 4. Immediately followed by a noun 5. Then the main sentence predicate (がある, を見みた, が必要ひつようだ, etc.)

Mental check: Ask yourself, "What noun does this whole conditional situation apply to?" The noun is always the next word after the clause.

docs.title: 8. Summary Table
docs.text:
Element Form Function 終おわらせない Causative-negative "not finish [it]" と Conditional "if" マズい Evaluative i-adjective "bad / problematic" 仕事しごと Head noun "job" (the gap object inside the clause) がある Main predicate "there is" の Explanatory particle softens / contextualizes

One-sentence takeaway: Japanese can pack an entire conditional obligation into a pre-noun modifier, whereas English must chase the noun with a relative clause. Spot the conditional before the noun, identify the gap, and read backwards.

End of analysis

docs.title: Verbs
docs.text:
