docs.title: Causative Form — Making and Letting Someone Do Something
Causative Verb Form — させる / せる
〜させる / 〜せる — Making or Letting Someone Do Something
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — Upper N4 / Lower N3

The causative form itself is N4. The nuance layer — reading whether a causative sentence means coercion or permission, and choosing the right form for the social context — is solidly N3.

Tier Pattern Description Example N4 — Foundation Godan causative 〜せる Replace the final う-row sound with あ-row + せる 書かく → 書かかせる "make/let someone write" N4 — Foundation Ichidan causative 〜させる Drop る and add させる 食たべる → 食たべさせる "make/let someone eat" N4 — Core ← You are here Causative in context Causative embedded in natural sentences — reading making vs. letting from context; causative + なきゃいけない for self-imposed obligation 今日中きょうじゅうに終おわらせなきゃいけない仕事しごとがあってさ。 N3 — Extended Social nuance layer Distinguishing controlling vs. permissive causative; softening strategies; reading the relationship from the causative choice 子供こどもに好すきなものを食たべさせてあげた。"I let the child eat what they liked." N2 — Awareness Causative-passive 〜させられる "Being made to do something" — double stacking of causative and passive 残業ざんぎょうさせられた。"I was made to work overtime."

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • The causative form (させる / せる) expresses that someone causes another person to do something — either by making them (coercion or authority) or letting them (permission or facilitation).
  • Which meaning — making or letting — is determined entirely by context and the relationship between the people involved, not by the form itself.
  • In the input sentence, the causative is self-directed: 終おわらせなきゃいけない means "I have to get [the work] finished" — the speaker is the one both causing and experiencing the obligation.
  • The causative also appears in common set expressions — させてください (please let me do X), させてもらう (I'll take the liberty of doing X) — that you'll encounter constantly even at N4 level.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Attach させる (ichidan / irregular verbs) or せる (godan verbs) to the verb stem to express that the subject causes someone else to perform the action — either by compelling them or by allowing them.

親おやが子供こどもに野菜やさいを食たべさせた。 The parent made the child eat vegetables. Literal: Parent child-to vegetables eat-caused.

~Context: Clear coercion — the parent has authority, the child complies. Making, not letting.~

今日中きょうじゅうに終おわらせなきゃいけない仕事しごとがあってさ。 I've got work I need to get finished by end of today. Literal: By-today's-end finish-must-cause work there-is, and...

~Context: The input sentence. Self-directed causative — the speaker is causing the work to reach completion. なきゃいけない adds obligation: "I have no choice but to get it done."~

docs.title: In Real Life
docs.text:
In Real Life

Situation: It's late afternoon on a weekday. ハル messages a friend to cancel plans they had for the evening.

ごめん、今日きょうちょっと無理むりかも。今日こんにち中ちゅうに終おわらせなきゃいけない仕事しごとがあってさ。

"Sorry, tonight might be tough for me. I've got work I have to get finished by end of day."

Why this form fits here: 終おわらせる is the causative of 終おわる (to finish/end) — the speaker is framing themselves as the agent causing the work to reach completion. Combined with なきゃいけない, the sentence packages the obligation tightly: "there exists work that I must cause to be finished." It's not "I have to do work" (仕事しごとしなきゃ) — it specifically frames the finishing as the goal.

What makes this natural: The trailing さ (あってさ) softens the excuse and invites the listener into the situation rather than just stating a fact. The casual contractions (なきゃ for なければ) mark this as close-friend register. The self-directed causative is extremely common in deadline and obligation contexts.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
The causative form adds a layer of agency to a verb — it doesn't just describe an action, it describes the causing of an action. The subject of the causative verb is not the one performing the action but the one making or allowing the action to happen.

The making vs. letting distinction is not encoded in the form itself — it comes from three sources: the relationship between the people involved, the particles used (に marks the person being caused; を can mark the person in some contexts), and surrounding context. A parent making a child eat vegetables and a parent letting a child eat cake are both expressed with the same causative form — the situation tells you which is which.

Self-directed causative — as in the input sentence — is a special and very common case. When the subject and the caused agent are the same person, the causative expresses control over an outcome: 終おわらせる means "cause [it] to end / get [it] finished." This is common with result-oriented verbs like 終おわる, 止とまる, 動うごく.

Layer — なきゃいけない: This is a double-contracted obligation form. なければ → なきゃ (casual contraction) + いけない (cannot go = must). Together: "must do X / have no choice but to do X." Combined with the causative: "must cause X to happen / must get X done."

子供こどもを泣なかせてしまった。 I ended up making the child cry. Literal: Child cry-caused ended-up. Self-directed causative of result — the speaker caused the crying unintentionally. てしまった adds regret.

docs.title: Real Speech vs. Textbook Form
docs.text:
Textbooks present the causative cleanly: 先生せんせいが生徒せいとに宿題しゅくだいをさせた ("The teacher made the student do homework"). Real speech is messier — the causative appears inside longer chains, in self-directed contexts, and in contracted forms that look nothing like the textbook version.

Form Example Note Textbook (clean) 先生せんせいが生徒せいとに宿題しゅくだいをさせた。 Both agents explicit, clean causative — rarely this neat in real speech Self-directed 終おわらせなきゃ。 "Gotta get it finished" — subject and caused agent are the same, dropped entirely Embedded in noun phrase 終おわらせなきゃいけない仕事しごと Causative embedded as a noun modifier — common in real speech Permission request させてください／させてもらえますか Set expressions — learn these as chunks

docs.title: Function in Context
docs.text:
• 終おわらせなきゃいけない is the core predicate embedded in a noun-modifying clause: 終おわらせなきゃいけない仕事しごと — "work that must be gotten finished." The causative is inside the relative clause, not the main predicate.
  • があってさ closes the sentence — 仕事しごとがある ("there is work") + さ (casual sentence-final softener). The full sentence is a soft excuse delivered as a shared situation, not a formal apology.
  • The causative of 終おわる (intransitive: "to end/finish") is 終おわらせる — the speaker becomes the agent who causes the ending to happen. This is common with intransitive verbs where the speaker wants to express control over an outcome they don't directly perform.
  • なきゃ (contracted なければ) + いけない creates the obligation: "if I don't cause it to finish, that won't do / I must get it finished." The double negation (なければ + いけない) is a standard N4 obligation pattern.
  • The subject (I/speaker) is entirely dropped — recoverable from context. The caused agent (the work reaching completion) is also implicit. This level of ellipsis is natural at this register.
仕事しごとを終おわらせなきゃ。 I've gotta get this work finished. Literal: Work finish-cause-must. Contracted casual form — natural in spoken deadlines and obligation contexts.

docs.title: Construction
docs.text:
Verb Type Dictionary Form Causative Form What Changed Godan (u-verbs) 書かく 書かかせる Final く (k-row) → か (a-row) + せる Godan 飲のむ 飲のませる Final む (m-row) → ま (a-row) + せる Godan 終おわる 終おわらせる Final る (r-row) → ら (a-row) + せる Godan 話はなす 話はなさせる Final す (s-row) → さ (a-row) + せる Ichidan (ru-verbs) 食たべる 食たべさせる Drop る + させる Ichidan 見みる 見みさせる Drop る + させる Irregular: する する させる Fully irregular Irregular: くる くる こさせる Fully irregular

Godan vs. Ichidan — the common confusion

Godan causative ends in 〜せる. Ichidan causative ends in 〜させる. Learners often apply させる to all verbs — 書かかさせる is wrong; the correct form is 書かかせる. If the verb is godan, you only need せる after shifting the final vowel to the あ-row.

終おわる is godan, not ichidan

終おわる ends in る but it is a godan verb — its causative is 終おわらせる (shift わ-row to わ + せる), not 終しまわさせる. This is one of the most common godan/ichidan misclassification errors.

docs.title: Causative Particles — に vs. を
docs.text:
The person being caused to act is usually marked with に. In some casual contexts を appears instead — the nuance shifts slightly toward directness or emphasis on the object.

Particle Example Nuance に 子供こどもに野菜やさいを食たべさせた。 Standard — child is the caused agent marked with に を 子供こどもを泣なかせた。 を marks the caused person when the verb is intransitive — cannot use に here

を vs. に rule

When the caused verb is intransitive (no direct object), mark the caused person with を. When the caused verb is transitive (has a direct object), mark the caused person with に to avoid double-を sentences.

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
English "make someone do X" and "let someone do X" are structurally different sentences. Japanese packages both meanings into a single grammatical form and relies on context, relationship, and surrounding words to disambiguate. This reflects a broader Japanese tendency to leave the social relationship implicit in the structure rather than spelling it out.

The causative also encodes something English often states separately: who holds agency over the action. When a manager says 残業ざんぎょうさせた, the listener immediately knows the manager had the authority to cause the overtime — the causative signals the power relationship without stating it. When a parent says 子供こどもに好すきなものを食たべさせてあげた, the てあげる reveals it was permission, not coercion.

  • Agency over outcomes. The self-directed causative (終おわらせる, 止とまらせる) reflects a Japanese tendency to frame control over results as caused rather than simply performed. "Getting work finished" is not just doing work — it is causing the state of completion to occur.
  • Reading the relationship from the form. Native speakers read the social dynamic from the causative choice automatically. Learning to do the same is the real N3 skill — not just producing the form correctly but understanding what it reveals about the relationship between the people involved.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text: Pattern Core Meaning Emotional / Pragmatic Nuance Trigger Context JLPT 〜させる／〜せる Make or let someone do X Neutral — could be either; context determines Any causative relationship N4 〜させてあげる Let someone do X (as a favor) Permissive, generous — speaker facilitates willingly Granting permission to someone below or equal N4 〜させてもらう I'll take the liberty of doing X Humble, self-permissive — speaker requests indulgence Politely claiming permission for yourself N3 〜させてください Please let me do X Polite request for permission Asking someone above or equal for permission N3 〜させられる Be made to do X Passive — speaker is the one being caused; often unwilling Describing unwanted obligation or coercion [N2 — awareness only] N2 〜てもらう Have someone do X for you Receiving-frame — speaker benefits from another's action Requesting or receiving help N4

docs.title: 〜てもらう (receiving frame)
docs.text:
Instead of framing yourself as causing an action, frame yourself as receiving the benefit of someone else's action. Softer and less authoritative than the causative.

Example ハルに手伝てつだってもらった。 English "I had Haru help me / I received help from Haru." Intent Receiving frame — the speaker benefits; Haru's agency is respected vs. original Causative (手伝てつだわせた) implies the speaker directed Haru to help. てもらう implies Haru helped willingly and the speaker received that help gratefully.

When to use this instead

Prefer てもらう when you want to acknowledge the other person's agency and goodwill. Using the causative when てもらう would be more natural can sound cold or presumptuous.

docs.title: 〜なければならない (obligation, more formal)
docs.text:
A more formal alternative to なきゃいけない for the same obligation meaning. Grammatically identical logic, different register.

Example 今日中きょうじゅうに終おわらせなければならない仕事しごとがある。 English "There is work that must be finished by end of today." Intent Same obligation, more formal delivery vs. original なきゃいけない is casual spoken; なければならない is written or formal spoken. Same meaning, different register dial.

docs.title: 〜ないといけない (casual obligation variant)
docs.text:
Another casual obligation form that swaps in ないと for なきゃ. Slightly different rhythm — both are equally natural in casual speech.

Example 終おわらせないといけない仕事しごとがある。 English "I've got work I've gotta finish." Intent Identical meaning to なきゃいけない — natural casual obligation vs. original Interchangeable in most casual contexts. なきゃ is slightly more contracted and emphatic; ないと is a touch softer.

Learner trap — てあげる overuse

Adding てあげる to every permissive causative (子供こどもに食たべさせてあげた) sounds natural in the right context, but overusing it can sound patronising — it emphasises that you were generous enough to allow something. When the permission is neutral or expected, leave てあげる out.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
Casual Basic Polite Extra Polite

今日中きょうじゅうに終おわらせなきゃいけない仕事しごとがあってさ。

"I've got work I gotta finish by end of today."

Shift: Input sentence as-is. Casual register — なきゃいけない contracted, さ as soft sentence closer.

今日中きょうじゅうに終おわらせなければならない仕事しごとがありまして。

"I have some work that I need to get finished by end of today."

Shift: なきゃいけない → なければならない (formal obligation); あってさ → がありまして (polite て-form, soft trailing). More composed but the situational explanation function is preserved.

本日中ほんじつちゅうに完了かんりょうさせなければならない業務ぎょうむがございまして、大変たいへん申もうし訳わけございません。

"I have work that must be completed by end of today — I sincerely apologise for the inconvenience."

Shift: 今日こんにち中ちゅう → 本日ほんじつ中ちゅう (formal date reference); 仕事しごと → 業務ぎょうむ (formal term for work duties); あって → ございまして (formal copula te-form); full apology formula added. Now a formal excuse to a client or superior.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: Step Pattern What It Adds Example N4 — Foundation Plain verb forms Core godan/ichidan conjugation system the causative builds on 書かく、食たべる、する N4 — Foundation て-form + あげる／もらう／くれる Giving and receiving frames — directionality of benefit 手伝てつだってもらった。"Had someone help me." N4 — Core ← You are here 〜させる／〜せる (causative) Adds a causer — subject makes or lets someone else do the action 終おわらせる。"Cause [it] to finish / get [it] done." N4 — Extended させてください／させてもらう Causative in set permission expressions — extremely high frequency させてください。"Please let me do this." N3 — Extended Causative + social nuance Reading making vs. letting from context; register-appropriate causative choice 子供こどもに好すきなものを食たべさせた。(Made? Let? Depends entirely on context.) N2 — Awareness 〜させられる (causative-passive) Double stacking — being made to do something against your will 残業ざんぎょうさせられた。"I was made to work overtime." [Coming soon]

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
子供こどもに好すきなものを食たべさせてあげた。 I let the child eat whatever they liked. Literal: Child-to liked-things eat-caused-gave.

~Context: Parent granting permission — てあげる confirms this is letting, not making. The parent held agency and chose to allow.~

部長ぶちょうが全員ぜんいんに残業ざんぎょうさせた。 The manager made everyone work overtime. Literal: Department-head everyone-to overtime work-caused.

~Context: Authority causing compliance — coercion reading is clear from the power relationship. No one chose to stay late.~

もう少し考えさせてください。 Please let me think about it a little more. Literal: A-little-more think-cause-please.

~Context: Set expression — させてください is a polite request for permission. Extremely common in business and everyday speech.~

ハルが泣ないてて、私わたしまで泣なかされた。 Haru was crying and it made me cry too. Literal: Haru crying, me-even cry-was-caused.

~Context: Unintentional emotional causation — 泣なかされた is a causative-passive-lite reading; the crying was caused without the speaker's intention or control. Preview of the N2 form.~

今日きょうだけは好すきなだけ食たべさせて。 Just for today, let me eat as much as I want. Literal: Today only liked-amount eat-cause [please].

~Context: Causative as permission request — the speaker is asking to be allowed to eat freely. させて (causative te-form) as a casual request for permission.~

docs.title: 1. Applying させる to godan verbs
docs.text:
Learners learn させる first and apply it to everything. Godan verbs take せる after the あ-row shift, not させる.

Incorrect

書かか[させる]{.mistake}

~Godan verbs don't take させる. This would sound ungrammatical to a native speaker.~

Correct

書かかせる

~Godan: shift final consonant to あ-row + せる. 書かく → 書かか + せる → 書かかせる.~

docs.title: 2. Misclassifying godan verbs ending in る
docs.text:
Verbs like 終おわる、帰かえる、走はしる end in る but are godan. Their causative takes せる after the あ-row shift, not させる.

Incorrect

終おわる → 終おわ[させる]{.mistake}

~終おわる is godan. Applying ichidan rules produces an ungrammatical form.~

Correct

終おわる → 終おわらせる

~Godan あ-row shift: わ → わら + せる. 終おわらせる.~

docs.title: 3. Particle confusion — double を
docs.text:
When the caused verb is transitive (already has a direct object marked with を), the caused person must be marked with に, not を — or you'll have two を-marked nouns in one sentence, which is ungrammatical.

Incorrect

子供こども[を]{.mistake}野菜やさいを食たべさせた。

~Two を in one clause — ungrammatical. 野菜やさいを already takes the direct object slot.~

Correct

子供こどもに野菜やさいを食たべさせた。

~に marks the caused person when the caused verb is transitive. 野菜やさいを keeps the direct object slot.~

docs.title: When NOT to Use It
docs.text:
Using causative when てもらう is more natural

If someone helped you voluntarily, using the causative to describe it sounds like you directed them — which can come across as cold or presumptuous.

Avoid Use instead ハルに手伝てつだわせた。"I made/had Haru help." — implies authority or direction ハルに手伝てつだってもらった。"Haru helped me / I had Haru's help." — acknowledges Haru's goodwill

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text:
Collocation Meaning 〜を終おわらせる "Get X finished / cause X to end" — goal-oriented self-directed causative; extremely common in deadline contexts させてください "Please let me do X" — set polite permission request; learn as a fixed chunk させてもらう "I'll take the liberty of doing X / I'll be allowed to do X" — humble self-permission; very high frequency させてもらえますか "Would you allow me to do X?" — polite permission question 〜を泣なかせる "Make someone cry" — common emotional causative; 泣なかせる is its own near-fixed expression 〜に待またせる "Make someone wait" — 待またせてごめん ("Sorry for making you wait") is a daily-use set phrase

~These collocations are worth learning whole rather than assembling from the causative rule each time. させてください and 待またせてごめん especially will appear before you finish N4.~

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• The causative of intransitive verbs is extremely common. Intransitive verbs (those without a direct object — 終おわる、泣なく、止とまる、動うごく) are frequently used in the causative to express causing an outcome rather than performing an action. 終おわらせる ("cause it to end"), 止とまらせる ("cause it to stop"), 動うごかせる ("cause it to move") are all natural and common. This is a productive pattern worth internalizing early.
  • させてもらう is a social lubricant. This form — literally "receive the causation of X / be allowed to do X" — is one of the most useful expressions at any level because it lets you claim permission for yourself in a humble, face-saving way. 確認かくにんさせてもらいます ("I'll go ahead and check on that") is politer than 確認かくにんします and more natural than させてください in many professional contexts.
  • Causative + て-form chains. The causative te-form (させて／せて) is the gateway to させてあげる、させてもらう、させてください — three of the highest-frequency causative expressions. Master the causative te-form and you unlock all three at once.
  • 待またせてごめん is your first causative set phrase. Most learners encounter the causative naturally for the first time through this apology — "sorry for making you wait." It's worth knowing from day one even if you haven't formally studied the causative yet.

docs.title: Coming Soon
docs.text:
Causative-Passive 〜させられる

Being made to do something — the causative and passive stacked together. Expresses unwilling obligation or coercion from an outside force. 残業ざんぎょうさせられた ("I was made to work overtime") is the classic example. This section will cover construction, nuance, and how to distinguish it from the plain causative.

させてください — Permission Requests in Depth

The させてください family of expressions (させてください、させてもらう、させてもらえますか、させていただく) covers one of the most nuanced areas of Japanese politeness. This section will cover when to use each form, the social distance each implies, and the keigo version させていただく.

Causative in Formal and Keigo Contexts

How the causative behaves in business Japanese and formal speech — including させていただく (the humble causative that has become ubiquitous in service and business contexts) and how native speakers actually feel about its overuse.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N4 Produce the causative form correctly for godan, ichidan, and irregular verbs; recognise it in sentences; understand the basic making/letting distinction; use 終おわらせる, 待またせてごめん, and させてください as working chunks Developing — N4→N3 Read the making vs. letting distinction from context without conscious analysis; choose between causative and てもらう based on social nuance; start using させてもらう naturally for self-permission Automatic — N3→N2 The causative registers immediately as a social signal — you hear who has agency before the sentence finishes; させていただく sounds natural rather than stiff; you notice when someone uses the causative where てもらう would be warmer Mastery You feel the social weight of causative choice in real time — you know when using the causative instead of てもらう signals authority, and when it signals coldness; you deploy させてもらう and させてください situationally without thinking

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 〜させてください — polite permission request form; built directly on the causative te-form
  • 〜させてもらう — humble self-permission; "I'll take the liberty of doing X"
  • 〜させられる — causative-passive; "being made to do X" [N2 — coming soon]

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 〜てもらう — receiving frame; softer alternative when the other person acts willingly
  • 〜てくれる — someone does something for you without being asked or directed
  • 〜てあげる — you do something for someone else's benefit

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• Plain verb conjugation (N5/N4) — godan and ichidan forms that the causative modifies
  • て-form (N5) — causative te-form (させて) unlocks させてください, させてもらう, させてあげる
  • なければいけない / なきゃいけない (N4) — obligation form that frequently combines with causative

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 〜させられる (N2) — causative-passive stacked form
  • させていただく (N2) — ultra-polite humble causative; ubiquitous in business Japanese
  • Causative in keigo contexts (N2) — how formal speech handles agency and permission

docs.title: Transitive vs. Intransitive Verbs — 他動詞・自動詞
他動たどう詞し (tadoushi) & 自動じどう詞し (jidoushi)
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — Core N4 / Upper N4

The individual paired verbs appear from N5 onward, but the systematic understanding of transitivity as a grammatical architecture — particle choice, causative overlap, passive ambiguity, and the conceptual shift from English — is solidly N4. Many learners reach N2 without truly internalizing the Japanese transitivity mindset.

Tier Pattern Description Example N5 — Foundation Basic paired verbs Individual memorization (開あける/開あく, etc.) ドアを開あける / ドアが開あく N4 — Foundation を vs が with transitivity Core particle geometry 本ほんを読よむ / 雨あめが降ふる N4 — Core ← You are here Systematic transitivity Understanding the three morphological patterns and the conceptual split from English 落おとす・落おちる・落おとされる N3 — Extended Exceptional を + intransitive Motion verbs, point of departure 家いえを出でる、道みちを歩あるく N3 — Extended Causative-passive overlap 落おとさせられる — "being made to drop" 子供こどもに落おとさせられた N2 — Awareness Transitivity in keigo 尊敬そんけい語ご・謙譲けんじょう語ご often alter transitivity お知しりになります (intransitive respectful)

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • Transitive verbs (他動たどう詞し) take a direct object marked by を. They describe an action performed by an agent on something: ドアを開あける, 本ほんを読よむ, 電気でんきをつける.
  • Intransitive verbs (自動じどう詞し) do not take a direct object. They describe events that happen, states that obtain, or changes that occur: ドアが開あく, 雨うが降ふる, 電気でんきがつく.
  • Japanese treats this distinction as lexical and absolute. Where English often uses the same verb for both ("I opened the door / The door opened"), Japanese almost always has two distinct verbs: 開あける (transitive) and 開あく (intransitive).
  • The single most important mindset shift: Stop thinking "Who did it?" and start thinking "What happened?" Japanese sentences often describe events without agents. English sentences usually demand one.
  • There are three major morphological patterns for paired verbs, plus a handful of irregulars. Learning the patterns is more efficient than memorizing pairs in isolation.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Use を + transitive verb when an agent acts on a target. Use が + intransitive verb when an event occurs or a state changes. Do not use を with standard intransitive verbs, and do not use が with transitive verbs unless the verb is passive or potential.

太郎たろうがドアを開あけた。 Taro opened the door [transitive, agent + object]. Subject: 太郎たろう (agent). Object: ドア (target). Verb: 開あける (transitive). The door did nothing — Taro acted on it. ドアが開あいた。 The door opened [intransitive, event]. Subject: ドア (thing undergoing change). No object. Verb: 開あく (intransitive). No agent is mentioned. The door simply changed state. 電気でんきをつけた。 I turned on the light [transitive]. Agent implied (I). Object: 電気でんき. Verb: つける (transitive). 電気でんきがついた。 The light came on [intransitive]. Subject: 電気でんき. No agent. Verb: つく (intransitive). The light entered a state of being on.

docs.title: The English Speaker's Trap: A Deep Dive
docs.text: English speakers face a specific and persistent set of illusions when learning Japanese transitivity. Addressing these directly is essential for comprehension.

docs.title: Trap 1: The "Same Verb" Illusion
docs.text:
English frequently uses identical forms for transitive and intransitive meanings:

English Transitive Intransitive open I opened the door. The door opened. break I broke the glass. The glass broke. start I started the engine. The engine started. change I changed my mind. My mind changed. melt I melted the butter. The butter melted.

This creates a powerful expectation that one verb can do both jobs. Japanese almost never allows this. The door opening and you opening the door are lexically distinct events.

English-calibrated error

ドアを開あいた。

~This treats 開あく as transitive. It is not. 開あく is strictly intransitive. The correct transitive is 開あける: ドアを開あけた。~

docs.title: Trap 2: The Passive Substitution Urge
docs.text:
When English speakers want to say "The door was opened" without naming who did it, they reach for passive grammar:

English-calibrated error

ドアが開あけられた。

~Grammatically possible (passive of 開あける), but bizarrely unnatural for a simple state change. In Japanese, if the door simply opened — wind, spring mechanism, someone unknown — you say ドアが開あいた. The passive 開あけられた implies someone specifically opened it and you want to obscure or emphasize their agency.~

Natural Japanese

ドアが開あいた。

~The intransitive verb is the default for "the door opened." No passive needed. No agent implied.~

Mindset correction: Japanese intransitive verbs cover the semantic territory where English uses both intransitive ("the door opened") and passive ("the door was opened"). The passive in Japanese is reserved for when you specifically want to mention that an agent acted, but demote them grammatically.

docs.title: Trap 3: The Adjective Confusion
docs.text:
Many English states are expressed as adjectives or participles, while Japanese uses intransitive verbs:

English (adjective/participle) Japanese (intransitive verb) The window is broken. 窓まどが壊こわれている。 The door is open. ドアが開あいている。 He is dead. 彼かれが死しんでいる。 The meat is cooked. 肉にくが焼やけている。

English-calibrated error

窓まどは壊こわれた。

~This is grammatical but means "The window broke" (event), not "The window is broken" (state). For the state, Japanese uses the progressive: 窓まどが壊こわれている。~

Mindset correction: Japanese intransitive verbs often describe resultant states through the 〜ている form, where English uses adjectives. The verb itself encodes the change; the progressive encodes the resulting condition.

docs.title: Trap 4: The "Happening" vs "Doing" Orientation
docs.text:
English is agent-oriented. A standard English sentence has a subject who does something. Japanese is event-oriented. A standard Japanese sentence has a topic or subject about which something happens.

English mindset Japanese mindset "Who broke it?" "What state is it in?" "I melted the ice." "The ice melted." (default if no agent matters) "The window was broken by the wind." "The window broke from the wind." (風かぜで窓まどが壊こわれた)

The critical reframe: When describing the world, ask not "Who acted?" but "What changed?" If the change itself is the point, Japanese selects the intransitive verb automatically. If the agent's role is the point, Japanese selects the transitive.

docs.title: The Japanese Mindset: Event vs. Agent
docs.text:
Japanese transitivity is not merely a grammatical rule — it reflects a cognitive preference for describing events over assigning agency.

  • The intransitive as default. When a Japanese speaker sees a door open, they describe the door's state change: ドアが開あいた. The English speaker asks "Who opened it?" or says "It was opened." The Japanese speaker often doesn't care about the opener — the event is the information.
  • Transitivity as marked choice. Using a transitive verb (ドアを開あけた) is a claim about agency. It says "Someone did this, and I am telling you who or that they did." This is more specific and more loaded than the intransitive.
  • The を particle as agency marker. を does not just mark objects — it marks the target of directed energy. When you say ドアを開あける, you are not just describing a door and an opening; you are describing a vector of force from an agent to a target.
  • The が particle as change marker. が with intransitive verbs marks the locus of change — the thing that enters a new state. It is not "doing" anything; it is "becoming" something.

This mindset explains why Japanese has so many more intransitive verbs than English, and why they are used so naturally in daily speech. The world, in Japanese grammar, is full of things that change, happen, and obtain — not just things that people do.

docs.title: How It Works: The Three Patterns
docs.text: Japanese transitive/intransitive pairs follow three major morphological patterns. Recognizing these patterns accelerates vocabulary acquisition dramatically.

docs.title: Pattern A: ~aru (intr) ↔ ~eru (tr)
docs.text:
The most common pattern. Intransitive verbs end in ~aru (often ~waru, ~karu, ~garu, ~maru, ~baru, ~taru, ~raru). Transitive verbs end in ~eru (often ~u-ru, ~ku-ru, ~gu-ru, ~su-ru, ~tsu-ru, ~nu-ru, ~bu-ru, ~mu-ru, ~ru-ru).

Intransitive (~aru) Transitive (~eru) Meaning 上あがる (agaru) 上あげる (ageru) rise / raise 下さがる (sagaru) 下さげる (sageru) fall (price) / lower 開あく (hiraku) 開あける (akeru) open (naturally) / open (by hand) 閉しまる (shimaru) 閉しめる (shimeru) close (naturally) / close (by hand) 止とまる (tomaru) 止とめる (tomeru) stop (moving) / stop (something) 始はじまる (hajimaru) 始はじめる (hajimeru) begin (event) / begin (something) 伝つたわる (tsutawaru) 伝つたえる (tsutaeru) be transmitted / transmit 変かわる (kawaru) 変かえる (kaeru) change (self) / change (something) 集あつまる (atsumaru) 集あつめる (atsumeru) gather (intrans) / collect 分わかる (wakaru) 分わける (wakeru) be divided / understand; divide 入はいる (hairu) 入いれる (ireru) enter / put in 切きれる (kiru) 切きる (kiru) be cut / cut 折おれる (oreru) 折おる (oru) break (snap) / fold, break 破やぶれる (yabureru) 破やぶる (yaburu) tear (intrans) / tear (trans) 離はなれる (hanareru) 離はなす (hanasu) separate (intrans) / separate (trans) 倒たおれる (taoreru) 倒たおす (taosu) fall over / knock over 汚よごれる (yogoreru) 汚けがす (yogosu) get dirty / dirty (something) 壊こわれる (kowareru) 壊こわす (kowasu) break (intrans) / break (trans) 冷ひえる (hieru) 冷ひやす (hiyasu) get cold / cool (something) 燃もえる (moeru) 燃もやす (moyasu) burn (intrans) / burn (trans) 消きえる (kieru) 消けす (kesu) go out, disappear / extinguish, erase 落おちる (ochiru) 落おとす (otosu) fall, drop (intrans) / drop (trans) 流ながれる (nagareru) 流ながす (nagasu) flow / pour, flush 逃にげる (nigeru) 逃にがす (nigasu) escape, run away / let escape 過すぎる (sugiru) 過すごす (sugosu) pass (time elapses) / spend (time) 育そだつ (sodatsu) 育そだてる (sodateru) grow up / raise, bring up 立たつ (tatsu) 建たてる (tateru) stand / build 生はえる (haeru) 生はやす (hayasu) grow (plants, hair) / grow (crops) 鳴なる (naru) 鳴ならす (narasu) sound, ring / make sound, ring 動うごく (ugoku) 動うごかす (ugokasu) move (self) / move (something) 沸わく (waku) 沸わかす (wakasu) boil (intrans) / boil (trans) 縮ちぢむ (chijimu) 縮ちぢめる (chijimeru) shrink / shrink (something) 伸のびる (nobiru) 伸のばす (nobasu) stretch, extend / stretch (something) 広ひろがる (hirogaru) 広ひろげる (hirogeru) spread (intrans) / spread (trans) 深ふかまる (fukamaru) 深ふかめる (fukameru) deepen (intrans) / deepen (trans) 強つよまる (tsuyomaru) 強つよめる (tsuyomeru) intensify (intrans) / strengthen 弱よわまる (yowamaru) 弱よわめる (yowameru) weaken (intrans) / weaken (trans) 進すすむ (susumu) 進すすめる (susumeru) advance (intrans) / advance (trans) 遅おくれる (okureru) 遅おくらす (okurasu) be delayed / delay 慌あわてる (awateru) 慌あわてさせる (awateru) panic (self) / fluster (rare pair)

The aru/eru pattern is productive

Once you recognize the sound correspondence, you can often guess the partner verb. If you know 上あがる, you can predict 上あげる. If you know 止とめる, you can predict 止とまる.

docs.title: Pattern B: ~reru (intr) ↔ ~su (tr)
docs.text:
Intransitive verbs end in ~reru. Transitive verbs end in ~su. This pattern often involves destruction, dispersion, or outward movement.

Intransitive (~reru) Transitive (~su) Meaning 壊こわれる (kowareru) 壊こわす (kowasu) break (intrans/trans) 倒たおれる (taoreru) 倒たおす (taosu) fall over / knock over 汚よごれる (yogoreru) 汚けがす (yogosu) get dirty / dirty 離はなれる (hanareru) 離はなす (hanasu) separate / separate 散ちる (chiru) 散ちらす (chirasu) scatter (intrans) / scatter (trans) 回まわる (mawaru) 回まわす (mawasu) turn around / turn (something) 出でる (deru) 出だす (dasu) come out / put out, take out 売うれる (ureru) 売うる (uru) be sold / sell 切きれる (kiru) 切きる (kiru) be cut / cut 割われる (wareru) 割わる (waru) crack, split / divide, split 折おれる (oreru) 折おる (oru) snap / fold 破やぶれる (yabureru) 破やぶる (yaburu) tear (intrans) / tear (trans) 取とれる (toru) 取とる (toru) come off / take 抜ぬける (nukeru) 抜ぬく (nuku) come out, be extracted / pull out 消きえる (kieru) 消けす (kesu) disappear / erase 落おちる (ochiru) 落おとす (otosu) fall / drop 鳴なる (naru) 鳴ならす (narasu) ring / make ring 動うごく (ugoku) 動うごかす (ugokasu) move / move 揺ゆれる (yureru) 揺ゆらす (yurasu) sway / shake 震ふるえる (furueru) 震ふるわす (furuwasu) tremble / make tremble (rare) 飛とぶ (tobu) 飛とばす (tobasu) fly / send flying 跳はねる (haneru) 跳はねさせる (haneru) bounce / make bounce 泳およぐ (oyogu) 泳およがす (oyogasu) swim / let swim (rare) 転ころぶ (korobu) 転ころばす (korobasu) fall over / knock down 沈しずむ (shizumu) 沈しずめる (shizumeru) sink / submerge 浮うく (uku) 浮うかべる (ukaberu) float / float (something) 凍こおる (kooru) 凍こおらす (koorasu) freeze / freeze (trans) 枯かれる (kareru) 枯からす (karasu) wither / let wither 減へる (heru) 減へらす (herasu) decrease / decrease (trans) 増ふえる (fueru) 増ふやす (fuyasu) increase (intrans) / increase (trans) 降おりる (oriru) 降おろす (orosu) get off, descend / lower, unload 直なおる (naoru) 直なおす (naosu) be fixed / fix, cure 治なおる (naoru) 治なおす (naosu) be cured / cure 戻もどる (modoru) 戻もどす (modosu) return / return (something) 渡わたる (wataru) 渡わたす (watasu) cross / hand over 残のこる (nokoru) 残のこす (nokosu) remain / leave behind 貯たまる (tamataru) 貯ためる (tameru) accumulate / save 包くるまる (tsutsumaru) 包つつむ (tsutsumu) be wrapped / wrap 畳たたまる (tatamaru) 畳たたむ (tatamu) be folded / fold 積つもる (tsumoru) 積つむ (tsumu) pile up / pile 包つつむ (tsutsumu) 包つつむ (tsutsumu) (same form, but transitivity differs by context) 並ならぶ (narabu) 並ならべる (naraberu) line up / arrange 飾かざる (kazaru) 飾かざる (kazaru) (same form, transitivity by context)

Overlap between Pattern A and B

Some verbs appear in both patterns (e.g., 壊こわれる/壊こわす). This is because the reru/su pattern is historically related to the aru/eru pattern through sound changes. Don't worry about the overlap — just recognize that ~su transitive verbs often pair with ~reru intransitive verbs.

docs.title: Pattern C: Irregular & Same-Form Pairs
docs.text:
Some pairs do not follow a clear pattern, and a few verbs use the same form for both transitive and intransitive meanings (context and particles disambiguate).

Intransitive Transitive Meaning Pattern 吹ふく (fuku) 吹ふく (fuku) blow (wind) / blow (instrument) Same form 開ひらく (hiraku) 開ひらく (hiraku) open (intrans/trans — rare, usually 開あける) Same form 笑わらう (warau) 笑わらう (warau) laugh / laugh at Same form 閉とじる (tojiru) 閉とじる (tojiru) close (intrans/trans) Same form (ichidan) 付つく (tsuku) 付つける (tsukeru) attach (intrans) / attach (trans) ~ku / ~keru 着つく (tsuku) 着つける (tsukeru) arrive / put on ~ku / ~keru 建たつ (tatsu) 建たてる (tateru) stand / build ~tsu / ~teru 生いきる (ikiru) 生いかす (ikasu) live / let live, utilize ~iru / ~asu 死しぬ (shinu) 殺ころす (korosu) die / kill Unrelated (guree ni suru) gray / make gray Adj + suru ベージュの (beeju no) ベージュにする (beeju ni suru) beige / make beige Adj + suru

Adjective + する is a major transitive pattern

When Japanese lacks a dedicated transitive verb for an adjective's change, it uses [adjective stem + ku] + する. This is productive and essential: 大おおきくする, 小ちいさくする, etc.

docs.title: The Particle Geometry: を vs が
docs.text:
The particle choice is the immediate signal of transitivity. This is non-negotiable in standard Japanese.

Particle Role Example を Direct object of transitive verb ドアを開あける が Subject of intransitive verb ドアが開あく が Subject of passive verb ドアが開あけられた が Subject of potential verb ドアが開あけられる に Indirect target / location ドアに鍵かぎをかける で Means / location of action ドアを鍵かぎで開あける

The を/が swap is the first transitivity test

If you can swap を and が and the sentence still makes sense with a different verb, you have a transitivity pair: - ドアを開あけた (transitive) ↔ ドアが開あいた (intransitive)

docs.title: The Exceptional を: Motion and Departure
docs.text:
Some intransitive verbs of motion take を to mark the point of departure or the space traversed. This is not true transitivity — the verb remains intransitive, but を has a special function here.

Verb を usage Meaning 出でる (deru) 家いえを出でる leave home (point of departure) 降おりる (oriru) 電車でんしゃを降おりる get off the train (space exited) 卒業そつぎょうする (sotsugyou suru) 大学だいがくを卒業そつぎょうする graduate from university 通とおる (tooru) 道みちを通とおる pass through the road 渡わたる (wataru) 橋はしを渡わたる cross the bridge 歩あるく (aruku) 道みちを歩あるく walk the road 走はしる (hashiru) トラックを走はしる run the track 飛とぶ (tobu) 空そらを飛とぶ fly through the sky 泳およぐ (oyogu) 海うみを泳およぐ swim the sea 散歩さんぽする (sanpo suru) 公園こうえんを散歩さんぽする stroll through the park 旅行りょこうする (ryokou suru) 日本にっぽんを旅行りょこうする travel Japan 避難ひなんする (hinan suru) 建物たてものを避難ひなんする evacuate the building 退院たいいんする (taiin suru) 病院びょういんを退院たいいんする leave the hospital 退学たいがくする (taigaku suru) 学校がっこうを退学たいがくする drop out of school 辞職じしょくする (jishoku suru) 会社かいしゃを辞職じしょくする resign from the company 退職たいしょくする (taishoku suru) 会社かいしゃを退職たいしょくする retire from the company 離婚りこんする (rikon suru) 妻つまを離婚りこんする divorce one's wife (colloquial) 結婚けっこんする (kekkon suru) 彼女かのじょと結婚けっこんする marry her (に/と, not を)

The departure を is a special case

These を-marked phrases are not direct objects in the transitive sense. The verbs remain intransitive (they don't take a を-marked thing and change its state). The を marks the boundary crossed or the space traversed. This is an N3 exception, but recognizing it early prevents confusion.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison: Transitivity vs. Passive vs. Causative
docs.text:
The same event can be described through multiple grammatical lenses. Choosing between them is a core N3+ skill.

Form Example Meaning Nuance Intransitive ドアが開あいた。 The door opened. Event-focused. No agent. The default description. Transitive 太郎たろうがドアを開あけた。 Taro opened the door. Agent-focused. Taro's action is the point. Passive ドアが太郎たろうに開あけられた。 The door was opened by Taro. Agent demoted but present. Emphasizes the door's experience. Causative 太郎たろうが花子はなこにドアを開あけさせた。 Taro made Hanako open the door. Taro controls Hanako's action. Causative-passive 花子はなこが太郎たろうにドアを開あけさせられた。 Hanako was made to open the door by Taro. Hanako's unwillingness or victimhood. Potential ドアが開あけられる。 The door can be opened. Capability. The door is openable. Potential (intrans) ドアが開あけられる。 (Same form, different reading) Ambiguous without context. Usually transitive potential.

The passive is not the intransitive

English speakers often conflate "The door was opened" (passive) with "The door opened" (intransitive). In Japanese: - ドアが開あいた = The door opened (natural intransitive) - ドアが開あけられた = The door was opened by someone (passive, agent implied or stated)

The passive 開あけられた specifically implies an agent acted on the door. The intransitive 開あいた simply describes the door's state change.

docs.title: 1. Using the same verb for transitive and intransitive
docs.text:
English transfer error

ドアを開あいた。

~開あく is intransitive. It cannot take を. The transitive partner is 開あける.~

Correct

ドアを開あけた。 / ドアが開あいた。

docs.title: 2. Using passive where intransitive is natural
docs.text:
English passive substitution

窓まどが割わられた。

~This means "The window was broken [by someone]." If you simply found a broken window and want to say "The window broke," use the intransitive: 窓まどが割われた。~

Correct

窓まどが割われた。 (intransitive, natural state change)

docs.title: 3. Confusing を on motion verbs with transitivity
docs.text:
Overgeneralization

家いえを出でた。 → Treating 出でる as transitive.

~出でる is intransitive. 家いえを出でる uses を to mark the point of departure, not a direct object. The house is not being "exited" in the transitive sense — the verb describes the subject's movement away from it.~

Understanding

家いえを出でた = "I left home." (intransitive verb, special を usage)

docs.title: 4. Using adjective + する incorrectly
docs.text:
Pattern error

部屋へやをきれいにした。 → This is actually correct.

~But learners sometimes say 部屋へやをきれいした, omitting the に. Adjectives require に before する when making something adjective-like.~

Correct

部屋へやをきれいにした。 / 部屋へやを静しずかにした。

docs.title: 5. Thinking potential form changes transitivity
docs.text:
Category error

このドアが開あけられる。

~The potential form of a transitive verb (開あける → 開あけられる) is grammatically tricky. It often takes が on the object (ドアが開あけられる = "This door can be opened"), but the verb is still fundamentally transitive in meaning. Don't treat this as intransitive.~

Understanding

ドアが開あけられる = "The door can be opened" (potential, object marked with が due to potential grammar, but still transitive semantics)

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• Transitivity is lexical, not contextual. In English, "cook" can be transitive or intransitive depending on context: "I'm cooking" vs "I'm cooking rice." In Japanese, 料理りょうりする is transitive (requires an object or context), while 火ひが通とおる describes the cooking process intransitively. You cannot simply "use the same verb both ways" as in English.
  • The aru/eru pattern has sound symbolism. The ~aru ending often feels "lower" or "grounded" (intransitive, receiving), while ~eru feels "active" or "directed" (transitive, sending). This is not etymological fact but a useful mnemonic.
  • Some "transitive" verbs in English are intransitive in Japanese. "To graduate" is transitive in English ("I graduated college"), but 卒業そつぎょうする is intransitive and takes を only for the departure exception: 大学だいがくを卒業そつぎょうする.
  • The passive of transitive verbs often sounds unnatural for simple description. ドアが開あけられた implies a story: someone opened it, and there's a reason to mention that fact. For simple state description, ドアが開あいた is always preferred.
  • Transitivity affects keigo choices. 尊敬そんけい語ご often shifts transitivity. お知しりになります (intransitive respectful) vs ご存知ぞんじです (nominal respectful). The transitivity of the humble/respectful form may differ from the plain form.
  • Potential forms of transitive verbs take が on the object. This creates a surface similarity to intransitive sentences: 本ほんが読よめる ("I can read books" — potential) looks like 本ほんが落おちる ("The book falls" — intransitive). The grammar is different, but the particle pattern is identical.
  • Some intransitive verbs have no transitive partner. 死しぬ (die) has no simple transitive partner (殺ころす = kill is a different root). ある (exist) has no transitive partner. These gaps reveal conceptual boundaries.
  • The ~garu suffix on adjectives creates a transitive-like observation. 暑あつがる ("to show signs of being hot") allows third-person psychological description, something adjectives normally resist. This is a special transitivity workaround.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: 太郎たろうがドアを開あけた。 Taro opened the door [transitive, agent-focused]. The point is Taro's action. The door is the target. ドアが開あいた。 The door opened [intransitive, event-focused]. The point is the door's state change. No agent is relevant. 風かぜで窓まどが割われた。 The window broke from the wind [intransitive, external cause]. The で marks external cause, but the verb remains intransitive. English might use passive ("was broken by the wind"), but Japanese uses intransitive + cause marker. 電気でんきをつけてもらった。 I had someone turn on the light [receiving frame, transitive]. The transitive verb つける is used, but the receiving frame (てもらう) shifts the social focus to the beneficiary. 電気でんきがついた。 The light came on [intransitive, natural]. No agent. The light simply entered a state of being on. 雨あめで試合しあいが中止ちゅうしになった。 The game was cancelled due to rain [intransitive + nominal]. 中止ちゅうしになる is an intransitive expression. English uses passive ("was cancelled"), but Japanese treats cancellation as an event that befalls the game. 子供こどもに花瓶かびんを割わられた。 I had my vase broken by the child [passive, suffering]. The indirect passive (迷惑めいわくの受身うけみ). The speaker is affected by someone else's transitive action. This is distinct from simple intransitive description. このボタンを押おすと、ドアが開あきます。 When you press this button, the door opens [causal intransitive]. The transitive action (押おす) triggers an intransitive result (開あく). This is a standard mechanical description pattern. 部屋へやをきれいにした。 I made the room clean [adjective + transitive suru]. When no dedicated transitive verb exists, Japanese uses adjective + にする. 家いえを出でる前まえに、電気でんきを消けした。 Before leaving home, I turned off the lights [departure を + transitive]. 家いえを出でる uses the special departure を. 電気でんきを消けした is standard transitive.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N4 Memorize core paired verbs (開あける/開あく, つける/つく, 壊こわす/壊こわれる, etc.); use を and が correctly with transitive and intransitive verbs; avoid passive substitution for simple state changes Developing — N4→N3 Recognize the three morphological patterns (<sub>aru/</sub>eru, <sub>reru/</sub>su, irregular); predict unknown pairs from known ones; handle motion-verb を exceptions naturally; distinguish passive from intransitive in production Automatic — N3→N2 Transitivity becomes an intuitive filter — you feel the wrongness of ドアを開あいた before analyzing it. You automatically select intransitive for event descriptions and transitive for agent descriptions. Mastery You control the full transitivity spectrum: intransitive (event), transitive (agency), passive (demoted agency), causative (compelled agency), and potential (capability). You know when English passive must become Japanese intransitive, when adjectives need する, and when motion verbs take exceptional を. You also understand how transitivity interacts with keigo and humility.

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• を (direct object marker) — the transitive particle
  • が (subject marker) — the intransitive particle
  • てしまう / ちゃう — completion; often pairs with transitive verbs for regret
  • られる — passive and potential; transitivity-related auxiliaries

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• Passive (受身うけみ形がた) — agent-demoting, not event-describing
  • Causative (使役しえき) — agent-compelling
  • Potential (可能かのう形けい) — capability, often with が on the object
  • Adjective + する — transitive state-change when no verb exists

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• Plain verb forms (N5) — required for all transitivity pairs
  • Particle を and が (N5) — the core transitive/intransitive distinction
  • Verb conjugation (N5/N4) — recognizing ~aru, ~eru, ~reru, ~su patterns
  • Passive and potential forms (N4) — transitivity-related voice changes

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• Causative form (N4) — making someone do something
  • Passive form (N4) — being acted upon
  • Causative-passive (N2) — being made to do something
  • Honorific/humble transitivity shifts (N3) — お知しりになる, ご存知ぞんじ
  • Exceptional を with motion verbs (N3) — 家いえを出でる, 橋はしを渡わたる

Breaking down clause-before-noun structures in real sentences

Japanese vs. English sentence structure and verb-final patterns

docs.title: 1. The noun and its modifier
docs.text:
The noun is 仕事しごと. Everything before it — 終おわらせないとマズい — is a single modifier that describes what kind of 仕事しごと this is. After the noun, がある tells you what the sentence actually says about it ("it exists / I have it"), and の adds the explanatory/softening frame.

So the skeleton is:

⟦big modifier⟧ + 仕事しごと + がある + の

docs.title: 2. Internal structure of the modifier, layer by layer
docs.text:
① 終おわらせる — the causative/transitive form of 終おわる. "To finish (something) / to make (something) end." This establishes the action.

② 終おわらせない — negative form. "Not finish it."

③ 終おわらせないと — the と here is the conditional と ("if"). 終おわらせないと = "if I don't finish it." This と sets up a consequence — something bad follows.

④ 終おわらせないとマズい — マズい is the consequence: "it's bad / I'm in trouble." So the full clause means "if I don't finish it, it's bad." But critically, this clause doesn't end with a period — it lands directly on a noun, which means it's functioning as an adjective.

The entire thing — "if-not-finished-then-bad" — becomes a single property of 仕事しごと.

docs.title: 3. Processing order: Japanese listener vs. English speaker
docs.text:
Japanese listener hears it forward, in this order:

終おわらせないと → "if I don't finish something..." (what?) マズい → "...it'll be bad" (okay, but what are we talking about?) 仕事しごと → "WORK." (ah — that's the thing) がある → "...exists / I have some" の → "...that's my situation"

The noun arrives after the listener has already absorbed the full conditional evaluation. When 仕事しごと lands, it retroactively collects everything before it. The listener was building a description, waiting for the thing being described.

English speaker builds it the opposite way:

"There's work" → noun first "that I have to finish" → relative clause unpacks condition after "or I'm in trouble" → consequence comes last

English reveals the noun, then explains why it matters. Japanese explains why something matters, then reveals what it is.

docs.title: 4. Why English can't do this
docs.text:
English noun modifiers are structurally limited. Before a noun, you can stack adjectives ("urgent, unfinished work") but you can't embed a conditional clause there. You'd never say:

"I have if-I-don't-finish-it-it's-bad work"

English requires a relative clause — a post-noun structure introduced by "that" or "which" — to attach complex logic to a noun. Japanese doesn't need a relative pronoun at all. Any clause that ends in a noun-modifiable form (dictionary verb, い-adjective, な + noun, の + noun) can sit directly before the noun it describes. No connector word, no structural workaround. The position alone does the job.

This is why Japanese noun-modifying clauses can get very long and dense — there's no grammatical ceiling on what you can pack in there.

docs.title: 5. More examples of the same pattern
docs.text:
Conditional inside a noun modifier:

食たべないと体からだに悪わるいもの "stuff that's bad for you if you don't eat it" (e.g., medicine, vegetables in a parent's logic)

The conditional 食たべないと体からだに悪わるい modifies もの the same way — the whole "if you don't eat it, it's bad for your body" clause sits before the noun.

Multi-layer predicate modifying a noun:

昨日きのう買かったのに全然ぜんぜん使つかってない服ふく "clothes that I bought yesterday but haven't used at all"

Here the modifier contains a concessive (のに), a time reference, and a negative progressive — all packed before 服ふく. English needs "clothes that I bought yesterday but haven't worn at all" — two relative clause verbs joined by a conjunction. Japanese just stacks the whole narrative before the noun.

docs.title: 6. The recognition skill to build
docs.text:
The practical takeaway: when you're reading or listening to Japanese and you hit a string of verbs, adjectives, and conditionals that don't seem to have a subject or a sentence-ending — don't panic. You're probably inside a noun-modifying clause, and the noun hasn't arrived yet. Everything you're hearing is building a description. Wait for the noun to land, and the whole thing will snap into place.

The signal to watch for: a clause that could be a sentence but doesn't end like one — no final particle, no です/ます, no period-feeling — and instead flows directly into a noun. That's your modifier. The more comfortable you get with holding that information in suspension and letting the noun collect it, the more natural long Japanese sentences will feel.

docs.title: Sentence: 終おわらせないとマズい仕事しごとがあるの
1. Identifying the Modified Noun and the Modifying Clause
docs.text:
• Head noun (the thing being described): 仕事しごと
  • Full modifying clause: 終おわらせないとマズい
  • Complete sentence core: [終おわらせないとマズい] + 仕事しごと + があるの

Natural meaning: "There's a job that I have to finish." Literal gloss: "A [if-I-don't-finish-it-bad] job exists."

The modifying clause sits before the noun and acts like a giant adjective. Everything inside that clause describes the noun before you even hear the noun itself.

docs.title: 2. Layer-by-Layer Breakdown of the Modifying Clause
Layer 1 — The Action: 終おわらせない
docs.text:
• 終おわる (intransitive: to end / be finished)
  • Causative: 終おわらせる = "to bring to completion" / "to finish [something]"
  • Negative: 終おわらせない = "not finish [it]"

> N3 Note: The noun 仕事しごと is understood here as the object. Inside the clause, the full thought is 仕事しごとを終おわらせない. The noun is "extracted" and placed after the clause so it can be modified.

docs.title: Layer 2 — The Conditional Link: と
docs.text:
• Attaches to the negative verb to create a condition.
  • 〜ないと = "if [I] don't…"
  • This is the "natural consequence" conditional: the result (マズい) follows automatically.

docs.title: Layer 3 — The Evaluation: マズい
docs.text:
• Colloquial i-adjective meaning "bad," "in trouble," or "not good."
  • 〜ないとマズい = "if I don't…, it's bad" → effectively expresses obligation.
  • In formal Japanese, this would be 〜なければならない or 〜ないといけない.

docs.title: Layer 4 — Attachment to the Noun
docs.text:
• The entire block [終おわらせないとマズい] is placed directly in front of 仕事しごと.
  • Japanese allows whole clauses to modify nouns this way without any relative pronoun (no "that," "which," or "who").

docs.title: 3. Processing Order: Japanese Listener vs. English Speaker
How a Japanese Listener Receives the Information
docs.text:
1. 終おわらせないとマズい… — hears a condition and a negative evaluation, holds it in working memory.
  2. …仕事しごと — discovers what the condition applies to.
  3. …があるの — learns the sentence is existential ("there is") and that the の adds an explanatory, justifying tone.

Result: The listener assembles meaning backwards: "There's some X, and X is something where if I don't finish it, it's bad."

docs.title: How an English Speaker Would Structure the Same Idea
docs.text:
1. "There is…" — existential frame first.
  2. "…a job…" — the head noun is delivered early.
  3. "…that I have to finish." — the condition and evaluation come after the noun in a relative clause.

Key difference: Japanese is head-final in noun phrases. The description piles up before the noun. English is head-initial: the noun comes first, then the description follows.

docs.title: 4. Why English Cannot Front-Load a Conditional Inside a Noun Phrase
docs.text:
English grammar does not allow a conditional to sit in front of a noun and modify it directly. The following is ungrammatical:

  • * "An if-I-don't-finish-it-bad job exists."
  • * "There is a must-finish-or-else job."

docs.title: English Structural Workaround: The Relative Clause
docs.text:
English solves this by placing the conditional logic after the noun, wrapped in a relative clause:

Japanese (Head-Final) English (Head-Initial Workaround) [if-not-finish-bad] + job a job + [that I have to finish] [行いかないとまずい] + 約束やくそく an appointment + [that I must attend]

English converts the embedded conditional obligation into: - A modal verb ("must," "have to," "need to") - Inside a relative clause ("that…," "which…")

This is the fundamental structural gap between the two languages: Japanese can embed an entire conditional sentence in front of a noun; English must split the information and append it.

docs.title: Example A — Conditional Obligation
docs.text:
行いかないとまずい約束やくそくがある

  • Modifying clause: 行いかないとまずい
  • Head noun: 約束やくそく
  • Meaning: "There's an appointment that I have to go to."
  • Structure: [if-not-go-bad] + appointment + exists

docs.title: Example B — Multi-Layer Predicate (Conditional + Result)
docs.text:
読よまないと意味いみが分わからない文章ぶんしょうがある

  • Modifying clause: 読よまないと意味いみが分わからない
  • Head noun: 文章ぶんしょう
  • Meaning: "There's a sentence that you won't understand the meaning of unless you read it."
  • Layer breakdown:
  • 読よまない — "don't read [it]"
  • と — "if"
  • 意味いみが分わからない — "the meaning won't be understood"
  • Structure: Two predicates linked by a conditional, all placed before the noun.

docs.title: The "Gap" Inside the Clause
docs.text:
In the modifying clause, the head noun 仕事しごと occupies a grammatical slot that is not spoken aloud:

  • Deep structure: 仕事しごとを終おわらせないとマズい
  • Surface structure: [___を終おわらせないとマズい] 仕事しごと

This empty slot is called a gap. Japanese noun-modifying clauses are gap relatives: the noun is understood to belong inside the clause even though it appears on the outside. Recognizing where the gap is (object, subject, location, etc.) is a core N3 skill.

docs.title: 〜ないとマズい as a Colloquial Obligation Pattern
docs.text:
At N3, you should recognize that casual speech often replaces formal obligation patterns with evaluative adjectives:

Casual / Spoken Formal / Written 〜ないとマズい 〜なければならない 〜ないとヤバい 〜ないといけない 〜ないと困こまる 〜ないと困こまります

When you see 〜ないと + evaluative adjective modifying a noun, the speaker is expressing necessity or urgency.

docs.title: The Explanatory の
docs.text: The sentence ends in があるの, not just がある. The final の (or ん) serves as an explanatory particle. It frames the sentence as background information, a justification, or soft new information. It makes the statement feel like an answer to an unspoken question: "Why do you look busy?" → "There's a job I have to finish."

docs.title: 7. Recognition Drill for Future Input
docs.text:
When reading or listening, look for this signature pattern:

> [Clause containing a conditional] + [Noun] + [Predicate]

Specifically: 1. A verb in negative form (〜ない) 2. Followed by と, たら, or ば 3. Followed by a consequence (adjective, verb, or noun + だ) 4. Immediately followed by a noun 5. Then the main sentence predicate (がある, を見みた, が必要ひつようだ, etc.)

Mental check: Ask yourself, "What noun does this whole conditional situation apply to?" The noun is always the next word after the clause.

docs.title: 8. Summary Table
docs.text:
Element Form Function 終おわらせない Causative-negative "not finish [it]" と Conditional "if" マズい Evaluative i-adjective "bad / problematic" 仕事しごと Head noun "job" (the gap object inside the clause) がある Main predicate "there is" の Explanatory particle softens / contextualizes

One-sentence takeaway: Japanese can pack an entire conditional obligation into a pre-noun modifier, whereas English must chase the noun with a relative clause. Spot the conditional before the noun, identify the gap, and read backwards.

End of analysis

docs.title: 1. The Core Question Answered
docs.text:
Why does 食べる come before 寝る?

Because Japanese is a modifier-before-head, chronology-before-comment language. In this construction, 食べる is the skipped prerequisite (the thing you didn't do), and 寝る is the main action (the thing you did anyway). Japanese places the modifier first and the main action last. English does the opposite.

Japanese English Order prerequisite → main action main action → prerequisite Structure [without eating] + [sleep] [sleep] + [without eating] Literal eat-not-TE sleep sleep without eat-ing

docs.title: Sentence 1 — Casual Warning
docs.text:
食べたべないで寝ねちゃダメだよ。

Piece Grammar Role 食べたべないで Negative て-form "without eating" (skipped prerequisite) 寝ねちゃ 寝ねては (contracted) "sleeping" (main action, topicalized) ダメだよ Evaluative + particle "is no good, okay?"

Full meaning: "Going to bed without eating is no good." Natural English: "Don't sleep without eating."

docs.title: Sentence 2 — Polite Request
docs.text:
食べたべないで寝ねないでください。

Piece Grammar Role 食べたべないで Negative て-form "without eating" (skipped prerequisite) 寝ねないでください Negative request "please don't sleep" (main action negated)

Full meaning: "Please don't sleep without eating." Natural English: "Please don't go to bed without eating."

docs.title: 3. Three Structural Reasons for the Japanese Order
A. Modifier-Head Order
docs.text:
Japanese places all modifiers before the word they modify. This includes adjectives, adverbs, clauses, and—crucially here—negative て-forms that describe how an action is performed.

  • English: Main verb first, then the adverbial phrase ("sleep without eating").
  • Japanese: Adverbial phrase first, then the main verb ("without eating, sleep").

This is the same logic that puts adjectives before nouns (赤あかい車くるま) and relative clauses before nouns (昨日きのう買かった本ほん).

docs.title: B. Temporal Iconicity
docs.text:
Japanese strongly prefers to order events as they occur in real time. Chronologically, eating happens before sleeping. Even when the eating is negated (食べたべないで), the sentence preserves that temporal slot: the time for eating is referenced first, then the time for sleeping.

  • Japanese timeline: [time for eating: skipped] → [time for sleeping: done]
  • English packaging: bundles the main action first, then backfills the skipped step.

docs.title: C. SOV and Predicate-Final Structure
docs.text:
Japanese sentences build toward the main predicate at the end. The "most important" verb—the one carrying the sentence's illocutionary force (prohibition, request, statement)—comes last.

Sentence Main Predicate (last) 食べたべないで寝ねちゃダメだよ ダメだ (the evaluation/prohibition) 食べたべないで寝ねないでください ください (the request)

The prerequisite (食べたべないで) is structurally subordinate and therefore comes earlier.

docs.title: 4. Why English Cannot Mirror the Japanese Order
docs.text:
English is head-initial in verb phrases. The main verb must appear early, and modifiers follow. English also lacks a direct grammatical equivalent to the Japanese 〜ないで construction that can front-load a skipped action.

Ungrammatical English attempts: - * "Without eating sleep." (Main verb buried) - * "Not-eating-ly sleep." (No such adverb form)

English workaround: Prepositional phrases and subordinate clauses appended after the main verb.

Japanese Front-Loaded English Back-Loaded 食べたべないで寝ねる sleep without eating 勉強べんきょうしないで試験しけんを受うける take an exam without studying 断ことわらないで承諾しょうだくする agree without refusing

docs.title: 5. The "Gap" of the Object
docs.text: An N3-level detail: in 食べたべないで, the object (dinner, breakfast, etc.) is omitted because it is recoverable from context. The full underlying clause is something like ご飯はんを食べたべないで. Japanese routinely drops the object when it is obvious, but the grammatical slot remains. The negative て-form then attaches directly to the verb stem, creating a compact modifier.

docs.title: 6. Nuance Difference Between the Two Sentences
docs.text:
Casual Polite Form 寝ねちゃダメだよ 寝ねないでください Grammar 〜てはダメだ (prohibition) 〜ないでください (negative request) Tone Scolding, warning, familiar Direct but respectful request Focus Evaluates the situation as "bad" Directly asks the listener not to do it

Both share the same modifier-head architecture: the prerequisite (eating) comes first, the comment on sleeping comes second.

docs.title: Example A — Skipped Study
docs.text: 日本語にほんごを勉強べんきょうしないで日本にほんに来きた。 - "I came to Japan without studying Japanese." - Prerequisite (studying) first, main action (came) last.

docs.title: Example B — Skipped Permission
docs.text: 許可きょかを取とらないで入はいらないでください。 - "Please don't enter without getting permission." - Two layers of negation: the skipped prerequisite (取とらないで) and the negated main request (入はいらないでください).

docs.title: Example C — Temporal Sequence with Affirmative
docs.text: 歯はを磨みがいて寝ねる。 - "I brush my teeth and (then) sleep." - Even in the positive, the chronological order is preserved: prerequisite action first, main action last.

docs.title: 8. Recognition Pattern for N3 Learners
docs.text:
When you encounter a negative て-form followed by another verb, read it in this order:

  1. Identify the main predicate (usually the last verb or evaluative expression).
  2. Everything before it modifies the manner/condition of that main predicate.
  3. Check temporal logic: does the first action normally happen before the second in real life? If yes, Japanese has almost certainly preserved that order.

Mental template:

[Skipped prerequisite] + [ないで] + [Main action] + [Sentence predicate]

docs.title: 9. Summary
docs.text:
• Japanese order: Prerequisite → Main action → Sentence judgment.
  • English order: Main action → Prepositional backfill.
  • Japanese reason: Modifier-head alignment + temporal iconicity + predicate-final sentence structure.
  • English reason: Head-initial verb phrases require the main verb to lead.

The "eating" comes first not because Japanese is illogical, but because it is chronologically and structurally literal: it marks the skipped step in the exact temporal slot where that step would have occurred, before the main action closes the sentence at the end.

End of analysis

docs.title: Vocabulary
docs.text:
語

Vocabulary

Words that carry nuance — near-synonyms, subtle distinctions, and emotionally loaded vocabulary.

甘

甘える — To Lean on Someone's Affection

Verb nuance and emotional use — when dependency is socially accepted

› 大

大切 vs. 大事 — Both Mean "Important"

Two near-synonyms — when to use each and where they diverge

› 遅

遅れる vs. 遅くなる vs. 遅刻する vs. 遅延する

Four ways to express lateness and delay — register, cause, and scope
