docs.title: 甘える — To Behave Like a Spoiled Child / To Lean on Someone's Affection
甘あまえる — To Seek Indulgence / To Lean on Someone's Love
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — Lower N3

甘あまえる is an N3 vocabulary item, but its cultural weight and pragmatic range — from romantic intimacy to social criticism — make it a word learners continue deepening their understanding of well into N2 and beyond.

Tier Understanding What It Looks Like N3 — Recognition Word meaning "To behave spoiled / to seek affection / to lean on someone." Can recognise in clear contexts. N3 — Core ← You are here Core usage Understands 甘あまえる in relationship contexts; can parse the input sentence 今日きょうはしっかり甘あまえていいよ and similar. N2 — Extended Cultural nuance Understands 甘あまえ as a cultural concept (Doi Takeo's Amae); recognises 甘あまえる in social criticism contexts (甘あまえた考かんがえ). N2+ — Mastery Productive use Can deploy 甘あまえる naturally in emotional contexts; feels the line between affectionate 甘あまえ and negative 甘あまえすぎ.

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 甘あまえる describes the act of leaning on someone's love, affection, or goodwill — behaving in a way that seeks indulgence, comfort, or special treatment from a person who cares about you.
  • It comes from 甘あまい (sweet), and carries that sweetness into its meaning: the behaviour is sweet, childlike, dependent.
  • In the input sentence — 今日きょうはしっかり甘あまえていいよ — the speaker is giving permission to be dependent and seek comfort. The tone is warm, nurturing, and intimate.
  • 甘あまえる can be positive (intimate affection between partners, parent-child bonds) or negative (immature dependence, taking advantage of someone's kindness). Context and relationship determine which.
  • The noun form 甘あまえ is a culturally significant concept in Japanese psychology — it names a kind of emotional dependence that is considered natural and even healthy in close relationships, but which has no direct English equivalent.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

甘あまえる means to seek and receive affectionate care, comfort, or indulgence from someone — to let yourself be vulnerable, needy, or childlike in a relationship where that vulnerability is welcomed.

子こどもが親おやに甘あまえる。 The child seeks affection / acts spoiled toward the parent. Literal: Child parent-toward sweet-behaves. The prototype usage — a child clinging, whining, or seeking comfort from a parent. 今日きょうは、しっかり甘あまえていいよ。 Today, you can lean on me all you want. / Today, it's okay to be completely spoiled. Literal: Today, firmly sweet-behave-good, you-know. The input sentence. A warm, nurturing invitation to be dependent and receive comfort.

docs.title: In Real Life
docs.text:
In Real Life

Situation: ハル has had an exhausting, emotionally draining week at work. He comes home visibly depleted. His partner, ミサキ, sees his state and wants to give him space to rest and be cared for without feeling like he needs to hold it together.

今日きょうは、しっかり甘あまえていいよ。

"Today — you can lean on me completely. You don't have to be strong."

Why 甘あまえる fits here: 甘あまえる names exactly what ミサキ is inviting ハル to do — to stop being self-sufficient and let himself be cared for. しっかり甘あまえる intensifies it: not just a little indulgence, but full permission to be needy and receive comfort without guilt. The sentence is an act of emotional generosity.

What makes this natural: 今日きょうは sets a time boundary — "today" is special; normal expectations of adult self-sufficiency are suspended. いいよ grants permission warmly — this is not a command to be needy, but an invitation that removes the shame from needing comfort. The entire sentence is a love offering wrapped in grammar.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
甘あまえる is built on the adjective 甘あまい (sweet) plus the verbalising suffix 〜える, which creates an intransitive verb meaning "to behave in a sweet way" or "to act sweetly toward someone."

Layer 1 — 甘あまい (sweet): The core image is sweetness — the soft, pleasant, slightly indulgent quality of sugar. A child wanting sweets is 甘あまえる in its most literal, etymological sense. From there, the meaning extends metaphorically to emotional sweetness: seeking the sweet treatment of being loved, comforted, and indulged.

Layer 2 — The relationship structure: 甘あまえる is inherently relational. You don't 甘あまえる alone — you 甘あまえる toward someone (marked with に). The person receiving the 甘あまえ is someone in a caring role: a parent, a partner, an older sibling, a close friend. The relationship is defined by an asymmetry of care — one person provides comfort; the other receives it.

Layer 3 — Positive and negative poles: In close, consensual relationships, 甘あまえる is warm and bonding — it's the glue of intimacy. But the same behaviour outside those relationships, or taken too far, becomes 甘あまえすぎ (excessive dependence) or 甘あまえた考かんがえ (entitled, immature thinking). The context and relationship are everything.

彼女かのじょは彼かれに思おもいっきり甘あまえた。 She let herself lean on him completely. Literal: She him-toward fully sweet-behaved. Romantic context — positive, intimate, consensual. そんな甘あまえた考かんがえじゃ社会しゃかいでは通とおらないよ。 That kind of entitled, naive thinking won't fly in the real world. Literal: Such sweetened thinking, society-in doesn't-pass, you-know. Critical context — 甘あまえた here means immature, presuming indulgence from a world that owes none.

docs.title: Verb Type and Conjugation
docs.text:
甘あまえる is an ichidan (ru-verb). Its stem is 甘あまえ〜.

Form Conjugation Example Dictionary 甘あまえる 甘あまえる Polite (ます) 甘あまえます 甘あまえます Negative 甘あまえない 甘あまえない Past 甘あまえた 甘あまえた Te-form 甘あまえて 甘あまえて Potential 甘あまえられる 甘あまえられる (can lean on / can be spoiled by) Causative 甘あまえさせる 甘あまえさせる (let someone lean on you / let someone be spoiled) Volitional 甘あまえよう 甘あまえよう (let's lean on each other / I think I'll let myself be spoiled)

docs.title: Function in the Input Sentence
docs.text:
Japanese Breakdown 今日きょうは "Today" + は (topic marker — frames "today" as the special time domain) しっかり "Firmly / thoroughly / properly" — intensifies the 甘あめえ; not half-hearted indulgence, full permission 甘あまえていいよ 甘あまえて (te-form) + いい (good/okay) + よ (warm sentence-final particle) = "it's okay to lean on me"

Why this structure: 〜ていい is the standard permission pattern — "it's okay to do X." The te-form of 甘あまえる links to いい, and よ adds warm insistence. しっかり upgrades the permission from "you may" to "please do, thoroughly." The sentence is grammatically simple but emotionally rich — the grammar itself is N4; the emotional literacy to deploy it naturally is what makes it N3 and beyond.

docs.title: Japanese Mindset — 甘あまえ as Cultural Concept
docs.text:
甘あまえる is not just a verb — it names a concept that Japanese psychology and cultural studies have identified as central to Japanese interpersonal relationships. The psychoanalyst Doi Takeo published 甘あまえの構造こうぞう (The Anatomy of Dependence) in 1971, arguing that 甘あまえ — the desire to be passively loved and indulged — is a fundamental structuring principle of Japanese social life.

Key dimensions of 甘あまえ:

  • Asymmetrical intimacy. 甘あまえ operates in relationships where one person is in a caring, protective role and the other is in a receiving role. This asymmetry is not shameful — it is recognised as a natural and valuable form of human connection.
  • The prototype is parent-child. The original 甘あまえ relationship is between mother and child. Adult 甘あまえ — between romantic partners, close friends, or even within workplace hierarchies (senpai-kouhai) — echoes this prototype.
  • Permission is key. 甘あまえ is only positive when it's welcomed by the person receiving it. Unwanted 甘あまえ is emotional imposition. This is why 甘あまえていいよ ("it's okay to lean on me") is such a powerful sentence — it grants the permission that transforms potential imposition into shared intimacy.
  • No clean English equivalent. "Spoiled" carries negative judgment (bratty, entitled). "Dependent" sounds clinical or pathological. "To lean on someone" captures the physical metaphor but misses the sweetness. 甘あまえる is affectively positive in its proper context — the sweetness is real and valued.
Concept English Approximation Problem 甘あまえる (verb) "To act spoiled" / "to seek indulgence" "Spoiled" is negative in English; 甘あまえる can be warmly positive 甘あまえ (noun) "Dependence" / "passive love" "Dependence" sounds pathological; 甘あまえ can be healthy and bonding 甘あまえん坊ぼ（ぼう） "Spoiled child" / "clingy person" Mildly affectionate in Japanese; "spoiled brat" is harsher in English 甘あまったれる "To be overly spoiled / to sulk" Negative in Japanese — this is 甘あまえ gone too far

docs.title: Nuance Spectrum — Positive to Negative
docs.text:
甘あまえる sits on a spectrum from warm and bonding to immature and entitled. The same verb can shift dramatically depending on context, relationship, and the speaker's stance.

Usage Polarity Context 今日きょうはしっかり甘あまえていいよ。 Strongly positive Intimate invitation to lean on someone; consensual, welcomed vulnerability 彼かれは彼女かのじょによく甘あまえる。 Mildly positive to neutral He often leans on his girlfriend. Descriptive; tone determines judgment. たまには甘あまえるのも大だい事じだよ。 Positive "It's important to let yourself be dependent sometimes." A friend giving permission. 甘あまえるのもいい加減かげんにしなさい。 Negative "Enough with the clinging / acting spoiled." Reproach; the 甘あまえ has become excessive. あいつは甘あまえすぎだ。 Strongly negative "That guy is way too dependent / expects way too much indulgence." Criticism of another's character. 甘あまえた考かんがえ Strongly negative "Entitled, naive thinking." The speaker judges the thinking as immature and presumptuous.

The line between positive and negative is the relationship

The same 甘あまえ behaviour — seeking comfort, asking for help, wanting attention — can be sweet or suffocating depending entirely on whether the receiving person welcomes it. This is why 甘あまえる is such a relationally sensitive word.

docs.title: Related Forms
docs.text: Form Reading Meaning Usage 甘あまえ あまえ Dependence, passive love (noun) The cultural concept; also used in compounds 甘あまえん坊ぼ あまえんぼう Spoiled child / clingy person Mildly affectionate label for someone who 甘あまえる often 甘あまったれる あまったれる To be overly spoiled; to sulk or whine Negative — 甘あまえる taken to an unpleasant extreme 甘あまやかす あまやかす To spoil / to pamper / to indulge (someone) The transitive counterpart — the action of the person receiving the 甘あまえ 甘あまい あまい Sweet (adjective) The root adjective; also means "naive" or "overly optimistic" 親おやが子こどもを甘あまやかす。 The parent spoils / indulges the child. 甘あまやかす is what the parent does; 甘あまえる is what the child does. Two sides of the same relationship.

docs.title: 頼たよる（たよる）— to rely on / to depend on
docs.text:
The neutral, practical counterpart. 頼たよる is about functional dependence — leaning on someone for help, support, or resources. It lacks the emotional sweetness of 甘あまえる and can be used in formal contexts.

Example 困こまったときは頼たよっていいよ。 English "When you're in trouble, you can rely on me." vs. 甘あまえる 頼たよる is practical support; 甘あまえる is emotional indulgence. You 頼たよる a colleague; you 甘あまえる a partner.

When to use this instead

Use 頼たよる when the help needed is practical, professional, or when emotional sweetness would be inappropriate — in workplace contexts, with acquaintances, or when asking for concrete assistance.

docs.title: すがる — to cling to / to hang onto
docs.text:
More desperate and physically immediate than 甘あまえる. すがる implies urgent need, often in distress — clinging to someone physically or emotionally because you're falling apart.

Example 彼かれは彼女かのじょにすがって泣ないた。 English "He clung to her and wept." vs. 甘あまえる すがる is desperation; 甘あまえる is sweet indulgence. すがる has no warmth — it's need without permission.

docs.title: わがままを言ゆう — to be selfish / to make selfish demands
docs.text:
The negative counterpart. わがまま is selfishness or willfulness — demanding one's own way without regard for others. 甘あまえる can shade into わがまま if the indulgence is not welcomed.

Example それは甘あまえじゃなくて、わがままだよ。 English "That's not sweet dependence — that's just selfishness." vs. 甘あまえる The speaker explicitly distinguishes the two. 甘あめえ is forgiven; わがまま is not.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
甘あまえる is inherently emotional and relational. It appears most naturally in casual and intimate registers. Formal equivalents exist but lose the warmth.

Casual Basic Polite Extra Polite

今日きょうはしっかり甘あまえていいよ。

"Today you can lean on me all you want."

Register: The input sentence. Casual, intimate, warm. Between partners, close friends, parent and child.

今日きょうはしっかり甘あまえていいですよ。

"Today, it's perfectly fine to lean on me."

Shift: いいよ→いいですよ. Slightly more composed but still warm. The です adds a touch of gentleness without creating distance. Possible between a slightly more formal couple or an older parent to an adult child.

本日ほんじつは、どうぞご遠慮えんりょなくお頼たよりください。

"Today, please do not hesitate to rely on me."

Shift: 甘あまえる cannot survive in extra-polite register — the emotional sweetness is incompatible with formal distance. The speaker switches to 頼たよる (to rely on), which is structurally similar but emotionally neutral. The warmth is lost; the practical reassurance remains.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: Japanese English 疲つかれてるときは、誰だれかに甘あまえたくなるよね。 When you're exhausted, you just want to lean on someone, don't you? Literal: Tired when, someone-toward sweet-behave-want-to-become, right? ~Context: A friend reflecting on a universal feeling. The たくなる form expresses a natural, almost involuntary desire to seek comfort.~ 今日きょうだけは甘あまえさせて。本当ほんとうに疲つかれたの。 Just for today, let me be spoiled. I'm really exhausted. Literal: Today only, sweet-behave-let-me. Really tired. ~Context: The speaker is actively requesting permission to lean — 甘あまえさせて is the causative te-form, "let me be dependent." Vulnerability voiced directly.~ 彼かれは猫ねこに甘あまえられると嬉うれしいみたい。 He seems happy when the cat is affectionate with him. Literal: He cat-by sweet-behaved-when happy seems. ~Context: 甘あまえる used for an animal — the cat seeks affection, rubs against him, wants attention. The word works for pets too.~ いつまでも親おやに甘あまえてるわけにはいかないから、自立じりつしようと思おもってる。 I can't keep depending on my parents forever, so I'm trying to become independent. Literal: Forever parents-on sweet-behaving cannot-go, so independence do-try thinking. ~Context: Self-reflection. 甘あまえる here is recognised as something that must eventually end — adult independence requires moving past it.~ 子こどもが熱ねつを出だして、ずっと甘あまえてるの。大変たいへんだけど、ちょっと嬉うれしい。 My child has a fever and has been clinging to me nonstop. It's tough, but I'm a little happy too. Literal: Child fever produced, continuously sweet-behaving. Tough but, a-little happy. ~Context: A parent's mixed feelings. The 甘あまえ is exhausting but also precious — it confirms the bond. The warmth and the burden coexist.~

docs.title: 1. Treating 甘あまえる as always negative ("spoiled")
docs.text:
English speakers often map 甘あまえる to "spoiled" and assume a negative judgment. In Japanese, 甘あまえる is affectively neutral or positive in its proper context — the sweetness is real and valued. Calling someone 甘あまえん坊ぼ can be affectionate, not critical.

Overly negative interpretation

今日きょうはしっかり甘あまえていいよ。

~"Today you can be really spoiled." — The English "spoiled" makes this sound like the speaker is permitting bad behaviour.~

Better rendering

"Today, you can lean on me completely." / "Today, let yourself be taken care of."

~The speaker is offering love, not tolerating misbehaviour. The warmth is the point.~

docs.title: 2. Using 甘あまえる in the wrong relationship
docs.text:
甘あまえる presumes a caring asymmetry. Using it toward a boss, a client, or a stranger is pragmatically bizarre — you're claiming an intimacy that doesn't exist.

Relationship mismatch

部長ぶちょうに甘あまえてもいいですか？

~"May I lean on you / be spoiled by you, department manager?" — Inappropriate. The relationship is professional, not nurturing.~

More appropriate

部長ぶちょうにご相そう談だんしてもよろしいでしょうか？

~"May I consult you / seek your advice, department manager?" — 相談そうだん is the professionally appropriate verb.~

docs.title: 3. Confusing 甘あまえる with 甘あまやかす
docs.text:
Learners sometimes mix up the intransitive 甘あまえる (to seek indulgence) and the transitive 甘あまやかす (to give indulgence). The child 甘あまえる; the parent 甘あまやかす.

Wrong direction

お母かあさんに甘あまやかす。

~"To spoil toward mother." Wrong — 甘あまやかす is transitive. You 甘あまやかす someone; you don't 甘あまやかす toward someone.~

Correct

お母かあさんに甘あまえる。 / お母かあさんが子こどもを甘あまやかす。

~"To lean on mother." / "The mother spoils the child." Two different verbs, two different directions.~

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text: Collocation Meaning しっかり甘あまえる "To lean on someone completely / without holding back" — the しっかり intensifies; full permission to be needy 思おもいっきり甘あまえる "To let oneself lean on someone with abandon" — similar intensification, slightly more emotional 甘あまえていいよ "It's okay to lean on me" — the standard permission-granting phrase 甘あまえさせて "Let me lean on you / let me be spoiled" — the speaker requests permission 甘あまえすぎ "Excessive dependence" — negative; the 甘あまえ has crossed a line 甘あまえた考かんがえ "Entitled / naive thinking" — strongly negative; criticising immaturity 甘あまえん坊ぼ "Clingy person / spoiled child" — mildly affectionate or lightly critical depending on tone Japanese English たまには思おもいっきり甘あまえるのも必要ひつようだよ。 Sometimes you need to just let yourself lean on someone completely. The collocation 思おもいっきり甘あまえる paired with 必要ひつよう — the speaker frames healthy dependence as a human need.

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• 甘あまえ is culturally foundational in a way that has no English parallel. The fact that Japanese has a single, everyday verb for "seeking affectionate indulgence" — and that this verb is the subject of a famous psychoanalytic book — tells you something about the culture. Emotional dependence is named, recognised, and valued (within its proper bounds) in a way that English-speaking cultures, with their emphasis on independence and self-reliance, struggle to articulate.
  • The 〜ていい permission structure is doing heavy emotional work. 甘あまえていいよ is not just "you may lean on me" — it's "I welcome this, I want this from you, you are not a burden." The いい transforms potential shame (am I being too needy?) into shared intimacy.
  • 今日きょうは is not just "today" — it's a bounded permission space. The は delimits the permission: today is special. This protects both people — the giver isn't agreeing to unlimited 甘あめえ; the receiver knows the permission is temporary and therefore can accept it without guilt. The time boundary makes the offering sustainable.
  • 甘あまやかす and 甘あまえる are a pair worth learning together. The caregiver 甘あまやかす; the care-receiver 甘あまえる. Mastering both gives you the full relational vocabulary for describing this dynamic from either side.
  • The word can describe animal behaviour, not just human. Cats 甘あまえる when they rub against you; dogs 甘あまえる when they nuzzle for attention. The extension to animals confirms that the core meaning is pre-verbal and physical — it's about warm, dependent body-to-body comfort.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N3 Recognise 甘あまえる in clear relationship contexts; understand the input sentence 今日きょうはしっかり甘あまえていいよ; know the word means "to lean on someone affectionately" and not just "to be spoiled" Developing — N3→N2 Distinguish positive 甘あまえる (intimate, consensual) from negative 甘あまえ (excessive, entitled); understand 甘あまえ as a cultural concept; use 甘あまえていいよ and 甘あまえさせて naturally in close relationships Automatic — N2+ Deploy 甘あまえる with emotional precision — you feel whether a situation calls for 甘あまえる, 頼たよる, or わがまま; you recognise when someone else's 甘あまえ is being welcomed or imposed Mastery The cultural concept of 甘あまえ is fully internalised — you understand not just the word but the relational logic it names, and you can navigate its boundaries intuitively across different relationship types

docs.title: Same Word Family
docs.text:
• 甘あまい（あまい） — sweet; naive; overly optimistic (the root adjective)
  • 甘あまやかす（あまやかす） — to spoil / to pamper / to indulge someone (transitive counterpart)
  • 甘あまったれる（あまったれる） — to be overly spoiled; to sulk or whine (negative extreme)
  • 甘あまえん坊ぼ（あまえんぼう） — a clingy person; a spoiled child (noun label, mildly affectionate or critical)
  • 甘味あまみ（あまみ／かんみ） — sweetness (flavour); also a sweet snack or dessert

docs.title: Related Concepts
docs.text:
• 頼たよる（たよる） — to rely on / to depend on (practical, neutral counterpart)
  • すがる — to cling to / to hang onto (desperate, urgent counterpart)
  • わがまま — selfishness / willfulness (the negative boundary 甘あまえ can cross)
  • 遠慮えんりょ（えんりょ） — restraint / holding back (the opposite of 甘あまえる — 遠慮えんりょ is what you release when you 甘あまえる)

docs.title: Cultural Reference
docs.text: • Doi Takeo, 甘あまえの構造こうぞう (The Anatomy of Dependence, 1971) — the classic psychoanalytic study of 甘あまえ as a structuring principle of Japanese relationships. N1+ reading level, but the concept is accessible much earlier.

docs.title: 遅れる vs. 遅くなる vs. 遅刻する vs. 遅延する — Lateness & Delay
Lateness & Delay — Four Ways to Be Late
遅おくれる・遅おそくなる・遅刻ちこくする・遅延ちえんする
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — Mid N3

The individual verbs are N4–N5 vocabulary, but distinguishing them pragmatically — knowing which to use for personal tardiness, schedule shifts, formal appointments, or systemic delays — is solidly N3. Native speakers never confuse these; learners often reach for the wrong one and reveal non-native intuition.

Tier Pattern Description Example N5 — Foundation 遅おそい (i-adjective) "Late" — descriptive state 電車でんしゃが遅おそい。"The train is late." N4 — Foundation 遅おくれる Intransitive verb — "to be delayed / to run late" 寝坊ねぼうして遅おくれた。"I overslept and was late." N4 — Foundation 遅おそくなる "Become late" — state change, often scheduled 会議かいぎが遅おそくなる。"The meeting will run late." N3 — Core ← You are here 遅刻ちこくする vs. 遅延ちえんする Formal lateness (personal) vs. systemic delay (organizational) 遅刻ちこくしました。"I was tardy." / 遅延ちえんしています。"There is a delay." N2 — Awareness 延のびる / 伸のびる "To extend / stretch" — duration expansion 会議かいぎが延のびた。"The meeting ran over."

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 遅おくれる — Personal, intransitive, often fault-implied. You missed the mark. "I was delayed / I ran late."
  • 遅おそくなる — Scheduled or predicted shift. The time changes, not necessarily anyone's fault. "It will be late / run late."
  • 遅刻ちこくする — Formal, institutional, personal tardiness. Used for appointments, school, work. "To be tardy / arrive late."
  • 遅延ちえんする — Systemic, organizational, mechanical delay. Trains, flights, projects. "To be delayed [system-wide]."
  • The key distinction: 遅おくれる is about failing to arrive on time; 遅おそくなる is about a schedule shifting later; 遅刻ちこくする is the formal institutional word for personal tardiness; 遅延ちえんする is the formal word for systemic delay.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Choose your lateness verb based on agency (whose fault?), formality (institutional or personal?), and system scope (individual or organizational?).

寝坊ねぼうして遅おくれた。 I overslept and was late [personal, my fault]. Literal: Overslept did [te-form] was-delayed. Context: Personal explanation. 遅おくれる is intransitive — the lateness happened to me, but the oversleeping implies my agency. Common for daily life, friends, informal workplace. 会議かいぎが遅おそくなる。 The meeting will run late [schedule shift]. Literal: Meeting late become. Context: Announcing a schedule change. No fault implied — the meeting simply extends. Used for plans, appointments, events that shift later. 電車でんしゃに遅刻ちこくした。 I was late for the train [formal, institutional]. Literal: Train-to tardiness did. Context: Formal apology or report. 遅刻ちこくする is the institutional word for failing to arrive by a designated time. School, work, appointments. 飛行機ひこうきが遅延ちえんしています。 The flight is delayed [systemic, organizational]. Literal: Flight delay doing [polite progressive]. Context: Airport announcement. 遅延ちえんする is never personal — it describes mechanical, operational, or systemic delays.

docs.title: 1. 遅おくれる — Personal Delay (Intransitive)
docs.text:
Attribute Value Transitivity Intransitive (自動じどう詞し) Agency Often implied fault, but grammatically the subject is affected Formality Casual to standard Scope Individual Collocations 電車でんしゃに遅おくれる, 寝坊ねぼうして遅おくれる, 少すこし遅おくれる

The grammar of 遅おくれる: - Takes に for the missed target (電車でんしゃに遅おくれる — "late for the train") - Often paired with reasons in て-form (寝坊ねぼうして遅おくれる — "oversleep and be late") - Past tense 遅おくれた is a common apology opener

電車でんしゃに遅おくれそう。 I think I'm going to miss the train [personal prediction]. The そう adds prediction. The subject is "I" (implied), not the train. 雨あめで遅おくれた。 I was delayed by rain [external cause, but still personal]. The で marks external cause, but the subject is still the person who failed to arrive on time.

遅おくれる vs. 遅刻ちこくする

Both describe personal lateness, but 遅おくれる is the everyday word while 遅刻ちこくする is the institutional/formal one. You 遅おくれる to meet a friend; you 遅刻ちこくする to class or work.

docs.title: 2. 遅おそくなる — Schedule Shift (Adjective + なる)
docs.text:
Attribute Value Structure i-adjective 遅おそい + なる (to become) Agency Neutral — no fault implied Formality Casual to standard Scope Event, schedule, plan Collocations 会議かいぎが遅おそくなる, 到着とうちゃくが遅おそくなる, 終おわりが遅おそくなる

The grammar of 遅おそくなる: - The subject is the event/time, not the person - Often used for future predictions (〜になる) - Can be modified with extent: 30分ふん遅おそくなる ("become 30 minutes late")

今日きょうの終業しゅうぎょうは遅おそくなります。 Today's closing will be delayed [schedule announcement]. The subject is 終業しゅうぎょう (closing time), not a person. This is a neutral schedule shift. 到着とうちゃくが一時間いちじかん遅おそくなる。 Arrival will be one hour late. The 一いち時間じかん specifies the extent. The subject is 到着とうちゃく (arrival), an event.

遅おそくなる vs. 遅おくれる

遅おそくなる is about the clock shifting. 遅おくれる is about failing to meet the clock. A meeting 遅おそくなる (starts later); a person 遅おくれる (arrives after the start).

docs.title: 3. 遅刻ちこくする — Institutional Tardiness (Formal Personal)
docs.text:
Attribute Value Register Formal, institutional Agency Personal fault (usually) Scope Individual within an institution Collocations 遅刻ちこくする, 遅刻ちこくしました (apology), 遅刻ちこく扱あつかい (treated as tardy), 無断むだん遅刻ちこく (unauthorized tardiness)

The grammar of 遅刻ちこくする: - Suru-verb (する verb) — formal and countable - Often used in institutional apologies (遅刻ちこくしました) - Can be nominalized: 遅刻ちこくは禁止きんしです ("Tardiness is prohibited")

朝礼ちょうれいに遅刻ちこくしました。 I was late for the morning assembly [formal apology]. Standard workplace/school apology. Using 遅おくれる here would sound too casual. 三回さんかいの遅刻ちこくで警告けいこくです。 Three instances of tardiness result in a warning. Nominalized usage. 遅刻ちこく becomes a countable institutional offense.

遅刻ちこくする is never systemic

You cannot say 電車でんしゃが遅刻ちこくした for a train delay. 遅刻ちこくする requires a person who has failed to meet an institutional schedule.

docs.title: 4. 遅延ちえんする — Systemic Delay (Organizational)
docs.text:
Attribute Value Register Formal, technical, public Agency Organizational/systemic — no individual fault Scope Trains, flights, projects, services Collocations 遅延ちえんする, 遅延ちえん情報じょうほう (delay info), 大幅おおはば遅延ちえん (major delay), 遅延ちえん証明しょうめい書しょ (delay certificate)

The grammar of 遅延ちえんする: - Suru-verb — formal and technical - Often progressive (遅延ちえんしています) for announcements - Used in public announcements, news, official reports

本日ほんじつの新幹線しんかんせんは大雪おおゆきの影響えいきょうで遅延ちえんしています。 Today's Shinkansen is delayed due to heavy snow [announcement]. Standard JR announcement. 遅延ちえんしています is the fixed public formula. 遅延ちえん証明書しょうめいしょを発行はっこうします。 We will issue a delay certificate. The 遅延ちえん証明しょうめい書しょ is a formal document from train companies to excuse workplace tardiness.

遅延ちえんする is never personal

You cannot use 遅延ちえんする for yourself. It is exclusively for systems, organizations, and mechanical operations.

docs.title: Comparative Matrix
docs.text: Scenario 遅おくれる 遅おそくなる 遅刻ちこくする 遅延ちえんする I missed my train ✓ ✗ ✗ ✗ The train is running late ✗ ✗ ✗ ✓ The meeting will end later ✗ ✓ ✗ ✗ I was late to work (informal) ✓ ✗ ✗ ✗ I was late to work (formal apology) ✗ ✗ ✓ ✗ The flight is postponed ✗ ✗ ✗ ✓ My arrival time shifted later ✗ ✓ ✗ ✗ I overslept and was late ✓ ✗ ✗ ✗ Three tardies = warning ✗ ✗ ✓ (nominalized) ✗ Project timeline extended ✗ ✗ ✗ ✓

docs.title: In Real Life
docs.text:
Morning rush, informal

Situation: タクヤ texts his friend シュン.

寝坊ねぼうして遅おくれた。10分ぷんくらい待まってて。

"I overslept and was late. Wait about 10 minutes."

Why 遅おくれた: Personal, informal, fault-implied. 遅刻ちこくした would sound oddly formal for a friend. 遅延ちえん or 遅おそくなる don't fit — this is about his personal failure to arrive, not a schedule shift or system delay.

Workplace announcement

Situation: Manager emails team about a meeting.

会議かいぎが30分ぷん遅おそくなります。

"The meeting will be 30 minutes late."

Why 遅おそくなる: The meeting time shifts. No fault, no person — just a schedule change. 遅おくれる would imply someone failed; 遅刻ちこくする or 遅延ちえんする are wrong register and scope.

Formal apology to teacher

Situation: Student arrives late to class.

電車でんしゃが止とまって、遅刻ちこくしました。

"The train stopped, and I was tardy."

Why 遅刻ちこくしました: Institutional context requires institutional language. Even though the cause was the train, the student takes responsibility for the tardiness. 遅おくれた would be too casual for a teacher.

Station announcement

Situation: JR East station PA system.

上野うえの方面ほうめんの電車でんしゃは人身事故じんしんじこの影響えいきょうで遅延ちえんしています。

"Trains toward Ueno are delayed due to a passenger injury incident."

Why 遅延ちえんしています: Public, systemic, organizational. The train company uses 遅延ちえんする as its technical term. 遅おくれる would sound personal and inappropriate; 遅刻ちこくする is for people; 遅おそくなる is too casual for public announcement.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text: Grammar Point Core Meaning Agency / Fault Register Scope JLPT 遅おくれる To be delayed / run late Often personal; fault implied Casual–standard Individual N4 遅おそくなる To become late Neutral; schedule shift Casual–standard Event/time N4 遅刻ちこくする To be tardy / arrive late Personal; institutional fault Formal Individual within institution N4 遅延ちえんする To be delayed [systemic] Organizational; no personal fault Formal–technical System/organization N3 延のびる To extend / stretch Neutral; duration expands Standard Time period N3

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• 遅おくれる can be used for things, not just people. 電車でんしゃが遅おくれた is acceptable in casual speech, though 遅延ちえんした is more formal. The boundary: personal perspective vs. public announcement.
  • 遅刻ちこくする can be nominalized and counted. 遅刻ちこくは3回かいまで ("Tardiness is limited to 3 times"). This institutional countability is unique to 遅刻ちこく.
  • 遅延ちえん証明しょうめい書しょ is a cultural institution. Japanese train companies issue these to excuse workplace tardiness. The existence of this document reveals how 遅延ちえん (systemic) and 遅刻ちこく (personal) are socially separated — the system acknowledges its fault to protect the individual from institutional penalty.
  • 遅おそくなる often pairs with specific times. 30分ふん遅おそくなる, 1時間じかん遅おそくなる. The extent specification is common because the shift is measurable and predictable.
  • 遅おくれる + そう = anxious prediction. 遅おくれそう ("I think I'll be late") is a common anxious self-talk pattern. 遅刻ちこくしそう is more formal prediction; 遅延ちえんしそう is systemic prediction.
  • The rain exception. 雨あめで遅おくれた (I was delayed by rain) uses 遅おくれる despite external cause. But 雨うで遅刻ちこくした sounds like you're blaming the rain for your institutional tardiness — slightly less acceptable. 遅延ちえん is never used for rain affecting a single person.

docs.title: 1. Using 遅刻ちこくする for systemic delays
docs.text:
Scope error

電車でんしゃが遅刻ちこくした。

"The train was tardy." — Trains cannot be tardy; they are delayed. 遅刻ちこくする requires a person with institutional obligations.

Correct

電車でんしゃが遅延ちえんした。

"The train was delayed." — Systemic, appropriate.

docs.title: 2. Using 遅延ちえんする for personal lateness
docs.text:
Register/scope error

寝坊ねぼうして遅延ちえんした。

"I overslept and was systemically delayed." — Bizarre. You are not a train.

Correct

寝坊ねぼうして遅おくれた。

"I overslept and was late." — Personal, appropriate.

docs.title: 3. Using 遅おそくなる for personal tardiness
docs.text:
Category error

私わたしが遅おそくなる。

"I will become late." — Grammatically possible but pragmatically odd. People don't "become late" like schedules do; they "are delayed" or "are tardy."

Correct

私わたしは遅おくれる。

"I will be late." — Personal delay, standard expression.

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text: Collocation Meaning 寝坊ねぼうして遅おくれる "Oversleep and be late" — classic personal delay 電車でんしゃに遅おくれる "Miss the train / be late for the train" — personal 会議かいぎが遅おそくなる "The meeting runs late" — schedule shift 到着とうちゃくが遅おそくなる "Arrival becomes later" — predicted shift 遅刻ちこくしました "I was tardy" — formal institutional apology 無断むだん遅刻ちこく "Unauthorized tardiness" — institutional offense 遅延ちえんしています "Is delayed" — public announcement formula 大幅おおはば遅延ちえん "Major delay" — news/announcement 遅延ちえん証明書しょうめいしょ "Delay certificate" — institutional excuse document 遅刻ちこく扱あつかい "Treated as tardy" — institutional judgment

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N4 Use 遅おくれる for personal lateness; recognize 遅刻ちこくする and 遅延ちえんする in context; avoid mixing personal and systemic scopes Developing — N4→N3 Deploy 遅おそくなる naturally for schedule shifts; choose 遅刻ちこくする vs. 遅おくれる based on institutional context; read 遅延ちえんしています without conscious decoding Automatic — N3→N2 The four-way distinction becomes intuitive — you feel the wrongness of 電車でんしゃが遅刻ちこくした before analyzing why. You automatically match register to context. Mastery You control the full lateness spectrum with precision — using 遅おくれる for personal fault, 遅おそくなる for neutral shifts, 遅刻ちこくする for institutional apologies, and 遅延ちえんする for public announcements. You also know when to use 延のびる for duration expansion and when 遅おそい as adjective is sufficient.

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 遅おそい (i-adjective) — "Late" — descriptive state
  • 早はやい / 速はやい — "Early / Fast" — the temporal opposites
  • 間まに合あう — "To be in time" — the positive outcome
  • 間まに合あわない — "To not be in time" — functional equivalent to 遅おくれる

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 延のびる — "To extend" — duration expansion, not lateness
  • 伸のびる — "To stretch / extend" — physical or temporal expansion
  • 後あとれる — "To lag behind" — falling behind, not time-specific
  • 遅おくらせる — Causative of 遅おくれる — "To make late / delay [something]"

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• 遅おそい (N5) — Base adjective
  • Intransitive verbs (N5) — 遅おくれる as 自動じどう詞し
  • 〜なる (N5) — State change pattern
  • Suru-verbs (N4) — 遅刻ちこくする, 遅延ちえんする as formal verbs

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 延のびる / 伸のびる (N3) — Duration expansion beyond lateness
  • 後あとれる (N3) — Falling behind in sequence
  • 遅おくらせる (causative) (N4) — Making something late

docs.text: Kimi 2.6 Instant

docs.title: 大切 vs. 大事 — Both Mean 'Important', But How Are They Different?
Two Words for "Important" — But Not Quite the Same
大切たいせつ（たいせつ） vs. 大事だいじ（だいじ） — Choosing the Right "Important"
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — N4–N3

Both 大切たいせつ and 大事だいじ are introduced around N4 as basic vocabulary meaning "important" or "precious." The ability to distinguish their nuances — emotional weight, formality, and collocational preferences — is a lower N3 skill.

Tier Understanding What It Looks Like N4 — Recognition Both mean "important." Knows 大切たいせつな人ひと and 大事だいじなこと as set phrases. N4–N3 — Core ← You are here Nuance and collocation Can choose between 大切たいせつ and 大事だいじ based on emotional vs. practical nuance, formality, and common pairings. N3+ — Refinement Full productive distinction Feels the emotional undertone of 大切たいせつ in contrast to the pragmatic weight of 大事だいじ without conscious analysis.

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • 大切たいせつ (taisetsu) and 大事だいじ (daiji) both translate as "important," "precious," or "valuable." In many everyday sentences, they are interchangeable.
  • 大切たいせつ leans emotional — it often describes things that are personally precious, irreplaceable, or deeply valued on an emotional level: people, memories, feelings, bonds.
  • 大事だいじ leans practical — it often describes things of consequence, priority, or essential importance for a particular outcome: rules, tasks, practical considerations, health.
  • 大事だいじ is slightly more common in spoken, casual Japanese, while 大切たいせつ carries a slightly more formal, heartfelt, or literary tone.
  • Many collocations are fixed: you 大切たいせつにする a person or a relationship (cherish); you 大事だいじにする a possession or your health (take good care of). The choice of word signals the nature of the care.
  • Despite these tendencies, the two words overlap heavily. In many contexts, the choice is a matter of nuance and personal style, not grammatical correctness.

docs.title: 大切たいせつ（たいせつ）
docs.text:
大切たいせつ "Precious," "valued," "dear," "important" — often emotionally weighty.

大切たいせつ comes from 切きれ (to cut / to be close/pressing) combined with 大だい (big/great). The 切きれ implies something that is intimately close, something that would "cut" you if it were lost. This gives 大切たいせつ a strong emotional resonance — it describes things that are irreplaceable in a personal, heartfelt sense.

家族かぞくが何なにより大切たいせつです。 Family is more important than anything else. Emotional weight — family is personally precious. 彼かの女じょからもらった手紙てがみは、全すべて大切たいせつに取とってある。 I've kept every letter she gave me — they are all precious. Emotional value — not monetary, but irreplaceable.

docs.title: 大事だいじ（だいじ）
docs.text:
大事だいじ "Important," "significant," "crucial," "of great consequence."

大事だいじ combines 大だい (big) with 事こと (matter/thing) — literally "big matter." This gives it a pragmatic, consequential feel: things that are important because they matter for outcomes, for success, for wellbeing. It can be emotional, but the core is about weight and consequence.

健康けんこうが一番いちばん大事だいじだよ。 Health is the most important thing. Practical importance — health underpins everything else. 大事だいじな話はながあるから、後あとで時間じかんをもらえる？ I have something important to discuss — can I have some time later? Consequential — a matter that needs attention.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison — The Core Distinction
docs.text:
Axis 大切たいせつ (taisetsu) 大事だいじ (daiji) Emotional vs. Practical Emotionally precious — personal, irreplaceable, heartfelt. Practically important — consequential, essential, weighty. Tone Slightly more formal, literary, earnest. Slightly more casual, spoken, pragmatic. Common with… People, memories, feelings, bonds, time spent together. Health, rules, tasks, decisions, matters of business. When you lose it… It would break your heart. It would cause serious problems. English feel "Precious," "dear to me," "treasured." "Important," "crucial," "of consequence."

One-sentence rule of thumb

If you're talking about something irreplaceable that you love — a person, a memory, a feeling — 大切たいせつ often fits better. If you're talking about something that matters for a reason — a rule, a task, your health — 大事だいじ often fits better. But in countless everyday sentences, both are perfectly fine.

docs.title: Where They Are Fully Interchangeable
docs.text:
In a large number of contexts, the two words overlap completely. The difference is a matter of nuance — 大切たいせつ adds emotional warmth; 大事だいじ keeps it practical — but neither is wrong.

Context 大切たいせつ 大事だいじ "This is an important document." これは大切たいせつな書類しょるいです。 これは大事だいじな書類しょるいです。 Slightly more formal; emphasises the value of the document itself. Slightly more pragmatic; emphasises the document's importance for a purpose. "Friends are important." 友達ともだちは大切たいせつだ。 友達ともだちは大事だいじだ。 Emotionally warmer — friends are precious. A bit more matter-of-fact — friends matter. "It's important to keep a promise." 約束やくそくを守まもることは大切たいせつだ。 約束やくそくを守まもることは大事だいじだ。 Slightly more ethical — keeping promises is a cherished principle. Slightly more pragmatic — keeping promises matters for trust and consequence.

In these examples, a native speaker might use either without a second thought. The nuance exists, but it's subtle — the kind of distinction that adds colour but rarely changes the meaning.

docs.title: Where Only One Tends to Appear
docs.text: Some collocations strongly favour one word over the other, usually because the nature of the thing described (emotional vs. practical) pulls toward one nuance.

docs.title: 大切たいせつ is strongly preferred with…
docs.text: Collocation Meaning Why 大切たいせつ 大切たいせつな人ひと A precious person / someone dear Emotional bond — the person is irreplaceable. 大事だいじな人ひと is possible but slightly less tender. 大切たいせつな思おもい出で Precious memories Memories are emotionally held, not practical. 大事だいじな思おもい出で sounds slightly off. 大切たいせつに育そだてる To raise / nurture with care (a child, a plant) Nurturing is a tender, emotional act. 時間じかんを大切たいせつにする To value time / use time preciously Time is treated as something personally precious, to be savoured. 大事だいじにする also exists but is slightly more about efficiency.

docs.title: 大事だいじ is strongly preferred with…
docs.text:
Collocation Meaning Why 大事だいじ 大事だいじな話はなし An important matter to discuss Consequential — a decision, a problem, a serious conversation. 大切たいせつな話はなし exists but sounds more emotionally heavy. 大事だいじな用よう Important business / an important errand Practical purpose. 健康けんこうを大事だいじにする To take care of one's health Health is practically essential. 大切たいせつにする is also common here — this is an area of heavy overlap. お体からだを大事だいじに。 Take care of yourself. (common farewell) Set phrase — almost always 大事だいじに, not 大切たいせつに, in the stock farewell. 大事だいじな試合しあい An important match / game The match is consequential for a tournament, ranking, etc. 大切たいせつな試合しあい is possible but less common. 大事だいじなのは結果けっかだ。 What matters is the result. Pragmatic, outcome-focused. 大切たいせつ would add an emotional weight that doesn't fit.

お体からだを大事だいじに — a fixed phrase

The standard farewell お体からだを大事だいじに ("take care of yourself" / "look after your health") almost exclusively uses 大事だいじ, not 大切たいせつ. This is a deeply entrenched collocation — using 大切たいせつに here would not be incorrect but would sound unusual, like an intentional departure from the set phrase.

docs.title: 〜にする — "To Cherish" vs. "To Take Care Of"
docs.text:
Both words form the phrase 〜にする meaning "to treat as important / to value / to take care of." The nuance distinction carries through.

Phrase Core Nuance Typical Objects 大切たいせつにする To cherish, to treasure — emotional care People, relationships, memories, gifts, time, feelings 大事だいじにする To take good care of — practical care Possessions, health, one's body, tools, opportunities 彼かれを大切たいせつにしたい。 I want to cherish him. (emotional — love, care, holding dear) 体からだを大事だいじにしてください。 Please take care of your body / health. (practical — rest, eat well, etc.)

The difference is again one of shading. 大切たいせつにする can be used with health (体からだを大切たいせつにする) and sounds somewhat warmer; 大事だいじにする with a person can sound slightly more about protecting them from harm than about emotional treasuring. In many contexts, both work.

docs.title: Emotional vs. Practical — A Deeper Look
docs.text:
The 切きれ in 大切たいせつ — meaning "to cut" or "to be pressing" — suggests something that is close to you, nearly touching, that would cut if it were gone. This etymology informs the word's emotional charge. 大切たいせつなもの are things that, if lost, would leave an emotional wound.

The 事こと in 大事だいじ — meaning "matter" or "thing" — keeps the word grounded in affairs and consequences. 大事だいじなこと are matters that carry weight in the world. If you neglect them, there are practical consequences.

This is why: - A 恋人こいびとからのプレゼント is usually 大切たいせつ (precious) — the emotional value outweighs the practical. - A 仕事しごとの契約書けいやくしょ is usually 大事だいじ (important) — the legal and financial consequences are what matter.

docs.title: Formality and Register
docs.text:
Both words are standard Japanese and can appear in polite and casual settings. The nuance difference is not about politeness — it's about tone.

大切たいせつ 大事だいじ Formal speech ✅ Natural — sounds earnest and considered. ✅ Natural — sounds clear and pragmatic. Casual speech ✅ Natural — slightly heartfelt. ✅ Very natural — common and unmarked. Written (essays, letters) Favoured — adds emotional weight. Less common — can sound too blunt for emotional topics. Business Used for values, relationships, principles. Used for priorities, tasks, practical matters. Set farewell ❌ お体からだを大切たいせつに is understandable but not the standard. ✅ お体からだを大事だいじに is the set phrase.

docs.title: 1. Using 大事だいじ exclusively for everything
docs.text:
Missed nuance

祖母そぼからもらった指輪ゆびわを大事だいじにしている。

~Correct, but slightly pragmatic — as if the ring is a valuable asset to maintain.~

Warmer

祖母そぼからもらった指輪ゆびわを大切たいせつにしている。

~Warmer — the ring is emotionally treasured, not just looked after.~

docs.title: 2. Assuming they are always interchangeable
docs.text:
Although overlap is broad, some collocations are fixed. Using the wrong word in a set phrase can sound odd.

Set phrase broken

お体からだを大切たいせつに。

~Not wrong, but not the standard farewell. Native speakers almost always say 大事だいじ.~

Standard farewell

お体からだを大事だいじに。

~The natural, expected expression.~

docs.title: 3. Using 大切たいせつ for purely pragmatic, consequence-focused importance
docs.text:
Overly emotional

納期のうきを守まもることは大切たいせつだ。

~Grammatically fine, but 大切たいせつ adds an ethical/emotional weight that feels slightly off for a purely practical deadline.~

More natural

納期のうきを守まもることは大事だいじだ。

~大事だいじ suits the pragmatic, consequence-focused context — deadlines matter because of what happens if you miss them.~

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• 大事だいじ has a second, rarer meaning: "serious matter" or "great event." In compounds like 一いち大事だいじ (いちだいじ — a grave affair) or the expression 大事だいじに至いたる (to become a serious matter), 大事だいじ carries a sense of gravity and potential crisis that 大切たいせつ lacks. 大切たいせつ does not share this "serious problem" meaning.
  • 大切たいせつ is more common in song lyrics and poetry. The emotional resonance of 大切たいせつ makes it the preferred choice for expressing heartfelt value in artistic contexts. 大事だいじ appears in songs too but tends toward more grounded or upbeat themes.
  • The 切きれ in 大切たいせつ is the same as in 親切しんせつ (しんせつ, kindness) and 適切てきせつ (てきせつ, appropriate). It implies closeness, precision, and care — the idea of being "close to the heart" carries across all these words.
  • 大事だいじ is far more common in the phrase 大事だいじなこと ("important thing / matter of importance") when the tone is explanatory or instructional. 大切たいせつなこと appears too but often sounds slightly more philosophical.
  • When in doubt, you can often default to 大事だいじ for speech and 大切たいせつ for writing about feelings. This is a rough heuristic, not a rule — but it will guide you correctly more often than not.

docs.title: Extended Examples — 大切たいせつ & 大事だいじ in Context
docs.text: Japanese English 子こどもとの時間じかんを大切たいせつにしたい。 I want to treasure the time I spend with my children. Emotional — the time is precious and irreplaceable. 今日きょうは大事だいじな会議かいぎがあるから遅おくれられない。 I have an important meeting today, so I can't be late. Practical — the meeting has consequences. あなたにとって一番いちばん大切たいせつなものは何なんですか？ What is the most precious thing to you? Personal, philosophical — 大切たいせつ invites an emotional answer. 大事だいじなのは諦あきらめないことだ。 What's important is not to give up. Pragmatic advice — the importance is about the outcome. 彼女かのじょは私わたしの大切たいせつな友達ともだちです。 She is a dear (precious) friend of mine. Emotional bond — the friendship is treasured. 大事だいじな知しらせがあるから落おち着ついて聞きいて。 I have important news, so listen calmly. Consequential — the news will affect things.

docs.title: Quick Decision Guide
docs.text:
Ask yourself these questions to choose between 大切たいせつ and 大事だいじ:

  1. Is the importance mainly emotional or practical?

    • Emotional (love, memories, feelings, personal bonds) → lean toward 大切たいせつ.
    • Practical (consequences, tasks, rules, health as a matter of outcome) → lean toward 大事だいじ.

  2. Is it a set phrase?

    • お体からだを大事だいじに → 大事だいじ is standard.
    • Other set phrases: check collocations.

  3. What register fits the situation?

    • Casual conversation: 大事だいじ is slightly more common and unmarked.
    • Formal or heartfelt speech/writing: 大切たいせつ can add weight.

  4. Are you describing a person or a relationship?

    • Person/relationship as something treasured: 大切たいせつ is warmer.
    • Person as someone you need to protect or look after: 大事だいじ is possible.

  5. When both feel possible (which is often) — don't overthink it. You are unlikely to be wrong either way.

docs.title: Related Vocabulary
docs.text:
• 重要じゅうよう — "important" in a formal, objective sense (important documents, important roles). More distant and analytical than both 大切たいせつ and 大事だいじ.
  • 必要ひつよう — "necessary" / "needed." Practical requirement, not emotional value.
  • 貴重きちょう — "precious" in the sense of rare and valuable (a precious opportunity, a precious resource). Not for people or feelings.
  • 愛あいしい — "beloved" / "dear." Much stronger emotional weight than 大切たいせつ; used for people you deeply love.

docs.title: Same Family (for 大切たいせつ)
docs.text:
• 親切しんせつ — kindness (literally "close-cutting" — care that is near and personal)
  • 適切てきせつ — appropriate (literally "suitable-cutting" — fitting the situation closely)

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• Both are な-adjectives and can function predictively (大切たいせつだ, 大事だいじだ) or attributively (大切たいせつな, 大事だいじな).
  • 〜にする construction (N4) — to treat as / to make into.
